## Thread: illegal operation error — 5 this month / 7 total

_Thread started [2001-02](2001-02.md#t-815be58f67)._

#### ↳ Re: illegal operation error

- **From:** "Anthony Borla" <ajborla@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T10:10:21+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lpfn6.5638$v5.21012@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<V48n6.1863$U33.36944@weber.videotron.net>`

```
"Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca> wrote in message
news:V48n6.1863$U33.36944@weber.videotron.net...
> Hi!
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Eric,

If all it took to fix software problems was a simple recompile (as the book
you consulted appears to say) then a lot of programmers and support staff
would be unemployed; we simply wouldn't need them ! Of course things aren't
so rosy in the real world.

The 'illegal operation' message, a truly wonderful feature of the Windows
family of operating systems, is usually caused by:

* File corruption. For example, a corrupt '.EXE', which is why the book
  you consulted suggested a recompile.

  Of course, the 'corruption' may also be present in one of the run-time
  modules, for example, a '.DLL'  or '.OCX' file. This is why a
  complete reinstall of all relevant application files is sometimes
  recommended (in this case, the RM compiler and run-time).

* Corrupted Hardware Drivers. Where any drivers (printer, etc) recently
   added / removed / updated ? Same deal as above, just re-install.

* Hardware problems, particularly memory. Was your machine recently
  altered / upgraded ?

The above are the 'classic' sources of the 'illegal operation' message.
However, before taking any drastic steps, perhaps you should consider any
recent changes you made to your program. Consider:

* Do other compiled executables run-ok when recompiled ?

* Does it run ok without calling the sub-program ?

* Has the sub-program been executed stand-alone, (within a
  test-harness) and does it work correctly in this way ? The fact that
  a 'window' is being opened when you execute your program
  suggests the use of a 'system' call. If so:

  - Does the specified executable actually exist ?
  - Does it operate correctly stand-alone ?
  - Is the correct 'path' used to access it ?

* Have you carefully checked your 'Linkage Section' and other
  relevant parameter passing issues ?

The above are just a few ideas to help pinpoint the problem source. It is
difficult to provide more specific advice in the absence of a specific error
code / error message, and without knowing what your program is actually
doing.

I hope this helps.
```

```

Eric Gauthier wrote in message ...
>Hi!
>
>What may cause an "illegal operation ..." error.  When we call a
>sub-program, we keep getting a small windows from Windows OS.
>
Can you be more specific?  What version of RM/COBOL are you using? (see
www.liant.com for the latest versions and upgrades).  What operating system?
Does it happen to call to a COBOL program or an internal subprogram?

>In the RM-Cobol book, it says to recompile the program or just to retry to
>call the program.
>
In which RM/COBOL book?  It might be possible that the COBOL object is
corrupt. Recompiling would fix that.

-Rob
```

Thank's Rob for your answer.

> Can you be more specific?  What version of RM/COBOL are you using? (see
> www.liant.com for the latest versions and upgrades).

For the moment we are using RM/COBOL v5.36 for DOS.
We are trying to install the last version 7 for windows but we run into
other problem, we have to fix first.

>What operating system?
Novell

The error happen on the call of a sub program ( cobol .cob) wich manage our
print manager (we can view our print in basic text mode, fax it ...).
I have never encounter this error before.

I begin to think there it's a strange error in the program or this is a
memory error because here in development, it work well.  At the customer
office, I tried for fun the report with the windows runtime and it work just
fine?!?.

I will have to do some more tests.
Thank's anyway.

Eric.

#### ↳ Re: What are Spanned Records ?

Thanks for the reply people. As mentioned by an earlier post these spanned
records must be bad news as to the I-O overhead.

The largest record I have used is certainly no more that 4096 bytes except
for a general purpose screen enquiry which contains 8960bytes (32 * 280
byte records used as a form of 'Bucketing)'.

Just out of interest, what type of systems are you people writing where
such large records are required ?

"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk...
> What are they and what are they used for ? I've been writing Cobol apps
> for 15 years using unix and dos as the target op-sys. Only once have I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What are Spanned Records ?

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T22:06:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA6B0CD.6A72E466@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <97k3lg$9ai$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:
> > 
> Just out of interest, what type of systems are you people writing where
> such large records are required ?
> 

I have a Heritage system feed (meaning the system was written some time
ago and I use the data from that system)

It is a mainframe system that has a 32K record length - when I download
that record to relational tables - it creates 8 different tables - one
of the GROUP areas is repeated as many as 450 times (which creates one
very, very large master record).

	Susan A
```

#### ↳ Re: What are Spanned Records ?

- **From:** "Don Nelson" <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T17:21:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%ACn6.2$ou6.102@gazette.loc1.tandem.com>`
- **References:** `<97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Although the replies seemed to indicate that these apply to mainframes only,
that is not true.  Many mini-computers support them as well as other
machines.  They are not limited to IBM either.  The ANSI tape standard
defines such records as well (in a much better way than IBM).  The other
comments do say what they are so I won't go into that.  I might also mention
that they do not slow down I-O, they generally speed it up.  The bigger the
blocks the faster the I-O (normally).  And, if a record can span blocks you
have blocks that are consistent in size.  For example, if your block is 5000
characters and your records are 132 some of them will span a block.  That
is, you can pack lots of records in a block and only the first part of the
block and last part of the block will have spanned ones.  Or, you can have
giant records where one may span more than one block.

Don Nelson
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What are Spanned Records ?

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T03:34:42
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97n48m$obb$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<97hm1g$eag$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <%ACn6.2$ou6.102@gazette.loc1.tandem.com>`

```
How is record locking handled when using blocking ? Does the 'block' act
as a cache ? (Last one) Are there any differences code wise in working with
blocks other than the definition ?

"Don Nelson" <don.nelson@compaq.com> wrote in message
news:%ACn6.2$ou6.102@gazette.loc1.tandem.com...

5000

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

_Thread started [2001-02](2001-02.md#t-49fcba2487)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T00:47:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e_gn6.3269$%4.337026@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ga2s$d1l$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <z00n6.1166$%4.120236@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A9D06C6.6741A7D3@brazee.net> <4w8n6.1640$7Y1.214511@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A9D56BF.5311CDF1@neo.rr.com>`

```

"Steve Thompson" <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:3A9D56BF.5311CDF1@neo.rr.com...
> Jerry P wrote:
> <snip>
> > ## Yeah, but, see, some of our users are not as diligent about
> > immediately upgrading operating systems as we so we have to make
sure
> > our product works on their dinosaur operating systems (Win98,
WinNT,
> > etc.).
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> out there?

No, not particularily. But things might change. Suppose your vendors
or customers wanted to invoice or pay electronically and you decided
that it would really be neat to have an accounting package that could
accept this new-fangled idea (plus you wouldn't have to key 10,000
transactions/month). Further, you've found an accounting package that
accepts electronic funds transfer: it's only $500. BUT it is written
to run with the VPN facilities of W2K (another $200). Throw in a
couple of pieces of hardware (another $1000) for a total of $2000. You
eliminate two data entry clerks and please your vendors/customers.

>
> The problem is, with the PC world, this was not taken into
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Office, and won't be able to exchange documents as a result.

Actually, I think most Microsoft products are backward compatible - at
least for a generation or so. In my view, Microsoft has to be its own
biggest competitor. It has to develop new features to generate new
revenue. In this regard, Microsoft differs from IBM: IBM was/is in the
HARDWARE business; software was just something that helped to sell
hardware. In general, IBM had/has schizophrenic views toward making
efficient software (slow software helps sell faster hardware).
Remember, Computer Associates got its start by selling a sort routine
that out-performed IBM's sort.


>
> I much prefer to pay a license fee on an annual basis for my
> software.

Some agree. Others don't. They want to use the software forever - as
someone (who shall remain nameless) suggested in the opening
paragraph.

>
> Now, competition comes in.  The competitor to product xxx does
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> copy every 2-3 years.

Not necessarily. There are no doubt some who are reevaluating their
commitment to Fujitsu upgrades vis-a-vis FJ's latest change.

!
>
> In the business world, particularly manufacturing, you don't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (e.g., not enough room for both).

Another reason is that a new machine may do the same job as the old,
but do it cheaper/faster/better; consider a brace-and-bit vs. an
electric drill.

>
> So, since we all know that software wears out ;-) , what do we do
> to make/earn money on what is essentially a turn key situation?

Ah ha! The Microsoft conundrum! You either offer new features or have
the software commit suicide if the maintenance fee is not paid.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T15:02:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a9e6213.1425999@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ga2s$d1l$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <z00n6.1166$%4.120236@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A9D06C6.6741A7D3@brazee.net> <4w8n6.1640$7Y1.214511@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3A9D56BF.5311CDF1@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com> wrote:

>Jerry P wrote:
><snip>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>change to the latest and greatest operating system?  

Steve:

I agree wholeheartedly with you.  In my opinion, you should not have
to change, unless a real financial gain can be made from the change.
If a genuine quantitative analysis reveals that there is revenue to be
earned or costs to be reduced as a direct result of the change, then
by all means do so.

I believe that too many people view PC hardware and software from a
purely technical viewpoint.  They see a new feature in a new version
and instead of first considering the cost/benefit of acquiring the new
version, they upgrade just for the sake of upgrading.

When PC hardware and software are treated purely as a business asset,
the decision of whether or not to upgrade is made in a far more
intelligent manner.

Instead, many take the approach that it is important to have the
latest version only for the sake of having the latest version.

"If it ain't broke....don't fix it."


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>"Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
>> Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>I totally agree!  Can we spell G-U-I? :-)

Judson:

You are 100% correct.  Many of our customers would much prefer to keep
their software running on reliable UNIX boxes using character mode
terminals, but feel compelled to convert their UI to Windows because
of market forces.

To them, their is no technological gain at all.....but if that's what
the customer wants, then there is certainly a potential negative
impact on their revenues if they don't provide a GUI version.

The reasons for moving toward GUI in these cases are purely business
oriented and have nothing to do with "better technology."


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.

```

<a id="t-1e2044dfab"></a>
## Thread: Fileaid for DB2 — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Fileaid for DB2

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T03:13:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010228221323.07342.00000068@ng-fu1.aol.com>`

```
Doc had asked about this a bit back and I said I'd ask around the shop.  Well
everyone that uses it gives it a big thumbs up.  Much easier to use than QMF
and not as 'kludgy' as one of my cohorts put it especially for fixing rows on
the fly or adding rows.  I was watching my cubemate work on a table this
afternoon and it appears to be as easy as Fileaid for the rest of the world. As
the systems I'm supporting have very little DB2 in them (for the time being) I
haven't had the opportunity to use it myself mostly using QMF to do the queries
I required.

As for the cost that was another story.  Seems they (the company I'm consulting
at) leases the mainframe and all software so the cost of Fileaid is included in
a bundle number and not readily available.  It was suggested that if you use
multiple CA products you can probably negotiate a good bundle rate on all of
them including Fileaid for DB2 next time the contract is negotiated.

Hope this sheds some light on the subject.

Eileen
```

YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote:
>>From: docdwarf@clark.net  ( NA)

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>information.

This, indeed, seems the case... it has been my experience that the mention
of *any* price gets the initial response of 'That's too much money!' and
this response usually suffices in de-railing any further inquiry.

(Interestingly enough there are times when simply re-stating the sum to
which the 'too much money!' response has been given changes this
response... consider:

'Hey, (x) would be *really* helpful.'

'Well, we've gotten along without it so far!'

'This company is (n) years old... in 1973 they could have said that about
the computer, too.'

'Aaaaalllll riiiiiiiight (sound of mental filing-cabinet drawer 'storing
For Future Use')... how much is it gonna cost?'

'5,000 a year.'

'Five Thousand a *Year*?  That's Too Much Money!'

'Whaddaya talkin' about?  Five thousand a year is a hundred bucks a
week and that's twenty bucks a day... we have at least five people here
who'd use it regularly so that means a *maximum* cost of four bucks per
user per day... four bucks a day is what, a little better than bus-fare?
The cleaning-crew rides the bus every day, how's that Too Much Money?'

'Aaaaalllll riiiiiiiight (sound of mental filing-cabinet drawer 'storing
For Future Use')... lemme see what I can do about it.'

DD
```

as I did with my MF Sentinal-C. Saddly this is where the piracy
starts. Just a thought !

I am self-employed and do not wish to purchase several copies
of one development system. I do wish to have it in a useable
state on more than one machine although only being used on one
machine at a time.

I would imagine all of us in this ng use a main development
machine and carry laptops(which don't always have floppy drives)
while visiting clients or just taking work home.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T13:13:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister Munro <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk...
>
> I would imagine all of us in this ng use a main development
> machine and carry laptops(which don't always have floppy drives)
> while visiting clients or just taking work home.
>

One reason I like PowerBASIC's compliers is their policy on this. They put
right in the license that you may install or store as many copies of the
software on as many machines as you want, provided there is absolutely no
possibility of more than one copy per license being used at any one time.
This is a realistic and fair policy, and for "one-man shops" like me, a real
plus. (Kinda nice to be treated as a grownup).

MCM

Michael Mattias wrote:

>
> One reason I like PowerBASIC's compliers is their policy on this. They put
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That really sums up what we all want regardless of whether we are referring to
Fujitsu or Net Express, (where the initial discussion is directed). I don't have
the luxury of a desktop and a laptop, but clearly see the advantage of a
developer wanting to put his 'only copy' on the laptop as well as his desktop.
And of course, I think the reaction appearing in the messages here is primarily
from '"one-man shops".

Based on what you've written - over and above the legalese that vendors use
about copyright how about a statement in clear, plain English ,"You can do what
Michael  quotes above. But beware. If you cheat on us or carry out piracy, we
will sue the pants off you !".

PS: I loved how one person got taken in by marketing techniques. "If you pay
maintenance you get the next update as a freebie". No sir ! You've already paid
for it in writing your annual cheques for maintenance <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T01:25:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97ne4o$5t1q$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3A9E99ED.7FBDF469@home.com>`

```
Jimmy, Calgary AB wrote:

<snip>
PS: I loved how one person got taken in by marketing techniques. "If you pay
maintenance you get the next update as a freebie". No sir ! You've already
paid
for it in writing your annual cheques for maintenance <G>.
<snip>

Maybe I've worked on mainframes way too long, but based on the rapidly
changing technology in today's world, we have found that it is always more
cost effective to purchase maintenance contracts for software, than to buy
new versions.  I agree that it is a marketing ploy to some extent, but if
you call for support on a product and you aren't running the currently
supported version, your support often becomes "Gee, we can't help you
because that version is no longer supported".  Even though we may not always
need the latest, greatest bells and whistles, the current list price for the
Fujitsu Enterprise Edition 6.0 (excluding maintenance) is $2750.  Annual
maintenance is $500/year.  I think you'll find that 6.0 is drastically
different that 3.0 and over 4 years of development, one is still money
ahead, especially if you need technical support.  We all play the
"maintenance" game with PC operating systems.  When's the last time any one
purchased the full version of Windows 95, 98, 2000, etc?   If we are using
Windows as an operating system,  we pay the "maintenance" by buying the
upgrade versus the full version.   As it is with Windows, there is no real
requirement to upgrade from Windows 3.1 to Windows 2000, unless, of course,
you wish to use all the features of Windows 2000 and get any current
application to run properly.  Nor is there any requirement to upgrade to
Fujitsu 6.0.  In fact, since we don't need the new Web features at this
time, we may not even install the upgrade.  But it's nice to know that we
can if we choose to.

It is still disappointing to see prices spiraling upward, but trust me, they
are not even close to Mainframe Software license fees at this point in time.
Yet many companies are indeed replacing their Mainframes with Server/Client
PC based platforms.  We'll see how it all sorts out in the future, but for
now, I'd much rather import some legacy code into Fujitsu than to try to
rewrite the application in another language.  As someone said earlier, "If
it ain't broke, don't fix it"!

Denny
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)

Jimmy, Calgary AB wrote:


>PS: I loved how one person got taken in by marketing techniques.
>"If you pay maintenance you get the next update as a freebie".
>No sir ! You've already paid for it in writing your annual cheques
>for maintenance <G>.

Jimmy:

I agree.....nothing in life worth having is *ever* free.

That's why we are very careful to say that "when you are current on
maintenance, the upgrade is 'at no additional charge'"

....although I was guilty in the past of stating that the Fujitsu
compiler with Thane book was "free" until one very observant newsgroup
fellow pointed out to me that if you have to buy the book to get the
CD, it's not necessarily free.  I changed my mantra and now state that
the compiler is provided at no additional charge.

I am living proof that it is possible to teach an old dog new tricks!!




Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com

except NetExpress, I am allowed to have on my notebook and desktop system.
Fortunately, most of my work is in AS/400 shops, but when I do need to go
out on the road (doing work on PC's/networks), I am really out of luck.
Merant will not cooperate at all.  I tried for months, through every channel
I could find, to get the ability to load NetExpress on both my systems.
They finally wore me down and I gave up.

(By the way, what I do is use PCAnywhere to dial in to my development system
when I am at a client and realize that I need to make a change to a
program).

the Merant license.  First - Fujitsu will install and run for 30 days
without the license.  Second, you can MOVE the license without any
consultation with Fujitsu (you just better have a floppy disk! -
Although it might work on a shared drive, I have not tried) so when
you go on the road, you can transfer it to your laptop.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T05:46:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9F3592.35E08F57@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Sorry Thane,

You are confusing two issues - installation giving you the licence and runtime
fees - both are sore points to N/E users !.Just like F/J, N/E can be installed
and used for thirty days without getting the Release Key. In theory nothing to
stop you installing it on 30 machines !

Now when I think back to RM/COBOL '74 on the Radio Shack Model II - I'm guessing,
but  about $900 for the hardware and the RM compiler $300. Mind you, no support,
(although over the years I've found so-called 'support' to be of dubious value,
regardless of vendor). Compare that now to where the software so often out prices
the hardware !

Now rather than offer a vendor's solution - it should be come up with a
consumer's solution.

The customer is always right.

Jimmy

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> It seems that there is an assumption that the Fujitsu license is like
> the Merant license.  First - Fujitsu will install and run for 30 days
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "David Begley" <dave@begley.com.remove>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T13:46:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
NetExpress can be loaded for 30 days without the license key, and that is
great for times when you might have a total system failure.  The transfer
back and forth would be a great option.  That way, you could keep both
systems up to date with the latest version and just transfer when needed.

It's not really feasible to reload the whole product when you go out on the
road, because there are so many updates that have to be applied.

I hate to be so repetitive, but I really don't understand why Merant can't
recognize that someone is a single developer and work out some arrangement
to have two copies of the software working.  I would even pay a small
additional fee, but I am not going to pay an addition $3500 just to have a
copy of NetExpress on my notebook.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T08:45:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
"David Begley" <dave@begley.com.remove> wrote:
>
> I hate to be so repetitive, but I really don't understand why Merant can't
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> copy of NetExpress on my notebook.

Discuss this with your sales person.  Both myself and my associate
were allowed two keys for this purpose.  Ask for the two keys when
they want your money. :-)  I asked when I bought a $2500 upgrade,
and my associate asked when he bought NetExpress.  As I said in an
earlier post, I do respect their license and do not misuse the extra
key, though I would happily install NE on all the computers that only
I use, if I could.  I've been burned badly by problems with the NE
license system, and do not feel secure, even with it on two machines.
You see, once you use a particular machine for a 30 day backup, you
cannot do it again on that particular machine, because NE remembers
the original install date.  This is true even if you install a later
version than the one originally installed.  Note: a side effect of
this is that if you have ever done a trial of NE on your computer,
you can't do another one with a later version.  To me, this was a bad
decision on MERANT's part, objections to restrictive licensing aside.

I found out all these little details in a frustrating attempt to get
NE 3.1 up and running when the 3.1 update trashed both 3.0 and 3.1 on
both the machines I had installed 3.0 on. <gnashing teeth>  I must
say that MERANT was quick to supply two new response keys to get 3.0
back up and running, but I had to wait several months before a fix
was available so that I could upgrade to 3.1.  I couldn't install
3.1 because it kept crashing when installed on any machine on which
3.0 had been previously installed.  This one fiasco cost me more in
wasted time and effort than NetExpress costs.  If you think I am
being paranoid, just wait until you get burned.  When you start
suffering hundreds and thousands of dollars in lost revenue, and
inability to deliver a product that requires an upgrade you have
purchased but cannot use, all because of a restrictive licensing
fiasco that isn't your fault, it helps you to see this issue from a
different perspective.

I must add that, other than with MERANT's licensing policy, I am
very happy with NetExpress.  It is purely the licensing policy with
which I take issue.
```

> You see, once you use a particular machine for a 30 day backup,
you

remembers

later

applications,

any updates
you have made on windows itself.

and delete. Like  FUJITSU or something. ;)  Also, forcing the install into
a known path is a good way to  deal with that craziness as well, rather
than letting it default into c:\Program Files\xxxx.

-Paul

- **From:** praul@isot.com

- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl> <97sj0s$lfr$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3aa1fe16$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3AA57D8D.E253D5C@cinci-rr.com>`

```
Try removing all the registry entries and re-isntalling. 
It isn't worth it to a pirate, because they would simply crack the
security. (Belive me, if any cracker had an interest in it, it would not
survive more than 1/2 a day.) 

The whole thing that bugs me is the real purpose of this licensing 
software is to keep honest people honest - not to deter crackers.  A
monitoring system that reported license usage and status would be much
more effective, and profitable. 

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Best Efficient Coding Tips?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** praul@isot.com
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T22:27:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aa5ba24$1$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl> <97sj0s$lfr$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3aa1fe16$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3AA57D8D.E253D5C@cinci-rr.com> <3aa5994a$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com>`

```
Hi folks.

I was going through my notebook today, looking for an algorith to solve a
particuarly nasty transactional problem, when I realized that I had quite
a few tips to myelf in there. It got me to thinking that this group
probably has the sun-total of all knowledge about COBOL tips and tricks to
make programs more efficient. 

So I figured I would post a couple of mine, and ask you all to post a
couple of your favorites. That way we call update our notebooks and maybe
learn something new. Besides, it might just be fun. :) 

My first favorite COBOL trick is put traces in the data division. I don't
often have to deal with COBOL dumps anymore, but when I do, it sure helps.
I tend to use 05 level items with descriptive names. 

My second favorite little COBOL trick is to use reference modification for
'standard' messages.  For example, if I write a program that initializes a
couple hundred files, I will use the same messge for each file and simpy
replace the name. Takes a bit of forethought, but saves a  lot of typing. 

My third favorite is pretty controversial I suppose, but tend to keep the
entire schema for an  application in one copy file. That way, I can
'cheat' and use the compiler to find what is going to break when I change
a data item. (Of course, this is the same way it works when I change a 
DataBase field, so it is only appropriate to do it in VSAM. :)  It makes
the data segments of a program a little larger than it has to be, but
since it doesn't have any effect on execution speed, I feel it is a good
tradeoff. 

I look forward to hearing from you guys about your favorites. :)  -Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Efficient Coding Tips?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T22:54:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<984ev2$6d94$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl> <97sj0s$lfr$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3aa1fe16$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3AA57D8D.E253D5C@cinci-rr.com> <3aa5994a$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3aa5ba24$1$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com>`

```
1.  I also like to keep a "trace table" in working storage.  It contains a
list of the most recently executed 64 paragraph names.  By using redefines,
I can shift the previous 63 paragraph names up one occurrence and add the
current one to the bottom of the stack using 2 move statements and NO
SUBSCRIPTS OR INDEXES.  At the end of the program (or in the case of a
programmed premature ending), one can optionally "dump" the trace table or,
in the case of an abend, the table shows up in the dump.  In either case,
it's available.
2.  Before doing any SEARCH or SEARCH ALL, I check the results of the
previous search by comparing the current search argument against the
previous search argument.  If they are the same, there's no need to execute
the search.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Efficient Coding Tips?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T08:17:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA6511B.650F06B7@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl> <97sj0s$lfr$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3aa1fe16$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3AA57D8D.E253D5C@cinci-rr.com> <3aa5994a$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3aa5ba24$1$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <984ev2$6d94$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```


Terry Heinze wrote:

> 1.  I also like to keep a "trace table" in working storage.  It contains a
> list of the most recently executed 64 paragraph names.  By using redefines,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> it's available.

Tight loops can screw this up.   One way around this is to set the paragraph
name before the perform, not within the loop.  This would be more efficient and
you can slightly change the name of the paragraph for each perform.  After all,
you care more about how you got to the paragraph than where you are.   Another
way would be to include a counter.  If the paragraph is the same, increment it.
You could combine the two.

You still have the trouble where you perform a paragraph 50,000 times which
performs another paragraph.  I suppose if you're willing to live with the
inefficiency, you can do a lot. Have a two level paragraph name for instance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Efficient Coding Tips?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T23:35:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9875nb$4tqa$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl> <97sj0s$lfr$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3aa1fe16$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3AA57D8D.E253D5C@cinci-rr.com> <3aa5994a$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3aa5ba24$1$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <984ev2$6d94$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <3AA6511B.650F06B7@brazee.net>`

```
Agreed.  Being aware of this, I add the paragraph name to the table at the
beginning of the paragraph EXCEPT when looping; in that case I add just one
entry that says "    FETCH (MULTIPLE TIMES)" before the paragraph, so my
trace table entries look like this:
.
.
.
A10-INITIATE
M10-BUILD-COST-CATEGORY-TABLE
S70-OPEN-CSTCURS
         FETCH (MULTIPLE TIMES)
M10-END
S72-CLOSE-CSTCURS
M20-GET-TOLL-RT
.
.
.
I do not add the paragraph names of fetches (in this case,
S71-FETCH-CSTCURS) to the trace table.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Best Efficient Coding Tips?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T23:58:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<984ihh$hbo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl> <97sj0s$lfr$1@sshuraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3aa1fe16$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3AA57D8D.E253D5C@cinci-rr.com> <3aa5994a$2$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3aa5ba24$1$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com>`

```
If you are talking about "efficiency" - my first suggestion is READ what
your specific vendor says about it (for your particularly environment).

I notice references to "dumps" and "VSAM" in your note.  This makes me guess
(assume?) that you are working on an MVS (OS/390 or z/OS) environment.
There are some SPECIFIC IBM suggestions on "efficiency" in the IBM COBOL
Programming Guide.  The interesting thing is that SOME of them apply to most
COBOLs - but others do not.  Therefore, I *strongly* suggest that when/if
you work in other COBOL environments (even if "porting" an existing
application) that you learn what THAT vendor suggests.

Just as an example,
   compare TRUNC/NOTRUNC COMP/COMP-5 issues on IBM mainframes vs PCs vs Unix

Similarly, compare the recent threads about "segmentation numbers".  There
are SOME COBOL implementations that do a MUCH better job of "memory"
management than you can (when you specify "priority" numbers in paragraphs).
On the other hand, there are still some environments where user priority
numbers may lead to real run-time efficiencies.

If you are talking about "development" efficiencies, then I think my best
advice is ALWAYS learn the tools that are available to you.  Don't just do
"debugging" the way that you always have - but take the time to learn what
other tools are available at your site or in your environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-05T07:38:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA3A4F8.7FD2D3EF@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl>`

```
The trouble with selling an easily duplicated product is that you need to
develop rules different from selling a hammer.

It seems that there are some choices:

1.  You limit it to being used by one person.
2.  You limit it to being used on one computer.
3.  You limit it to having one usable copy.
4.  You make them pay for what the tool produced.
5.  You make the product difficult enough that you can make your money in
support.

The easiest of these to enforce is #2.  None of these is ideal, but we all know
how much stealing is done of software, so I can understand the desire to make
stealing difficult.

So if you have a product with a relatively limited customer base, so you must
charge a significant price to pay for your R&D, what policy should you have to
discourage thievery?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-05T09:22:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oaOo6.109$642.7767@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl> <3AA3A4F8.7FD2D3EF@brazee.net>`

```
Or, you make your product so useful and easy to use, and so economical
that you significantly increase your customer base.  You also make your
support so useful that people will be happy to pay the maintenance fees
to get the support and continued updates.

Just remember, if people stop buying your product because they find it
or your copy protection too onerous to use, it doesn't matter how good
your copy protection is. :-)
```

Judson McClendon wrote:

> Or, you make your product so useful and easy to use, and so economical
> that you significantly increase your customer base.  You also make your
> support so useful that people will be happy to pay the maintenance fees
> to get the support and continued updates.

It happened with Turbo Pascal, for a while.  I don't see that a CoBOL compiler
company has much leeway here.   The best CoBOL compiler will take market share from
other CoBOL compilers, but in order to make a profit by selling 1 out of 4 compilers
out there, it would need to find 4 times as many customers.  It ain't gonna happen.


> Just remember, if people stop buying your product because they find it
> or your copy protection too onerous to use, it doesn't matter how good
> your copy protection is. :-)

Or if they stop buying your product because they have no morals and decide that
making one copy is not really stealing.   After all, you designed it to be copied!

I don't know the solution.  I do know that I would be reluctant to stay in the
business of providing CoBOL compilers.    Too bad, I want there to be a healthy
business and good competition there.

> --

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T16:46:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Yv8p6.31176$c7.9642160@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ki58$rsp$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <cWrn6.58$DI4.34718@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <FVwn6.842$YC1.769143@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3a9f268d.2860775@news1.attglobal.net> <ZuNn6.5514$Fz.1616000@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <MlOn6.2329$e37.121365@news3.atl> <3AA3A4F8.7FD2D3EF@brazee.net> <oaOo6.109$642.7767@news2.atl> <3AA3C372.AE40D3A9@brazee.net> <3aa46f67$1$cenhy$mr2ice@news.tpe.isot.com> <3AA4EC04.E6BF88F9@brazee.net>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3AA4EC04.E6BF88F9@brazee.net...
> praul@isot.com wrote:
>
> > How many people steal language compilers? And out of the few who  do
steal
> > language compilers, how many of them ever see any
> > profit from it?
>
Sadly, several of my company's clients have stolen development tools,
including compilers and seen profit, or at least reduced cost from doing so.
They purchase some number of licenses and start development. Then they add a
team member and need another copy. They follow the path of expedience and
install another copy. Maybe they intend to make it legal, but it gets
forgotten. Another member is added, and another copy is needed. Eventually
the person who understood the licensing issues leaves and nobody can
remember the details. Time passes and the entire corporation is using the
product, but they've only paid for a handful of copies.

In theory, the vendor could seek damages in court. In practice, the process
is long, expensive, and detrimental to future sales opportunities. Many
years ago 60 Minutes ran a story about a small software company that sold a
copy of it's product to a large corporation.  The corporation tweaked it
then resold the package to others, making a profit and stealing potential
customers from the small software company. The small company sued, won, and
has yet to collect a dime for their troubles. Ironically, the large
corporation was the U.S. Department of Justice.

I am not a fan of any licensing scheme that issues a perpetual license but
fails to provide a means for insuring that said license can, in fact, be
used in perpetuity. That's the big weakness with MERANT's scheme. Dongles
circumvent the problem but are inconvenient and unpopular in North America.

Maybe biometrics will alleviate the problem in the future. Licenses could be
tied to a user rather than the hardware being used. Granted there's the
problem of losing your finger or eye...

Karl
```

> In theory, the vendor could seek damages in court. In practice, the
process
> is long, expensive, and detrimental to future sales opportunities. Many
> years ago 60 Minutes ran a story about a small software company that sold
a
> copy of it's product to a large corporation.  The corporation tweaked it
> then resold the package to others, making a profit and stealing potential
> customers from the small software company. The small company sued, won,
and
> has yet to collect a dime for their troubles. Ironically, the large
> corporation was the U.S. Department of Justice.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> circumvent the problem but are inconvenient and unpopular in North
America.
>
> Maybe biometrics will alleviate the problem in the future. Licenses could
be
> tied to a user rather than the hardware being used. Granted there's the
> problem of losing your finger or eye...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T04:41:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3a9f22bb.1883096@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl>`

```
On Mon, 26 Feb 2001 14:44:49 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>Pardon me if this topic has been discussed recently; I have not
>been reading the newsgroup closely for several weeks.

Me neither, as I've been busy working....

However, after reading the messages in this thread I would like to
offer some facts that seem to be overlooked.

1.  The runtime is Free to distribute.  You can easily test your
applications on as many machines and OS versions as you desire, simply
install the runtime.

2.  The license is transferable to your laptop from your desktop and
back.

3.  Disaster Recovery - If your hard drive fails and you have to
reinstall, guess what?  You have THIRTY DAYS of unlimited use to get
the license issue straightened out.  You are not in a lurch.

4.  If you require multiple simultaneous access on multiple computers,
then you can buy multiple licenses.  Fujitsu makes money selling
software and maintenance.  They have NO RUNTIME FEES.  They are
attempting to protect that revenue stream and to stem abuse.  Users
who do not abuse the license have nothing to be concerned about.

5.  What if Fujitsu goes out of business?  Those of you using Merant
products and AcuCOBOL products are in the same boat - you can't
reinstall without a license.  If Fujitsu DOES go out of business, its
time to find a different compiler.

6.  Lastly - to the few of you who are saying that because Fujitsu
requires a license manager, it's time to choose a different
development tool and to give up COBOL - listen to yourselves.  How
silly is that?  Start pricing Microsoft or Oracle site licenses
sometime.  

I see limited exposure here.  If we want a good, clean, inexpensive
compiler it has to be supported.  ONE way to help keep it supported is
to respect the license.  I am sure Fujitsu would not have done this if
they had no evidence of license abuse.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T08:19:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IZNn6.2322$e37.120442@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <3a9f22bb.1883096@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> back.

Slow, clumsy, and irritating.  Does your wife ask permission from
the manufacturer of her mixer if she takes it somewhere else?

> 3.  Disaster Recovery - If your hard drive fails and you have to
> reinstall, guess what?  You have THIRTY DAYS of unlimited use to get
> the license issue straightened out.  You are not in a lurch.

Aha! Aha! Aha!  *Only* if the software *has never* been installed
on that particular machine before.  Once you use your notebook as
a backup, you don't have the 30 days on it anymore.

> 4.  If you require multiple simultaneous access on multiple computers,
> then you can buy multiple licenses.  Fujitsu makes money selling
> software and maintenance.  They have NO RUNTIME FEES.  They are
> attempting to protect that revenue stream and to stem abuse.  Users
> who do not abuse the license have nothing to be concerned about.

Runtime fees are stupid anyway.  It's like a hammer company charging
a fee if I sell anything I built with the hammer.  A compiler is a
tool, and its use should be as free as any other tool.  Would you
buy a physical tool from anyone who insisted on such a restrictive
use policy?  I doubt it. :-)

> 5.  What if Fujitsu goes out of business?  Those of you using Merant
> products and AcuCOBOL products are in the same boat - you can't
> reinstall without a license.  If Fujitsu DOES go out of business, its
> time to find a different compiler.

This thread isn't just about Fujitsu, it's about restrictive licensing
policies in general.  Fujitsu triggered this by shutting the door on
the last available high quality compiler without such a restriction.

> 6.  Lastly - to the few of you who are saying that because Fujitsu
> requires a license manager, it's time to choose a different
> development tool and to give up COBOL - listen to yourselves.  How
> silly is that?  Start pricing Microsoft or Oracle site licenses
> sometime.

I don't have to buy a site license to use Microsoft products, because
I am a single developer.  If I need two copies of something, I just
buy two copies.  I currently have an associate working in my office
with me using NetExpress.  MERANT was kind enough to permit me two
keys to install NetExpress on my desktop and notebook, and I could
have let my associate use one of them.  But we never considered doing
it, and bought another copy of NetExpress for him.  We were happy to
comply with MERANT's rights.  But we have had so much aggravation with
their licensing system, you could often hear the gnashing of teeth in
my office.

> I see limited exposure here.  If we want a good, clean, inexpensive
> compiler it has to be supported.  ONE way to help keep it supported
> is to respect the license.

I do respect their license.  I just don't appreciate their making me
jump through hoops and cause me lots of unnecessary trouble and expense
because they don't respect my honesty.

Look, the recording industry has been dealing with this issue for
decades, and wailing all the time that they will be ruined by it.
Illegal copying must be many times worse in the recording industry
than here, yet it seems to me the recording industry is doing very
well.  I abhor theft as much as anybody, and have spoken out against
software piracy many times.  But I also despise dealing with people
who are not interested in providing me with a product that I can
freely use without needless trouble and expense.  They are free to
do as they wish, but so am I.  I do not have to buy their products,
because a free market has provided other products without this
annoyance.  Which is, of course, the whole point of this thread. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "DENNIS A BROUSE" <DENDEN@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-05T08:23:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9803ej$1f38$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <3a9f22bb.1883096@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
To All,

As I noted before, the restoration of the product on a different machine is
my major concern with the new licensing scheme.  So I'll try to make some
time to install 6.0 on my machine, do my normal backup process, then try to
restore the backup set at our disastery recovery center.  The "30 days to
work out the licensing" is clumsy, in my opinion, especially if there have
been updates/patches to the original release.  Accepable in a pinch, maybe,
but certainly not ideal.  Believe me, it's tough enough to go through a
restore on a different "plug and pray" machine in the first place, let alone
try to figure what you've done with the original product since install.
Most other products restore fine from the  backup, with a little nudging and
registry work, and I have a hunch that Fujitsu will too.  Once I find out
for sure, I'll report back to the group.

The other product I mentioned in an earlier post, actually marked a specfic
unused sector of your hard drive bad, wrote the sector number on the setup
floppy. Then the unstall process looked for this sector before it would
uninstall or re-install again.  Obviously, not a real restorable solution.
I was about to strangle the support person who kept saying, "Sir, you must
uninstall the product before you can install it on another PC.".

Stay tuned!

Denny
```

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

- **Message-ID:** `<3aa6d408.159844655@news1.attglobal.net>`

for yearly maintenance on the extra licenses.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T16:09:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vrTp6.4045$L67.97507@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <3aa6d408.159844655@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
> For those of you following this thread  -- For small developers who
> want to take a copy on their laptops and the like - Fujitsu recently
> announced that you can purchase additional licenses simply by paying
> for yearly maintenance on the extra licenses.

Wow!  Charge me another extravagant maintenance fee (I never call for
support anyway, except when I have already determined that there is a
bug in the compiler) to use what I have already paid for.

As far as I'm concerned, that's simply adding insult to injury.  At
least, MERANT permits me to do that without extra charge.

I have another anecdote to add to the growing list of my gripes about
this issue.  I have bought some new hardware for my office, and I am
in the midst of moving computers, installing larger drives, etc.  I
was putting in a larger, faster drive, and a high speed ULTRA IDE
controller in my primary machine (which will soon become my backup
machine), and I used Partition Magic to copy all of the partitions
from the old drive to the new one.  All was fine, and NE worked fine
on the new drive, license intact.  Since the new drive was connected
to the new (secondary) controller, I (being a good boy) reconnected
the old drive temporarily to uninstall NE from the old drive.  The
system booted from the old drive on the primary controller, but could
still see the new drive.  I uninstalled NE as promised, removed the
old drive, put the system together and booted on the new drive.  I
was amazed to discover that NE uninstall had removed *both* copies,
even on the inactive but visible new drive!  I thought I had saved a
text file with the Revoke Key, just in case, but I can't find it.
Every time I turn around, this thing is driving me nuts!  Poked in
the eye for trying to do the right thing!  If I can't find the Revoke
Key, I will have to beg MERANT for another response key, and I will
have to reconfigure the compiler again in any case.  Note that since
NE has already been installed on the machine, the 30 days of use is
not available anymore.  I am dead in the water until I get a Response
Key.  I am getting really, really sick and tired of this!

You know, if somebody with enough money pressed this issue legally,
I'm not sure MERANT and Fujitsu could get away with it in the U.S.
A few years ago the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the buyer of any
software package had the *right* to make up to two backup copies of
any software, *no matter what the licensing agreement said*.  Since
a copy of the install CD is worthless without the Response Key, I
believe it could be reasonably argued that an install disk that can
not be freely used to restore your software does not constitute a
true backup, because the buyer is constantly at the mercy of the
vendor's whim and stability.  If I cannot restore my system on my
own, I do not have honest-to-goodness true backup.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T09:09:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cm6q6.8191$642.99983@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <3aa6d408.159844655@news1.attglobal.net> <vrTp6.4045$L67.97507@news1.atl> <3aa82dfb.248410646@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> available at a MUCH discounted rate.  I see no foul here.

It depends on how you define 'foul'.  I agree, of course, that the
COBOL vendors have the right to license their products as they see
fit, within the bounds of the law.  My question is "Is their policy
both wise and fair to the consumer?"  I believe it is neither.  It
certainly aggravates any customer who experiences problems such as
mine, which is bad business, and cannot be called 'wise'.  When a
vendor *willfully* employs a policy that costs a completely innocent
customer much time and money and puts them at the mercy of the vendor
in such a way as to be a potential threat to the customer's very
ability to do business, this cannot be called 'fair' or 'wise'.

My guess is that if you had experienced problems similar to mine,
your opinion would be a bit closer to mine as well. ;-)  How much
sympathy would you have for these policies if you had personally
lost $6,000 to $8,000 out of your own pocket, entirely because of
them, when you were completely innocent of any wrongdoing?  Even
if it's to a large company that can easily afford the loss, it is
still neither fair nor wise.  If these restrictive policies are not
stopped, the companies involved will pay dearly for it, you can be
assured.  A company simply cannot continue to play hardball with
its customers and survive, long-term.  This applies to Microsoft
as well.  Many of Microsoft's policies are causing such a ground
swell of animosity that, as soon as a real alternative is presented,
will cost Microsoft greatly.  If you look at any company that has
been in business and flourished for many years, you will find either
a monopoly or a company that people *like* doing business with.
This principle does not always work quickly, but it is as sure and
certain as gravity.

For the first time, I have become gravely concerned about the
survival of COBOL as a development language, not only in the long
term, but in the short term as well.  No one in this newsgroup could
be more grieved than I over such a sad and unnecessary occurrence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T13:00:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8Ruq6.5639$887.137272@news3.atl>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <3aa6d408.159844655@news1.attglobal.net> <vrTp6.4045$L67.97507@news1.atl>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>
> I have another anecdote to add to the growing list of my gripes about
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Key.  I am getting really, really sick and tired of this!

Just an update.  I *did* save the Revoke Code, and in fact, I saved the
text file to a diskette.  When I sent it to MERANT, along with the new
Request Code, they promptly (same day) returned a Response Key.  I
applaud MERANT for trying to give good service, and I really like Net
Express.  My associate Mark, who also bought NE to work with me, likes
it very much, and dreads moving back to mainframe programming. :-)
But we both hate their licensing key system.

I know one way we could get both MERANT and Fujitsu to back off the
restrictive key thing with absolute certainty, but I don't know if
we could organize it.  If we could get a really substantial portion
of their customer base to contact both companies and adamantly refuse
to do any more business with them until they dropped the key thing,
MERANT & Fujitsu would have to yield or get out of the COBOL business.
Fujitsu has only just started this practice, and a sharp response from
their customers right mow might make a dent.  Anybody think there is
even a tiny chance of organizing such a thing?
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> breath.

Ah, but I *did* submit my gripes to members of the J4 committee, as
suggested in a message posted by Bill Klein.  My comments were snubbed
off as if I were some peasant approaching royalty.  This resulted in a
number of unpleasant email exchanges which I have no interest in
repeating.  It did teach me, I believe, why the new standard has been
so long delayed and why it has so many lame-brained things in it, while
leaving out many simple but important things.
  I have met many people
more concerned with procedure than substance.  They rarely, if ever, do
anything sensible, let alone do anything worthwhile.  Just say I am no
longer surprised. :-)

Considering how things are going in the trenches right now, I suspect
the draft standard will become moot, if it isn't already.  "A day late
and a dollar short" hardly begins to cover it. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T02:09:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aacdac3_3@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <3aa6d408.159844655@news1.attglobal.net> <vrTp6.4045$L67.97507@news1.atl> <8Ruq6.5639$887.137272@news3.atl> <3AAA9A51.82735AEC@home.com> <uLyq6.9691$642.127788@news2.atl> <3AAB0BA1.95B397E3@home.com> <3aac52cf_1@news.newsfeeds.com> <3AAC7D34.B17EA2A9@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <3AAC7D34.B17EA2A9@home.com>...
>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ah, Jimmy. You're always one jump ahead...<G>  mind you, I had a beer with a
bloke who knew a taxi driver who drove a J4 person to the airport...does
that count?

While being dead doesn't have a lot to recommend it, I agree that I would
consider it preferable to sitting on an impotent committee, achieving
nothing, and spending most of my time doing paperwork.   Hmmmm...ever worked
for IBM?<G>

Maybe we should start a thread on things that are worse than being dead...

1. Being married to a shrewish woman for fifty years or so...

2. Having your wife choose your clothes.

3. Spending 20 years imagining your child saying: "I gratefully accept this
Nobel prize on behalf of my colleagues and the team..."

Then actually hearing: "Would you like fries with that?"

4. Hearing your daughter say:" I know he's trash, but I can change him..."

5. Watching your favourite Programming Language being strangled to death by
a Bureaucracy that can't even see what they're doing wrong, won't listen
when anyone tells them, and then get petulant when the User Community is not
delighted with their performance... Irrespective of who wins the current
argument about re-affirmation, the ANSI COBOL committee has been irreparably
damaged and (at least in the eyes of some of us) has lost all credibility.
Even though I believe COBOL will survive this, it would certainly have been
better for all concerned if it had never happened.

Pete.

hierarchy, regalia, and pomp. The avowed 'goal' is often an excuse to
get organized.

Compare the Shriners and the PTA.

<a id="t-76924a4900"></a>
## Thread: Creaky Obsolescent Boring Old Language — 21 msgs

### Creaky Obsolescent Boring Old Language

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <dleahy@wave.home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T15:23:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OPtn6.342190$JT5.11384022@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Twenty years ago when I started programming, I was advised by several
seemingly well-informed people in the DP (as it was called then) business to
stay away from COBOL because it was a dying language.  "It's too wordy.. and
besides we're all going to be replaced by code generators anyway".

Over the years, the "value" of this advice has become obvious to all of us
who, perversely, like to use a business oriented programming language to
solve business problems.

Obviously, COBOL has an image problem.  One solution is to follow the lead
of the university that, once they renamed a course from "Requirements
Analysis for Information Systems" to "Requirements Analysis for e-Business",
found that the course went from moribund to over-subscribed.

I therefore propose that we rename our favorite language to COmmon
e-Business Oriented Language:  COeBOL.

;-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Creaky Obsolescent Boring Old Language

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiammD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T21:26:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9EBE8E.A13C42B5@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<OPtn6.342190$JT5.11384022@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Don Leahy wrote:
> 
> Twenty years ago when I started programming, I was advised by several
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> e-Business Oriented Language:  COeBOL.

I think you're onto something here. How about eCOBOL? We could hire the
Stones for the splashy product release, etc. We need more flash, less
substance.

LiamD :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Creaky Obsolescent Boring Old Language

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T04:20:52
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<97o86n$r6t$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<OPtn6.342190$JT5.11384022@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Doh !

What language isn't ?

What is outstandingly good about Cobol is its way of defining data... 8)
There is also the question of performance / machine requirements and
compiler availability all of which the younger ciders out there seem to
miss. That said, I can see my self moving all my code from Cobol to C
(not the visual kind) simply because the language lacks any real support
for doing low-level control and I can't see writing a 'converter' being that
difficult as I use the laungauge almost as a macro assembler.

"Don Leahy" <dleahy@wave.home.com> wrote in message
news:OPtn6.342190$JT5.11384022@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Twenty years ago when I started programming, I was advised by several
> seemingly well-informed people in the DP (as it was called then) business
to
> stay away from COBOL because it was a dying language.  "It's too wordy..
and
> besides we're all going to be replaced by code generators anyway".
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

e-Business",
> found that the course went from moribund to over-subscribed.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Creaky Obsolescent Boring Old Language

- **From:** Enoch Jones <e902j592@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-04T19:41:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA29A0B.1799917B@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<OPtn6.342190$JT5.11384022@news20.bellglobal.com> <97o86n$r6t$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Uhm, one reason COBOL was introduced to commerce is that it was
an abstraction from low-level bit flipping (literally, on some of the older
hardware from what the grey-haired shamans tell me), and while C is
a must for writing some of the low-level bit-mangling work, I would
shudder to see code for a payroll app in C, [although it has been done
using assembler and Univac, I hear], Fortran or Basic.

I would also not like to see a simulation using advanced mathematic
algorithms written in COBOL or C.

ej
Alister Munro wrote:

> Doh !
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

#### ↳ Re: Creaky Obsolescent Boring Old Language

- **From:** foodman <foodman_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T13:57:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fFNn6.982$45.1055@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<OPtn6.342190$JT5.11384022@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
>Obviously, COBOL has an image problem.  

Hi:

True, image is everything.

In order to appeal to the slack-jawed youth of today, we need
to use their lingua franca, something which will appeal to their
'Dude' mentality, how about: COol Biz-Operating Lingo.

While we are at it, we should revise the lingo to appeal to
their semi-literate mentality. New words should be added to
the COBOL list, such as: like, umm, doh, y'know, dude, rules,
and so on.

This would allow them to code stuff like:

Like move this to that, y'know.

umm add something to something-else dude.

stop run doh

The compilers would just ignore these new words. 

The programmer might feel more comfortable using terms they
are familiar with and therefore conclude that COBOL is quite
cool after all.

Thanks

TOny Dilworth

(posted via newsranger.com)

foodman
```

#### ↳ Re: Creaky Obsolescent Boring Old Language

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)

"Don Leahy" <dleahy@wave.home.com> wrote:

>Twenty years ago when I started programming, I was advised by several
>seemingly well-informed people in the DP (as it was called then) business to
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>;-)

Don....

What a great idea!!  But I think that we need something completely
different.  Consider Java.....is "java" an acronym for something?  I
don't believe that it is.

In the US, it is a slang name for coffee.  It's an island much warmer
than Pennsylvania.  It's also a category (race?) of prehistoric man.

I think that COBOL needs a slang name for a beverage, just like Java.
THAT will make it instantly popular.

How about some slang names for a fun drink such as beer?

SUDS - Standard Universal Development System
COLD ONE - Common Outstanding Language for Development of Outrageous
New E-applications
BREW - Bodacious Real E-application Workbench

Of course, Ken Foskey will want to name it FOSTERS!!

;-)


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-a18a1df534"></a>
## Thread: convert3.exe, anyone? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### convert3.exe, anyone?

- **From:** "David Begley" <dave@begley.com.remove>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T18:35:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EDwn6.837$YC1.760323@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Is there anyone that could get me a copy of a file called convert3.exe that
used to come with the MicroFocus Workbench?  I'm a valid user of NetExpress
and I'm up-to-date on my annual support/maintenance fee.  And I used (and
own) a valid licensed copy of the MicroFocus compiler for years (I just
never bought the Workbench).  So I'm not trying to steal anything, honest.

Someone asked me if I could get data out of an old RM COBOL data file and I
was trying do them a favor and help them out.  (They are going to start
using new a new software package).  I asked at the Answer Lab on Merant's
support site, and using convert3.exe was one option they gave me.  Another
option was to create an RM COBOL program and extract the data.  I figured it
would be easier to track down convert3.exe than to get a copy of the RM
compiler and runtime.

I thought there was a way for MF programs to access the RM data, but the
compile time setting I found only effects the syntax of the program code.

thanks,
Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: convert3.exe, anyone?

- **From:** "David Begley" <dave@begley.com.remove>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T20:32:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<plyn6.972$YC1.814450@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<EDwn6.837$YC1.760323@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Never mind.  I went back through my old COBOL install diskettes and found
convert3.exe.  I guess I didn't need the Workbench after all.

But it didn't help, it creates a program that is supposed to convert the
file, but I can't open the file with a MF program, so this put me back at
square one.  I had hoped convert3.exe was an actual conversion utility.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: convert3.exe, anyone?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T22:46:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9ED34D.8035DA87@home.com>`
- **References:** `<EDwn6.837$YC1.760323@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <plyn6.972$YC1.814450@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```


David Begley wrote:

> Never mind.  I went back through my old COBOL install diskettes and found
> convert3.exe.  I guess I didn't need the Workbench after all.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Whoops ! Just read your second message. What are you saying ?

1. Run Convert3

2. Tell it the file format you will be using from RM - needs if you like, a
dummy program containing as minimum the RM copy files for File Control and File
Section, just like you'd give the layout to a DB package. It now generates an
N/E source which you compile.

3. Going from memory, your compiled program asks for (a) name of the RM INPUT
file and (b) name of the N/E OUTPUT file. Then converts unless it hits an error.

However if RM/COBOL 85 - game's over !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert3.exe, anyone?

- **From:** "David Begley" <dave@begley.com.remove>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T23:12:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vHAn6.1156$YC1.893319@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
I ran convert3 and created the program.  When I run it, it says it can't
read the input file.  Maybe it is an RM/COBOL 85 file or maybe I just have a
corrupted file.   I told them it looked like I wouldn't be of any help and
suggested a couple of file conversion tools I saw on the web, and if that
didn't work, they could find someone still using RM COBOL to help.

I actually used RM COBOL a long time ago, but I didn't save any RM stuff.

#### ↳ Re: convert3.exe, anyone?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T22:33:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9ED03D.82E44112@home.com>`
- **References:** `<EDwn6.837$YC1.760323@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```


David Begley wrote:

> Is there anyone that could get me a copy of a file called convert3.exe that
> used to come with the MicroFocus Workbench?  I'm a valid user of NetExpress
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dave

Dave,

From what you've written, it doesn't seem that this would broach any M/F rules,
so I can let you have a copy. However, one proviso. Convert3 was for the 'old'
RM file formats - Version 2.? ANSI 74. It wont do it for RM/COBOL 85 - they are
a different format. (I don't know what old M/F DOS tools you have, but Convert3
along with other COBOLs, (DEC, IBM etc.), was part of the basic DOS package V
3.0 onwards).

If you are talking RM/COBOL '85 - seems the only way would be to generate Ascii
Delimiteds(CSV) from RM and feed them into N/E as line sequentials.

Let me know if you still want it.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: COBOL newbie has question about .lst — 4 msgs

### COBOL newbie has question about .lst

- **From:** "Jeff McDonald" <jeffmcdonald@cinci.rr.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SBxn6.105316$446.4925017@typhoon.kc.rr.com>`

```
I'm running MicroFocus for win95, and have a question.  I wrote a program
for a class I'm taking a while back, and now I need to print the .lst file.
But I can't find it.  How can I get it back?  I've tried re-compiling, and
rerunning it.  I even tried copying my code to a new program and compiling
and running that, but still no lst.  Any thoughts??

Thanks

Jeff
```

> I'm running MicroFocus for win95, and have a question.  I wrote a program
> for a class I'm taking a while back, and now I need to print the .lst file.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff

Not a feature I've ever used. However, look at your compiler options - a list
file is only generated if you set the appropriate directive.
