<a id="t-e9bad36bf5"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu following MERANT's nefarious licensing prectices?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-01T14:19:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x9yn6.3414$P.106738@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<Zezm6.2366$P.33100@news1.atl> <97ga2s$d1l$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <0xRm6.24$5_1.271@client>`

```
Thank you Buddy, for your reply.  But I understood all of your points
before I posted my complaint.  Let me respond with a few facts in
counterpoint.

First, the *only* value that Fujitsu COBOL, or any other product, has
for me, or any other user, is how it serves my needs.  When a product
fails to meet my legitimate needs, it is irrelevant to me, in a very
practical sense, why the vendor failed to meet them.  Even if I have
sympathy for them, I'm not going to spend my money unless the product
serves my needs.  No consumer would or should feel otherwise.

Second, I received a NetExpress 3.1 update last Fall.  But I have only
had it running for about a month.  The primary reason is problems with
the upgrade involving licensing issues.  I lost months of use of an
upgrade that I needed, and had paid for, because somebody stumbled in
the implementation of the licensing module, or the update install.
This fiasco cost me thousands of dollars in lost time and energy.  So
it *is not* only concern for the total failure of MERANT or Fujitsu
that bothers me.  And don't scoff, for I have received updates from
Fujitsu that required my uninstalling the old version before installing
the new version.  Lots of extra trouble for no benefit to me, and that
is *without* the extra complication of the licensing function. ;-)

Third, there is great pressure on the entire COBOL community right
now, to "move away from the archaic platform" to something "nifty"
like VB.  Though I *do not* share those sentiments, as a consultant
in a free market, I have to deal with them.  It does not help when my
most important compilers' prices soar through the roof, and all the
while the vendor plays more and more hardball about licensing, and
I don't know when and how I am going to be shut down because of a
licensing module or computer failure, and I am not free to install
the compiler on a backup computer without a lot of hassle and delay.
What good does it do for my compiler vendor to stay competitive if
I cannot?

Fourth, though I understand the reasons why it is so, I can buy a
copy of Visual Studio, install it on all my office PCs (nobody but
me touches them), and a computer failure is but an annoyance.  I am
also a few thousand dollars better off.  True, it takes longer to
write applications using VB or (shudder) VC++, and they won't be
as reliable as my COBOL apps, but my development speed and the
reliability of my systems is already so far above what the community
is used to that it won't really hurt me.  It grieves and aggravates
the daylights out of me, but it won't hurt me.

Fifth, though Pascal lost out in the long run, because it was being
used for things it wasn't designed for, it is doubtful to me if
Pascal would have been more than an academic blip if not for Turbo
Pascal.  A high quality, very fast Pascal compiler at a very good
price made Philippe Kahn a very successful man, and put Pascal on
the map, big time.  Even more so, by putting *free or cheap* BASIC
on virtually every computer sold, Bill Gates sold the world on BASIC.
Microsoft *still* gives away a very good BASIC interpreter with every
copy of Windows 9x/NT (don't know about 2000).  Is there any doubt
about the financial viability of Microsoft or Bill Gates?  Instead
of fretting about COBOL compiler thieves to the point that you drive
away your legitimate users, why not make limited versions that will
compile EXEs that can be distributed, but limit the size of the
programs that it will compile, and practically give them away?  A
'student edition' for $100?  Why not sell anybody who wants one a
fully working copy (limited program size) for $39?  This principle
is called "planting seed".  Right now, nobody is planting any COBOL
seed.  I grew up on a farm, and I can tell you that if you plant no
seed, you will harvest no crop.  Apple and IBM have spent billions
doing this in schools, and have made it back many fold.  Just don't
stop with schools.  I realize that Fujitsu, at least, understands
this principle.  Just keep it up, and do it better.  Another point:
get humans to write your manuals, and make sure they understand both
COBOL *and* English grammar.  Well.  Another reason Apple initially
made it big; their manuals won awards for clarity.

Sixth, never but never play hardball with your customers.  I was part
owner of a microcomputer store in 1979-1983.  In 1979 Apple started
playing hardball with dealers and customers, and listening to their
own inner voice (Steve Jobs) rather than their customers.  I said then
that Apple would pay dearly for it, and they did.  The Apple III and
Lisa died on the vine, and the Mac came very, very close.  Apple would
have been dead had it not been for the almost fanatical loyalty that
Apple had engendered with previous policies, and for Woz' amazing
Apple II, which Apple sold many years after it was obsolete.  And the
final insult: for Bill Gates to bail out Apple with $350 million was
to Apple about like it would be for the Pope to accept a bailout from
Satan (or vice versa :).  The bottom line is, if you irritate people
enough, they're not going to buy anything from you, even if it's to
their best interests to do so.  To have purchased a compiler, but to
be physically denied the ability to install it when and where you need
it to make a living, rates a 10 on the 1-10 irritation scale.

Seventh and last, if enough of Fujitsu's and MERANT's customers become
as concerned about the above issues as I am, it really won't matter
in the end whether anybody steals your software or not, for nobody
will be buying it.  In my opinion, if MERANT and Fujitsu are looking
toward the long haul with COBOL, they should be very concerned about
keeping as many people using COBOL as possible.  Higher prices and
irritating licensing policies will not do this.
```

```
> Actually, I have always worked on mainframes, though not IBM since

You may have been developing on 'personal computers' since 1977, but IBM did
not trademark 'IBM Personal Computer' until 1980, which is when the acronym
"PC" came to be.

So I think you meant, " ...since 1977 I have been developing for *what we
now call* PCs, too"

(Does this make me anal-retentive?)

MCM
```

## Thread: Help! Lower/upper case translation in CICS — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help! Lower/upper case translation in CICS

- **From:** jason <cz.systems@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T12:46:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9F97BD.F4F6CBF0@home.com>`

```
Hi, all.

If you are kind enough to reply to this, please send mail to: 
Jason_Thorpe@ch.honda.com  as I don't have newsgroup or internet access at
work.

I'm having the following problem:

I need to be able to accept mixed case data in a field and pass it, via a
RECEIVE MAP to a cobol program.

Unfortunately, whenever lower case is entered, it gets converted to upper. 
Now in my sleep last night, I was wondering if it is translated in the
following SEND MAP, after data validation, and I'll check that out this
morning... but I digress.

I tried the ASIS command on the receive, but apparently it doesn't work on
the first receive in a program, so is useless for pseudoconversational
programming.  I could find no options for the map definition itsself, other
than CASE, which converts everything to upper for KATAKANA terminals.  No
good.

I can send and receive lower case, so long as the field isn't modified by
the user.

This is CICS 4.something, running under MVS, COBOL/370.

Help!  We're in system testing and I'm under the gun!.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Lower/upper case translation in CICS

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T13:57:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7FNn6.4493$c7.1369153@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3A9F97BD.F4F6CBF0@home.com>`

```
Ask your sysadmin how the terminals are defined to the region. I think it's
the TCT (or is it TFT) but it's been a while. If they're set to force
uppercase you can fix the problem by disabling the option.


"jason" <cz.systems@home.com> wrote in message
news:3A9F97BD.F4F6CBF0@home.com...
> Hi, all.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming.  I could find no options for the map definition itsself,
other
> than CASE, which converts everything to upper for KATAKANA terminals.  No
> good.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hard Sci-Fi
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! Lower/upper case translation in CICS

- **From:** Simon Elliott <"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T16:58:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3A9FD13B.13A9D238@CapGemini.Co.UK>`
- **References:** `<3A9F97BD.F4F6CBF0@home.com> <7FNn6.4493$c7.1369153@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
"Oscar T. Grouch" wrote:

> Ask your sysadmin how the terminals are defined to the region. I think it's
> the TCT (or is it TFT) but it's been a while. If they're set to force
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Hard Sci-Fi

Something we installed for a client was -

*
* GET THE CURRENT TERMINAL USING THE ASSIGN COMMAND.
*
         EXEC CICS ASSIGN FACILITY(TERMID)
*
* HAVING GOT THE TERMID, SET UCTRAN OFF.
*
         EXEC CICS SET TERMINAL(TERMID) NOUCTRAN

Warning - You'll need to use the SP option to compile this. See
http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/DFHLA814/2%2e87?SHELF=DFHLSH25
for more details
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! Lower/upper case translation in CICS

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T13:43:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B0901A614A6C634E.B16ED2516861B4F3.4D32947D41718D45@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3A9F97BD.F4F6CBF0@home.com> <7FNn6.4493$c7.1369153@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3A9FD13B.13A9D238@CapGemini.Co.UK>`

```
On Fri, 02 Mar 2001 16:58:35 +0000, Simon Elliott
<"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK> enlightened us:

>"Oscar T. Grouch" wrote:
>
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
>for more details

Combining ideas here - first you must have ASIS on your RECEIVE MAP
command, otherwise the lower case will be translated to upper case.
You need this even if you issue the SET commands in your program.

Next, if it is possible for your CICS Sys Admin to change the TCT
terminal definitions so that lower case is not automatically changed,
then that needs to be done and you don't need to put any SET commands
in your program.

However, if it is not possible (most likely case due to other apps),
then you need to issue the SET commands detailed above.  Just remember
to change it back before the program exits.  You could be causing some
undo headaches somewhere else if you leave the termianl changed or
your program abends before you are able to change it back.  Use
caution.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Jesus loves you... It's everyone else that thinks you're an asshole.

Help Save The Rainforest
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! Lower/upper case translation in CICS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiammD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-03T06:36:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA090C7.CD012761@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3A9F97BD.F4F6CBF0@home.com> <7FNn6.4493$c7.1369153@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3A9FD13B.13A9D238@CapGemini.Co.UK> <B0901A614A6C634E.B16ED2516861B4F3.4D32947D41718D45@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> On Fri, 02 Mar 2001 16:58:35 +0000, Simon Elliott
(snip)
> >
> >Something we installed for a client was -
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >         EXEC CICS SET TERMINAL(TERMID) NOUCTRAN

You don't need to ASSIGN for the termid, it's in the EIB (EIBTRMID).

> >Warning - You'll need to use the SP option to compile this. See
> >http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/DFHLA814/2%2e87?SHELF=DFHLSH25
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> caution.

Better issue a HANDLE ABEND LABEL(.....) and reset the terminal there,
too, so other programmers don't beat you around the neck & ears.

I've tried the above two steps and still got upper case (S390 & CICS/ESA
4.1), didn't have time to pursue it any further. Please post the
solution if you find one.

> Regards,
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jesus loves you... It's everyone else that thinks you're an asshole.

Hey, thanks for clearing that up:-)

LiamD
```

```
On Fri, 2 Mar 2001 15:30:16 -0000, "Steve Millward"
<mindhawk@beeb.net> wrote:

>Hi,
>I am trying out the trial of Powercobol, and like it.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: POWERCOBOL PROBLEM!!!!!!

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-03T01:04:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lqXn6.9111$7Y1.957852@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3a9fbc2e@news-uk.onetel.net.uk>`

```
It would be far easier to take your machine to the demonstration.

In sum, you have to create an installation distribution for your
program. Fujitsu provides an installation 'wrapper' which you
incorporate with your application. This 'wrapper' is an OEM version of
InstallShield with much stuff predefined (like required directories).
In addition to your code, the installation process installs maybe 200
DLLs and updates the target machine's registry.

You have no hope of simply copying a few (or many) files to the target
machine - well, there's one way: Install the full PC package on the
demo machine and copy in your PPJ file. Compile and execute. Of course
with Fujitsu's new license scheme this trick won't work.

Sorry.


"Steve Millward" <mindhawk@beeb.net> wrote in message
news:3a9fbc2e@news-uk.onetel.net.uk...
```

<a id="t-09f91c49c2"></a>
## Thread: Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0 — 8 msgs

### Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** "Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-02T22:45:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<foVn6.327843$w35.53721213@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
Hello,

I've been having a problem processing some rather large "record sequential"
files.   The program reads the input file OK until it gets to about the
50,000th read, then I get an error 114 (boundary violation) on the next
sequential READ.

I think this may be the result of some sort of stack overflow.  I've tried
running the program on a couple of different PC's with the same result.   If
anyone has encountered this problem, and knows a workaround, any input would
be much appreciated!!!

Thanks,
BG
```

#### ↳ Re: Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-03T00:04:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA03703.342EE5EB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<foVn6.327843$w35.53721213@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```


Bill Gentry wrote:

> Hello,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> be much appreciated!!!

Always a tricky one to solve this type of problem, and I deal with nowhere near
the volumes you are talking about. I work entirely in OO and it is
calling(invoking) parameter passing which give me my Errors 114. So a set of
'possibles' if you haven't already tried them :-

1. Are you calling - and is it always the same call routine and set of
parameters (I suspect your answer is "'No,  I'm doing reading/writing in one
program").

2. Fixed length - or are you using syntax "record varying etc......".

3. Are you using file-status checking for each read/write. Not necessarily the
solution but I would have thought a file-status <> "00" SHOULD trap the error
before you get to an Error 114.

4. Still stumped - a nuisance I know - from the IDE can you output the file
using the
 Tools--->Data Tools----> (a) File fix index or (2) Convert format - this might
convey something wrong with a record(s).

5. You mentioned stack overflow - check out the on-line help on limitations for
the various features. (I'm fairly certain there's something in there about the
stack).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** "Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-04T19:41:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oTwo6.334593$w35.54929534@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`
- **References:** `<foVn6.327843$w35.53721213@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA03703.342EE5EB@home.com>`

```
James,

Thanks for the reply, see below...

news:3AA03703.342EE5EB@home.com...

tried

would

>

near
> the volumes you are talking about. I work entirely in OO and it is
> calling(invoking) parameter passing which give me my Errors 114. So a set
of
> 'possibles' if you haven't already tried them :-
>
> 1. Are you calling - and is it always the same call routine and set of
> parameters (I suspect your answer is "'No,  I'm doing reading/writing in
one
> program").

It's a stand-alone program.  I suspect the problem could be related to a
module that's part of the run time system.  I've tried running this thing as
a stand-alone executable with the same results.

>
> 2. Fixed length - or are you using syntax "record varying etc......".

Fixed length.  Organization is line sequential.  It processes the records OK
until it gets to a certain number of reads.   I have five files like this to
process, and I get the same result with ALL of them after a certain point in
the I/O ( different for each file, though ).

>
> 3. Are you using file-status checking for each read/write. Not necessarily
the
> solution but I would have thought a file-status <> "00" SHOULD trap the
error
> before you get to an Error 114.

I'll give that a try.   The (114) error occurs upon execution of the READ
statement.  I'll capture the status code.... it may tell me something.

>
> 4. Still stumped - a nuisance I know - from the IDE can you output the
file
> using the
>  Tools--->Data Tools----> (a) File fix index or (2) Convert format - this
might
> convey something wrong with a record(s).

I've tried doing a few conversions... I still get the same result.   I think
my next try will be to create an indexed/relative file out of one of these
suckers and try doing random reads and/or starts after the "error" point.

>
> 5. You mentioned stack overflow - check out the on-line help on
limitations for
> the various features. (I'm fairly certain there's something in there about
the
> stack).

I've tried running this on 2 windows 98 machines, AND on a NT machine that I
have with the same results.  I'm not sure exactly how to manipulate the
stack allocations under these operating systems ( probably have to get help
on a windows NG  LOL! )

>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-04T20:27:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA2A728.1DA9E843@home.com>`
- **References:** `<foVn6.327843$w35.53721213@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA03703.342EE5EB@home.com> <oTwo6.334593$w35.54929534@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```


Bill Gentry wrote:

> > 3. Are you using file-status checking for each read/write. Not necessarily
> the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I've got my fingers crossed that when you introduce file status checking you
will utter that famous programmer word  - "AHHHHhhhhhh.......". Was it the Amex
ad that used to say, "Don't  leave home without it...". I ALWAYS check file
status for every VERB. I've been puzzled where I've done a 'quickie' without
file status and running the program just didn't generate anything - until I
introduced file status and got the error telling me I hadn't yet opened the
input file - DUMBO !

If that doesn't resolve it - the stack. Check out 'Nested Statement
Restrictions" in the on-line help. Now the 'guts' of programming is not my
forte, but in layman's English - You mention the program is referencing 5 files
so obviously you've got a set of PERFORMS taking you to each process. Are those
performs nested 'neatly' so that you return to the 'starting point' after
diverting to process for a particular file. Just a wild guess, if you are
returning from PERFORM loops 'randomly' to the start point - and you are talking
about 50,000 records plus, is there a possibility the stack is getting
overloaded with perform references which in theory should have been
"killed'/removed from the stack ..

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Bill Gentry" <billgentry2@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-05T19:57:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NcSo6.345062$w35.55467278@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`
- **References:** `<foVn6.327843$w35.53721213@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA03703.342EE5EB@home.com> <oTwo6.334593$w35.54929534@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA2A728.1DA9E843@home.com>`

```
Know what the problem was?   I was doing some reference modification on a WS
item and exceeding it's length:  THAT caused the memory overflow, but not
enough to bomb the program.  When I subsequently did the READ, I guess the
run-time system was so exhausted, THAT (read) statement produced the error.
Weird.  Anyway, when I cleaned up the reference modification, it ran OK.

Thanks for all your input, it got me pointed in the right direction


James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3AA2A728.1DA9E843@home.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** auto73594@hushmail.com (Aleph3)
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T10:00:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tac1htbvit78e0@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<foVn6.327843$w35.53721213@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA03703.342EE5EB@home.com> <oTwo6.334593$w35.54929534@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA2A728.1DA9E843@home.com> <NcSo6.345062$w35.55467278@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
Hi Bill

That's a classic. 114 on MF like 0C1 or 0C4 om IBM mean that somewhere in your 
program you smeared your memory. There is no warning to the fact, 
since checking this type of error wil reduce performance significantly. 
Therefore, you put some garbage in the address used by the read statement, and 
when applying it your program did not know what to do.

This error 114 is usually very hard to find since as I mentioned, the cause of 
the problem is not the place where the program fails.

Regards

Ilan

In article <NcSo6.345062$w35.55467278@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>, "Bill Gentry" 
<billgentry2@home.com> wrote:
>Know what the problem was?   I was doing some reference modification on a WS
>item and exceeding it's length:  THAT caused the memory overflow, but not
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T07:43:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<985dvo$5kh$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<foVn6.327843$w35.53721213@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA03703.342EE5EB@home.com> <oTwo6.334593$w35.54929534@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA2A728.1DA9E843@home.com> <NcSo6.345062$w35.55467278@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <tac1htbvit78e0@news.supernews.com>`

```
One "debugging" hint, that I can suggest when you get a 114 is to run/debug
with BOUND checking turned on.  This *would* have shown you a reference
modification problem - and also shows table overruns - which is another
common cause of these problems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error 114 on NetExpress 3.0

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** auto73594@hushmail.com (Aleph3)
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T06:26:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tae9eed25nfm6a@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<foVn6.327843$w35.53721213@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA03703.342EE5EB@home.com> <oTwo6.334593$w35.54929534@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <3AA2A728.1DA9E843@home.com> <NcSo6.345062$w35.55467278@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com> <tac1htbvit78e0@news.supernews.com> <985dvo$5kh$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
To which I can add SSRANGE on IBM MF, that does the same but eats at least 30% 
extra CPU.

In article <985dvo$5kh$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>One "debugging" hint, that I can suggest when you get a 114 is to run/debug
>with BOUND checking turned on.  This *would* have shown you a reference
>modification problem - and also shows table overruns - which is another
>common cause of these problems.
>
```

<a id="t-87b89ff3de"></a>
## Thread: HELP: Cobol 4.1 rev 30 and AIX 4.3.3 on an F80 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### HELP: Cobol 4.1 rev 30 and AIX 4.3.3 on an F80

- **From:** james@rubiconcs.co.uk (James Garwood)
- **Date:** 2001-03-03T09:21:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aa0b58c.599602@news.dircon.co.uk>`

```
Do you by any chance have Micro Focus (Merant) Cobol 4.1 rev 30
working on an IBM RS/6000 F80 with AIX 4.3.3. The F80 has a dual RS64
III processor card so is running bos.64bit.

Basically I have a customers machine in this configuration and at
random the cobol run-time will fail to open sequential files with the
MFIO error file containing an errno = 22 error on the file open. The
last status is either 3/5 or 0/0. 

The problem is not isolated to our application sequential files as
they also get errors against the ADISCTRL and cobkeymp files from
/usr/lib/ocobol which causes them screen display and key mapping
issues.

We never get errros related to open our ISAM files and the problem can
manifest when we have a single users on the system or 40 plus.

Thanks for your help/time.

James Garwood
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

```
I'm having trouble printing to an non default windows printer in PowerFROM.

It seems to me that PowerFORM only prints to the Windows Default Printer.

The problem occurs when executing 5 sequential programs.

If you desire to print the 4th program (Checks) to another printer you are
out of luck.  You must stop and change the printer to default (in Windows).

Has anyone else had this problem?  If so do you have a solution?

Joe Hunter
```

```
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>putting "infrequently used" PERFORMed code "inline" adds any performance
>degradation.  If you have some evidence of this, can you share this (and

For the last time, IBM (in COBOL II) supplied the option to avoid
this.  I think it's up to you to prove no performance degradation.  I
admit the difference is small, all right?
>
>Personally, I am STRONGLY opposed to COBOL programmers controlling compiler
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>work.
Well, I think I'm smarter than a compile program, at least in judging
what should be done in an actual situation.  Guess you don't think you
are.  Fine with me.
>P.S.  If you have a specific set of "code" that is INFREQUENTLY used - and
>that you do not want "copied inline" - have you tried making it a nested
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>entire "thrust" of this was to not "copy inline" infrequently used code.
My shop doesn't presently permit nesting, but I have done exactly that
using dynamic CALL.
```

```
What is Natural/Adabase. Seems to show up quite a bit in job listings for
Cobol programmers. If you just have just the Cobol they don't want you, so
that's why I am wondering what it is and where I can get some info about it.
Could use a job in Utah, but will probably just have to leave the state to
find one.
```

#### ↳ Re: Natural/Adabase - What is it?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
uta wrote:

> What is Natural/Adabase. Seems to show up quite a bit in job listings for
> Cobol programmers. If you just have just the Cobol they don't want you, so
> that's why I am wondering what it is and where I can get some info about it.
> Could use a job in Utah, but will probably just have to leave the state to
> find one.

Check out google.com - Natural Adabase - Client Server, Sun Unix. Job wise looks
like you're SOL. Two jobs in Memphis and Ohio - guess you weren't contemplating
moving that far-r-r-r-r EAST or SOUTH.

Seriously - COBOL programmers are going to get hammered if they don't pick up
additional skills, C++, Java etc., and tools like RAD, UML etc. For good or bad
the market is DEMANDING these skills, with a paucity of ads for COBOL
experience. ( I suppose if you work on a mainframe, are knocking fifty, then
maybe you can 'coast' until retirement - but even that is "iffy").

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Natural/Adabase - What is it?

- **From:** Enoch Jones <e902j592@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-04T20:04:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA29F8C.843BF7E4@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<GAeo6.10438$sD.663837@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com> <3AA18187.86E06CA3@home.com>`

```
To answer the original question:   NATURAL is the reporting tool for ADABAS
(no "e"!) which is similar to Easytrieve, Culprit, and Focus [blech!! and double
blech!!].  Before the advent of SQL, each database vendor derived their own
proprietary adhoc reporting/query tools to save application maintainer the cost
of coding in COBOL.  These were interpreted languages, but most use a vector-
function call approach now and have improved efficiencies on the higher-speed
enterprise servers.  ADABAS is the database engine from Software AG [Germany?];
it is one of the few "inverted-list" engines running.

I've found focus on application experience not tech experience,
and skill of problem-solving [regardless of tools invoked] to be
transferrable across varied tool platforms.  Tools come and
go, but opportunities to resolve crises will always exist.

"Have mind, will travel"
ej

> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
up
>additional skills, C++, Java etc., and tools like RAD, UML etc. For good
or
bad
>the market is DEMANDING these skills, with a paucity of ads for COBOL
>experience. ( I suppose if you work on a mainframe, are knocking fifty,
then
>maybe you can 'coast' until retirement - but even that is "iffy").
>
If a company is using COBOL today they will probably not be wanting to
migrate
away from that.  It takes a huge investment to migrate away from COBOL based
programs.  In fact many medium to small companies do not even use a formal
database and have opted for the use of VSAM files instead.    COBOL can still
process huge amounts of records in a short amount of time.  Just going from
VSAM files to DBase is a huge step and usually requires a large scale
investment in equipment, time, and money.  I see experienced programmers who
know CICS to be in high demand.  However a new college student needs at least
one course in COBOL and extensive System and Database design training to make
it in this ever evolving world.  If you already have just about every program
possible written in COBOL why pay to reinvent the wheel?  They make
applications that take output from COBOL programs and send that to web pages
through cgi scripting and whatever else you want to use.  
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Natural/Adabase - What is it?

- **From:** "Barry K. Clarke" <bkclarke@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-04T11:22:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA295F3.770592E4@qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<GAeo6.10438$sD.663837@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com>`

```
I'll tell you what it is, but you probably don't want to know.  This is known as
a 4th Generation Language.  It was developed by two guys in Germany and the
company is headquarters in Germany.  The north american hqs is in Denver and is
called Software AG of North America.  You can probably find there web site.  I
used it while working for the State of Washington.  You can develop programs
much faster than COBOL, that's the good news, now the bad news, its not too hot
and fully functional as COBOL is.  It is a real resource hog and isn't
documented very well.  You can't have more than two dimensional arrays.  Batch
programs take forever to run.  The language is called Natural2 and the database
is call ADABAS.  It's an inverted database like FoxPro.  If something abends in
this language, good luck.  Some people love it, but it had too many quirks for
me.  Sometimes the production modules will just not execute and they don't know
why, neither does Software AG.  We had a ABABAS problem where I was working at
Dept of Transportation and they never did get it fixed.  How's that for vendor
support.  You can jump in, but make sure you have a life raft, inner tube, etc.
Good Luck.

Barry Seattle

> What is Natural/Adabase. Seems to show up quite a bit in job listings for

> that's why I am wondering what it is and where I can get some info about it.
> Could use a job in Utah, but will probably just have to leave the state to
> find one.
```

#### ↳ Re: Natural/Adabase - What is it?

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T21:54:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eLkTNTA6xVp6Ewq1@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<GAeo6.10438$sD.663837@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <GAeo6.10438$sD.663837@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com>, uta
<uta@unibasetech.com> writes
>What is Natural/Adabase. Seems to show up quite a bit in job listings for
>Cobol programmers. If you just have just the Cobol they don't want you, so
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Natural is an English language programming language similar in many
respects to Cobol. If you know Cobol then, with the aid of a manual, you
will be able to read and understand the Natural source code. Natural was
developed as a 4gl tool to extract data from the Adabas database (an
inverted list database) developed by Software AG of Darmstadt in
Germany. IMHO it's strength is in developing online applications using
the inbuilt map painter. It is true, as pointed out elsewhere, that it
is not as efficient as Cobol but then you should use the right tool for
the right job. It is generally easier to debug than Cobol (I have used
both languages in equal measure) but you can get stuck for no obvious
reason and sometimes have to recompile core modules or go to SAG for a
fix. Cobol can access the Adabas database by either using ADAMINT
(precompiled access modules) or ADA/SQL. Natural can access VSAM and
DL/I and DB2 databases. There are pc versions of Natural (eg Natural
Lightstorm) which attempt to enforce a project environment upon the
user. Adabas/Natural also exists on IBM, ICL mainframes, and Unix boxes.
The editor is ideosyncratic but for some extra money you can use an ISPF
lookalike. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: TINYCOBOL: MOVE CORR

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-04T23:36:02-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<97v8le$v75$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn98qksr.7e.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3A8E01B1.D3522BAB@arvotek.net> <3AA29644.55203165@earthlink.net> <3AA3214E.2B098035@arvotek.net>`

```
David - I don't know where you got the MIS-information the CORRESPONDING
option. All CORR phrases are "fully" supported in the draft Standard.  They
are *not* marked as either "archaic" or "obsolete" in this Standard - so
they are expected to remain in all "future" COBOL Standards.

I think that your "confusion" *might* have to do with the fact that they
were removed from the JOD at one time.  I can't remember for certain - but I
believe they were added back- even there.

CORRESPONDING is a "basic" (and to be kept) feature of COBOL.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: TINYCOBOL: MOVE CORR

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T14:00:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<slrn98qksr.7e.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3A8E01B1.D3522BAB@arvotek.net> <3AA29644.55203165@earthlink.net>`

```
Enoch Jones wrote in message <3AA29644.55203165@earthlink.net>...
>David Essex wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So what part of "Tiny" do you not understand?

If it was "a fully-functional compiler--competitive with commercial
versions" it WOULDN'T be tiny, now, would it? (Ever seen any that were?)

Your criticism is unfair. Standard COBOL took years to implement
MOVE...CORRESPONDING  (mind you, they take years to implement ANYTHING...so
that doesn't say much...)

On the subset of the existing standard that it implements, Tiny COBOL IS
fully functional and you COULD build commercial applications with it.
(We did for years with a smaller subset than Tiny COBOL; it was called COBOL
59...no PICTURE clause (used CLASS and SIZE instead), no STRING, UNSTRING,
SEARCH,  SORT, or PERFORM...VARYING to more than one level...just to name a
few of the limitations.... You youngsters don't know you're
born...(harrumph!)<G>
And yet most commercial computer installations wrote their applications with
it up until around 1967.)

The Open Source Movement (as represented by Tiny COBOL in this instance)
deserves our support and encouragement, not being damned before it gets off
the ground by unrealistically high expectations and unfair criticism.

(I have no affiliation with, vested interest in, or hidden agendas regarding
Tiny COBOL or the Open Source movement. As a long time COBOL user I simply
wish them well.)

Pete.
```

<a id="t-a995680769"></a>
## Thread: Is COBOL Dying? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Is COBOL Dying?

- **From:** Warren G. Simmons <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-04T21:26:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103_983741242@micron-450>`

```
Just for the hell of it, I looked into another comp.lang. group that was 
familar to me.  Here is one of the messages I found, and it struck me as 
funny.  Also, just the numbers of messages in other language groups like 
Fortran shed light on the question in my mind.  Enjoy, or delete!

comp.lang.cobol readers might find this message from the 
comp.lang.mumps newsgroup funny.  I did.
Mumps was built by the Medical Branch of Business and was coordinated 
via the National Bureau of Standards.  The guy with the job called me off 
and on to learn how we were doing things in CODASYL, and the COBOL 
Committee.  Had this approach continued, the Bankers would have had 
their own language, as well as every major segment of Business and 
Government and Others.  To the credit of the Mumps gang, they had to 
deal with terminals long before the advent of the PC.

Warren Simmons


news://netnews.worldnet.att.net/comp.lang.mumps/15113


Subject: Re: Mumps is dying
Date: Tue, 23 Jan 2001
Message-ID: <3a6dfb42.49639196@news.knoware.nl>

On Tue, 23 Jan 2001 18:27:40 GMT, mimiseh@my-deja.com wrote:

>Is that true all mumps programers are all over 42 yrs old and all the
>young and talented progs have gone to Oracle or SQL ??
>

I think this is a bit of an annoying question, I may be waisting my
time writing and answer to it (the question isn't even properly
signed), but here is the answer:

No, not at all. We have two young and talented programmers around 25
years of age, two others around 35 (and OK I admit: I myself am 43,
but than again: I founded the company 12 years ago, and I hardly do
any programming anymore these days)

and here is the funny thing: even these youngsters who came from
relational environments and schooling, they like M and Cachï¿½ just as
much as I do, and make beautiful, fast and efficient programs.

Oh, and one last thing: young and talented may not be so synonymous as
you might think. Having some experience in IT projects is an important
factor in making a software project really work !

Henk Westerhof
Simar automatisering, Amsterdam.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COBOL Dying?

- **From:** "Terry Milan" <tmilan@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T20:04:17
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98eit0$dk7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1103_983741242@micron-450>`

```
When I go to the CICS Conferences (good number of COBOL programmers attend)
I think what I am seeing is not that the language is dying, but many of the
people who know it are.  COBOL programmers are not a renewable resource.
Most colleges in my area (Boston) don't even know that it still exists.

IBM is pushing Java, in order to go with the flow.  From now on you will
code a Bean via a series of Visual Age wizards, drag and drop it to a
container (deploy it to CICS) and fill out the Bean property sheet (really
an HTTP enabled BMS map).

In the meantime, I am watching COBOL programmers count their gray hairs.
But to answer your question, I don't think COBOL is dying.

Regards,
Terry
```

```
Fujitsi COBOL.  I can read them fairly easily with Fuji COBOL97 but
```

```
cobol.txt file related to use of btrieve files.

  K) Notes on using Btrieve files

       - The Btrieve file record lock is not effective within the
         same process.
       - Start W32MKDE.EXE prior to using the Btrieve file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Btrieve & Fujitsu - any tips please?

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T00:11:17+00:00

```
As far as I know they will work exactly the same way as indexed or
sequential files (although with some limitations in the record and
number of keys).

Just define the file name as "filename,BTRV" either in the assign or
as a environment variable, and then use them as you would do to a
indexed or sequential file


FF
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA458CF.C17DCBD1@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3AA2BF8D.71F6E552@cinci-rr.com>`

```
Thank you for your replies (and I really do appreciate it), but what you
told me was actually the easy part. The BTRV file assignment in the
select statement is probably the only piece of BT documentation that
exists in the Fuji manuals.  But there are a few other things I've
discovered along the way ...

It is not at all necessary to reference any sort of key to read these
files sequentially.  Simply omit any ISAM-like refences in the select
statement and do READ NEXT's all the way thru to the end.

I have been able to do STARTS once I figured out how to identify the
primary key.  The first byte of the key (and all alphameric fields for
that matter) is the field length in binary form.  Upon opening the file,
I read the first record and store the field lengths for later WRITEs. 
It's a workaround and there's probably a better way to do it but at
least it works.

Speaking of field lengths, the CURRENCY data type is stated in the
manual as being equivelent to PIC s9(14)v9(4) COMP-5.  But using that
picture really screwed things up.  Turns out what seems to work is
S9(14)v999 COMP-3 with a left-over filler byte (note comp-3 not
comp-5).  Go figure.

These files have a number of alternate keys, but if I specify any of
them in the Select statement then Fujitsu gives me an open error.  I
guess I'll have to keep playing with them and something will click, but
at least for now I've discovered enough that I can hobble together a few
quick queries.

Again ... if anyone out there (like someone who has done this before)
could possibly refer me to some documentation or give a few tips it
would save me a lot of time.  Thanks.
 
-------------------------------------------------
(To reply by email change dash to dot in address)
```

<a id="t-9ff2c7e801"></a>
## Thread: Novell + icon + batch file — 1 msgs

### Re: Novell + icon + batch file

- **From:** "Gerald Moull" <gerald@moull.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-05T23:09:44
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98154a$4g$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<wLhl6.1292$t51.17673@weber.videotron.net> <9760o8$mig$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <p9Rm6.2$U33.449@weber.videotron.net>`

```
I think you'll find that you're using DOS methods in a Windows environment
that don't work any more.

The windows runtime will be executed as a new process and reset back to the
initial 'new process' settings.

Have a look in the Define Device part of the manual (User Guide
Configuration File).  This will basically map your traditional 'dumping' of
data to a printer port into the correct windows Print Manager/Spooler/Print
Driver etc.  You can also look at the Synonyms (Windows Config in UG) to map
printer names to different devices for different programs or select a
different Config file for each Icon.....

Another bit of advice is to make sure you've upgraded the Runtime to the
latest V7.00.03 with patches.  These can be downloaded from www.liant.com.
There are some enhancements in printing over earlier V7 releases.

For more details/specific help email to support@liant.com

The Windows printing support in V7 is very good.  You should be able to
support 'legacy' character reporting (Esc sequences etc) in a Windows
environment without any code changes (so long as you haven't done silly
things like specifically for Epson printers (nobody would be that sillly
would they......)).  You can also do sexy new things that people outside
this NG would certify you for if you ever talked about them - so don't tell
anyone!

Gerald Moull

PS Can anyone tell me why a NT Server with shared printers attached (ZetaFax
& HP 4500 Net Printer) can be accessed from Win98 PCs... you can explore the
volumes etc.. but as soon as you do anything with printers (eg open the
printers folder) the openation hangs and thrashes the network?  Not COBOL
related but hopefully one good turn deserves another?


Eric Gauthier <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca> wrote in message
news:p9Rm6.2$U33.449@weber.videotron.net...
> Hello and thank's for your answer.
>
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…

seems
> to
> > > work well.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…

Windows?
> > > Is there a special configuration we have to do in Novell so it could
> work
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Was wondering if anyone has heard of a Windows based editor that interfaces
with a DEC VAX.  I've inherited COBOL code and would like to work with it in
an easier format then EVE editor.  I've seen these for M Code and others.

The program would have to be able to accept an IP address for the VAX,
validate a username and password to run the login.com, and then read the
.cob code (even nicer if it could handle file manipulation in the VAX
environment as well, but I'm not pushing my luck hehe.)

Any Ideas or software that you all might have run across?


Thanks in advance

Don
```

```
I like eve. 8-)

That said, you might want to look into SAMBA for your VAX. Once set up you can
mount a network drive on the VAX's cobol directory to make the source files
you want to edit available.

SAMBA is freeware and available from WWW.SAMBA.ORG.
```

#### ↳ Re: VAX Editer??

- **From:** freewheelin <mano@mano.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T18:47:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mbelat0ngutda53hsece1mgi1tctujrv0c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<lYZo6.735$lt4.375285@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
My editor of choice on the PC is UTRAEDIT, whether I map a net drive
or use an emulator's file tranfer to access a  mini's files.  I've
been using it for years, since version 2.0   Its up around version 8.0
now.  Do a search for it on the web and you'll easily find it.   You
can install it for 45 days trial then it stops working.  For $35 you
can buy it.   If you spend a lot of time editing text files, this
program, or one similar to it, is an absolute must have.   

If you have ever used the old Brief editor on a PC, before GUI, you
can think of Ultraedit as a Window's GUI style Brief editor, only
better, because its GUI.  Its a real programmer's text editor and it's
my most important tool.  The MDI user interface is very well designed.
The configuration is complete.  It's functionallity is rich and can be
added or removed from the tool bar to suit your needs.    The MDI
interface is also flexible.  you can use file tabs or file trees to
view loaded files.  You can load two programs and display them side by
side and make them both scroll at the same time.   I've loaded dozens
of files and done mass edits on them or mass searches with results
loaded into a new buffer.  You can load a big COBOL program and 
have it find lines containing a string which it inserts into a mini
window.  Just click on a line in the mini window and the editor jumps
to that line in the currently displayed buffer.  

I was asked to insert a line of code after a specifc line in roughly
250 JCL files.   I was expected to load, edit and save each one.  The
boss assigned groups of files to 4 different programmers so the job
would be finished by the end of the day.  Why bother.  FTPed them onto
the PC.  Loaded them all into the editor and told it not make .bak
files.  Edited the first file using Home/Find/ArrowDown/Paste with
macro recorder turned on.   Saved macro then clicked through all 249
files hitting Run macro key.  Did a Save All then FTPed them back.
Job was done in 30 minutes.  One person did it.  Boss's jaw hit the
floor when he heard.

The most usefull feature is Column mode cutting and pasting.  This
feature has saved me 100s of hours of work - I kid you not.   The
editor doubles as data file editor, so it's usefull with say a  200MB
data file that needs byte number 138 removed from every line.  No
problem, 5 minute job.

I went out their web site and found a utility that makes Windows think
that Ultraedit is Notepad.  Whenever I click on a text file, Ultraedit
loads.  If its already loaded, it inserts the file into a new MDI
window.  The editor can also be told to run seperate instances of
itself.  

Have been doing some HTML work lately.  The Editor is smart and can
display all HTML tags in a utility list window.   It will also fetch
the Browser and pass the file to it.  

It has syntax highlighting for various languages, a crude spell,  two
up page printitng for long programs,  color configuration, margin
markers, byte rulers, logical line number display,  compare, sorting
functions, unix conversion, etc.   Can't say enough about it.  

For $35 its one of the most usefull programming tools I have ever
used.


On Tue, 06 Mar 2001 04:46:09 GMT, "res03tc4" <res03tc4@gte.net> wrote:

>Was wondering if anyone has heard of a Windows based editor that interfaces
>with a DEC VAX.  I've inherited COBOL code and would like to work with it in
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: COBOL and Linux — 11 msgs

### COBOL and Linux

- **From:** "Stephen Plotnick" <thepla@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-05T23:59:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aa47e1a_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
I have a system that was written for SCO Unix in MF COBOL. I want to port it
to Linux, which COBOL should I use?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Linux

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T05:03:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<982g77$d34$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa47e1a_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
I assume that when you say "MF COBOL" - you mean "Micro Focus COBOL" not
"Mainframe COBOL".  If it is Micro Focus COBOL, then I think your EASIEST
port would be to stay with Micro Focus (MERANT).  The chances are that the
application uses significant "Micro Focus" extensions and your easiest port
would be to stay with the same compiler vendor.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Linux

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T06:57:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA5DBEB.9012E223@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3aa47e1a_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
Stephen Plotnick wrote:
> 
> I have a system that was written for SCO Unix in MF COBOL. I want to port it
> to Linux, which COBOL should I use?

If it has been compiled using a recent MF Cobol and is compiled to .int
or .gnt then 'porting' is probably not necessary, just get a Linux
run-time 'Application Server' and run it.
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Not true - MERANT *does* now have Linux product now.  (Of course you can
> also use the emulation approach also works)

What, specifically, did you think was 'not true' in what was actually
said ?

In fact I run SCO compiled .int and .gnt using the Linux run-time.  To
run SCO compiled .gnt the IBC2 "SCO emulator" may well be required even
when using the Linux run-time (due to .gnt containing OS dependancies). 
The other way of running Linux compiled .gnt on SCO run-time would
probably require lxrun linux emulator.

Of course SCO, or other, compiled .int should run using the Linux
run-time without any requirements for IBC2.


> Bill Klein
```

```
>I have a system that was written for SCO Unix in MF COBOL. I want to port it
>to Linux, which COBOL should I use?

We ran applications written in Cobol and unsing the Micro Focus
compiler on a SCO system and also Unixware.
We decided to switch to Linux.
We installed the MF compiler for Linux, moved the source and data
files to the Linux box, compiled and ... ran the applications.

It's that simple!

Alain Reymond
```

```
Alain,

I'm curious. While I know there are the odd (small ?) software houses here in
Calgary that promote Linux, generally speaking it hasn't yet hit any credibility
level with the oil patch - arguments being, 'We can use all these packages with a

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Linux

- **From:** alain.reymond-nospam@ceia.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T11:07:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aaa0b0d.3014033@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<3aa47e1a_2@news1.prserv.net> <3aa73ce2.480681@news.skynet.be> <3AA7DFA2.95905C0A@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

Our choice might be curious but I can explain the reasons that lead us
to that conclusion.

We have been developping application for 30 years on various system
but always in Cobol. We develop for our own needs and not for resale.
We switch to Unix and MF at the beginning of the 80s. We are only 2
programmers, so the internal human resources are quite limited but we
have an experience of Cobol under Unix.

Due to historical reasons, we had to maintain programs (written in MF
Cobol) under different systems : Unixware 2, SCO 3.2. It meant the
cost of different compilers and, although all Unix, dealing with small
differences from machine to machine.

We arrived at a point where two of our main servers became old and
needed to be replaced (one in France, the other in Belgium) and where
we had to implement other programs in France and Canada and probably
in Germany and Spain. We decided it was time to rationalize and
standardize all our systems. Switching to NT was a step we didn't
wanted to do (cost and expertize). We evaluated the cost of upgrading
all our systems to SCO. But when you calculate the price of the
different licences, it became a budget we couldn't afford. 

Linux appeared to be a reasonable option : 
- it's a unix-like system so there no waste of time for us,
- it is known to be stable,
- the MF compiler is avalaible and we pay only one compiler, not for
different systems,
- the cost of licence is nihil and we can deploy as many systems as we
need with the compiled code and the run-time fee.

We have now began the process of changing from our old server to brand
new system. A Dell server is not expensive and comes with everything
installed. We can even install Linux on our old server and use it as a
backup system. The second server will be installed in the next days in
Paris and then Brussels, Montreal.

I hope it was not a too boring explanation of our choice...

Alain Reymond


>Alain,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Making a conditional, multiple random number generator — 4 msgs

### Making a conditional, multiple random number generator

- **From:** "J. Reis" <jreis26@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T08:59:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iZ6p6.2325$dV3.114811@news.uswest.net>`

```
Greetings all.

For part of a class that I am taking, I am developing a random number
generator.  I want this number generator to display eight unique,
non-repeating numbers at random in a range of 1 - 99 and a nineth number to
be drawn from 1-99 that could duplicate one of the first eight.  Basically I
am making a bigger lottery Powerball game.  This supposed to work on the
fujitsu compiler on a PC.  I have figured out how to use the random number
feature but I cant seem to get the right commands to make it funtion
multiple times in the manner described above.  If anyone can give a little
help or some advice, it would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks.

J.  Reis
```

```
Floyd Johnson
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Making a conditional, multiple random number generator

- **From:** "J. Reis" <jreis26@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T09:53:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fK7p6.2788$dV3.145307@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<iZ6p6.2325$dV3.114811@news.uswest.net> <982uci$vvs$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
Not a problem.  Btw - its for a GrandView class.

Ok, I have the single random number code worked out.
Looks like this:
100 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
110 01  RANDOM-SEED      PIC 9(8)     VALUE ZEROS.
120 01  RANDOM-NUMBER    PIC 99       VALUE ZEROS.
130 01  RANDOM-GENERATE  PIC V9(18)   VALUE ZEROS.

160     MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE (9:8) TO RANDOM-SEED
170     COMPUTE RANDOM-GENERATE = FUNCTION RANDOM (RANDOM-SEED)
180     COMPUTE RANDOM-NUMBER = (RANDOM-GENERATE * 99) + 1
190     DISPLAY RANDOM-NUMBER

Where I run into difficulty is making it do multiple numbers.  Can you add
the same statement multiple times to complete the same function and then add
a statement to compare the numbers and make it recalculate and of the
duplicate numbers?  That would seem a bit clunky for coding.  It would seem
to me that maybe there is a line command to produce multiple numbers at
once.

J.  Reis

number

little
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making a conditional, multiple random number generator

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T11:46:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA51483.E1F67415@netins.net>`
- **References:** `<iZ6p6.2325$dV3.114811@news.uswest.net> <982uci$vvs$1@ins21.netins.net> <fK7p6.2788$dV3.145307@news.uswest.net>`

```
Some hints that might help - 

It looks like you have the code right for a single value.  

How you handle the other three would depend on what features of COBOL
you have previously seen.  For example the four random values could be
stored in a table or as four separate variables.  Either technique would
be valid.

You will need to use a looping structure (i.e. IF-THEN-ELSE) to
determine whether you are repeating any values.  it might reduce the
complexity if you could initialize the random variables to illogical
values (e.g. negative numbers).  Finally, complexity could be reduced by
using a separate paragraph or subprogram (if you have seen these in your
COBOL course) to handle the duplication problem.  If a number is
duplicated, it will need to be re-COMPUTEed.  

Let us know if this helps or if more information is needed.

Floyd Johnson

"J. Reis" wrote:
> 
> Not a problem.  Btw - its for a GrandView class.
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> > +-----------------------------------------------------------+
```

<a id="t-d28233e4e8"></a>
## Thread: Health Followup — 1 msgs

### Health Followup

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T10:47:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA50688.B20A331E@netins.net>`

```
One of the longtime members of Reborn has written to ask about my
health.  Rather than answer just this one person, I thought it might be
helpful to let the entire group in on what is going on.

It has now been about two months since I started my medication.  As I
have written previously, there are three choices concerning the
medication available for the type of MS that I have been diagnosed
with.  I chose to take COPAXONE which involves a daily, relatively
painless  injection with minimal side effects.  The medication is not
designed to make one better - but to prevent the disease from
progressing.  So, even if there are further exacerbation from the MS,
once can only guess that they come less frequently or with less
severity.  There are times that I feel like skipping the shot - even
after two months it feels like a drag!

At this point I am doing much better than when I was earlier this school
year (i.e. mid October).  At that point both legs were numb from my feet
to my knees and my waist also had significant numbness.  Most of the
numbness is gone - at this point my feet feel very raw, as if someone
has scraped them with a large wood file.  Most of this improvement had
occurred before I began medication.  

I continue my daily job.  Also, since being diagnosed last December I
have made significant trips to Illinois to attend the Urbana Missionary
Convention and Charlotte, NC, to attend a Computer Science Education
conference.  I have had no further attacks since the major problems last
fall.  Probably the biggest lost is the ability to drive.  I will try
again once this snow is gone and I am convinced that I can do so safely.

On a more important note, my spiritual life continues to grow.  Urbana
had a major spiritual impact on my (and my wife's!) life.  In fact,
Sandra, who has long struggled with baptism (she was baptized at 12
years of age, before she understood what it was to be a Christian), has
decided to be baptized next summer when our pastor will have a baptismal
service.  

I do appreciate your prayers - both past, present, and future.  God has
been good.  I hope that sort of brings you up to date.  Feel free to
ask  for more if there are questions.  I expect for some, I have given
too much information; though others will want more details.

Yours because His,

Floyd Johnson
```

---

## Thread: Cyclic Redundancy Check 16 — 6 this month / 7 total

### Cyclic Redundancy Check 16

- **From:** George <WR3B@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-06T20:30:09
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<taai6hnpjn4g46@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Okay, has anyone ever implemented a CRC16 field using COBOL?  I have a "C" 
language source code and will convert that if necessary, but before I do, 
I thought I might search the web to see if someone else invented this 
wheel before me.  If you have the source in COBOL or COBOL II, I would 
appreciate it.  I really would hate to have to pull out my "C" manuals so 
I can remember what the << ^^ characters mean.  

It is terrible to not use a programming language for twelve years then 
need it again!@!
  

e.g.
CRC16(USHORT CrcValue, PUCHAAR pBUFFER, USHORT BufLen);
Huge Table:
static USHORT CrcTable[] = {
0x0000, 0x1021, 0x2042, 0x3063, 0x4084, 0x50a5, 0x60c6, 0x70e7,
0x8108, ....


while (BufLen--)
{
   CrcValue = (CrcValue << 8) ^ CrcTable[ (CrcValue >>8) ^ *pBuffer++];
}
return (CrcValue);
```

#### ↳ Re: Cyclic Redundancy Check 16

- **From:** Rob Lec <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T00:03:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA57AE1.2149C70E@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<taai6hnpjn4g46@corp.supernews.com>`

```
The code referenced in your post has something to do with shifting the
position of the elements in the table.  The "<<8" shifts the data 8 bits
to the left.  I don't remember exactly how it works, and I can't provide
any COBOL source code. I merely lifted some C code from Chuck Forsburg's
DSZ documentation when I needed a CRC routine. There is also a
bare-bones DOS assembler CRC program in the old PC Mag archives.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cyclic Redundancy Check 16

- **From:** "Mike Lucich" <lucichm@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T08:16:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%7mp6.19503$YF4.4118819@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com>`
- **References:** `<taai6hnpjn4g46@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I don't have COBOL source, either.  ^ is the "bitwise exclusive or"
operator.  It's used to compare two operands, bit by bit.  If the bit being
compared is a 1 in either one operand or the other, but not both, then the
corresponding bit in the result is set to 1, otherwise it's set to 0.  The
function you show expects to be passed three parameters: a 16 bit unsigned
integer (presumably appropriately initialized), a pointer to a character
string, and a 16 bit unsigned integer (presumably set to the length of the
string).  The value of the first parameter is shifted right eight bits, so
the low-order eight bits are dropped as the high-order eight bits are moved
right and replaced by zeros.  This value and the first character of the
string are then compared with the "bitwise exclusive or".  The result is
then used as an index to obtain a 16 bit unsigned integer value from the
huge table.  The original value of the first parameter is then shifted left
eight bits, so the high-order eight bits are dropped as the low-order bits
are moved left and replaced by zeros.  This value and the value obtained
from the table are then compared with a "bitwise exclusive or", and the
value of the first parameter is replaced with the result.  Next the pointer
to the buffer is incremented and the process is repeated, using the next
character and the updated value of the first parameter.  This is repeated
for each character in the buffer.  Upon completion, the function returns a
sixteen bit unsigned integer equal to the value of the final updated value
of the first parameter, which is the 16-bit CRC checksum hash of the
character string.

I think that's what it will do...

"George" <WR3B@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:taai6hnpjn4g46@corp.supernews.com...
> Okay, has anyone ever implemented a CRC16 field using COBOL?  I have a "C"
> language source code and will convert that if necessary, but before I do,
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cyclic Redundancy Check 16

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T02:35:31-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<984rtn$fh7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<taai6hnpjn4g46@corp.supernews.com> <%7mp6.19503$YF4.4118819@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
I have *no* knowledge of "CRC16" or the "^" operator in C - *but* as the
original question mentioned "COBOL or COBOL II" - my *guess* is that this is
an IBM mainframe question.

If the person asking the original question has access to the LE (rather than
VS COBOL II) run-time system, it *does* provide a set of callable routines
for doing bit manipulations.  I believe that they would be MUCH easier to
use (if you really do need/want to implement this in COBOL) than trying to
"x-or" and shift specific bits.

Generally, this does *not* sound to me like a "normal" COBOL application -
but that isn't to say that it can't be done in it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cyclic Redundancy Check 16

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>

- **Message-ID:** `<E479E4F58B613836.48F77C5FBE86E4C6.DC6477EA3DB3FC38@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<taai6hnpjn4g46@corp.supernews.com> <%7mp6.19503$YF4.4118819@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
On Wed, 07 Mar 2001 08:16:59 GMT, "Mike Lucich" <lucichm@home.com>
enlightened us:

>I don't have COBOL source, either.  ^ is the "bitwise exclusive or"
>operator.  It's used to compare two operands, bit by bit.  If the bit being
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here is a little simplier explanation/flow of CRC16 that might help
you code it in Cobol:

Remember a CRC16 has a 17bit divisor. Bit 16 (As you know we start at
bit 0) is 1. When not we have again a smaller divisor.

     CRC : wordvalue

     { This routine has to be executed 8 times }
     IF CRC bit 15 = 1
     then
     shift left 1, divide by divisor (16 bits)
     else
     shift left 1, {do not divide cause we can't}

     {After this put in the next byte}
     CRC = CRC + inputbyte

     {end of this routine}


Since you have a problem reading C, maybe this routine in Pascal will
be easier:

      inpbyte = input byte for CRC
     oldCRC = old crc value
     divisor = the least 16 bits of the divisor string

     Function CRC16 ( inpbyte : byte, oldCRC : word, divisor : word )
:
     word ;

     var
     tel : word;
     temp : word;  {A simple variable to use to store the CRC)

     begin
     temp := oldCRC;
     for tel := 1 to 8 do
     begin

       If (temp and $8000)= $8000
       then
         begin
           temp := temp shl 1;
           temp := temp xor divisor;
         end
       else
         begin
           temp := temp shl 1;
         end;

       { Now we have to put in the next byte }

       temp := temp xor inpbyte;

       CRC16 := temp;

     end;

     {End of routine}

This routine is easily to expand to CRC32. In that case you have to
expand your divisor and temp and CRC function to LONG value's.

Some additional information about CRC's :

CRC16 divisor =  $1021 ( + bit 16 = $3021 )
The CRC16 feed value (when you first call the CRC routine) is $0000
CRC32 divisor =  $77073096 ( + bit 32 = $17707306 )
The CRC32 feed value (when you first call the CRC routine) is
$FFFFFFFF

You will need to look at the Cobol LE callable routines for bit
shifting.

HTH

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I feel like I'm diagonally parked, in a parallel universe.


Help Save The Rainforest
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Cyclic Redundancy Check 16

- **From:** "Walter Murray" <walter_murray@hp.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T08:54:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<985pvt$a1b$1@web1.cup.hp.com>`
- **References:** `<taai6hnpjn4g46@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"George" <WR3B@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:taai6hnpjn4g46@corp.supernews.com...
> Okay, has anyone ever implemented a CRC16 field using COBOL?

Here's something I wrote years ago.  Not exactly what you want, but it might
be useful.  The routine COMPUTE-XOR contains code to calculate an 8-bit
exclusive or.  It should be highly portable.

Walter

-----cut here-----
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. DEMO.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  MSG          PIC X(80).
 01  LEN          PIC 9(4)     COMP.
 01  RESULT       PIC X.
 01  HEX          PIC XX.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 BEGIN.
     MOVE "THIS IS A TEST" TO MSG, MOVE 14 TO LEN
     CALL "COMPUTE-XOR" USING MSG, LEN, RESULT
     CALL "CHAR-TO-HEX" USING RESULT, HEX
     DISPLAY QUOTE, MSG (1:LEN), QUOTE, " --> ", HEX
     STOP RUN.
 END PROGRAM DEMO.
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. COMPUTE-XOR INITIAL.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  CHARNUM      PIC 9(4)     COMP.
 01  TEMP1        PIC 999      COMP.
 01  TEMP2        PIC 999      COMP.
 01  BIT          PIC 9        COMP.
 01  RUNNING-XOR.
     02  XOR-BIT  PIC 9        COMP
                  OCCURS 8     INDEXED BITNUM
                  VALUE 0.
 LINKAGE SECTION.
 01  MSG          PIC X(80).
 01  LEN          PIC 9(4)     COMP.
 01  RESULT       PIC X.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MSG, LEN, RESULT.
 BEGIN.
     PERFORM VARYING CHARNUM FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL CHARNUM > LEN
         COMPUTE TEMP1 = FUNCTION ORD (MSG (CHARNUM:1)) - 1
         PERFORM VARYING BITNUM FROM 8 BY -1 UNTIL BITNUM < 1
             DIVIDE TEMP1 BY 2 GIVING TEMP2 REMAINDER BIT
             MOVE TEMP2 TO TEMP1
             IF BIT = 1
                 IF XOR-BIT (BITNUM) = 0
                     MOVE 1 TO XOR-BIT (BITNUM)
                 ELSE
                     MOVE 0 TO XOR-BIT (BITNUM)
                 END-IF
             END-IF
         END-PERFORM
     END-PERFORM
     INITIALIZE TEMP1
     PERFORM VARYING BITNUM FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL BITNUM > 8
         MULTIPLY 2 BY TEMP1
         ADD XOR-BIT (BITNUM) TO TEMP1
     END-PERFORM
     MOVE FUNCTION CHAR (TEMP1 + 1) TO RESULT
     EXIT PROGRAM.
 END PROGRAM COMPUTE-XOR.
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. CHAR-TO-HEX.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  HEX-TABLE    PIC X(16)    VALUE "0123456789ABCDEF".
 01  CHARNUM      PIC 999      COMP.
 01  HEX1         PIC 99       COMP.
 01  HEX2         PIC 99       COMP.
 LINKAGE SECTION.
 01  CHAR         PIC X.
 01  HEX          PIC XX.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING CHAR, HEX.
 BEGIN.
     COMPUTE CHARNUM = FUNCTION ORD (CHAR) - 1
     DIVIDE CHARNUM BY 16 GIVING HEX1, REMAINDER HEX2
     MOVE HEX-TABLE (HEX1 + 1 : 1) TO HEX (1:1)
     MOVE HEX-TABLE (HEX2 + 1 : 1) TO HEX (2:1)
     EXIT PROGRAM.
 END PROGRAM CHAR-TO-HEX.
-----cut here-----
```
