```
    05  FILLER		    PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
    05  DESC-PR	            PIC X(15).
    05  FILLER		    PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.
    05  UNITS-PR              PIC ZZZ,ZZ9.
    05  REVENUE-PR            PIC ZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.
 01  SUMMARY-DETAIL.
      05  SUM-PROD-LINE          PIC X.
      05  SUM-REVENUE OCCURS 3 TIMES.
            10  FILLER                   PIC XXX.
             10  SUM-REV-PR        PIC $$$$,$$9.
 01 DETAIL-LINES.
   05 PRODUCT-DL. 
      10 FILLER	              PIC X(13) VALUE "Product Line:".
      10 FILLER 	              PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACE.
      10 DL-SOFT-PRODUCT-LINE   PIC X     VALUE SPACE.
   05 PROGRAM-NAME-DL.
      10 FILLER		      PIC X(14) VALUE "Product Name: ". 
      10 DL-SOFT-PROGRAM-NAME   PIC X(18) VALUE SPACES.
   05 SELL-PRICE-DL.
      10 FILLER     	      PIC X(12) VALUE "Sell Price: ".
      10 DL-SOFT-SELL-PRICE     PIC $$$,$$9.99.
      10 FILLER		      PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.
      10 FILLER                 PIC X(21) VALUE "Total Variable Cost: ". 
      10 DL-SOFT-VARIABLE-COST  PIC ZZ9.99.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 MAINLINE.
 	   PERFORM 100-INITIALIZATION.
     PERFORM 200-MAIN-PROCESS UNTIL EOF-FLAG = 'Y'. 
     PERFORM 600-PRINT-DETAIL-SUM.
     CLOSE SOFTWARE-FILE.
     CLOSE PRINT-FILE.   	
	   	 STOP RUN.
 100-INITIALIZATION.
* INSERT DATE LOGIC
     OPEN INPUT SOFTWARE-FILE
          OUTPUT PRINT-FILE.

     
     PERFORM VARYING S-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
          UNTIL S-SUB > 6
         MOVE SPACES TO PRODUCT-LINE (S-SUB)
         PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
            UNTIL A-SUB > 3
                 MOVE 0 TO WS-TOTAL (A-SUB)        
         END-PERFORM
     END-PERFORM.



	   PERFORM 400-CLEAR-ANALYSIS.
     READ SOFTWARE-FILE. 
     PERFORM 300-LINE-BREAK.
 200-MAIN-PROCESS.
      
      IF SOFT-PRODUCT-LINE NOT = PRODUCT-LINE (S-SUB)
			PERFORM 300-LINE-BREAK
	   	END-IF.

	    MOVE 0 TO A-SUB.

      PERFORM 201-VARIABLE-COST-CALC.
      PERFORM 202-BREAKEVEN-CALC.
 	    PERFORM 203-REVENUE-CALC.
      PERFORM 204-MOVE-TO-TABLES.

      PERFORM 205-PROFIT-UNITS.
      PERFORM 203-REVENUE-CALC.
      PERFORM 204-MOVE-TO-TABLES.

      PERFORM 206-DECLINE-CALC.
      PERFORM 203-REVENUE-CALC.
      PERFORM 204-MOVE-TO-TABLES.

      PERFORM 500-PRINT-DETAIL.
      PERFORM 700-READ-NEXT-RECORD.

 201-VARIABLE-COST-CALC.
      
     COMPUTE WS-VARIABLE-COSTS = ((SOFT-LOADING-PER-DISK + 1) * 
             SOFT-NO-DISKS-USED) + SOFT-MANUAL-PRINTING +
		   SOFT-SHIPPING-N-HANDLING
     END-COMPUTE.

 202-BREAKEVEN-CALC.
     COMPUTE  WS-UNITS ROUNDED = SOFT-FIXED-COST / 
			(SOFT-SELL-PRICE - WS-VARIABLE-COSTS)
     END-COMPUTE.
     
 203-REVENUE-CALC.
     COMPUTE WS-REVENUE = (WS-UNITS * 
             SOFT-SELL-PRICE)
     END-COMPUTE.
     
 204-MOVE-TO-TABLES.
     ADD 1 TO A-SUB.
     MOVE WS-UNITS TO PROD-UNITS (A-SUB).
     MOVE WS-REVENUE TO PROD-REVENUE (A-SUB).
	   ADD WS-REVENUE TO LINE-REVENUE (S-SUB A-SUB).
     ADD WS-REVENUE TO WS-TOTAL (A-SUB).
 205-PROFIT-UNITS.
     COMPUTE WS-UNITS = (50000 + SOFT-FIXED-COST) /
	     (SOFT-SELL-PRICE - WS-VARIABLE-COSTS)
	   END-COMPUTE.

 206-DECLINE-CALC.
     COMPUTE WS-DECLINE = (SOFT-SELL-PRICE * .75)
	   END-COMPUTE.

 300-LINE-BREAK.
     ADD 1 TO S-SUB.
*     MOVE SOFT-PRODUCT-LINE TO (S-SUB).

 400-CLEAR-ANALYSIS.
	   PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
     	UNTIL A-SUB > 3
	 	   MOVE 0 TO PROD-UNITS (A-SUB)
		   MOVE 0 TO PROD-REVENUE (A-SUB)
     END-PERFORM.

 500-PRINT-DETAIL.
     MOVE SOFT-FIXED-COST  TO TOT-FIXED-COSTS.
     MOVE SOFT-PRODUCT-LINE TO DL-SOFT-PRODUCT-LINE.
     MOVE SOFT-PROGRAM-NAME TO DL-SOFT-PROGRAM-NAME.
*     MOVE WS-UNITS TO 
     MOVE SOFT-SELL-PRICE   TO DL-SOFT-SELL-PRICE.
     MOVE WS-VARIABLE-COSTS TO DL-SOFT-VARIABLE-COST.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TITLE-LINE-1 AFTER PAGE.
	   WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TITLE-LINE-2 AFTER 1.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TITLE-LINE-3 AFTER 1.
*     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TOT-FIXED-COSTS AFTER 1.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PRODUCT-DL AFTER 1.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PROGRAM-NAME-DL AFTER 1.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM SELL-PRICE-DL AFTER 1.
     
     PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
		UNTIL A-SUB > 3
		MOVE  DETAIL-DESC (A-SUB)  TO DESC-PR
		MOVE  PROD-UNITS (A-SUB)   TO UNITS-PR
		MOVE  PROD-REVENUE (A-SUB) TO REVENUE-PR
		WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER 1
     END-PERFORM.
     PERFORM 400-CLEAR-ANALYSIS.
 600-PRINT-DETAIL-SUM.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TITLE-LINE-1     AFTER PAGE.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TITLE-LINE-SUM-2 AFTER 1. 
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TITLE-LINE-SUM-3 AFTER 1.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TITLE-LINE-SUM-4 AFTER 1.
     WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM TITLE-LINE-SUM-5 AFTER 1.
      PERFORM VARYING S-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
          UNTIL S-SUB > 6
         MOVE PRODUCT-LINE (S-SUB) TO SUM-PROD-LINE         
           PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL A-SUB > 3
                MOVE LINE-REVENUE (S-SUB  A-SUB) TO SUM-REV-PR (S-SUB)   
           END-PERFORM
       WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM SUMMARY-DETAIL AFTER 1 
      END-PERFORM.

     MOVE 'TOTAL' TO SUM-PROD-LINE   
     PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
      UNTIL A-SUB > 3
         MOVE WS-TOTAL (A-SUB) TO SUM-REV-PR (A-SUB)
     END-PERFORM.
 700-READ-NEXT-RECORD.
     READ SOFTWARE-FILE
       AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-FLAG
     END-READ.
*********JOKE*********

* what do u call a cow with no legs?
*
*
*-Ground Beef
*What are four hundred rabbits hopping backwards?
*-A receding hare line
```

#### ↳ Re: question(homework problem)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T10:44:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<985od5$fp7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010306220836.19425.00000129@ng-ba1.aol.com>`

```
I don't know if it is the ONLY problem, but I do see one thing (possibly not
clear when you read ahead a chapter),

Look at:


>
>  01 DETAIL-TABLE.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

How big is the original item (DETAIL-TABLE)?  Now, how big is the redefined
(01-FILLER)?

There is a GENERAL rule in redefines (with some exceptions - and some
exceptions for specific compilers - but you are always safer if you follow
this rule)

NEVER redefine a smaller field by a larger field.

When you (eventually) reference
    Detail-Desc - with a subscript of 3,
what storage will it get?

Basically, the "results are unpredictable".  (If you weren't doing 01-level
redefines, things can get even messier).

Some (many? most?) compilers give warning messages against doing this - but
if yours doesn't, you still shouldn't do it.
```

#### ↳ Re: question(homework problem)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T10:59:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<985pet$7tq$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010306220836.19425.00000129@ng-ba1.aol.com>`

```
I think that I just saw the second problem. Look at:

   <snip>
>  01 DETAIL-LINE.
>     05  FILLER     PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     05  REVENUE-PR            PIC ZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.
   <snip>
>
>      PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>      END-PERFORM.

After you have done the first WRITE, what is in the full "DETAIL-LINE"?  Do
you ever clear it out?  What is in DETAIL-LINE after the 2nd write and
before you loop back to "format" the 3rd detail line?  Do you ever clear it
out?  If you don't "clear" your detail lines between writes, then you MUST
"Fill in" everything that you don't want to show up from the last line.

P.S.  Did you want to have any filler between UNITS-PR and REVENUE-PR?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: question(homework problem)

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T02:40:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4Mfr6.2716$54.3025@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<20010306220836.19425.00000129@ng-ba1.aol.com> <985pet$7tq$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hi Derrick,

I may be missing something, but I think maybe you're making things hard for
yourself by coding a table for the detail line info. Why not just code 3 detail
lines, e.g.:

01  dtl-1.
05  d1-desc           pic x(014) value 'blah blah blah'.
05  fil                   blah
05  d1-amt            pic zzz blah.


01  dtl-2.
05  d2-desc           pic x(014) value 'blah blah blah'.
05  fil                   blah
05  d2-amt            pic zzz blah.

01  dtl-3.
05  d3-desc           pic x(014) value 'blah blah blah'.
05  fil                   blah
05  d3-amt            pic zzz blah.


Then just move and write the Breakeven info (dtl-1), move and write the Profit
info (dtl-2), and 
move and write the Decline info (dtl-3). No tables, no subscripts, just moves
and writes.

Hope this helps, Jack.   




In article <985pet$7tq$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>, William M. Klein says...
>
>I think that I just saw the second problem. Look at:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>

- **Message-ID:** `<74D47046134AB286.C6CE1CAD2933440D.FC09E3288DD4D474@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<20010306220836.19425.00000129@ng-ba1.aol.com>`

```
On 07 Mar 2001 03:08:36 GMT, deek40@aol.com (Deek40) enlightened us:

>I have a problem i cant get my program to function properly and have tried to
>fix it many times.
>

I'm not sure I found everything wrong, but scroll on down for a couple
of things I found that were suspect.


>here is what i am getting with my report:
> Nexus Software INC.                                          
…[185 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Those previous PERORMs - you have a PERFORM within a PERFORM -I think
you are clearing the wrong fields -  You should maybe have them as:

     PERFORM VARYING S-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
          UNTIL S-SUB > 6
             MOVE SPACES TO PRODUCT-LINE (S-SUB)
             PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
                  UNTIL A-SUB > 3
                       MOVE ZEROES TO LINE-REVENUE (A-SUB)
             END-PERFORM
     END-PERFORM.

     PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
          UNTIL A-SUB > 3
                 MOVE 0 TO WS-TOTAL (A-SUB)        
     END-PERFORM.

>
>	   PERFORM 400-CLEAR-ANALYSIS.
>     READ SOFTWARE-FILE. 


Okay big problem here.  In your 300-LINE-BREAK you are adding 1 to
S-SUB then inserting an item into the table.  Problem is S-SUB is
sitting at 7 because you used it in the PERFORM VARYING prior to this.
So, simply do MOVE 0 TO S-SUB before you PERFORM 300-LINE-BREAK.  This
may clear up your problems.


>     PERFORM 300-LINE-BREAK.
> 200-MAIN-PROCESS.
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Okay here's another trouble spot.  You've calculated WS-DECLINE but
you don't save it anywhere!!

> 300-LINE-BREAK.
>     ADD 1 TO S-SUB.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>     

The following print routine is where your DECLINE line (A-SUB = 3) is
printed, but as I pointed out previously you didn't save any amounts
and your table is probably askew due to the first error I showed you.


>     PERFORM VARYING A-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
>		UNTIL A-SUB > 3
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>*-A receding hare line 

Correct errors and try again and remember..patience and perseverance
are a programmers two most valuable assets.

BTW..what do you call a man in the middle of the ocean with no arms
and no legs?
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
BOB
.
.
Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

http://www.ran.org/ran/

Steve
```

## Thread: COBOL needs your help. — 110 msgs

### COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T04:29:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net>`

```

COBOL needs your help.     A US public review is underway on the
reaffirmation of the current ANSI COBOL standard.   If not reaffirmed,
the standard will be withdrawn.  You might think it does not matter if
the current standard is withdrawn.   But IT DOES MATTER.  Withdrawal
of the ANSI standard will create a perception that nobody cares about
COBOL and the media will use it for more articles saying COBOL is
dead.  It is a *black eye* for COBOL.

Can you do anything to ensure the standard is reaffirmed.  Maybe not.
Due to an administrative oversight the public review was not started
in time to complete reaffirmation by the required date IF THERE ARE
negative comments. I'm sure you know that Bill Klein has submitted a
comment opposing reaffirmation.

Does that mean your support of reaffirmation would be wasted.
ABSOLUTELY NOT.

Even if not reaffirmed, do you want it said that COBOL died and nobody
cared?   Positive support for reaffimation would do a lot to blunt the
harm that will be done by withdrawal of the standard.  PERCEPTION IS
EVERYTHING.

What can you do?   Send your comment in support of reaffirmation BY
APRIL 4, 2001.     You do not need to be a US citizen.    The
following gives the information you need to submit a comment (send to
both NCITS and ANSI, reference the COBOL standard and its two
amendments):

To:  Deborah Donovan, NCITS    ddonovan@itic.org
cc:  PSA Department, ANSI        psa@ansi.org

Subject:   Public review comment on Reaffirmation of:
   1)    X3.23:1985 [R1991] - Programming language COBOL;
   2)    X3.23a:1985/ADD 1:1989 [R1991], Addendum to ANSI X3.23-1985,
             Programming Language - Intrinsic Function Module for
COBOL;
and
   3)    X3.23b:1985/ADD 2:1993 [R1998], Correction Amendment to ANSI
X3.23:1985,
             Programming Language COBOL and ANSI X3.23-1989 Addendum 1

??. your comments ??..

_____

You do not need to say much more than that you support reaffirmation
of these standards.   If you want to, add more about the importance of
reaffirmation or impact of not reaffirming.

If I can be of help to you, let me know.


---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T00:06:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Alternatively, (sorry about this Thane),

 If you think that the ONLY way that COBOL will survive and grow in the
future is to "get it out of the hands of ANSI (and eventually ISO) - then
send in a Public Review comment suggesting that the existing Standard (and
both of its amendments) be withdrawn.  Again, you can add as much or as
little in the way of supporting argument as you think best.

Note: You may do whatever you think best to make certain that it is ANSI/ISO
(and their current processes) that "get the black eye" - and not COBOL.  If
you think that having an ANSI Standard is important, then share your
experiences with actually USING the ANSI (or FIPS) flagging available with
your "standard conforming" compiler today.

Similarly, PLEASE (no matter which way you feel about reaffirmation and/or
ANSI/ISO) send in your Public Review comments (they must be separate from
the reaffirmation issues) on the FCD (Final Committee Draft) that is
currently out for review.

My recommendations on HOW to go about a review and comment on this can be
found at:

    http://www.cobolreport.com/columnists/bill/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T08:24:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My trouble with the next standard is that I figure I will be wishing to have
those features where I work.   Enterprises moved to the latest compilers with
the Y2K conversions.  But generally, they don't wish to upgrade their
compilers.   Multiple compilers are a headache, and moving to one compiler could
break things unassociated to the maintenance being done - unless a big project
had been done to change working programs from one compiler to the next along
with extensive testing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T10:35:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Howard,
  Your reply also assumes that implementors will implement

A) all the "required" parts of the new Standard
B) the "processor dependent" parts that you are interested in

My guess (at this time) is that it will be a LONG time before you see both
(possibly either) of those.
```

```
innovation can drive the language?  That makes NO SENSE.  You offer NO
substitute for the standards - another body or anything - simply it's
absence.   If the vendors won't implement what is in the standard (
and I do NOT think that is true ) what hope is there of them doing it
"on their own".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T21:21:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<986tsn$uc2$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa6d506.160098897@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Well,
  Look at the portability of COMP-3, GoBack, USAGE Pointer today

Was it any "Standard" that made this "portable"?  NO - it was user-demand.

Does Fujitsu implement CBL_ routines (to provide portability with Micro
Focus) - Yes
Does MERANT implement CEE routines (to provide portability with IBM) - Yes

Is Fujitsu publicly "committed" to providing whatever *new* syntax/semantics
are needed for .NET - Yes

The answer is that implementors do what users and their environment
"demand".  Today, however, they OFTEN use "the Standard isn't final yet" to
*not* implement what users are asking for:

  Compare
    Table Sorts
     EXIT PERFORM/SECTION syntax
     INITIALIZE enhancements
     Recursive programs

All of these are really "quite" stable in the DRAFT standard - and users
have asked various vendors to provide support for them.  Yet, I know of at
least 3 (unnamed) vendors who have used the "we will do it when the Standard
is approved" as an excuse for NOT doing them  yet.

Take away that EXCUSE and there is at least a CHANCE that vendors will be
MORE responsive to their own customers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T04:13:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aa7059c.172538167@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa6d506.160098897@news1.attglobal.net> <986tsn$uc2$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Your examples are minor featurettes not anything substantive.  What
are the odds for a common OO syntax without the standard?  ABSOLUTELY
ZERO.  What about typdefs?  What about user defined functions?   Dare
I say, common exception handling?  These are all great ideas - and
many origninate in the draft.  While I agree that the draft ALSO
implements constructive and successful vendor functions, it works BOTH
ways.

What about common syntax for record/file locking?  I already fight
conversion issues with this due to differeing syntax and behavior -- I
expect these problems to be alleviated by a common standard -- just as
the originators of '68 COBOL did when there were numerous disparate
versions.

What about open source COBOL compilers?  What would be their TARGET?
Without a public international standard they would be "stealing" and
would be sued out of existence.

The standards committee is a way for vendors to come together without
the intellectual property baggage and extend the language to meet user
demand.  

Yes, mistakes were made in timeliness - yes it's late.  It's virtually
DONE however, and it's very good. 

What you suggest is foolhardy.


On Wed, 7 Mar 2001 21:21:20 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Well,
>  Look at the portability of COMP-3, GoBack, USAGE Pointer today
…[80 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T22:36:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<987251$t0r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa6d506.160098897@news1.attglobal.net> <986tsn$uc2$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa7059c.172538167@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
As far as file-sharing/record-locking goes, would you like to explain WHY
you think there will be any more portability WITH the Standard than there is
today - given that this is in the "processor dependent" list and some
vendors have already indicated that they have NO intention on implementing
it?

As far as OO syntax goes:
  1) Do you think that those vendors who do support OO today, will STOP
supporting the syntax that is already allowed?  (If not, this means there
won't even be portability within the SAME vendor's implementation.  If so, I
can't wait to see existing programmer reaction to that.)
  2) Which vendors who do NOT currently provide OO support have indicated
any serious interest in providing OO support - once the Standard is
approved?  (Will IBM, for example, add it to OS/400 or VM?  Have you seen
any serious interest in it from AcuCorp, RM, LegacyJ, COMPAQ/Tandem,
COMPAQ/Dec,  etc)

***

I am *not* a Java programmer, but Java has "thrived" without any ANSI/ISO
Standard.
The ANSI PL/I Standard did *not* succeed and was withdrawn (and vendors who
used to support PL/I - still do).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T13:36:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<988jn0$2ijr$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa6d506.160098897@news1.attglobal.net> <986tsn$uc2$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa7059c.172538167@news1.attglobal.net> <987251$t0r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa78565.205252079@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Perhaps two standards are better than one.

1. The official standard from either ANSI or ISO, or both.
2. An industry standard from a consortium of COBOL
    vendors, users, and other interested parties.

At all times, the industry standard will be a proper superset
of the official standard.

The official standard provides a high degree of stability
and portability by having no options and being revised
or amended no more frequently than every five years.

The industry standard provides optional features where
the rules apply only to those choosing to implement the
features. This standard may be amended much more
frequently, perhaps annually.

When an optional feature from the industry standard has
been widely accepted across a range of processors, it is
migrated to the official standard.

While there are an amazing number of details required
to set up a consortium, it is possible and was done with
OMG < www.omg.org >.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T15:37:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<988qpl$2qrg$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa6d506.160098897@news1.attglobal.net> <986tsn$uc2$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa7059c.172538167@news1.attglobal.net> <987251$t0r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa78565.205252079@news1.attglobal.net> <988jn0$2ijr$1@news.hitter.net> <988mfm$j0d$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Actually, it is very different!

The X/Open COBOL standard < www.opengroup.org > specifically
excluded parts of the ANSI/ISO standard and required syntax
that was not present in the ANSI/ISO standard. That meant
conforming programs for one standard could be non-conforming
with the other standard. What I am proposing is that, at no time,
will a program that conforms to the official standard not conform
to the industry standard.

X/Open COBOL was defined with Unix and PCs, in mind, not
mainframe operating systems. Excluding more than half(?) of all
COBOL programs and programmers kept the X/Open standard
from being widely implemented!
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <988mfm$j0d$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>...

>there was/is an X/Open COBOL Standard.
```

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> X/Open COBOL was defined with Unix and PCs, in mind, not
> mainframe operating systems. 

The X/Open standard addressed issues that were appropriate to desktop
and interactive systems, such as record and file locking and screen I/O.

> Excluding more than half(?) of all COBOL programs and programmers 

It wasn't the X/Open standard that excluded mainframe OSes, it was the
Mainframe OSes that excluded the facilities that X/Open addressed.  If
Mainframes do not cater for interactive systems with a process (or
several) running on behalf of each user and direct access to adressable
screens that is not the fault of X/Open or of systems that do cater for
this, such as *nix, Multiuser-DOS and W*ndows.

> kept the X/Open standard from being widely implemented!

Actually the X/Open standard was widely implemented, even by IBM, but
only on systems where it made sense.
```

```
Agree,
Especially since the committee seems to have decided to be 'the
girl who couldn't say no'. Everything but the kitchen sink is in
the new standard. 

Why a character based screen interface should be a mandatory part
of a standard release in 2002 escapes me. Similarly why Report
Writer has to be mandatory is a mystery. Frankly there are
specialised reporting tools that do it better (eg useful support
for fonts and images). Not to mention the OO being mandatory -
this would be like mandating a C compiler to support all of C++.

In spite of the huge volume of mandatory material, it will not be
possible to write a progran that does anything, which is
guaranteed to work on a conforming compiler. 

(Bill by the way I am about half way through the draft standard
and I will be putting in lots of comments).

Tim Josling
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.n0spam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T23:29:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aa86ad9$0$23348@wodc7nh6.news.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA7F2B6.99389304@melbpc.org.au> <3AA837C2.81FE64F6@home.com>`

```
I can live with OO in the COBOL compiler, but living without the screen
section is
not all that much fun. On an MVS mainframe, it isn't much of a problem,
because
you can always code a few panels and just call them.

But on open systems, say UNIX, it is a PITA not to have a screen section.
You wind up using something like CICS just to get screen displays. (Wow-
what a concept - 3270 devices on a UNIX machine. Well, good terminal
emulatin is rarely a problem... :)

In truth, you could use a GUI to deal with the situation, but why? People
want
several things in their programs nowadays - they want to be able to run it
from their PC computer, they want the thing to work correctly, they want the
interface to be steady, unchanging, and easily learned, and they want it to
go FAST.

You *can* code web pages to meet all those needs, but a screen section and a
CUI
does the same thing, cheaper, easier, and it will support more users.

Just my $0.02.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T00:13:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<989s8d$ldv$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA7F2B6.99389304@melbpc.org.au> <3AA837C2.81FE64F6@home.com> <3aa86ad9$0$23348@wodc7nh6.news.uu.net>`

```
Re Screen Section, Report Writer, OO or anything else being "required"
versus "optional'.

At the beginning of the development of this revision, their was STRONG
consensus to get rid of "optional" things in the COBOL Standard.  (This was
almost demanded by WG4 and was strongly supported at J4.)

The EXAMPLE that was used for the reasoning on this was Report Writer which
was optional - but would have been useful to many COBOL programs and
programmers in the '70s and '80s - but was NOT implemented *because* it was
optional. Therefore, programs that did use it (because it was available or
their primary platform) found that their applications were NOT portable.
Furthermore, because some vendors didn't implement it, many programmers
didn't use it and therefore MORE vendors dropped support for it in
subsequent releases.  This situation was viewed as BAD for any feature in
the next COBOL Standard.

This is a position that I *still* support.  As much as I *love* Report
Writer, I would prefer seeing it removed completely from the Standard than
having it be "optional".  The same for Screen Section, OO, etc.

UNFORTUNATELY, somewhere along the line, the draft standard keep the
"removal" of OPTIONAL as a term - but ended up with the EXACT same
"concept" - now called "processor dependent'.  This ORIGINALLY included only
those items that "used to be" in the "hardware dependent" list (i.e. things
that don't make sense if your compiler is targeted at a hardware/operating
system that doesn't support it, e.g REEL for systems without tape drives or
WRITE AFTER ADVANCING for systems without printers).

For various historical, political, and/or country-specific reasons,
Processor Dependent is now defined so that the compiler vendor has COMPLETE
choice in whether or not to implement those features - and need not document
any reason for NOT supporting them.  (This sounds like "optional" to me -
but at the last WG4 meeting, one attendee - who works for a compiler
vendor - said words to the effect of "Let's not call it optional - so our
developers may not notice that they aren't required to support everything in
this list".)

Currently Screen Section, READ PREVIOUS,  and File-Sharing/Record-Locking
*are* in this list; report writer, ISO 10646/Unicode codesets (in some but
not all uses), and OO are not in this list.

I do *not* think that there will be much support at either J4 or WG4 to
change the contents (add or subtract) to this list at this late date.
However, I would suggest that the readers of this note "look at" the current
list - and the list of "enhancement" in the Substantive Change NOT effecting
list - and decide if you think some things are in the processor dependent
list that shouldn't be or aren't and should be.  This is a totally
REASONABLE public review comment - I just don't know how "successful" such a
comment would be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T08:59:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98ar2g$v2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA7F2B6.99389304@melbpc.org.au> <3AA837C2.81FE64F6@home.com> <3aa86ad9$0$23348@wodc7nh6.news.uu.net> <989s8d$ldv$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3aa8d605.41305485@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Because it is the label "optional" (or "processor dependent") which allows
vendors to IGNORE user demand for such features today.  There is more than
one vendor who - when requested to include Report Writer - has indicated
"well it is only an optional module in the Standard, so we don't need to
worry about it".

If *nothing* was "in the Standard" - then vendors would be REQUIRED to
determine which features were requested/required/demanded from their
customers and would implement them  - if not immediately - at least in a
timely enough manner to keep their customer base happy.  They would have NO
excuse for ignoring this customer input.

I do NOT know for certain that having no Standard will work - but I do know
that having one has not and does not.

What is the opposite of the old adage "If it ain't broke don't fix it!" ???
  It must be something like, "If it IS broke, don't try and do a management
reorg to fix it!"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T03:40:52+00:00

```
I don't think you'll find much argument that the Optional items were a
problem - since many vendors did ignore them -- but NOT MOST -- only
the "biggest".

From my experience having a standard has worked.  My company has
acquired businesses and assimilated COBOL Source code into our various
systems from WANG COBOL, RM, OS/390, HP, and the like.  The fact that
it was mostly standard COBOL (WANG has some interesting screen
handling extensions) made this possible.  So I think having one has
worked -- but I fully agree with your take on the "optional" items.

<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Because it is the label "optional" (or "processor dependent") which allows
>vendors to IGNORE user demand for such features today.  There is more than
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

```
acceptance across the various compiler vendors regarding implementation
of "OPTIONAL" features.   The decision should that the feature is or is not
part of the STANDARD.
This would at least put everyone on the same footing when making assumptions
about what is available to use in COBOL in ANY shop.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T09:30:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hG6q6.8199$642.100214@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA7F2B6.99389304@melbpc.org.au> <3AA837C2.81FE64F6@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
> Tim Josling wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Agreed - but I suspect you'll get some objections - from Judson for one <G>

Can't disappoint you, Jimmy. ;-)

Just one reason: Some applications are far and away better served and
implemented by a character interface than a GUI one.  For example,
there are still thousands upon thousands of people sitting at their
keyboards all day keying data into business systems.  For the vast
majority of this type of application, character based interfaces are
superior to GUI in just about every measure.  No tool ever designed by
man is best tool for every possible use.  Why should anyone think GUI
is the sole exception? ;-)

Just as it is important to know several languages so you can use the
one better suited to a particular task, it is better to have both GUI
and character interfaces available, so you can use the one better
suited to a particular task. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T08:54:21+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA9510D.C29A67A5@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA7F2B6.99389304@melbpc.org.au> <3AA837C2.81FE64F6@home.com> <hG6q6.8199$642.100214@news2.atl>`

```
Bill has pointed out it is not actually mandatory because of the
"processor dependent" loophole. 

I totally agree that character based is better for many things eg
bank tellers. Why wade through a GUI when you can just enter a
few fields and press enter. No swapping from mouse to keyboard
etc.

What I take issue with is making a mandatory part of a GP
programming language. Why not mandate a GUI as well? What about
printing that includes color and graphics?

They make all these things mandatory but at the same time the
standard is so lax that it is not possible to write any program
that is guaranteed to compile, let alone run, on a conforming
implementation. Example, a conforming implementation can add
whatever keywords they please and they are not required to
provide a mechanism to turn them off. So any user defined word
could clash with a vendor keyword. There are no standards for
minimum supported complexity except for 7 levels of nested
occurs. Can you have five levels of nested IFs? Try it and
"hope". The vendors are not even required to document these
limitations. Try and compile a really big program in one of the
big name compilers and see how you go. There are lots of fixed
size tables in there. One more patch and you blow the limit and
have to restructure your program. This has happeened to me
several times. And basic features like "packed" are "processor
dependent". You can do packed on any CPU, slowly of course but it
can be done without too much trouble. 

COBOL 85 was ridiculous (viewed from today's perspective) the way
they had umpteen optional features and most features could be
implemnented on any of three levels. So I agree some reduction in
options was needed. 

But I don't see why having a CUI/GUI and printing support, plus
transactional support (lock record etc) are not discrete enough
and of limited interest that they could be made optional.

I do actually plan to do the screen support because I can
leverage Tiny COBOL's runtime quite easily. 

Tim Josling

Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> suited to a particular task. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T07:11:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AACD8FE.50662FE6@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <984j2b$cu3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA6529A.4B0F5844@brazee.net> <985o24$62o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3AA7F2B6.99389304@melbpc.org.au> <3AA837C2.81FE64F6@home.com> <hG6q6.8199$642.100214@news2.atl> <3AA9510D.C29A67A5@melbpc.org.au>`

```
We all know of the jokes about "designs by committee", where the focus is lost and
the product produces many (sometimes bizarre) variations from the original goal.

When CoBOL tries to become all things to all people, is it strengthened?  Is it
weakened?  Neither?

Is this a sign that it is proactively advancing needs or fighting a rearguard
action?

Is Coffee's article that the rapidly changing C++ is the dinosaur and CoBOL the
older, more stable shark no longer true?
```

```
>Just one reason: Some applications are far and away better served and
>implemented by a character interface than a GUI one.  For example,

Hi:

One of the advantages of my approach is that you can make character-oriented
programs using the Windows API via Flexus.  Just because all those buttons
and things are there doesn't mean you have to use them.  The programs look
nicer than the DOS equivalents but work just the same way. Actually, I
suppose it is the best of both worlds - the 'modernity' of Windows with
the efficiency of character-oriented operation. See my example at
foodman123.com/tcs.htm

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

Minimalism IS wonderful
```

```
Actually I'd like to hear more arguments both ways on this issue.  I
know that in Europe and Asia, ISO certification is extremely
important.  Software products that are not ISO (is it 9000 now?)
certified will seldom get sold.  ANSI standard I believe is more
important in North America.  But, I haven't heard either used in
reference to Cobol for many, many years.  Hence, I'd like to hear more
about the effects of reaffirmation or non-reaffirmation before I send
in my comments.

Thanks.

Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-07T21:16:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
We can't "bring back" the ENTRY statement - as it has NEVER been a part of
any ANSI or ISO COBOL Standard.

P.S.  This may also answer (in small part) why I view it as pretty "useless"
to keep the "official" ANSI Standard.
 - No (or incredibly few) programmers use it (restrict code to it)
 - There are currently no vendors validating (by independent testing) that
they conform to the Standard
 - J4 (The ANSI COBOL Technical Committee) does ever "finish" interpretation
requests - so that vendors provide "increased" portability when they
disagree on what the Standard actually says.
```

```
interpretation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T21:17:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```


Alister Munro wrote:

> My comment was supposed to be read as a  bit of
> sarcasm.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well you know what they say about sarcasm - you misfired on this one <G>

Agree, I can't understand why entry points were excluded. Never used 'em - as a
loner couldn't figure them out - wasn't until about two years ago that I saw a
source code example by Stephen Gennard in Merant's Answerlab - then I twigged.

Now the following offers a partial solution to the functionality that you want
PROVIDING your vendor of choice implements these features :-

1. User Defined function - mini programs. For practical purposes you wouldn't
want hundreds of these floating about so you would probably want to include them
in a utility library. (They really are another way of saying OO "methods").

2. OO Methods - don't get hysterical - you don't have to get totally embroiled
in OO and 'objects' to use them. Set yourself up a source file, MyUtilities and
:-

Class :    MyUtilities            is class "myutils".  (... this is the source
filename  )

Working-Storage Section.
01 MyUtilities                                object reference.
01 picture1                                    pic x(...
01 picture2                                    pic 9(....
01 picture3.
    05 pic3A                                    pic x(.....
    05 pic3B                                    pic 9(....

        invoke MyUtilities "new" returning os-MyUtilities

now for each of your current 'entry' functions you can now invoke :-

    invoke os-MyUtilities "do-this-job"
            using picture1, picture2 returning picture3.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T07:02:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Agree, I can't understand why entry points were excluded. Never used 'em - as a
> loner couldn't figure them out - wasn't until about two years ago that I saw a
> source code example by Stephen Gennard in Merant's Answerlab - then I twigged.

I never figured out the advantage of using them.  I would rather have separate
programs for maintenance purposes.


> Now the following offers a partial solution to the functionality that you want
> PROVIDING your vendor of choice implements these features :-
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> in a utility library. (They really are another way of saying OO "methods").

Please explain.  I see two choices which seem preferable to me:
1.  Have hundreds of programs - not floating around but organized.
2.  Have in-line subprograms.

Both of these have the one program/one function mentality which I like for ease of
maintenance.

But since I have not actually seen multiple entry points used in real life, I am
open to be persuaded.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T19:04:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAD1FD4.FDD0B30D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com> <3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> open to be persuaded.

Well firstly, I have no enthusiasm for User Defined Functions. As I said they seem to
me to be parallel to OO methods. As regards entry points they are obsolescent to me
now that I use OO methods.

You haven't said categorically, but are your programs all encompassing or do you use
the three-tier system ? It's my personal belief that the latter is the way to go - the
concept being picked up from the 'Kiwi' many moons ago. See Pete's reply to you and
mine to Tony Dilworth. It may seem that 'Components' are geared to GUI-ing - that's
not the case, hence I suggested Universal Language Utilities.

I'm not clear whether or not down there in Colorado you are just supporting the
University with applications or act as a software house. Let's assume for discussion -
it is the former. Regardless of whether you have designed a Student System (enrolment,
timetables, exam results etc.), and you are in Colorado, Nanuivik or Wonga-Wonga  you
have all produced routines (re-invented the wheel) for functions common to you all.
You may already do so - but it seems to me that these 'generalized' functions can be
'shared' by academic institutions - do you really care, if it works, what language it
is written in ? We are biased, and would suggest COBOL is the best. Rather than
include them as copyfiles or sub-programs, (which I think is what you are suggesting),
why can't they be callable/invokeable as required.

I can anticipate your response - MAINTENANCE. You've got to trust somebody eventually
Howard. Do you use the COBOL intrinsic functions ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T13:09:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAD2CFA.8438820E@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com> <3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net> <3AAD1FD4.FDD0B30D@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> I'm not clear whether or not down there in Colorado you are just supporting the
> University with applications or act as a software house. Let's assume for discussion -
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> why can't they be callable/invokeable as required.

Most of the CoBOL is written around purchase applications designed for smaller university
systems using styles which have solidified programming philosophies which I had before I
came here.


> I can anticipate your response - MAINTENANCE. You've got to trust somebody eventually
> Howard. Do you use the COBOL intrinsic functions ?

I do.  I wish we used fewer copy members and IDD copies, as when we have minimal changes
we have tremendous testing to do.  We do the same testing when we upgrade to a new
compiler.

But I am missing the connection with multi-entry programs - How are they easier than
multiple programs?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T12:49:59+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aad65e6_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com> <3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net> <3AAD1FD4.FDD0B30D@home.com> <3AAD2CFA.8438820E@brazee.net>`

```
Don't know if it helps Howard, but I'll try to answer your question. (In the
good old days (before OO) I used multiple Entry points frequently...)

1. Multiple Entry points allow you to reflect different "facets" of the same
code. The "advantage" was not so much a programming one (you could certainly
achieve the same result with multiple programs, as you pointed out) as an
efficiency one.

As a general rule, the less code you have, the better. There is a very high
risk of error when the same code must be maintained in multiple places.

For example:

Let's say I have a  called program that gets passed a buffer of characters
and converts them to Hex in another buffer.

Sometimes I want to convert Character (ASCII or EBCDIC) to Hexadecimal, and
sometimes I want to convert Hexadecimal back to character . The lookup table
would be duplicated if I used 2 modules. By using 2 different Entry points
to the same module I get ONE piece of code which deals with "conversion" and
I don't have to duplicate any reference data. (It is also self documenting
and easy to remember; call 'Char2Hex' using char-buffer, hex-buffer or, call
'Hex2Char' using char-buffer, hex-buffer...simple, efficient and
consistent.)
(This is a very simple example but it is taken from real life and I actually
have such a subprogram which runs across platforms. I wrote it 30 years ago;
today I would use different Methods in a component, exactly as Jimmy
described for OO.)

2. I have had much more complex examples with up to six different entry
points to manipulate the SAME data in different ways (hence, different
"facets" of the same code or data.)

Sometimes different Entry points can be used to pass different formats of
parameter to the same underlying code "engine".
(This is a variation on "function overloading" in modern parlance.  A
"variation" because function overloading technically requires different
parameters to be passed to the SAME object; although we are using the same
engine, it will have different named entry points...but it is a pedantic
argument. The reality is that the same code can accept various different
parameters. There are times when this can be very useful...)

It would be fair to say that there is no advantage in using entry points in
today's World. I can't think of a single example which could not be
(better?) handled using OO Methods.

Both entry points and Method coding are MUCH better solutions than
duplicating code into separate modules.

Pete.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3AAD2CFA.8438820E@brazee.net>...
>"James J. Gavan" wrote:
>
>
>But I am missing the connection with multi-entry programs - How are they
easier than
>multiple programs?
>

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T03:22:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010312222219.20431.00000598@nso-ba.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3aad65e6_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
In article <3aad65e6_4@news.newsfeeds.com>, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> writes:

>
>It would be fair to say that there is no advantage in using entry points in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'll have to agree with Pete that multiple entry points provides a better
solution to code duplication.  The only argument that I have against
OO is that it is not part of 'shop' standard and I've yet to see anyplace 
that is making OO the primary standard.
Some environments do not support multiple entry points via 
ENTRY PROCEDURE <entry name> USING <parameter list> 
              GIVING <parameter list>
and I have seen the use of a MainLine decision block that branches
based on a control field within the passed parameter list.
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING <function> <function parameter block>
MAINLINE.
      If <function-1>
           perform function-1
      else if <function-2>
           perform function-2
      else .....
      exit program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ ENTRY statements PROS/CONS (was: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T23:08:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98ka40$c6r$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com> <3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net> <3AAD1FD4.FDD0B30D@home.com> <3AAD2CFA.8438820E@brazee.net> <3aad65e6_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
>
> Both entry points and Method coding are MUCH better solutions than
> duplicating code into separate modules.
>

Alternatively, you could use fully ANSI/ISO ('85 Standard) conforming source
code with nested programs and Local and Global data.

I have YET to see any advantage that ENTRY statements have over this
technique.

(Personally, I consider both OO and user-defined functions as doing
"different" things, so I don't really compare them to the facilities of
ENTRY statements or nested programs.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ENTRY statements PROS/CONS (was: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T23:01:09+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aadf1bd_2@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com> <3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net> <3AAD1FD4.FDD0B30D@home.com> <3AAD2CFA.8438820E@brazee.net> <3aad65e6_4@news.newsfeeds.com> <98ka40$c6r$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <98ka40$c6r$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>...
>>
>> Both entry points and Method coding are MUCH better solutions than
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Alternatively, you could use fully ANSI/ISO ('85 Standard) conforming
source
>code with nested programs and Local and Global data.
>
That's a good point you raise, Bill.

For some reason, it seems to me that nested programs just never "caught on".

I use them in PowerCOBOL because PowerCOBOL organises common code that way,
but I wouldn't write them specifically myself. Can't think why. As you point
out it is a valid solution. (The ANSI compliance is a sore point with me at
the moment and carries no weight whatsoever...<G>).

Is anybody else reading this an ardent fan of nested subprograms?

I am aware of the technique but have never used it from choice. What does
that say? Guess I feel more comfortable with what I know...(Christ! I sound
like Foodman...<G>! [Tony: 1, Dashwood: 0])

I'm going off to a darkened room to contemplate my sins of omission...<G>


>I have YET to see any advantage that ENTRY statements have over this
>technique.
>
Well, you would need to write some convoluted nested programs to achieve the
"Entrance overlap" which is achievable with Entry points, but that is a
whole other can of worms and we don't want to go there...

I agree (and have already stated) that we could easily live without ENTRY
statements in this day and age. (Doesn't mean I want them dropped from the
Language though...Ah! That's right, they were NEVER a part of it were they,
Bill? )

And to think all those programs I wrote just keep on running, in total
violation of an ANSI standard...Just as well they're not aware (as their
author wasn't when he coded them) of what that standard doesn't say about
them...<G>.

Hey, I just had a whimsical thought...suppose we sneak into the computer
room and tell the programs they can't use ENTRY points because it violates
an external (not even installation imposed) standard... Reckon they'd all
fall over?   Yeah, laughing....

>(Personally, I consider both OO and user-defined functions as doing
>"different" things, so I don't really compare them to the facilities of
>ENTRY statements or nested programs.)
>
Well actually, neither do I. It just so happens that in this instance the
"benefit" obtained from ENTRY statements can also be obtained from the use
of OO Methods, or, as you pointed out, nested subprograms.

Pete.
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d%rr6.425$Wg7.127154@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com> <3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net> <3AAD1FD4.FDD0B30D@home.com> <3AAD2CFA.8438820E@brazee.net> <3aad65e6_4@news.newsfeeds.com> <98ka40$c6r$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3aadf041_5@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote in message
news:3aadf041_5@news.newsfeeds.com...
>
>
> Is anybody else reading this an ardent fan of nested subprograms?
>

I am. My main reason is to facilitate maintenance. If you are developing a
piece of an application where you do some number crunching or something, I
like to work on that code "in isolation."

My second reason is not data sharing: rather it is data ISOLATION. For some
reason I just cannot name variables uniquely (perhaps it's because I am
repairing old, bloated code) and this helps a lot. Ideologically, I only
believe in "sharing" data across programs only when there is no pragmatic
option - it makes a lot more sense to me to "pass" and "return" values
rather than "modify" some GLOBAL or EXTERNAL value.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T07:15:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE2B9E.50F1776D@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com> <3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net> <3AAD1FD4.FDD0B30D@home.com> <3AAD2CFA.8438820E@brazee.net> <3aad65e6_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Thanks.

Many mainframe environments are not set up for programmers to start creating
methods.  But they could do multiple entry programs.   I have written many
programs which do different things depending on what's passed to them.   But
instead of being driven by entry points, they are data driven.   For instance, I
have one which is used to translate IDMS DB-KEYS into something usable and
back.  (IDMS stores an 11 character database key in PIC S9(8) COMP).   I pass
two fields, if one is populated such as 04292500-005, then I return the DB-KEY
in the
02  PASSED-DBCONV-X.
 03  FILLER                 PIC X(4).
 03  PASSED-DBCONV-DBKEY    PIC S9(08) COMP.

Otherwise, I do the reverse.   Of course CA should never have used a S9(8)
picture, but that wasn't my point.   I didn't think of making this program a
multiple entry program.  It would be a good candidate, but it is very simple
anyway, so why bother?  If it were big and complicated, I would have considered
two programs.  I suppose there is a compromise where a multiple entry program
would be the best, if I would just think of it.  And if other programmers would
understand what I did!

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Don't know if it helps Howard, but I'll try to answer your question. (In the
> good old days (before OO) I used multiple Entry points frequently...)
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
> -----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T00:24:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAD6AF6.84E5F36D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <986f6o$aie$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <986tjs$f01$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <98beq5$7vr$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3AA94A8F.4DAED420@home.com> <3AACD6EA.6D873162@brazee.net> <3AAD1FD4.FDD0B30D@home.com> <3AAD2CFA.8438820E@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

>
> But I am missing the connection with multi-entry programs - How are they easier than
> multiple programs?

Gawd - you're persistent <G> You entry-point folks correct me if I'm wrong, but they
represent the same concept as Classes with different methods. For each of your sub-programs,
Howard, you have Division statements as appropriate, plus of course Working-Storage and
sending/returned values.

Entry Programs/OO Classes have individual pieces to do a particular function/method - BUT -
they have the ability to share the other Division entries common to all entry-points/methods.
This surely has to be more concise, packaging like items together in one source, re-using the
same fields, saveing a plethora of source files to maintain.

Then take that a stage further, as I've previously illustrated, I can have 30 indexed files
using the ONE common source creating an instance for each against the file template.

Put it another way - I originally generated programs to create dialogs. New at the game
(GUIs), each one looked different and I finished up using inconsistent coding. Again reduce
that to a template for all dialogs, and my Business logic programs easily move into being
consistent to call the ONE source, again creating as many instances as I have dialogs.

My use of Date routines is limited, but where I use the intrinsics I've got those hived off
into a DateTime Class because invariably associated with a date there is also validity
checking which is called 'internally' within the class invoking SELF.

I can easily utilize the following (as part of a Class 'set') with no apparent overhead - but
I doubt you would bother to treat this as a called program, opting rather for in-line code
:-

*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "imp-to-met".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-inches                   pic 9(03)v9(03) comp-3.
01 lnk-mms                      pic 9(03)v9(02) comp-3.

Procedure Division using lnk-inches returning lnk-mms.

   if  lnk-inches <> zeroes
       compute lnk-mms rounded = lnk-inches * 25.4

   else move zeroes to lnk-mms
   End-if

End Method "imp-to-met".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------

This code is independent, but could be utilized by another method within the same class to
generate a pic zzz.99 Blank When Zero for display purposes. (I have no idea whether that is
possible with entry-points - seems like it should be).

Now the above is an easy one 'cos 25.4 is ingrained, but the other way around, if I had
written it as in-line code - is that 0.39378 or whatever, and psi to kpa, and what's F to C
or vice versa  - now I've got to look it up. Above gives me write once and re-use.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** foodman <foodman_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T13:06:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
In article <3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net>, Thane Hubbell says...
>
>
>COBOL needs your help

Hi:

Unless Bill Gates decides that COBOL is important, it will probably die.

How would COBOL die?  If the various COBOL manufacturers all closed shop
it wouldn't mean that COBOL would be dead, it would just be unsupported
by the manufacturer. If the manufacturers did go out of the COBOL business,
they should be required to place the compiler (and source) in the public
domain. Of course, if this happened the corporate ninnies would all
decide that they would have to reprogram everything in some other
language because COBOL was no longer supported. One would think that
the thought of the cost of re-programming everything would make big
users of COBOL be more vocal in their support, if they support it at
all other than by having someone on their staff involved somehow.
Why can't big COBOL users like Goldman Sachs and General Motors and 
those guys start shouting from a corporate level?

Would we really care that COBOL would no longer be supported? I don't.
I have been using the no-longer-supported Microsoft V5 compiler for years.
Since I am a minimalist, I have no need to upgrade to higher versions
which would only contain things I don't need or want. I don't need
support. So, I can continue happily into the future without worrying about
whether it is supported or the manufacturers give up. I make sure I have
numerous backups of the compiler on-site and off. 

Let's face it - if you need to be calling the manufacturer for support,
something is wrong somewhere, you shouldn't have to. If you run into
a problem with something which would require support, just do it some
other way.

Further, all the new things which get stuck in each new version of
COBOL are usually unnecessary so who needs them anyway. Things which
might be stuck in the compilers can be stuck in by companies like 
Flexus.

Companies like Flexus can continue to provide all the necessary features
which might be required for modern systems (GUI, Thin Client, etc.) and
can continue to do so indefinitely.  I would be more worried about Flexus
going out of business than Merant, although, since I have established
my minimalist SP2 conventions,  I never need to call Flexus for support 
either. 

So, with my obsolete compiler and Flexus, I can develop applications which
will run on all flavors of Windows until the end of my time (I'm 65).

Isn't minimalism wonderful?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T07:09:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com>`

```
foodman wrote:

> Unless Bill Gates decides that COBOL is important, it will probably die.

Your arguments all have to do with CoBOL on Microsoft platforms.   But most
CoBOL today exists on mainframe machines.  Do you have any reason to speculate
that mainframe customers will rewrite their enterprise business applications to
Windows and non-business oriented languages?   This very expensive task had
better have a tremendous upside.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** Foodman <Foodman_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T14:36:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net>`

```
In article <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net>, Howard Brazee says...
>
>foodman wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>CoBOL today exists on mainframe machines. 

Hi:

Why wouldn't my comments apply to mainframe compilers? 

>Do you have any reason to speculate that mainframe customers will rewrite their enterprise business applications to Windows and non-business oriented languages?   

No, that is why I suggested that the big users of COBOL get together
at a higher level to ensure that they don't have to. I just don't think
that the various COBOL committees have any power. COBOL needs increased
recognition and that can only come if the biggies get involved. Big Corporations
should sponsor COBOL courses in universities and schools, etc., so as to have
a supply of COBOL programmers for the future. This might do wonders for
COBOL's credibility.

If for some reason an enterprise application had to be re-written, wouldn't it
be re-written for Windows?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T07:47:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA79B9B.C8EA26BC@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:

> In article <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net>, Howard Brazee says...
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why wouldn't my comments apply to mainframe compilers?

Bill Gates doesn't effect the mainframe environment yet.


> >Do you have any reason to speculate that mainframe customers will rewrite their enterprise business applications to Windows and non-business oriented languages?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL's credibility.

They don't care about CoBOL's credibility.  They just want to do the job cheaply and correctly.  If they really want CoBOL programmers, they will train them in-house
instead of giving Universities enough money to train for their competitors.

> If for some reason an enterprise application had to be re-written, wouldn't it
> be re-written for Windows?

Not until mainframes can run Windows efficiently.    Also - if CoBOL dies, it won't be because another language takes its place.   It will be because we will switch
from thinking about programming to thinking about data management.   Users will have access to databases to use whichever tools they choose, while the mainframe
shops worry about data access, data integrity, etc..
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T08:06:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA88F08.85CDF3EC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA79B9B.C8EA26BC@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> >
> > Why wouldn't my comments apply to mainframe compilers?
> 
> Bill Gates doesn't effect the mainframe environment yet.

Nor never will.  IBM is ensuring that.  

MS requires increasing revenues to support itself, the desktop market is
no longer growing sufficiently and so MS has been trying to position
Windows as servers to replace Unix and small mainframes and as Data
Centres with clusters of Intel multi CPU machines.

This would take revenue from IBM and other mainframes.  IBM either had
to change to becoming just a reseller for MS and Intel products or come
up with a better strategy.

They chose to produce better products than MS by adopting Linux across
all platforms from PDAs to mainframes to give a single development
environment alongside their various proprietry ones.  This means that
mainframes do not have to be replaced just because someone wants to run
a desktop application and the data centre is running AS/400.

More importantly Bill Gates is no longer in control of IBMs potential
revenue or direction, nor of IBM's customers.  IBM finally learnt the
lesson of OS/2 on desktops and does not want to repeat this on
departmental servers and mainframe data centres.  Nor do IBM customers
want to repeat OS/2.  Those that bought OS/2 finally had to change to
Windows.  Now they need not have to change everything else to Windows,
they can continue with existing systems and add Linux for new
developments.

Now we need the Linux versions of IBM's Cobol (at Linux prices) as well
as versions from the others to ensure a competitive market place.

Of course, IBM's strategy _may_ fail and then we will all be paying a
MicroSoft tax for every service related to computers, communication, TV,
entertainment, etc into the next century   ;-)
```

```
Foodman wrote:
> >
> >> Unless Bill Gates decides that COBOL is important, it will probably die.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why wouldn't my comments apply to mainframe compilers?

Because Bill Gates has not managed to have Windows imposed on
mainframes.  Because mainframe Cobol manufacturers have a reasonable
revenue stream so that they can provide updates with new facilities.  

> If for some reason an enterprise application had to be re-written, wouldn't it
> be re-written for Windows?

That is like asking (in the 1960s) 'if an international trucking comapny
running Macks wanted to re-equip then wouldn't it buy VW Beetles?'.

Just because Windows is the most numerate system doesn't make it
suitable for every (or indeed any) task.  You've been reading MS
marketing propaganda for too long.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-08T15:11:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

> As time goes by the mainframe becomes less and less relevant. There always
> will be a need for them but many of them can be replaced by Wintel things.

As time goes by, the need for very large databases becomes more and more important.
This is increasing the demand for high end,  reliable machines.  They may or may not
use CoBOL, but they certainly they won't be running Windows.


> Therefore Bill Gates does have an affect on the mainframe market.
> The revenue stream will dry up, especially in the case of the smaller
> mainframe as/400 markets which will be replaced by Wintel.

If those markets continue to have small needs.  If their database needs grow, they
will be replaced by big machines.


> I can only guess, but isn't the majority of COBOL development currently
> done for/on the PC?

That's what you see, as that's what you do, with small customers.  I am really
unaware of that market, as I have done very little work away from large enterprise
companies, where there is significant demand for programmers.  I wonder where we can
find the answer to this question.


> >> If for some reason an enterprise application had to be re-written, wouldn't it
> >> be re-written for Windows?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> have to be compelling reasons to develop for any other.

Again, if the dominant platform is personal automobiles, should corporations design
their freight small enough to fit in trunks?   Or would they continue to use larger
vehicles which meet their needs?

I know, let them change their loading docks so that passenger cars can unload
directly into their trunks.

Which vehicles are dominant?  Passenger cars, trucks, trains, ships, airplanes?   It
all depends on what the needs are.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **Date:** 2001-03-09T12:54:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net>`

```
>Foodman wrote:
>
>> As time goes by the mainframe becomes less and less relevant. There always
>> will be a need for them but many of them can be replaced by Wintel things.


Hi:

I don't have any statistics to support my opinion, but I think there are
two mainframe worlds. Unless things have changed since the 360 was
introduced, there must be hundreds of thousands of businesses which have
the equivalent of a 360/20 or 360/30 which would be categorized as
mainframes, albeit little ones. Conversations in the forum which revolve
around mainframes, appear, to me, to be about giant installations for
giant companies. In reality, there must be billions of little mainframe
installations which are candidates for replacement by Wintel stuff. Thus
the market for little mainframes dies which must affect the big mainframe
market, no? (Include the AS/400 in the mainframe world).

I know that when I was active in the consulting world, the majority of
companies used 360/30 and 360/40 systems and it was a rare treat to
see a /50 or a /65 except in the DataCenters.

Any comments on this?

Thanks

TOny Dilworth

Minimalism IS wonderful!
```
