###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T13:41:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1D10EA1DA68513F0.8E191C8314F28511.14EE6AC8F44B1159@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com>`

```
On Fri, 09 Mar 2001 12:54:34 GMT, Foodman
<Foodman_member@newsranger.com> enlightened us:

>In article <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net>, Howard Brazee says...
>>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well here's what really happened and you've been out of the mainframe
world for far too long.

Those folks that had 360/20/30/50 370/115 etc.  more than likely grew
and as they grew so did their computing needs.  At the same time, IBM
was cranking out OS upgrades and Hardware like there was no tomorrow.
We entered the 4300 series of computers which were small and very fast
and required normal air cooling.  Memory got cheaper, and mainframes
have gotten smaller and faster.  PC's, for all their growth, still
cannot operate at the speed of a mainframe and there isn't a hard
drive, CD or anything made for the PC that can match a mainframe
disk's I/O access speed and capacity.

Those folks that didn't grow as much probably settled on System36
systems which have evolved into AS400 machines.  The AS400 is a very
good reliable, good speed machine that can do things a PC can only
dream about.

Everyone else whose needs don't warrant a mainframe have gone to
either the Server/Lan/Wan environments or LAN environments.

It is not just giant installations that are running mainframes.
You'll find them in a variety of different sized installations.  

It's like deciding which language to code a program in, you pick the
hardware that meets your needs.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I feel like I'm diagonally parked, in a parallel universe.


Help Save The Rainforest
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T15:22:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98bheh$444$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com> <1D10EA1DA68513F0.8E191C8314F28511.14EE6AC8F44B1159@lp.airnews.net>`

```
There is one "category" that Skippy's note ignored - and that was/is the
P/390 - which is basically a "heavy-duty" PC running OS/390 (or VM or VSE).
There has been a LOT of discussion about this user base of "small business"
recently - as the new z/OS operating systems won't work on it.  There is a
"replacement" strategy - but for the life of me I can't quite get a "handle"
on it yet.
```

```
If you have time, could you elaborate on the things the AS400
can do which a PC can only dream about.  Why could not a Wintel
network accomplish the same things as an AS400 for less cost?
If one had the opportunity to develop a new application and could
acquire whatever hardware/software one wanted, what would govern
the choice - AS/400 vs Wintel?

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T15:10:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C9C20E1E3B36EAC7.FFFE53E0643A3E29.70400843391FF6D4@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com> <1D10EA1DA68513F0.8E191C8314F28511.14EE6AC8F44B1159@lp.airnews.net> <98bheh$444$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <jppq6.1823$54.1923@www.newsranger.com>`

```
On Sat, 10 Mar 2001 12:49:19 GMT, Foodman
<Foodman_member@newsranger.com> enlightened us:

>>>
>>> Those folks that didn't grow as much probably settled on System36
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Someone who works on AS400 all the time will probably have to answer
you.  But generally, the AS400 is still much faster than most any PC
and has the advantage of the higher speed disk I/O enjoyed by its big
brother System 390 machines.  In addition, it has and can support a
CICS like environment giving users an on-line access.  There are
complete banking systems written for the AS400.  Community banks would
be the most likely users of such a system.

I may be prejudiced, but given your above question, if it only cost me
a few dollars more to use something other than a Wntel machine, I'd
spend the money.  Assuming of course it was the most complete answer
to whatever computing problem I was trying to solve.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAB4AD9.8E5137EE@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com> <1D10EA1DA68513F0.8E191C8314F28511.14EE6AC8F44B1159@lp.airnews.net> <98bheh$444$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <jppq6.1823$54.1923@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> If you have time, could you elaborate on the things the AS400
> can do which a PC can only dream about.  Why could not a Wintel
> network accomplish the same things as an AS400 for less cost?

It is like asking why a Container Trucking business can't use a fleet of
pickups.

Pickups may be the most popular form of transport (in Texas eg), and may
be really useful for many other things, they can go places that trucks
can't, and everyone can use them (no special training or licence).  They
are also very cheap compared to a truck.

In fact, for many purposes, a fleet of pickups _could_ be used, there
are many delivery jobs that could be done by unpacking the container and
loading the pickups (c/f having a cluster of Windows servers), so a case
could be made for this.  In fact MS marketing _are_ making this case,
some (such as you) are even believing it.
```

```
really are and why one would choose one over the other? Is there some
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T12:44:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAB7327.E913ADA6@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com> <1D10EA1DA68513F0.8E191C8314F28511.14EE6AC8F44B1159@lp.airnews.net> <98bheh$444$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <jppq6.1823$54.1923@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB4AD9.8E5137EE@Azonic.co.nz> <t_xq6.1941$54.2048@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> >It is like asking why a Container Trucking business can't use a fleet of
> >pickups.
> 
> Well, a typical container would crush a pickup.
 
Exactly.  And an AS/400 workload would crush a Windows box.  A dozen
pick-ups could move the contents of a container, a dozen Windows servers
might handle the workload of an AS/400.

> Making analogies like that doesn't answer the question. 

> Can someone please
> be specific about what the advantages of an AS/400 over a Wintel network
> really are and why one would choose one over the other? 

You probably need to be more specific about what you mean by 'Wintel
network'.  Do you mean NT Fileserver + local ethernet fat clients, or
TSE plus thin clients, or multi-tiered central clustered servers with
fail-over plus remote client-server nodes ?

While a 'NT fileserver plus fat clients', or even TSE or other thin
client may seem cheap and cheerful it doesn't scale well.  You can't
just add more CPU power and memory and hope to get more throughput (just
as getting the 7 litre V8 pickup doesn't help it shift containers). 
What works with a handfull, or even a couple of dozen users on Windows
can't be made to work with hundreds of users.  With Windows that means
you have to build clusters of multi-CPU servers and then add fail-over
and load-balancing.  This makes it _very_ expensive.

With multi-tier on multiple sites there are also severe support and
distribution problems.  The more units in the system the more likely
that some will fail.  With Windows the user is often tied to a machine
by his configuration and his private data.  Users can't just switch to a
different screen/keyboard and carry on working (without added software
to cope with this that then requires even more server capacity).

By having everything on a central box with reliable built-in fail-over
the system keeps running regardless of any failure.  It doesn't matter
which screen a user is at they can continue even if 'theirs' failed, the
business still runs.

It is not so much the compelling features of an AS/400 (or AIX or
UnixWare or HP/UX or Sun or ..) but what Windows lacks.  Most of what it
lacks can be added by clustering multi-CPU servers, having sufficient
redundancy, and adding additional software, but then the costs add up to
more than the AS/400, and certainly lots more in support costs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T09:39:07
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98i5dq$20oof$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com> <1D10EA1DA68513F0.8E191C8314F28511.14EE6AC8F44B1159@lp.airnews.net> <98bheh$444$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <jppq6.1823$54.1923@www.newsranger.com>`

```

Foodman <Foodman_member@newsranger.com> wrote in message
news:jppq6.1823$54.1923@www.newsranger.com...
> >>
> >> Those folks that didn't grow as much probably settled on System36
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

For somebody running a business, the AS400 has quite a lot of advantages
some of which are:
- Ease of use (don't need lots of techies around to keep it running).
- Security.
- Upward compatibility.  The same compiled programs will still run in the
same way on different versions of the operating system, different boxes and
different chips.   When AS400 moved from CISC to RISC chips the same
executables could be transferred to the new boxes, going through an AS400
builtin conversion program, and start running.
- Scalability.

The AS400 is not greatly interesting to techies who like to mess about.
You can do a lot of 'clever' things on Unix which you can't do on AS400.
However businesses don't necessarilly want their IT staff to do 'clever'
things.  They just want their computing systems to carry on running.

BTW I develop on and for Wintel, Unix ( various flavours) and AS400.  The
first two are more fun, but, IMHO, the AS400 is easier to keep running.

Rick
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T15:30:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE3C95.C17206EC@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com> <1D10EA1DA68513F0.8E191C8314F28511.14EE6AC8F44B1159@lp.airnews.net> <98bheh$444$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <jppq6.1823$54.1923@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
<snip>
> If you have time, could you elaborate on the things the AS400
> can do which a PC can only dream about.  Why could not a Wintel
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> support etc.?
<snip>

<soapbox>

Let me try to answer this from a mainframe point of view (an
AS/400 is *not* a mainframe from where I sit).

The AS/400 runs CISC - complex instructions.  1 CPU cycle on an
AS/400 will get more accomplished than about 6 cycles on a PC.  I
must assume that the AS/400 runs a cached-pipelined processor (as
I recall, the chip lines are basically the same between the
mainframe and the PC for the CPUs -- difference is the micro-code
that gets "burned" onto the chip?).  This means (assuming the G
series chips are similar to the Amdahl mainframe pipes) that
there are 5-7 stages in a pipe.  1 cycle = 1 movement down the
pipe.  Cache is another story.  A cache line can be 16Bytes to
64Bytes in size.  

Lastly, if the AS/400 is built like a S/390 architected system,
memory is central, not the bus (as it is in WIntel).  This means
that the CPU is not waiting to fetch from RAM while I/O is being
done.  What this does for through put is enormous!

Next let us look at security issues.  Is there any proof that one
can put a virus, trojan, worm, or other malicious code on either
an AS/400 or S/390 system, *W/O* the help of someone on the
"inside"?

What is the UPTIME stats on an AS/400?  6 months w/o having to do
a reboot?  I can't get my NT system to run a week w/o a reboot
and we aren't developing software that is O/S aware! [Now, were I
writing in x86 ASM...]

So, the minimum cost of an AS/400 compared to a WIntel based
system?  Well, let me see, what does a virus software
subscription cost?  How about the DNS/DHCP etc. software?  How
much of this is built into the AS/400 (or VTAM for S/390 - this
is not my area of expertise)?  How many people does it take to
maintain an AS/400 system?  How many AS/400 machines does it take
to run a Post office, DataBase, print manager, terminal/client
server?  How many NT systems (or LINUX or other UNIX) does it
take?  How many seat licenses?  What is the complexity of Primary
& Backup servers?  So, how many people does it take to run that
WIntel system?  How many to install and test new
software/upgrades?

If an AS/400 entry level system with all that stuff on it costs
about $60K and plugs into the wall (no special power), and, I buy
two of them (which I think would cost us $90K total if I remember
the quotes correctly) so that I have a primary and a backup
(which given the MTBF is a waste of money to my thinking), how
would that compare to the following WIntel Config:

2 Dual processors w/ 256M RAM, 128GB RAID-5 DASD, W2K Server,
Server Level Virus Software, 2 magazine DAT loaders (1 for each
server), 100BASE-T adapters, PROXY server, 16 seat license, etc.

Now, if two AS/400s can be controlled and managed by 1 person,
would it take more to manage a 16 client LAN with all the
printers and Internet connections?  What does down time cost? 
How much effort does it take to install software on the WIntel
system (each server has to be re-booted how many times to install
that software/upgrade?) v. the AS/400 (only if you put up a new
O/S release do you have to re-boot from what I'm told)?

Which system is more stable (as in fire it up and then forget
it?)?

</soapbox>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T15:47:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE4072.766C1B61@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com> <1D10EA1DA68513F0.8E191C8314F28511.14EE6AC8F44B1159@lp.airnews.net> <98bheh$444$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> There is one "category" that Skippy's note ignored - and that was/is the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>

The P/390 (original 500 model) was capable of replacing a 4381
single CPU system and depending on the I/O load, blow that sucker
away.  I know of a company that replaced two 4341 systems running
DOS/VSE with 1 P/390.  Total cost $80K!  They kept their old tape
drives and got a channel adapter and connected them and the
printer(s) they had.  The amount of internal DASD (RAID-5 based)
was MORE than their two strings of shared 3380s!

By getting rid of the two 4341 boxes, they got rid of TONs of
A/C, so, this lowered their environmental & electric costs which
paid for the P/390 and the migration to it w/in 6 months!

Their throughput was increased so that the 12 hours of processing
time they had was lowered to 8 hours (as I recall).

This was just with the early P/390.  The new PCI based boxes have
been tuned so that they run even faster than the original boxes
(micro-channel based).

The 64bit architecture is a waste of time to use on these boxes,
unless you were trying to do what one group was attempting -- Run
40 users against an IDMS data base.  You can't get enough memory
on the box to put that database into C-Store.  So, 64bit
architecture is not feasible.

I have no idea what IBM is going to do for solution developers in
this area.  Will they make a "crippled" Multiprise/3000 available
(crippled from the viewpoint that the processor is degraded to
say 8MIPS? Perhaps the IOP would also be degraded to only support
4 channels)?
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T09:15:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA9F097.9A009E62@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net> <NNMp6.1032$54.953@www.newsranger.com> <3AA88A5A.4C897A31@Azonic.co.nz> <_0Sp6.1180$54.1202@www.newsranger.com> <3AA803AE.50ED905@brazee.net> <eo4q6.1396$54.1463@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> I don't have any statistics to support my opinion, but I think there are
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> mainframes, albeit little ones. 

Today's 'equivalent of a 360/20' would piss all over a Pentium 4.

> Conversations in the forum which revolve
> around mainframes, appear, to me, to be about giant installations for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> market, no? (Include the AS/400 in the mainframe world).

Certainly it is MS's aim to expand their business into server and data
centre markets.  However, they, as yet, only have a marketing capability
and have yet to produce a product that could actually stand up to a Unix
system or a small mainframe.  'The best operating system in the world is
the _next_ one from MS, and always will be'.

Sun recognised the threat from MS early on.  The desktop market would
flatten out and, to support its required growth, MS would have to lever
its desktop monopoly to move into the small server market (to put Novel
out of business) and then into the Unix based departmental server and
finally into the data centre.  IBM has recently recognised this too. 
Either MS was going to put Sun, IBM, and others out of business, just as
it did to Novel, DRI, and many others using marketing and illegal tie-in
selling, or IBM was going to fight back.

Now they are fighting back (at last).  MS is actually poorly placed. 
While it has a large revenue stream for desktop PCs, this is not growing
(which is why they are releasing more frequently with SE, ME, XP to try
to pick up more upgrade revenue).  They are not recoving their
expenditure on 2000 server (only 1 million sold in a year) and will be
losing up to 2 billion on XBox before it becomes profitable in 3 years
or so (if ever).

Meantime they have pissed off their 'partners' such as IBM, Compaq and
HP by attacking the mainframe market, such as Sony by attacking the
games console market.  Currently these are supporting MS even as they
are attacked - MS is biting the hands that feed it.

These companies are now spending billions to produce better products
than Windows from games machines, PDAs, appliances, through desktops,
servers to mainframes - all based on Linux.

Even if Windows was up to the job (which it isn't) it won't get there.

> I know that when I was active in the consulting world, the majority of
> companies used 360/30 and 360/40 systems and it was a rare treat to
> see a /50 or a /65 except in the DataCenters.

That was, what, a quarter century ago ?

> Minimalism IS wonderful!

Perhaps you only had minimal clients that could only afford 360/20s.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <3AA7928B.D8C509C3@brazee.net>...
>foodman wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>CoBOL today exists on mainframe machines.  Do you have any reason to
speculate
>that mainframe customers will rewrite their enterprise business
applications to
>Windows and non-business oriented languages?   This very expensive task had
>better have a tremendous upside.
>
Yes, Howard, this is almost exactly what will happen.

And it won't be expensive. Emerging technology will facilitate it. And it
will have a tremendous upside: The Business will develop their own
applications by plugging components together with the help of a Visual
frontend. (I predicted this three years ago in this very NG; now it is
coming to pass...)

The Business rules embodied in existing Legacy code will be re-factored,
extracted, and wrapped as components stored in the Enterprise Component
Repository.

The Component Repository will contain a common OBJECT code (NOT source) and
will be a collection of components that will have been developed in many
different Languages, including COBOL.

Some of these components will have been developed in house (mainly, the
business oriented ones) others will have been bought in (the Network and Web
Oriented ones). It is becoming too expensive to maintain multi-lingual IT
departments and there is really no need to. The network needs different
tools from the Business, but they all must co-exist and work together.
Component technology achieves this.

All of them will run on any platform that is part of the Corporate network
(including the mainframe).

Watch for a forthcoming article on About Legacy Coding (ALC) which will be
published at the end of this month, and gives the background, reasoning, and
basis for these conclusions.

Pete.



-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** foodman <foodman_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-09T12:43:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In article <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz>, Richard Plinston says...
>But will you continue to add features into the SP/2 versions that Tony
>uses - MS Cobol 5 for DOS and Windows 3.1 ?
>

Hi:

Just a little clarification: The V5 compiler can produce EXE or DLL
for EITHER DOS or Windows. Thus, I can support my old DOS customers
and my new Windows customers with the same compiler (Obviously the
source code is different for each version). The compiler is only
16-bit but so what? 

Even if Flexus were to abandon the version of SP2 which I use, it
wouldn't bother me because I have already determined the minimal
subset of SP2's features which I require, and, with very rare exceptions,
I never need to call on FLexus for support.

Also the programs will run on 3.1, 95 98 NT ME and 2000, and, presumably,
every future version of Windows.

Minimalism IS wonderful!

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T09:15:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AA9F09D.D9A55305@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz> <ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com>`

```
foodman wrote:
> 
> Just a little clarification: The V5 compiler can produce EXE or DLL
> for EITHER DOS or Windows. 

Yes, I do have a copy which I used for a while.  It is a reissue of MF
version 3.0 with OS/2 2.0 support ripped out and the rather poor MS
tools replacing MF ones.  Not that I use the MF ones either.

> Thus, I can support my old DOS customers
> and my new Windows customers with the same compiler (Obviously the
> source code is different for each version). The compiler is only
> 16-bit but so what?

How much difference do you think is needed ?  I have systems that run as
DOS (under multiuser/multitasking OS), Windows, Unix or Linux with _no_
source differences.  Some are SP2 and others are ADIS based.

> Even if Flexus were to abandon the version of SP2 which I use, it
> wouldn't bother me because I have already determined the minimal
> subset of SP2's features which I require, and, with very rare exceptions,
> I never need to call on FLexus for support.

Well you don't have access to the Thin Client system which you claimed
as advantage for SP2.

 
> Also the programs will run on 3.1, 95 98 NT ME and 2000, and, presumably,
> every future version of Windows.

Think again.  Windows XP has no DOS box.  Windows 3.x could be dropped
at any time, and may be with XP, more likely with Whistler.  Windows 95
did not run Windows 2.x programs at all, there is no reason to think
support for 16 bit will continue.

In fact the Win98 beta distributions did remove all capability to run
DOS and Win3.x, but MS had to add it back in (this was the reason for a
6 month delay in final release) because the beta testers rejected this
'feature'.

The reason that MS want to rip it out is to force all those users of
Word 6 (and others) to finally give it up and pay money for a later
version.  MS needs revenue stream to support its operation and users
continuing to use old versions of software aren't contributing enough to
Bill's wealth.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Foodman <Foodman_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T13:05:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2Epq6.1825$54.2007@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz> <ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F09D.D9A55305@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
>Well you don't have access to the Thin Client system which you claimed
>as advantage for SP2.

Hi:

Thin Client is not supported by Flexus for my compiler. I played with
Thin Client unsuccessfully some time ago and had to convert my code
to the Fujitsu compiler (32-bit). This was a very easy job because
of my minimalism.

The thought that Microsoft might do something in the future which
would make my DLLs useless is often present. The reason I like
the fact that my stuff runs on all current flavors of Windows is
because my little customers try to save money my using whatever
old PCs they can get their hands on. Of course many of them are
buying new PCs which come with ME or whatever.

If the worst comes to the worst, I can always convert my code
to Fujitsu or something, but I would prefer that not happen.
After all, I am semi-retired and not in the mood to be forced
to embark on a conversion project caused by the detestable Microsoft.

Thanks

TONy Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T01:08:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98f865$cpq$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz> <ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F09D.D9A55305@Azonic.co.nz> <2Epq6.1825$54.2007@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB317C.FCA76A6E@Azonic.co.nz> <Pivq6.1890$54.1866@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Rather than "minimalism" - why not use the ANSI flagging mechanism that is
available with your compiler.  This should indicate whenever you are using
something that shouldn't be "portable" to any other ANSI compiler.

NOTE: I am still not in favor of the ANSI Standard - but as long as it DOES
exist and your GOAL is to have code that is "easily" ported,  why not use
the tools that are available?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T09:12:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAB417D.E76AF9A4@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz> <ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F09D.D9A55305@Azonic.co.nz> <2Epq6.1825$54.2007@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB317C.FCA76A6E@Azonic.co.nz> <Pivq6.1890$54.1866@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

> >> to the Fujitsu compiler (32-bit). This was a very easy job because
> >> of my minimalism.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >'minimalism'.

> 
> Perhaps not, but the minimalism helps, 

What what I saw of what you call 'minimalism', it was simply that you
saved key-strokes by having two or three character names that meant you
could type it in faster but then only you would understand it, others
would need a training course so they could memorise that 'P3' meant
'throw a new page' or whatever.

This would have no effect on converting to another Cobol - it would
still be meaningless encryption to anyone else.

It also seemed that you used it as a synonym for 'spagetti' where you
thought the code was simpler by just GOing to the next bit to execute. 
Again this makes no difference to the compiler - it would still be
untouchable by anyone without your 'program map' burnt in to the brain.

> and, every bit helps. You would
> surely admit that going from one COBOL to another can be a pain if
> stuff peculiar to the source was used.  

Your MS Cobol has less specific MF functions in it than later ones and
Fujitsu does try to implement MF extensions, so they wouldn't be many
issues anyway.

> As I recall, the only thing
> I had to convert in my program was the COPY statement. There might
> have been one other, but I don't recall.

Yes, Fujitsu is a pain with its COPY, but a simple process of all the
source files with a Perl script fixed mine.  This meant that I could
reconvert as required with no effort, just put it in the makefile.

As I had almost no problems at all I don't see that your claim for
'minimalism' being superior is at all accurate, or even relevant.

What _is_ relevant is that you seem to have derived new source for every
system and each client (according to what I read) so that each is
'minimalist' to their needs and derived from skeleton source.

This means that to convert you would need to process each and every
program for each system (as required).  

Most of my systems are based on a core of several quite complex
programs. One for file maintenence that maintains most master files for
most systems using dictionaries and screen layout definitions.  One for
report writer which will produce reports and enquiries for most files
(plus accessing secondary file data as well) from report scripts.

When I converted the core programns (which in fact was trivial anyway) I
had already done most systems main programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Foodman <Foodman_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T22:22:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cOxq6.1937$54.2108@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz> <ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F09D.D9A55305@Azonic.co.nz> <2Epq6.1825$54.2007@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB317C.FCA76A6E@Azonic.co.nz> <Pivq6.1890$54.1866@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB417D.E76AF9A4@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
>
>As I had almost no problems at all I don't see that your claim for
>'minimalism' being superior is at all accurate, or even relevant.

Hi:

In the context of this thread, I don't think I said it was 'superior'
I merely mentioned minimalism.
>
>What _is_ relevant is that you seem to have derived new source for every
>system and each client (according to what I read) so that each is
>'minimalist' to their needs and derived from skeleton source.
>
Yes, that is basically true but the user's needs are never minimalized.

But, there is more to minimalism than my coding techniques which I
assume you read about in 'The Big Picture'. 

Minimalism is also about confining your development tools to
the bare minimum without resorting to developing systems using 
umpteen languages, 'tools' and things (see 'Skill Set' and 'Hodge-Podge').
I only use two things, COBOL and SP2. That is minimal to me. 

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T12:11:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAB6B63.D7964CD0@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz> <ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F09D.D9A55305@Azonic.co.nz> <2Epq6.1825$54.2007@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB317C.FCA76A6E@Azonic.co.nz> <Pivq6.1890$54.1866@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB417D.E76AF9A4@Azonic.co.nz> <cOxq6.1937$54.2108@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> In the context of this thread, I don't think I said it was 'superior'
> I merely mentioned minimalism.

You claimed it as an easy job _because_ of the minimalism.  This implied
that others would find it less easy if they didn't use your minimalism,
or even any minimalism.

> >What _is_ relevant is that you seem to have derived new source for every
> >system and each client (according to what I read) so that each is
> >'minimalist' to their needs and derived from skeleton source.
> >
> Yes, that is basically true but the user's needs are never minimalized.

Perhaps you have never had a client who's needs are more than your
toolset can achieve.  I have clients that require mechanisms that
couldn't be done with just MS Cobol 5.  Some involving EMail processing,
EDI and ftp, some accessing web sites pretending to be a browser to
capture data automatically.

> Minimalism is also about confining your development tools to
> the bare minimum without resorting to developing systems using
> umpteen languages, 'tools' and things (see 'Skill Set' and 'Hodge-Podge').
> I only use two things, COBOL and SP2. That is minimal to me.

That is fine if you are prepared for minimal results or heavy investment
in developing things that these tools don't do well.  Interpreting an
HTML page is a easy in python in just a few lines of code.  Using Cobol
for that is painful (try it before you comment).

I don't do carpentary with just an axe and a hammer, though this would
certainly be possible, for limited results.

Of course, Cobol + SP2 is more like a Multi-Mate (is that the right
name) and a power drill with all attachments, but I wouldn't build a
boat with just those either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Foodman <Foodman_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T13:59:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YwLq6.2107$54.2084@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz> <ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F09D.D9A55305@Azonic.co.nz> <2Epq6.1825$54.2007@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB317C.FCA76A6E@Azonic.co.nz> <Pivq6.1890$54.1866@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB417D.E76AF9A4@Azonic.co.nz> <cOxq6.1937$54.2108@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB6B63.D7964CD0@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In article <3AAB6B63.D7964CD0@Azonic.co.nz>, Richard Plinston says...
>
>Foodman wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>or even any minimalism.

Hi:

Well, wouldn't that be the case if they used a Hodge-Podge of stuff
to develop the application?

>
>> >What _is_ relevant is that you seem to have derived new source for every
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>capture data automatically.


The 'toolset' covers the essential functions of any system. The
application-specific portions of it can boggle the imagination,
as you know. No toolset is going to write application-specific
code automatically and it is the development of the application-
specific code which takes the time and effort.

Regarding 'mechanisms' that cannot be done with COBOL: Of course,
there may be peculiar requirements such as ftp, etc., which
are realistically solved by using something other than COBOL. 
My customers frequently want to take data from their POS system
to their head office. I just tell them to email it or something,
for sure, I am not going to write a COBOL program to do that.


>
>> Minimalism is also about confining your development tools to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>for that is painful (try it before you comment).


I wouldn't even think of writing a COBOL program to interpret
HTML and I doubt many would. Interpretation of HTML is beyond
the intent of COBOL.  My primary interest in COBOL is in the
development of boring stuff like payroll and accounting, or, to
be more specific, the methodology for developing such
applications.

>
>I don't do carpentary with just an axe and a hammer, though this would
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>boat with just those either.

But we aren't talking about boats or carpentry. Those sorts of analogies
don't help your argument, at least, I don't think they do. Boats are
made out of wood, metal, plastic, so, you need wood tools and metal tools
and plastic tools, etc. Programs are made out of bytes (only).

Try to come up with a real-world case where you can justify the
use of thing 'x' in addition to COBOL and SP2 and let's chat about that.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T18:26:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AABC562.17C3CB63@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz> <ce4q6.1389$54.1470@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F09D.D9A55305@Azonic.co.nz> <2Epq6.1825$54.2007@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB317C.FCA76A6E@Azonic.co.nz> <Pivq6.1890$54.1866@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB417D.E76AF9A4@Azonic.co.nz> <cOxq6.1937$54.2108@www.newsranger.com> <3AAB6B63.D7964CD0@Azonic.co.nz> <YwLq6.2107$54.2084@www.newsranger.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:

>
> The 'toolset' covers the essential functions of any system. The
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That is not strictly true Tony. And this is where us fuddy-duddies have to catch
up (I'm five years older than you <G>). There is a movement or realization,
particularly in other languages, (and of course I'm referring to OO), to use what
I would coin as 'Universal Language Components" or if you like "Universal
Language Utilities". Using the BEST language to do the job you build a
'component' which is transportable between ALL languages. (Hearken back to the
fact that Richard said he uses PYTHON to produce something easily not available
in COBOL - I'm fairly certain Richard isn't using OO COBOL - but if he was - then
that PYTHON feature could well be codeable in OO COBOL - just don't know without
specifics).

As an illustration - your POS systems - you no doubt have a standard feature as
to whether or not your Vendor A/c Numbers are alpha/aplha-numeric/numeric; one
could even throw in whether parts of the Vendor Key are Uppercase/Lowercase - and
most likely you have a standard length for the Key.
.
In this respect COBOL is SUPERB because of our text/numeric handling.. This
'function' in Structured, or 'method' in OO can easily be accommodated in COBOL.
There is no reason why such a generalized COBOL 'function' couldn't be passed to
other languages or vice versa. Less so with Net Express, (I'm not saying it can't
be done), but Fujitsu POWERCOBOL lends itself easily to this Component concept,
i.e., the way their 'Form Designer' lets you couple design with generated code .

'Componetizing'  in Windows terms, allows you to impose restrictions on what the
user can key in, (certainly not dissimilar to your use of SP2). What you and I
currently see as COBOL carrying out 'validity checks' can be part of a Universal
Component - using a combination of Level 88 checks or even the new VALIDATE verb
- and the finished product can be passed to other languages.

Agreed - you will never completely get away from the Business Logic which must
verify data, but 'components' created from toolsets most certainly will shortcut
the process - and are callable or invokeable as required.

>
> Regarding 'mechanisms' that cannot be done with COBOL: Of course,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now I'm not as yet into the Internet jazz - but again using OO COBOL there is the
possibility of doing some of the above. Certainly Net Express provides a Forms
feature for building Internet access - but I haven't as yet studied it.  Note
that Richard states that he follows the ordering up with a confirmation e-mail,
quite easily produced from his application..

> I wouldn't even think of writing a COBOL program to interpret
> HTML and I doubt many would. Interpretation of HTML is beyond
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

COBOL is a powerful tool - but if linked to other languages, as I suggest above,
then the assumed 'dinosaur' can hold its own against the newcomers. (And this
particular thread was 'originally' about the Standards - that assumes if we ever
get the new one !).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <3AABC562.17C3CB63@home.com>...

>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
A couple of points Jimmy. When we had this discussion in private mail a few
days ago I didn't comment on your suggestion of 'Universal Language
Components/Utilities' as a new name for the technology I described to you.

The reason for this is because it is redundant.

Those of us involved in developing components and using component based
development Methodology KNOW that these components are "Universal" and that
they are "Language independent" (developed in whatever Language is BEST
suited to the purpose, including COBOL) and able to run on any platform
where their RTS is installed. (With the advent of .NET, this will no longer
be a requirement.)

No Java programmer (for instance) would want to see his beloved "beans"
re-labelled as "Universal Language Utilities". As a COBOL Developer of this
same technology I don't want to see it either...<G>

Let's just stick with "Component"; it may be confusing to the uninitiated,
but it is very precise once you understand what they are about.

In every other respect your comments on Tony's mail were right on the
button.

All too often we see comments like "yada yada can't be done..." or "yada
yada will never get away from...". They simply mean that the person saying
it doesn't know how to do it, or hasn't the imagination to conceive of it
ever being done.

Pete.

>particularly in other languages, (and of course I'm referring to OO), to
use what
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T13:48:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aacd005.529243@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3aa80424.30848457@news.epix.net> <3AA8D4EE.BAE0F8BF@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard:

Good questions......

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Bob Wolfe wrote:
>> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>uses - MS Cobol 5 for DOS and Windows 3.1 ?

We still provide support for an MS-DOS version and 16 bit Windows
version of the sp2 runtime.  The development systems have been frozen
because very few are actually doing development on DOS or 16 bit
Windows platforms.

In order to continue to support advanced 32 bit features such as
Active X controls and treeview controls, it is necessary to enhance
the 32 bit dll with its own set of unique features.

We have some customers who still deliver both 16 bit and 32 bit
versions of their applications.  Some features such as JPG compressed
bitmap display can be and are provided for in both dynamic link
libraries.  For those customers who want to deliver both 16 bit and 32
bit versions of their applications, they must avoid using special "32
bit only" controls.  

So the short answer to your question is no.  We can't really add
certain advanced features in both 16 bit and 32 bit dll's unless the
feature is generally supported in both 16 and 32 bit versions of the
Windows environment.

>And just how is the 'Thin Client' that works with this compiler ?

It doesn't.  I can't think of anyone who would seriously consider
using the 16 bit Windows environment as a server platform and very few
would like to use the 16 bit Windows environment as a client platform.

>(actually I would like this to happen too, but never mind).

In some cases, a 16 bit client does make sense for the customer
(reluctance to buy a new PC, etc.).  But cheap PC's and better
winsocket software in the 32 bit version of Windows make it less
desireable as a client side platform.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
Not going to happen. Do you really believe GM, Citibank, Ford, IBM,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** Foodman <Foodman_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-10T13:50:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciqq6.1834$54.1999@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F962.24B38F41@optonline.net>`

```
>
>Not going to happen. Do you really believe GM, Citibank, Ford, IBM,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>LiamD

Hi:

Of course not, they couldn't even if they wanted to because there aren't
enough programmers in the world to even think about such a monstrous
effort. (Worse than that, who the heck knows what those legacy systems
do anymore due to decades of patching, fixing, hacking-about, etc. and poor or
no documentation?)

Because of the extreme dependency which the business world
has on COBOL, you would think that they would do something about ensuring
that COBOL programmers are around for the foreseeable future. It would seem
to me that some major effort is required to ensure that COBOL continues to
be taught and that newcomers to the field are assured that they will have
a future if they learn COBOL. All the squabbling about standards and stuff
doesn't help ensure COBOL's future. If the big corporations don't do
something, who will? Based on stuff I have read in the CLC, COBOL 
courses are being dropped all over - how long before they stop teaching
it at all? 

Do any of the major corporations you mention DO anything about teaching
COBOL to their employees and newcomers?

Occasionally, my customers ask what language my applications are
written in. These are not IT people, but I always feel as though I 
need to explain that COBOL is the best choice for such applications.
Nevertheless, sometimes, you can sense that they don't like the
idea that COBOL is used, even though they don't know anything but
they have heard of Java or C++ and assume (in ignorance) that if
the latest language is not used, then the software must be old
and inefficient, like COBOL. COBOL just has a bad image as being ancient
and clunky, even among non-technical people. Image is everything, it 
seems. (I had one non-customer who insisted that she wouldn't
buy anything unless it was '32-bit' although she didn't know what
that meant.)

COBOL is legacy, it is boring, it is sneered at by the C, Java, VB, etc.
community and something needs to be done about COBOL's IMAGE to make it
more attractive to the newcomer and the population at large. Arguing
about standards does not help. COBOL needs a good PR guy.

(How many people in the forum use no-longer-supported compilers?)

Thanks 

TOny Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T18:10:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aac5c6e_3@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F962.24B38F41@optonline.net> <ciqq6.1834$54.1999@www.newsranger.com>`

```

Foodman wrote in message ...
>
>Of course not, they couldn't even if they wanted to because there aren't
>enough programmers in the world to even think about such a monstrous
>effort. (Worse than that, who the heck knows what those legacy systems
>do anymore due to decades of patching, fixing, hacking-about, etc. and poor
or
>no documentation?)
>

Your head is so far in the sand, Tony, that you are unaware of new emerging
technologies. Yes, there is a lot of "unmaintainable"  code in existence but
there are also a lot of computers that are capable of taking it apart. New
software technologies (Factoring, f'rinstance) are theoretically capable of
dealing with this task (whether they are practically capable of it, only
time will tell... My money's on the technology because, if something is too
expensive to do manually it's only a matter of time before someone invents a
machine to do it. Famous example is the Nottingham Lace museum. They have
machines invented in the 19th century which can make the most intricate lace
you can imagine. Everyone said it couldn't be done; the artisans were very
smug that no machine could ever do this job. It took 10 years (and some lost
fortunes and lives...) Be very careful before you say "it can't be done"
Just because YOU can't do it doesn't mean NO-ONE can...!) (In fact, in your
case it probably means "anybody else can"...oops, sorry, cheap shot...I take
it back.)

>Because of the extreme dependency which the business world
>has on COBOL, you would think that they would do something about ensuring
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What "extreme dependency"? Obviously, if there were such a dependency,
something WOULD get done about it. Although COBOL is certainly the best
language for business application development, the world is changing and
there are other alternatives which CAN be used.


>Do any of the major corporations you mention DO anything about teaching
>COBOL to their employees and newcomers?
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>that meant.)

I found it extremely revealing to see what you think about your customer
base. You offer a 16 bit product  and anyone who is foolish enough to
question this is a "non-customer"... I think she had a lucky escape...  It
doesn't matter whether she understood the advice she was given; it was good
advice. In this instance it probably saved her a lot of heartache.

You are on record in this forum as admitting that "everything you want to do
you can do in COBOL". As others have pointed out, this just shows limited
horizons. Unfortunately, these limitations extend to customer service and it
is only a matter of time before an alternative is presented to your
disgruntled customers. Watch the base slowly erode... If a vendor told me to
snail mail my POS returns or even e-mail them, when I know full well there
is a disk and a modem to hand in each device, I would certainly be looking
for another vendor.

Maybe you've just been doing it for so long you no longer question it? It's
obvious that you don't care.

>
>COBOL is legacy, it is boring, it is sneered at by the C, Java, VB, etc.
>community and something needs to be done about COBOL's IMAGE to make it
>more attractive to the newcomer and the population at large. Arguing
>about standards does not help. COBOL needs a good PR guy.

I deal regularly with C, Java, VB, and Delphi programmers and have never
been sneered at by any of them, although I make no secret that my favoured
language is COBOL. Maybe it has to do with people rather than programming
languages? They are usually extremely interested to see that I am doing (or
attempting to do) very similar things in COBOL to what they are doing,and
achieving results.

The sound which is most musical to my ears is "I didn't know COBOL could do
THAT!" when they see component technology, developed in COBOL, working
alongside components developed in VB, or C++, they see complex multimedia
interfaces running videos and animated corporate logos, all in COBOL, and
sometimes with much less effort than would be required in another language.

I firmly believe in using the BEST language for the job, but I sometimes
stretch it, just out of mischief...<G>

On the other hand, I have heard COBOL programmers putting down Languages
they know nothing about, not even a conceptual understanding of. (They know
there is something "different" but don't understand the talk about
polymorphism and inheritance and encapsulation and objects, so they "dig in"
and resist the newcomers.)

>
>(How many people in the forum use no-longer-supported compilers?)
>
How many people in the forum wear neck-to-knee bathing suits, cuff links,
collar studs and spats? (OK, not all at once, at any time, separately...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T11:54:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ABF9E82.323DC8CF@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F962.24B38F41@optonline.net> <ciqq6.1834$54.1999@www.newsranger.com> <3aac5c6e_3@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

>
> What "extreme dependency"? Obviously, if there were such a dependency,
> something WOULD get done about it. Although COBOL is certainly the best
> language for business application development, the world is changing and
> there are other alternatives which CAN be used.

The use of the word best can certainly be challenged.   Although I am not
anti-COBOL,  I do wonder what criteria we use to justify the superlative
form in evaluating it relative to other languages.

COBOL, the tired old COBOL 68 that I first learned, evolved in COBOL 74,
something of an improvement even though some felt it was a step backward.
COBOL 74 evolved into a really good 1985 standard that made structured
programming
actually possible.  Again, some felt it was unnecessary.   The evolution to
object technology is yet another important step.    One of the things that makes

COBOL strong is its very ability to evolve as new technologies and new ways
of thinking about the software development process emerge.   If we want COBOL
to survive, we must encourage COBOL to continue to evolve, train old and new
programmers
to use the new versions of it, and persuade managers that it does represent the
modern view of
software development some other, less mature, languages are only just
discovering.

Richard Riehle
```

```
Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T09:04:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC0C820.4B552D60@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F962.24B38F41@optonline.net> <ciqq6.1834$54.1999@www.newsranger.com> <3aac5c6e_3@news.newsfeeds.com> <3ABF9E82.323DC8CF@ix.netcom.com> <3ac01934_2@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Lao Xiao Hai wrote in message <3ABF9E82.323DC8CF@ix.netcom.com>...
> >"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Answers on a postage stamp...<G>)

This is a little different from your original claim.  The word "better" is not
the same as the word "best."   That being said,  either word requires that we
define the criteria by which we reach our conclusion.    These criteria could
be layed out quite simply, maybe even on a postage stamp.  For example,

       1.  Data Representation

            Business applications, particularly accounting applications, require

            a decimal data type that behaves according to the strict rules of
            standard accounting practice.   What data type is supported by the
            following languages that conforms to that requirement?

            Language                                   Data
Type                     Conformant

                  C
None                                No

                  C++
None                                No

                  COBOL                                  Picture
Clause                 Yes

                  Java
None                                No

                  Ada                                         Decimal
Type                  Yes

                  Perl
?                                        ?

                  Python
?                                        ?

                  Eiffel
None                                 No

                  NOTE:

                         For class-based languages, one can design a decimal
class but that
                         is not the same as built-in language capability.


         2.  Decision Tables

              Complex business problems require complex conditional
relationships between
              entities.  Ideally, these can be mapped directly between user
requirements and the
              implementing language, often as decision table relationships.
Which languages
              support this?

              Language
Construct                              Conformant

                   C
switch..case                            No

                   C++
switch..case                            No

                   COBOL
EVALUATE                           Yes

                   etc., etc., etc.

Many of you could easily add more criteria and corresponding examples.   When
one examines
the criteria closely, it becomes apparent that C++, the peanut brittle of
programming languages,
is one of the worst choices for replacing COBOL, but it is one of the most
widely selected by
uncritical management.

> See, you pushed what I said into "evaluating it relative to other
> languages". That ISN'T what I said and ISN'T what I was doing. There are
> other languages which are MUCH better for doing certain things; that's why I
> carefully qualified what I said.

I agreee with that statement.  Perhaps I interpreted your original comment
incorrectly.
The solution to almost any problem _can_ be expressed in almost any language.
However,
"can" is not the same as _should_ .   I distinguish between expressivness and
expressibility.
Some languages are more expressive of a certain class of solutions than others,
even though
nearly all solutions are expressible in nearly every language.   Ideally, we
select the language
most expressive of the problem space in which we are toiling.    Sometimes that
language
might be COBOL

> Mind you, in this forum I'm quite used to having " the words you've spoken,
> twisted by knaves to make a trap for fools" so don't worry about it...<G>

I am not setting a trap.  I apologize if I seem to have "twisted" your words.

> Now, put up or shut up...<G>

Perhaps that abruptness is called for given your sense that I was challenging
you
inappropriately.   I thought I was simply observing the need for criteria when
using
the word "best."   Obviously, my response was perceived as hostile.    That was
never its intent.  I much prefer this kind of dialogue be thoughtful and
courteous.

Thanks for your comments.

Lao Xiao Hai
```

```
Foodman wrote:

> COBOL is legacy, it is boring, it is sneered at by the C, Java, VB, etc.
> community and something needs to be done about COBOL's IMAGE to make it
> more attractive to the newcomer and the population at large. Arguing
> about standards does not help. COBOL needs a good PR guy.

I don't care that they sneer.  I don't see that an image makeover will make them
stop sneering.  Unless they persuade CIOs and CEOs that they need to be stylish
instead of profitable, CoBOL will remain.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T18:28:25+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3AAD176F.B37261EE@home.com>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> Foodman wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> instead of profitable, CoBOL will remain.

I know that Pete Dashwood will be gung-ho with you in support of communicating with
CIOs and CEOs that COBOL is the right tool for big business. But who is going to do
the communicating ? It most certainly wont be J4, WG4, ANSI, NCITS or ISO. They
operate like the Holy Trinity, there are no apostles or disciples to go spread the
Good News.

Let's be honest - those of us 'longer in the tooth', given back our youth and
starting all over again - would we seriously consider COBOL as a career path,
confronted by all the options in other languages ?

And perhaps the biggest problem is kids aren't learning COBOL - so where are
Howard's replacements when he goes off into the sunset. Yes, it is still being
taught in the States, but I've confirmed, about an hour ago,  that DeVry (big COBOL
school in Chicago because of the association there with mainframes), will not be
taught by DeVry in Calgary in two years time - they see the emphasis on
client/server and teaching Net tools. Canada's highest tech city - and DeVry is
currently the last place teaching COBOL.

Question - The kids that DeVry, Calgary teaches, where do they go to make COBOL
careers - Answer - the States, particularly Texas. (I can see it already  - 'These
##$%@!!! are getting green cards !").

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T11:58:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAD1C3C.1FFDFC25@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F962.24B38F41@optonline.net> <ciqq6.1834$54.1999@www.newsranger.com> <3AACDAE9.F8D8575C@brazee.net> <3AAD176F.B37261EE@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> And perhaps the biggest problem is kids aren't learning COBOL - so where are
> Howard's replacements when he goes off into the sunset.

There's a local (Denver) shop which needs assembler programmers.  So it hires in
house.  The interesting thing is that when they need to hire contractors, they hire
CoBOL programmers who charge much more - they can work elsewhere.

So there will be some choices:
Hire in-house and train - if you are the only shop using that skill, you will keep your
workers.
Supply will rise as demand (and pay) rises.  Night time grad schools are very aware
about what students demand.
Demand will lower as they find alternate solutions.

Some combination of the above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T07:14:34-07:00

```
With the Report Writer required, is there a significant number of shops starting
to use it?



If I were to change my company's standard for reports, I would stick the data out
where the users can pull it into a PC based database and/or Crystal reports.
These days, I would rather have meta-data standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T13:03:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aad65e9_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F962.24B38F41@optonline.net> <3AACD9C9.3314E9F1@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <3AACD9C9.3314E9F1@brazee.net>...
>With the Report Writer required, is there a significant number of shops
starting
>to use it?
>
>
>
>If I were to change my company's standard for reports, I would stick the
data out
>where the users can pull it into a PC based database and/or Crystal
reports.
>These days, I would rather have meta-data standard.
>
 Way to go, Howard!

This is exactly what I've been trying to get across to the COBOL Community
for some time now. Systems anchored in the closed COBOL file system are NOT
doing COBOL any favours...

The data resource needs to be open and accessible.

Why? Because that's what the market wants. If COBOL can't do it, they'll use
other tools that CAN.

I would encourage every COBOL programmer who frequents this NG to have a go
at  using Relational Database with COBOL. Learn SQL. (There are many good
free forums and tutorials available on the NET. Besides, it isn't difficult.
Cut through the jargon and get to the nitty gritty and you have maybe six or
seven constructs that can do EVERYTHING!)

It is not important, it is ESSENTIAL!

If you have MS ACCESS on your system, start with that. It is a remarkable
full implementation of the Relational Model for a few hundred bucks and is
included on many systems.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T07:24:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE2DB7.96760B86@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net> <stLp6.990$54.1027@www.newsranger.com> <3AA9F962.24B38F41@optonline.net> <3AACD9C9.3314E9F1@brazee.net> <3aad65e9_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
> If you have MS ACCESS on your system, start with that. It is a remarkable
> full implementation of the Relational Model for a few hundred bucks and is
> included on many systems.
>
> Pete.

Three of years ago, when I was a contractor here,  I got a request for a
report.  It was a relatively complicated report with line level and page level
summaries which required an internal sort.   After I got hired on, I talked with
the customer and found out what she did with that report.  She typed it into a
spreadsheet by hand!

A decade ago, I wrote similar programs to create comma delimited fields which
were imported into spreadsheets.  Today I wouldn't want to do that, instead we
should provide the users with the tools to access the data directly.   This
wouldn't necessarily be from the database of record, but we can efficiently
create databases which they can use easily without interfering with anything
(and which they have security access to).

Whenever we move towards this direction, the users are ecstatic!

Of course, if we give them the tools, our jobs will go away - as they did when
CoBOL was invented!
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T17:04:57+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aac4bbc_1@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Having now sorted the problems with my ISP I "popped in" to CLC and read the
whole of this thread (hey, it's Monday...and I'm on holiday...<G>)

Passionate and well reasoned arguments from both Bill and Thane.

(And the thread then deterioriates off topic as we expect from CLC...<G> I
loved Tony's defense of 'Minimalism'.... and Richard's incisive refutation
of it. Is it  "Minimal" in terms of what you can do with it? I don't believe
that anything said here (or anywhere for that matter) is likely to change
Foodman's mind about anything...Fortunately it really doesn't matter, and,
for once, I found myself in agreement with some of what Tony had to say...)

Back to the Standards.

I offer the following:

1. COBOL does not need anybody's help. It is alive and kicking and probably
in better shape now than it has been for its entire life. The Language is
fine; the public perception of it is wrong. This perception has arisen
historically because COBOLlers have allowed it to. It needs to be changed
and I believe it will be. (I am certainly doing everything I can at every
level possible to get it changed...<working my little fingers to the bone
writing stuff that will hopefully stimulate some thought>)

2. The ANSI standard made very little contribution to this. Bill has made
the point eloquently in his article on COBOLReport that years have gone by
with nothing to show for it except more meetings, more documents, and very
little achieved.

3. In another private forum I made these same points a couple of months ago;
you can put in all the effort you like, until it MOVES no WORK is done.
Personally, I believe the members of the committee are dedicated people.
They are just not being managed properly. Maybe they are constrained by laid
down procedures. If so, these procedures should be changed.  If they CAN'T
be changed, then Bill is 100% right to question the value of the whole
process.

4. The best point Thane made was that Open Source won't happen without an
approved standard. It certainly won't be helped by not having a standard.
Thane says they would be sued by vendors but I'm not so sure. If the Open
Source movement were the most responsive to User needs, we might see them
implementing stuff that is needed and wanted before the established vendors.
A competitive marketplace would certainly keep everyone on their toes...

5. I think we need to consider what has been achieved by the current
situation. COBOL has not died because there was no new standard. There is
still (and will be for some years yet) an established marketplace. History
has shown that the Companies who respond to this marketplace do well.
(This is not aimed at Merant ,and I realise they are a different company
than Micro Focus, but I'll never forget how extremely angry I felt when they
shafted the User base on VISOC. I actually enjoyed and supported the Micro
Focus products and then they just pulled the rug...What happened? A free
offer of a COBOL compiler from Fujitsu. An alternative. Frankly, I would've
been glad of any alternative. ANYTHING to get out of Micro Focus...! Nearly
four years later we see Fujitsu establishing themselves as a Market leader.
I have never regretted switching. I have bought and will continue to buy
their products, as long as they continue doing business the way they are.
The point? LISTEN to the Marketplace if you want to do business there....)

6. How many of the end users you develop applications for, care (or even
know) whether you are using standard COBOL?

Would you stop developing applications if they did? No. Because as long as
there is a Market for what you can produce, you will produce it...

7. I see a future for COBOL. But it will be different to the past. Very
different. Some forthcoming articles on COBOLReport and About Legacy Coding
will postulate where I see it going. I can tell you certain things right
away...

7.1 The days when everything revolved around Source code and the Language we
used was paramount, are almost over. Modern Networks require different
Languages to support them and the solution is to use the BEST Language for
the job, NOT any particular ONE language. COBOL is still the BEST language
for programming Business solutions. But these solutions will be developed in
an altogether different way in the near future.

7.2 The second nail in the coffin of Source code being King, is the rapidly
increasing availablility of Components. These are Objects with a standard
interface that can run on any platform and are consistent. They do what they
are specified to do and can be "plugged" into various "containers" (Windows
forms, existing source code (OO), and  Web Pages.) Corporations will buy
them off the shelf for less than the cost of a COBOL programmer/Day. It's a
bit like "outsourcing" the corporate IT development in the same way the
motor pool is outsourced, you buy or lease the vehicles but you don't
maintain them... As these Objects are supplied (appropriately enough) as
OBJECT code, there is no longer any need to maintain a skilled Source
Language Department. If the component doesn't do its job you bin it and get
another one...exactly analogous to the assembling of modular stereos or
other electronic equipment. (You wouldn't manufacture your own PCBs, neither
do you care what factory produced the bricks you built your house from, just
as long as they perform to the specification required of them.)

Against the background described above, it really seems a bit pointless for
those of us on the shop floor to worry about the Standard.  The Vendors will
respond to the needs of the Market place (if they don't, they won't be
vendors for very long...) and the essential functionality that is required
for component development and use will be part of COBOL as it  is part of
every other Modern Language (Java, C++, even VB...) namely, Object
Orientation.

The Standards committees are currently arguing about OO COBOL but that
hasn't stopped both Merant and Fujitsu producing useful implementations of
it.

I am sorry to see Bill standing as a "Voice in the Wilderness" (I know how
that feels...<G>) and I am sorry to see Thane feeling that the efforts of
the Committee are being undervalued and undermined.

The truth is that, in the long run, it really doesn't matter.

Pete.






Thane Hubbell wrote in message <3aa5b838.87188106@news1.attglobal.net>...
>
>COBOL needs your help.     A US public review is underway on the
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3AACDD9D.89353019@brazee.net>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> 1. COBOL does not need anybody's help. It is alive and kicking and probably
> in better shape now than it has been for its entire life. The Language is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Everybody says its perception needs to be changed.  Not many people say why.  (I
have some ideas).

Why does it need to be changed?
At what cost are we willing to change it?  (cost in changing CoBOL from what it
```
