> news:uuzq6.9371$p66.3321671@news3.rdc1.on.home.com...
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> may have come across the 'BY REFERENCE' and 'BY VALUE' clauses of the
'CALL'
> verb) which means that the *address* of the linkage item is passed to the
> sub-program. This allows two-way communication:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> To answer your other question, yes, you can also use multiple parameters,
if
> you wish, or just use a single grouped item (each of its elements would be
a
> particular parameter value).
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: subprograms help me with parameter?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T01:23:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98f96u$8ln$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<uuzq6.9371$p66.3321671@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <McBq6.12278$v5.52935@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <%_Eq6.11326$p66.4008778@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
No, you do NOT need to put the parameter of the main program in Linkage
Section.  Normally it is either in the Working-Storage or File Section.

Remember that "linkage section" has no STORAGE by itself.  Therefore, if
your main program calls a subprogram and passes one or more parameters, then
they should be in WS (or file section).

If, on the other hand, your main program passes a parameter to a subprogram
and that subprogram passes the parameter to ANOTHER subprogram, then the
field would be in WS of the main program - but Linkage section of the two
subprograms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: subprograms help me with parameter?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T16:30:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010311113058.24849.00000313@nso-bg.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<%_Eq6.11326$p66.4008778@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
In article <%_Eq6.11326$p66.4008778@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>, "MadG"
<patrick4133@hotmail.com> writes:

>So I assume that :
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I may be all wet here, but it was my understanding and practice
that the called program is the only one that 'needs' the Linkage Section.
The called program also needs a USING statement on the PROCEDURE
DIVISION line.  The calling program can have the fields defined
just about anywhere that is defined and allocated at the time of the
call to the sub-program.

A perfect example, if you are using FujiCOBOL, is the CALLDLL.ZIP 
file available at the adtools.com download area.
```

#### ↳ Re: subprograms help me with parameter?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T06:29:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3XEq6.4151$R_6.426945@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<uuzq6.9371$p66.3321671@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
You can try this syntax:

05  RAW-VALUE   PIC ...
05  NEW-VALUE   PIC ...

CALL 'MYPROG' USING RAW-VALUE   NEW-VALUE


In the subprogram (you must have an LINKAGE SECTION 01 level (in the
subprogram) for each data name mentioned in the subprogram's  USING
statement(s)):

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  RAW-VALUE   PIC ...
01  NEW-VALUE  PIC ...

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING    RAW-VALUE    NEW-VALUE

(calculation stuff yielding NEW-VALUE)

GOBACK
```

Just use ENVIRONMENT DIVISION alone.

:>  PERFORM 110-CALCULATE-REG-PAY

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: subprograms confused with 3 errors

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T13:37:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8FF4411620E684A7.EDE766AD025FD25B.7ECD10EE108F6A69@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<uuzq6.9371$p66.3321671@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3XEq6.4151$R_6.426945@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <OYLq6.13039$p66.4572524@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
On Sun, 11 Mar 2001 14:29:02 GMT, "MadG" <patrick4133@hotmail.com>
enlightened us:


Close but no cigar.  Scroll down and I'll show you some of the errors
of your ways!


>sorry, I still can't get this to work...
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I assume you are using subroutine PAYROLL-SALARY to calculate an
employees monthly pay from his annual salary.  Also, I'm assuming that
PF-ANNUAL-SALARY contains the employees annual salary and that you are
expecting the subroutine to return the monthly salary in SV-REG-PAY.

If that is true, then your CALL statement should be:

CALL "PAYROLL-SALARY" USING PF-ANNUAL-SALARY, SV-REG-PAY.

>
> IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

DELETE INPUT-OUT SECTION, FILE-CONTROL...in this case you don't need
them.

> WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Change PROCEDURE DIVISION to:

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LS-ANNUAL-SALARY, LS-REG-PAY.

> 000-PRODUCE-REG-PAY-SALARY.
>  PERFORM 100-INITIALIZE-JOB-VARIABLES
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>**********   USING PHRASE OF PROCEDURE DIVISION HEADER        **********

This error will go away when you change your PROCEDURE DIVISION.  

>  MOVE 0 TO WS-REG-PAY.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>**********

This error will go away when you change your PROCEDURE DIVISION.  

> EXIT PROGRAM.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You're getting there!!

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I feel like I'm diagonally parked, in a parallel universe.


Help Save The Rainforest
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

#### ↳ Re: subprograms help me with parameter?

- **From:** "csbn" <chiasb@mbox5.singnet.com.sg>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T21:35:59+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98ijpn$59d$1@mango.singnet.com.sg>`
- **References:** `<uuzq6.9371$p66.3321671@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Hello Partick,
Well, you just have to move the working storage back to the linkage storage.
Just make sure that both the calling and the called program contains the
same linkage storage declaration.
Yes, it's the same regarrdless of single/multiple parameters.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: subprograms help me with parameter?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T08:19:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98ilui$qcf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<uuzq6.9371$p66.3321671@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <98ijpn$59d$1@mango.singnet.com.sg>`

```
See other answers in this thread.  It is *not* usual to

  "make sure that both the calling and the called program contains the same
linkage storage declaration"

Especially if the CALL is from a main program to a subprogram, the "usual"
way is to have the same definition in the Working-Storage of the CALLing
program as in the Linkage Section of the CALLed subprogram.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: subprograms help me with parameter?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T19:48:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7K9r6.1619$Ke3.190891@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<uuzq6.9371$p66.3321671@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <98ijpn$59d$1@mango.singnet.com.sg> <98ilui$qcf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:98ilui$qcf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...
> See other answers in this thread.  It is *not* usual to
>
>   "make sure that both the calling and the called program contains the
same
> linkage storage declaration"
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


PROGRAM-ID. CALLER.
...
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
..
01  SUBPROGRAM-PARMS.
      COPY PARMS001.
....

PROGRAM-ID. CALLEE.
...
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 SUBPROGRAM-PARMS.
     COPY PARMS001.


Adjust for multiple '01' if using. I usually pass a group item with lots of
elementary items in lieu of multiple 01/77 items precisely so I CAN use a
copylib without the need for all kinds of gyrations.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T07:47:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AACE17A.D7C7BC86@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<1103_984279703@micron-450>`

```
One big advantage of the container ship is that labor costs are low when you
can go from truck to train to ship without unloading and loading everything a
bunch of times.  But the container isn't really necessary to do the job - it
is just efficient.

Let's say I want to download a big file to my PC.  I could use a 300 BPS
modem, I could even write an algorithm so that I could use a bunch of PCs with
300 BPS modems.  But things would be easier if I had broadband access.  If I
had to do this daily, I would look into buying a T1 line.

Most mainframes don't NEED to be mainframes.  Sure there are some "cargos"
which cannot be moved any other way - but most mainframes are doing things
which NT farms could do.  But the businesses which are running them are very
interested in cutting costs, so they make studies and go for the cheapest
solution with the required security, throughput, maintenance costs, etc..   If
NT farms were cheaper for their needs, there wouldn't be mainframes.

It is true that Wintel machines are getting more powerful.  But it is also
true that our data needs are growing even faster.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-03-17T21:20:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1Xbt6.2792$A2.820078@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<1103_984279703@micron-450> <3AACE17A.D7C7BC86@brazee.net>`

```
Howard, have to disagree, most Mainframes are doing work that would require
RFEW  (Redundant Farms of Expensive Wintels) could hope to do.
The advantage to Client/Server on Wintel/Unix/et al, is that its cheap to
get up
and running, the maintenance costs are high.

While the Mainframe has a large 'startup' cost, it is very stabil and has
amazing
bandwidth:

How many Wintels would it take to allow 300,000 terminals to accept and
process
Credit Card / check transactions all day?  We have 36 IBM RISC machines
handling
the 'front-end' sending the Data to a CICS transaction in one region that is
also
handling another half a million terminals doing similar work, while the
mainframe
process Web access requests, passes CICS-stored data back to VB/Web apps,
and while the RISCs are working hard, the mainframe is 'cruisin'.


Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3AACE17A.D7C7BC86@brazee.net...
> One big advantage of the container ship is that labor costs are low when
you
> can go from truck to train to ship without unloading and loading
everything a
> bunch of times.  But the container isn't really necessary to do the job -
it
> is just efficient.
>
> Let's say I want to download a big file to my PC.  I could use a 300 BPS
> modem, I could even write an algorithm so that I could use a bunch of PCs
with
> 300 BPS modems.  But things would be easier if I had broadband access.  If
I
> had to do this daily, I would look into buying a T1 line.
>
> Most mainframes don't NEED to be mainframes.  Sure there are some "cargos"
> which cannot be moved any other way - but most mainframes are doing things
> which NT farms could do.  But the businesses which are running them are
very
> interested in cutting costs, so they make studies and go for the cheapest
> solution with the required security, throughput, maintenance costs, etc..
If
> NT farms were cheaper for their needs, there wouldn't be mainframes.
>
> It is true that Wintel machines are getting more powerful.  But it is also
> true that our data needs are growing even faster.
>
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM mainframes (was: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T22:03:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98jh3e$rgh$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<1103_984279703@micron-450>`

```
We have a small AS400 on our network at our Community College and 
it allows Students to sign on from home over the network via telnet, 
write source code from home, and be able to receive their output 
without even coming to school.  It is also able to be used as a 
web server and has networking support built in for TCP/IP or AIX.  
When it is backed up properly it usually only goes down when 
the School's network makes it unreachable.  I have seen this computer
run for several months with no down time.  We have more problems with
the printers than the AS400.  Since each user can have their own outque,
even when the printers are down they can still keep working.  It is a 
pretty dependable piece of hardware.  The only drawback is it takes 
some specialized training to set up and get running properly; Of Course 
so does a network.



In article <1103_984279703@micron-450>, Warren G. Simmons 
<warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> writes:

>www.ibm.com source for this kind of detail.  Or call a salesman! vbg
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

you do not need a lot of memory, a lot of power, or a lot of speed to log
onto an AS/400 an older P100 computer or a 368 is just fine to act as an
emulator. You still need some kind of network support.  We used Win 3.1 and
TCP/IP through routers for networking all the stations along with Internet
access from home or other locations when traveling.  You do not have to
reboot an AS/400 to get network services started again when the network
fails.  It just keeps going!

This article has been posted to announce the availability of a Cobol/Tcl 
Interface. We have succesfully used in our Cobol application since two 
years. In a few weeks, the interface will be available at sourceforge.net 
in opensource. Actually, we have succesfully tested MicroFocus and AcuCobol.

well as all the tcl extensions (ldap servers, mail, www, etc.).
We actually run on Windows 9x, nt, 2k and LINUX.

Does anybody find this new of interest ? Bye!

## Thread: data reference error 104 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### data reference error 104

- **From:** "MadG" <patrick4133@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T18:21:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<omPq6.14150$p66.5009066@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I don't understand the descriptions that I have found of this error.
It appears in the following code when I divide in 110-CALCULATE-REG-PAY

Do I need to post the calling code as well?

Pat.


 01 JOB-VARIABLES.
   05 BI-MONTH  PIC 99 VALUE 26.

 LINKAGE SECTION.
        01 LS-ANNUAL-SALARY PIC 9(6)V99.
 01 LS-REG-PAY  PIC 9(5)V99.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LS-ANNUAL-SALARY LS-REG-PAY.

 000-PRODUCE-REG-PAY-SALARY.
  PERFORM 110-CALCULATE-REG-PAY
  PERFORM 120-WRAP-UP.

 110-CALCULATE-REG-PAY.
  DIVIDE LS-ANNUAL-SALARY BY
  BI-MONTH GIVING LS-REG-PAY.
```

#### ↳ Re: data reference error 104

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T08:32:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAC89AD.52F1508D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<omPq6.14150$p66.5009066@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
MadG wrote:
> 
> I don't understand the descriptions that I have found of this error.
> It appears in the following code when I divide in 110-CALCULATE-REG-PAY

>  PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LS-ANNUAL-SALARY LS-REG-PAY.
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>   BI-MONTH GIVING LS-REG-PAY.

You haven't indicated what 120-Wrap-Up does.  Assuming that it does not
much then the code starts executing at PROCEDURE DIVISION, does the
perform 110-.. (and the code therein) does the perform 120- (and the
code therein) and then the next staement executed is ?

You would probably like to say 'it returns because that it what I want
it to do'.  But you haven't told it to do that so it executes the next
available statement (DIVIDE) and then whatever follows until end of
source code.

The 104 error is most likely a completely spurious one caused by
overwriting data.  You probably don't have matching sizes for the passed
parameters of the calling program may have these in LINKAGE (which is
probably wrong).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: data reference error 104

- **From:** "MadG" <patrick4133@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T19:34:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lrQq6.14349$p66.5153958@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<omPq6.14150$p66.5009066@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3AAC89AD.52F1508D@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
YEP, IT WAS THE SIZE.

I caught it a minute before I checked back here.

Thanks Richard!
```

<a id="t-6f1e6b6713"></a>
## Thread: Einstein's Riddle — 28 msgs

### Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** "Steve Graham" <js.graham@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T18:45:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
Who would be interested in using his/her brain (and his computer) to solve
the following?


Steve Graham

===

Einstein's Riddle

Albert Einstein wrote this riddle this century [ed. 20th century].  He said
98% of the world could not solve it.

There are 5 houses in 5 different colors.  In each house lives a person with
a different nationality.  The 5
owners drink a certain type of beverage, smoke a certain brand of cigar, and
keep a certain pet.  No owners
have the same pet, smoke the same brand of cigar or drink the same beverage.

The question is: "Who owns the fish?"

Hints:

The Brit lives in the red house.

The Sweed keeps dogs as pets.

The Dane drinks tea.

The green house is on the left of the white house.

The green house's owner drinks coffee.

The person who smokes Pall Mall rears birds.

The owner of the yellow house smokes Dunhill.

The man living in the center house drinks milk.

The Norwegian lives in the first house.

The man who smokes Blends lives next to the one who keeps cats.

The man who keeps the horse lives next to the man who smokes Dunhill.

The owner who smokes Bluemasters drinks beer.

The German smokes Prince.

The Norwegian lives next to the blue house.

The man who smokes Blends has a neighbor who drinks water.
```

#### ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** David Ness <DNess@Home.Com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T19:55:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AABD82E.C4D4E5CB@Home.Com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
Steve Graham wrote:
> 
> Who would be interested in using his/her brain (and his computer) to solve
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
...

I can't speak for others, but this isn't much of a puzzle, and belongs to a 
category of puzzles that appear in low quality monthly puzzle magazines. The
highest quality puzzles of this type used to appear regularly in Games Magazine, 
but I gave up reading that when Will Shortz departed for the Times Crosswords
about a decade ago.

I have nothing against puzzles, but it does strike me as being _very_ important
to draw the line at only those of particular interest or of the highest quality. 
Mediochre puzzles are of _no_ interest to me, and do not accomplish what the last 
round associated with the `New Scientist' did, namely expose lots of different and 
contrasted technique.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** "Steve Graham" <js.graham@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T19:58:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<yNQq6.396747$ge4.138625399@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AABD82E.C4D4E5CB@Home.Com>`

```
David,

I'm surprised at the remark.  This puzzle strikes me as much more complex,
and it will utilize some of the same techniques as the other one:
Backtracking, constraints.

Steve

===

news:3AABD82E.C4D4E5CB@Home.Com...

solve

said

> >

a
> category of puzzles that appear in low quality monthly puzzle magazines.
The
> highest quality puzzles of this type used to appear regularly in Games
Magazine,
> but I gave up reading that when Will Shortz departed for the Times
Crosswords
> about a decade ago.
>
> I have nothing against puzzles, but it does strike me as being _very_
important
> to draw the line at only those of particular interest or of the highest
quality.
> Mediochre puzzles are of _no_ interest to me, and do not accomplish what
the last
> round associated with the `New Scientist' did, namely expose lots of

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** David Ness <DNess@Home.Com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T20:23:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AABDED3.951B59E0@Home.Com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AABD82E.C4D4E5CB@Home.Com> <yNQq6.396747$ge4.138625399@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
Steve Graham wrote:
> 
> David,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

We all differ. I find this category of puzzle _very_ simple. I have done hundreds 
(perhaps thousands) of them over the years and in general the  significant 
problems are always involved in the `interpretation' of the English  used in 
specifying the conditions. In general once that is exposed and modeled there isn't  
anything very challenging left in the puzzle. A clue that this is the  case is 
found in the fact that the item mentioned in the final question is never mentioned 
elsewhere---thus indicating a kind of `word-cleverness' is at the core.

Added to this, the search space of the puzzles is often 5^5 or 6^6, and therefore
wouldn't represent much of of a challenge, or much room for particular algorithms
to distinguish themselves were one to treat the problem computationally.

Of course, YMMV. It isn't my job to discourage or encourage others. All I can
say is: 
   (a) I don't find the category interesting; and
   (b) will be surprised if anything interesting results should others tackle it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** "Jim Lucas" <jel@danbbs.dk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T01:58:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<4rVq6.559$ou4.12675@news.get2net.dk>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AABD82E.C4D4E5CB@Home.Com>`

```
"David Ness" <DNess@Home.Com> wrote ...
> Steve Graham wrote:
> >
> > Who would be interested in using his/her brain
> > (and his computer) to solve the following?

> ...this isn't much of a puzzle,...
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> technique.

You appear to be making an interesting claim, i.e., that the range of
techniques for solving this puzzle computationally is limited (and also too
simple to be interesting?), regardless of the language.  Certainly, simple
enumeration of all the combinations followed by selection of the (presumably)
only one that satisfies all the constraints seems easy enough to program,
though I'd still be curious to see how the constraints are specified
programmatically in different languages.

But I would hope that other, more clever solutions are also possible, and
perhaps there might be variation among languages as to which are the simplest
or most efficient.  Personally, I thought the New Scientist puzzle was
ridiculously simple, at least in APL and after one realized that it was nothing
more than simple pattern matching.  As I see it, the constraint matching in
this "Einstein" puzzle is much more complex than the New Scientist problem,
*especially* if one wants to aim for efficiency instead of brute force.

By the way, for you the problem may seem simple because it's one you're
familiar with and because you have honed your skills in solving that particular
type of puzzle, but 1) *most* people *do* have difficulty with that kind of
problem (although the quoted 98% figure may be a bit high) and 2) problems that
are "simple" for the human mind to solve are not always simple to program.
Consider, e.g., parsing English sentences, or identifying the "brown" regions
in a painting.

I, for one, would like to see people submit solutions to this one, and see
how -- or if -- they vary.  And if it's really as simple as you imply, perhaps
you could prove it by whipping off a program and being the first to submit.  Or
if you're claiming -- as I think you are -- that this particular puzzle is an
unnecessarily simple example of it's genre, pose us a more difficult version,
then give us a program that will solve both... or preferably *any* problem of
the same type.

            /Jim Lucas
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** David Ness <DNess@Home.Com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T02:15:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAC3139.5703EFD1@Home.Com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AABD82E.C4D4E5CB@Home.Com> <4rVq6.559$ou4.12675@news.get2net.dk>`

```
Jim Lucas wrote:
> 
> You appear to be making an interesting claim, i.e., that the range of
> techniques for solving this puzzle computationally is limited (and also too
> simple to be interesting?), regardless of the language.  Certainly, simple
...

No. I wasn't making any claim, other than to say that I've done enough of the
kind of puzzle suggested to lead me to believe that answers were likely to
be boring, and would mostly involve discussion about modelling the (human)
language that was used to present the problem. So far almost all of the 
responses that have appeared have suggested that this particular point is true.
There is argument about `owning fish'. We have the possible answer `none'.There 
can be argument about whether `first house' means left-most or right-most. Stuff 
like that.

Since I believe my own point, I'm not interested in playing with the problem,
so I demur from worrying about a solution.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jim Lucas" <jel@danbbs.dk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T10:47:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<711r6.40$fo5.7330@news.get2net.dk>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AABD82E.C4D4E5CB@Home.Com> <4rVq6.559$ou4.12675@news.get2net.dk> <3AAC3139.5703EFD1@Home.Com>`

```

"David Ness" <DNess@Home.Com> wrote in message
news:3AAC3139.5703EFD1@Home.Com...
> Jim Lucas wrote:
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> No. I wasn't making any claim, other than...

I disagree.  By praising the New Scientist puzzle for "exposing lots of
different and
contrasted technique" and characterizing the "Einstein" puzzle as "mediochre"
[sic] and unable to "accomplish" the same variety of technique, you have indeed
made a claim, even if it was by implication.


> ...to say that I've done enough of the kind
> of puzzle suggested to lead me to believe
> that answers were likely to be boring,...

Answers to the puzzle as stated, or answers in the sense of programming
solutions?  I'm not expert at such logic problems, but I found it easy enough
with pencil and paper to determine that the German owns the fish (assuming that
the problem is intended to be one of logic, and not one of linguistic
quibbles).  I'm not convinced that it will be as easy to write a computer
program to do it for me, though I would hope that such a program would also be
able to handle more "difficult" versions of the same type of problem.

But in all your experience with solving this type of problem, you haven't said
whether you've ever written a program to do it.  It's the programs that I'm
curious about.

 > ...and would mostly involve discussion about
> modelling the (human) language that was used
>  to present the problem.

Yes, that *is* the interesting part:  how to specify and process such
conditions as "next to"; how to apply the different sorts of conditions; how to
select which conditions to apply first in order to maximize efficiency.  Maybe
it's simple, but maybe not to someone who hasn't tried it before.  And is there
really only one way to do it?  At least in APL, there are certainly multiple
ways to eliminate duplicate rows from a matrix, which I think is logically a
much simpler "puzzle".

> So far almost all of the responses that have
> appeared have suggested that this particular
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> like that.

So far, most of the responses have *not* been about *modelling* the language,
but about quibbling with the semantic interpretation, where the *intended*
semantics are quite clear.  They have been attempts to avoid the real, intended
puzzle.  I can only wonder if that's because it's *not* so easy to write a
program to solve that sort of puzzle.  I find it interesting that John R. Clark
indicates that *his* solution "would be too long to post here."

> Since I believe my own point, I'm not
> interested in playing with the problem,
> so I demur from worrying about a solution.

Fair enough, but *I* hope that others *will* submit solutions.  I even hope
that I'll find some time to try it, since I haven't previously tried
programming this kind of problem.

                /Jim
```

#### ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** Kees Nuyt <k.nuyt@zonnet.nl>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T21:34:46+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<eunnats80ui1ureplbqk0kmnmqjtcnl0k0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
On Sun, 11 Mar 2001 18:45:09 GMT, "Steve Graham"
<js.graham@home.com> wrote in comp.lang.awk:

>Who would be interested in using his/her brain (and his computer) to solve
>the following?

[...]

>Einstein's Riddle

[...]

That problem probably can be solved using Prolog, or any other
declarative language, certainly not that easy using awk. 
In other words: 'somewhat' off-topic in comp.lang.awk
```

#### ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** Doc O'Leary <droleary@subsume.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-11T14:40:27-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<110320011440274719%droleary@subsume.com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
In article <VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>, Steve
Graham <js.graham@home.com> wrote:

> Albert Einstein wrote this riddle this century [ed. 20th century].  He said
> 98% of the world could not solve it.

Given that the heart of this is a simple logic problem, I have doubts
that 90% of the world could not figure it out.  Einstein, if he really
was the one posing the riddle, clearly had something else in mind than
the solution to a simple logic problem.

Question: Who owns the fish?

Answer: Houses are property, and thus owned by the men, but no man has
the right of ownership over another animal.

Note that the language of the puzzle never used ownership in referring
to pets, only in the final question.

Of course, the history of Germany has been one of attempted conquest,
so what's the big deal of owning a fish or two when you think the world
belongs to you?  :-) <-- flamer's please note
```

Sadly, you don't need to be Einstein to see that Einstein had nothing to do
with this.

They claim he wrote it in the early 19th century (he wasn't even born in the
early nineteenth century), he certainly was not familiar with Pall Mall
cigarettes, although Dunhill are a very old British firm.

Besides, he didn't like fish. (He never wore socks either (maybe because
they smelled like fish after a day on his feet?)) so it is unlikely that the
"German" in the riddle would be the fish eater....

Poor old Albert. Everyone wants a piece of his action...

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** "m_l_g3@my-deja.com" <m_l_g3@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T12:48:02+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AADECD2.D78C9CDA@my-deja.com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <110320011440274719%droleary@subsume.com>`

```
Doc O'Leary wrote:
> 
> Question: Who owns the fish?
> 
> Answer: Houses are property, and thus owned by the men, but no man has
> the right of ownership over another animal.

Among them (the guys from the riddle), only one has the right
to take the fish with him to the city, or make money using it,
or sell it, of eat it fried. If other guys do that without a
legal reason, this would be a crime. So that guy owns the fist.

And you are playing words.

BTW, in Russian the question would sound as "u kogo ryba?" 
(lit. "with whom fish?") where the verb is implied.
A translation underlining legal issues would be inadequate
in the context of a puzzle.

> Note that the language of the puzzle never used ownership in referring
> to pets, only in the final question.
```

- **From:** "John Passaniti" <nntp@JapanIsShinto.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T01:15:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.basic,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth

- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <110320011440274719%droleary@subsume.com> <YWbt6.2791$A2.819860@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```

mangogrower <mangogrower@telocity.co> wrote in message
news:YWbt6.2791$A2.819860@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net...
> Who Cares, or there is not an answer that can be
> determined from the facts.

I'm amused by this thread.  On the one hand we have the people who were
forgiving of a bit of imprecision in language and who worked out the
puzzle.  Then we have the people who spend more time debating language and
semantics who in their quest for precision apparently couldn't see that
this was a pretty simple puzzle, demanding only a few minutes and some
logic.

At first I thought that the claim that only 2% of the people in the world
could solve the puzzle was stupid, but now I see that it probably is true.
That is, probably only 2% of the world would find it enjoyable to do a
simple logic puzzle, and the other 98% would rather waste their time locked
in seemingly endless debate over if animals can be owned and if a cigarette
qualifies as a cigar.
```

It took me under 20 minutes, notepad (that's the extent of computer usage!)
and a pen & paper to solve this. It was actually kinda fun =)

)=(             )=(             )=(             )=(                )=(
Norwgn.    Dane          Brit           German         Sweed
Yellow       Blue           Red           Green           White
Water        Tea            Milk           Coffee          Beer
Dunhill       Blends        Pall Mall     Prince          Blue Masters
Cats           Horse        Birds            FISH           Dogs

The german keeps the Fish


---
I don't chack newsgroups too often, please reply to my email as well
Virulent@bigwig.net

Steve Graham wrote:
> 
> , and keep a certain pet.  No owners have the same pet, 
> The question is: "Who owns the fish?"

While the clues mention 4 pets: dog, cat, bird, horse, there is nothing
to indicate that the 5th 'pet' is actually fish.  Thus the answer may be
'noone', the 5th may be a goat.

Other than that the problem is either unsolvable (if in fact there is
insufficient information) or mechanistic (if there is). I suspect that
98% can't be bothered with it.  I also don't believe that Einstein wrote
it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** "Jim Lucas" <jel@danbbs.dk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T19:56:35+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org>`

```
"Jerry Avins" <jya@ieee.org> wrote ...
> Richard Plinston wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> There is insufficient accurate information to solve this.

Absolutely true, but also completely false.  In one sense, there's not enough
information in *any* text -- including yours and mine -- to make any sense of
it, whatsoever.  Communication and understanding are based on assumptions of
shared meaning, perception, and even reasoning processes.  E.g., I assume that
your message was written in English, and that our individual understandings of
the words and phrases (and the abbreviation "e.g.") are sufficiently similar
that I have understood your content (though perhaps not your intent), and you
will understand mine.  I'm inclined to think that your English is more precise
than that used in stating the puzzle (could the cigar-cigaret confusion be due
to inexpert translation from the German?), and using that assumption I am
reasonably confident that I have understood the intended logic of the puzzle
sufficiently to agree with the others who have actually solved it as to the
intended meaning of all the constraints, as well as the final, unique solution.

> While each individual smokes a different brand of cigar,
> Pall Mall (at least) is a cigarette.

So is Prince, but that doesn't require that they're not also cigar brands.  I
believe that Dunhill makes both cigarettes and cut tobacco, but as a
non-smoker, I haven't bothered to become an expert.

> Engineering is the art of making what you want from things you can get.

You must be an engineer.  You got a logic puzzle, and from it you made a
semantic-philosophical nit pick.

I'm tempted to ask you if you can write a program to do such nit picking for
you, but I'm sure that would be a *much* more difficult problem than the one
attributed to Einstein (Andrew Einstein, perhaps?).

                :-)            /Jim Lucas
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

```
Jim Lucas wrote:
> 
  ...
> > There is insufficient accurate information to solve this.
> 
> Absolutely true, but also completely false.  In one sense, there's not enough

Consistency is the hobgoblin of small minds?

> information in *any* text -- including yours and mine -- to make any sense of
> it, whatsoever.  Communication and understanding are based on assumptions of
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Pall Mall (at least) is a cigarette.

Pall Mall is known not to be a cigar. It is not logically required, but
it is a fact.
> 
> So is Prince, but that doesn't require that they're not also cigar brands.  I
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> semantic-philosophical nit pick.

My altogether too subtle way to say that when -- indeed if -- Einstein
claimed that most of the world's population couldn't solve this trivial
logic puzzle, that was not a reference to the level on intelligence
required.
> 
> I'm tempted to ask you if you can write a program to do such nit picking for
> you, but I'm sure that would be a *much* more difficult problem than the one
> attributed to Einstein (Andrew Einstein, perhaps?).

That would require bringing to bear a great deal of artificial
intelligence. Ninety-nine percent of the world, me included, doesn't
know how to do that. Indeed, the first programmer (who will likely also
be an engineer) who creates a sound embodiment of artificial stupidity
will probably be awarded a Nobel Prize.  ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½
> 
>                 :-)            /Jim Lucas

Jerry
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T08:04:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org>`

```
Jerry Avins wrote:

> That would require bringing to bear a great deal of artificial
> intelligence. Ninety-nine percent of the world, me included, doesn't
> know how to do that. Indeed, the first programmer (who will likely also
> be an engineer) who creates a sound embodiment of artificial stupidity
> will probably be awarded a Nobel Prize.  ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½

I've seen plenty of artificial stupidity already.

But the trouble with defining whether or not we have AI is that there is no solid
arrival point.  We can judge whether a computer appears to act like a person, but
intelligence can be many different things without that.  It is basically problem
solving.   Lots of problems can be solved via computers, lots cannot.  At what level
do we say we have the first AI?   I suppose we have had it for a long time.  And we
are gradually incrementing the intelligence level to new levels.

Some people were upset when a computer beat the World Chess champion.  But computers
could long beat 99% of the population.  And bicycles could long beat 100% of the
population.  Just because intelligence is specialized doesn't mean it doesn't exist.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jerry Avins <jya@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T15:10:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE7EC2.E11AFC8D@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org> <3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Jerry Avins wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I've seen plenty of artificial stupidity already.

You have better vision and better sources than I. All I've seen is
programmer stupidity, much of it my own, and all of it real. When we can
program a machine to be as stupid and as effective in the real world as
a housefly or an earthworm, that will be the artificial-stupidity
precursor to AI. Expert systems and fuzzy logic may be useful tools for
the purpose, but so is assembly language. None of those embody
intelligence. (Or so it says here!)
> 
> But the trouble with defining whether or not we have AI is that there is no solid
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> population.  Just because intelligence is specialized doesn't mean it doesn't exist.

It's the same way with animal intelligence. People have long maintained
that what sets humans apart from the rest of the animal kingdom is ____.
(Pick one or insert your own: tool making; verbal communication; self
consciousness; tool use; altruism; symbolic reasoning.) As soon as an
instance of _____ is demonstrated in animals, it is removed from the
list, sometimes replaced by another. I have a second-hand report of an
encounter with a large bear that stood 7 feet tall on his hind legs. He
had been hanging around watching my friend's friend build a log cabin,
but didn't give the impression of becoming a threat. On coming to watch
one day, the bear picked up a log that would have been too heavy for one
man, stood, and walked into the woods with it balanced on his shoulder.
It seemed a case of "See! I can do it too!" I can't imagine what the
bear was actually thinking, but he was surely thinking something.

Jerry
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T13:27:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE82C1.9EEDE7BB@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org> <3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net> <3AAE7EC2.E11AFC8D@ieee.org>`

```
Jerry Avins wrote:

> It's the same way with animal intelligence. People have long maintained
> that what sets humans apart from the rest of the animal kingdom is ____.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> list, sometimes replaced by another.

What's funny is when we pick "man kills his own kind".  Even if we are unique by being
bad, it is preferable to not being unique.  But this is just as false as "man is the only
animal which dreams".

Same things happen with AI.  As soon as one criterion is met, it is discarded.

I have read that the hardest things to do are from early in the evolutionary ladder.
Making a chess playing robot is easier than making one run or swim well.

About a year ago, I read an article in the Smithsonian.  This guy was making very dumb
robots without the type of programming we think of.   They worked.  He would break off a
leg and they would still crawl ahead like a bug.   The first thing I did was turn the
magazine over to make sure it wasn't the April issue, but it appeared to be serious.  If
this has a future, it is a fascinating one which I haven't seen in any SF.  (although
Hogan had a novel with evolutionary robots which this could fit in).
```

http://members.home.net/js.graham/einstein/index.html.

Steve Graham

<a id="t-856d7892c6"></a>
## Thread: COBOL needs your help. — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL needs your help.

- **From:** Warren G. Simmons <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T03:40:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1103_984368425@micron-450>`

```
On Mon, 12 Mar 2001 15:46:35 +1300, in comp.lang.cobol you wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote in message 
<3AABC562.17C3CB63@home.com>...
> >
What you say is as I am sure you know not new.  This prediction has been 
the objective of users for decades.  Objective, make programming like 
Engineering.  Learn to read the books, keep up with the new materials and 
methods, and "it will be like something we can't get rid off anyway."  Quote 
is my thought as I can't seem to verbalize my point.

My only point in this whole discussion is that each branch of engineering 
has a continuing growth in facilities, methods, and demands.  The pieces 
of yesterday have proven that what is done by the book today will not 
stand up in the future.  Change is inevitable.  In each area of development 
of the tools there will be a need to refine, and invent, and throw away.  New 
insights will cause some of the difficult things of today to become simple 
in the guide book of tomorrow.  Currently, not everyone involved is working 
on the book in which they have the most knowledge .   A lot of refinement 
will occur, and still there will be new things that make much of it obsolete.

In my lingo, BFD.  I won't see it, and I feel most of the current speculation 
is miss directed as most of what we know is coming will not be here in our 
lifetime. Our effort might be better made in the area of the refinement of 
the tools we are now using.

Just my opinion.

Warren Simmons

Warren Simmons

<a id="t-0d0f7e2f06"></a>
## Thread: Reaffirmation - JUST the Correction Amendment — 2 msgs

### Reaffirmation - JUST the Correction Amendment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T02:57:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98i342$t80$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I hate (almost <G>) to start another thread on REAFFIRMATION, but there is
one issue that is actually "up in the air".  It has now been confirmed by
ANSI and NCITS there is ABSOLUTELY no way to get ANSI X3.23-1985 (The '85
Standard) and X3.23a-1989 (the intrinsic function amendment) reaffirmed or
reinstated (because of the administrative snafu - and my taking advantage of
it).

HOWEVER, there is a real question of what will happen with X3.23b-1993 (The
"Correction Amendment).  I believe (but am not positive) that there is the
possibility to reaffirm it - even though it is written as a "change
document" to a no-longer approved/valid Standard.  (E.g. it has lines like

Page I-12/X3.23-1985 (A-4/X3.23a-1989)
 Change the entry for "Reference-modifier" to "Reference modification".

*NOWHERE* in the amendment (that I have found) does include a statement that
parts of the earlier standards that are *not* changed remain in effect.

I plan to suggest (in a public review comment) that IF (more accurately
WHEN) the other Standards are withdrawn, then this should be withdrawn as
well - as it simply makes no sense as a Standard by itself.

At the risk of seeing lots of comments that disagree with me (again), I
would be interested in what this group thinks about this issue.

Again, you may send comments in (even if you have already sent in another
"general" comment) indicating whether you do or do NOT want this Standard
reaffirmed - when the others are withdrawn.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reaffirmation - JUST the Correction Amendment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T03:06:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98i3kf$j38$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<98i342$t80$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI - if anyone reading this thread is interested in getting (buying) a copy
of the Correction Amendment as a downloadable PDF file, you should go to:

 http://webstore.ansi.org/ansidocstore/dept.asp

and search on X3.23b
```

<a id="t-96dd0ae69f"></a>
## Thread: determining the OS — 2 msgs

### determining the OS

- **From:** Marcello.Clarizia@siae.it ("Marcello Clarizia")
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T11:49:40+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<000801c0aae1$dc36b430$0a02320a@fsi.dg.siae>`

```
Is there a way, in COBOL, to tell which version of Windows is running ?
I have to create files with longname, using a DOS shell and a batch file. The shell is COMMAND.EXE in Win95, or CMD.EXE in WinNT, so the COBOL program has to know which OS it's running under, before calling the batch file.
```

#### ↳ Re: determining the OS

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T15:59:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-DRk8yP3FKWLd@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<000801c0aae1$dc36b430$0a02320a@fsi.dg.siae>`

```
On Mon, 12 Mar 2001 10:49:40, Marcello.Clarizia@siae.it ("Marcello 
Clarizia") wrote:

> This is a multi-part message in MIME format.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snipped HTML out of the post - please turn this off>

If you are using the MERANT (Microfocus) COBOL products they provide a
runtime routine called "cbl_get_os_info" that will tell you what OS is
running. 

If you are not using the MERANT compiler check the documentation of 
the one you are using to see if they offer an equivalent routine, if 
one is not available, you could always call the appropriate Windows 
API (you only mentioned Windows(TM) varients so I assume that is the 
only OS you are using).
```

<a id="t-f60fc53a87"></a>
## Thread: Is there a way to concatenate strings? — 17 this month / 86 total

### Is there a way to concatenate strings?

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T10:30:46-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
I'm looking for a simple way to provide visual feedback for a
long-running program. What I'd like is is one of those ever-extending
strings of dots. In other languages I'd set up a string variable, start
it with the value '.' and on each display loop I'd concatenate another
'.' before displaying it.

Maybe it's because it's Monday, but I can't seem to think of a way to do
that in COBOL, at least not without either limiting the string (by using
a pre-configured array of different length strings) or using excessive
overhead.

Actually, the more I think about it the less I see it as even being
possible without excessive overhead, at least for an unlimited length
string. If you've got some suggestions I'd appreciate it but maybe I
just need to give this a little more thought. Thanks.
```

- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us>`

#### ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T18:12:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC29A26.2DFC70E1@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us>`

```


Calvin Crumrine wrote:

> I'm looking for a simple way to provide visual feedback for a
> long-running program. What I'd like is is one of those ever-extending
> strings of dots. In other languages I'd set up a string variable, start
> it with the value '.' and on each display loop I'd concatenate another
> '.' before displaying it.

If you want to build a string of catenated strings in a line for display,
you can also do it using the reference modification version of the MOVE
verb.  This, I find, is one of the most frequently overlooked features of
COBOL, especially by old-timers who are unaccustomed to such a simple
capability for manipulating string slices.

Lao Xiao Hai
```

```

input records. Every 10 thousand input records, the program displays the
time/date and a record counter. If you want to show a program is really
running
OK - not just looping, give some information like the input record key as
proof.

do you process this 3 million records?

logic to amplify the interval by a factor of 10 each time the status 
display is given.  Not that this does much for run time , but is kinder to 
the console area that receives your displays.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-30T12:18:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kQ_w6.83928$Xt3.11469317@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <3AC29A26.2DFC70E1@ix.netcom.com> <eHxw6.79177$Xt3.10822675@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3AC39367.6731B197@home.com> <3AC4125C.38E338D2@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Tape mounts are also a good indicator - but my process didn't do any.

I've just finished testing a replacement for my long runnin job. The
replacement
runs in around 3 hours, instead of 60!

The problem was unsing an HDAM DLI DB. I need to read a root, read a child,
use the thild to get the key value of an XREF'd root and then read that
root.
I process what I've read, then read the next input in key sequence order,
get
it's root, get it's child,etc.

My replacement process builds a DB2 table. I extract all roots and all
children
of the type that contain the xref key value. Then I use synchsort,
slice'n'dice
and put them together read to load into the DB2 table - total run time about
30 minutes - not in the critical path.

Now I can run my process to read the cross references and get all the
information I
need in a single read.

P.S. For the DLI read, I tried using 2 PCBs, I tried 2 pass processing and I
tried
various RBA sorts, and none made a huge difference.

P.P.S. The reason my job did no tapes mounts is .......my client has
outsourced their
tape management, and now uses "Virtual Tape". Virtual Tape has all of the
drawbacks
of physical tape and none of the advantages - it's actually using DASD to
mimic tape,
but doesn't allow you to reference it like DASD. As a result, programmers
now code
as much as possible on physical DASD, rather than Virtual Tape - hence no
tape mounts!



"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3AC4125C.38E338D2@worldnet.att.net...

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ac49650_2@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <3AC29A26.2DFC70E1@ix.netcom.com> <eHxw6.79177$Xt3.10822675@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3AC39367.6731B197@home.com> <3AC4125C.38E338D2@worldnet.att.net> <kQ_w6.83928$Xt3.11469317@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
This was very interesting Rufio.

Your solution is smart and efficient.

However, I believe if your original IMS DB had been physically organised as
HISAM you would've got much better performance from it.

The problem with sequential access to HDAM roots is that they are accessed
by hashing, which is extremely efficient for random access but not so good
when you are accessing in key sequence.

By changing the organisation to HISAM you have a very good chance that
sequential records will be physically buffered together and you can still
access them randomly.

Poor old IMS suffers from being perceived as "outmoded" technology (a bit
like COBOL really...<G>) but for many years (up until 1983 actually) we used
it as the "only game in town" and found it very useful. By fiddling with
buffer sizes for indexes and data I believe the performance you describe
could have been improved many times. However, I don't blame you for going
RDB, and the solution you describe is excellent.

In my opinion, IMS-DC is still an infinitely superior TP Monitor to CICS,
but again, it has been tarred with the same brush as DL/1 IMS, "outmoded".

We need to remember that just because something is old, does not mean it
isn't any good... <G>

<picks up walking stick and hobbles off into the distance, elderly dog
staggers to arthritic legs and struggles to join him...>

Pete.

Rufio wrote in message ...
>Tape mounts are also a good indicator - but my process didn't do any.
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>and put them together read to load into the DB2 table - total run time
about
>30 minutes - not in the critical path.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>P.S. For the DLI read, I tried using 2 PCBs, I tried 2 pass processing and
I
>tried
>various RBA sorts, and none made a huge difference.
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > > OK - not just looping, give some information like the input record
key
>as
>> > > proof.
>> > >

files
>or a
>> > DB ?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks Peter. unfortunately, my team doesn't own the DB, we were one of
around 70 applications that access the data. Just changing VSAM to OSAM
and using dynamic allocation (USERLIBs), cost tens of thousands. They won't
allow anyone else to change the DB, and the XREF information is fairly minor
to the owning group.

We are now waiting for a fight to break out between IMS & DB2 DBA's.
They are both trying to convince us that theirs is better. I'd say
sequential reads
will be quicker on equivilent DLI databases, but I can extract more data
quicker
with DB2/SQL online processes - and DB2-CONNECT & MQ-Series just
opened up a whole new world to us!

#### ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T04:45:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
Lao Lao Lao,

I'm an oldtimer and thank God and the Standards Committee for giving us
reference modification.

Back to the problem at hand. I like Rufio's solution; it shows that PRODUCTIVE
work is being done. But I think all "long running" programs should be
performance reviewed (preferably before it goes into production). A case in
point: a few years ago I was asked to write a pgm to print invoices for a
telecom company. The specs ran something like this:

Prepare invoices for printig. Each invoice can contain up to 4 page types, each
type can contain a varying # of pages. All pages must be numbered. Each page
type is contained in its own dataset, sorted by acct#.

Read all invoice pages for an account and write to a temp dataset. Read the temp
d/s, number the pages, and write the invoice set to a final d/s.

OK, the pgm processed 20,000 accounts and created 3 million print lines and ran
3+ hours. It did other things of course, but this is the area that caused the
problem.

I mentioned to the PM that wrote the specs that opens/closes of the temp d/s is
probably eating up that time. I noted that the vast majority of the invoices
were 8 pages or less (one was 2500 pages) and suggested that an in-memory table
of 10 pages could be used to reduce the opens/closes. She didn't think it was
worth the effort. Anyway, I did it on my own time.

The results were AMAZING. I thought I could reduce the run time by an hour or
so: the job ran in 10 MIN!!!!! Who would have thunk? 

The moral of this little tale is: spend some time looking at why a job runs x
hours. Sometimes the answer is that's the best that can be done. But sometimes
the answer is 10 minutes.

Jack               



In article <3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us>, Calvin Crumrine says...
>
>I'm looking for a simple way to provide visual feedback for a
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-30T07:14:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com>`

```
"John H Sleight Jr" <John_member@newsranger.com> wrote:
>
> I mentioned to the PM that wrote the specs that opens/closes of the temp d/s is
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the answer is 10 minutes.

Opening and closing files rank among the most expensive I/O operations
you can do, except on groups of records.  *Any time* open and close are
used in a loop, it should be viewed with extreme skepticism.  However,
when you write intermediate records, that alone doubles the number of
I/O's (from READ/WRITE to READ/WRITE/READ/WRITE).  This demonstrates
gross ignorance to the point of incompetence in the PM (PHM? :-).

My son recently wrote a strikingly similar program.  Though he has only
been programming professionally for less than three years, he spotted
the potential performance hit immediately and coded it using a table
the first time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a way to concatenate strings?

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-31T15:52:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl>`

```
Judson,

With charity toward the PM (BTW, whats a PHM?) there were some accounts that
generated invoices with upwards of 2500 pages. Since we had to stage the large
invoices to a temp file, why not do it for all to simplify the pgm logic?
Reasonable, but wrong given the timings involved. I knew open/close was a pig,
but I was amazed by the 1800% runtime difference.

The R/W savings weren't significant, since the # of pages tabled represented a
very small %age of the total pages, but a large %age of the total accts.

Jack.

Treat others with kindness and God will smile upon you. JS, 4/1/01

- **References:** `<3AAD23E5.C74E3EFD@dced.state.ak.us> <B5zw6.714$bY4.1106@www.newsranger.com> <oG%w6.2956$aD4.184689@news2.atl> <E2nx6.2544$bY4.7822@www.newsranger.com> <3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au>`

```
I'm not sure that open & close operations are particularly important,
when considering performance, unless you have a very high volume
of files and a very small number of records in each.

In a mass process, I wouldn't expect to open/close more than
20 files maximum. If each file has 500,00 records average,
that's 40 open/close operations and  10,000,000 reads.

But then I've only ever worked for clients with 10,000,000+
customers, so I'm not as familiar with smaller shops!


"Tim Josling" <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:3AC65CD3.ED87ED40@melbpc.org.au...
> Open close is particularly expensive on MVS (z/OS). I once did a
> benchmark of Unix vs MVS and the Unix pgm ran in less time than
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >you can do, except on groups of records.
```

<a id="t-391d03b411"></a>
## Thread: Age Discrimination — 9 msgs

### Age Discrimination

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-12T16:36:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kdbr6.103$4R2.1097@client>`

```
In several threads recently - "Where are all the COBOL Jobs??" and "COBOL
needs your help." there has been considerable discussion of the future of
COBOL programming.  I recently had a reader e-mail me with the following in
response to an article in www.aboutlegacycoding.com about the COBOL job
market:

"You are guilty of the same thing most other articles on COBOL
are guilty of - you fail to mention the age descrimination towards COBOL
programmers!"

I'd love to write an article (or find someone to write it - any volunteers?)
about Age Discrimination but I've never experienced it personally.  And
since the average age of staff here is about 45, it doesn't exist in our
company.  So I don't feel qualified to comment.

I assume what the writer was talking about is the discrimination that older
COBOL professionals feel when they apply for programming jobs where they
will learn and use newer languages; either exclusively or in parallel with
COBOL?

If any of you have any stories that you can share (with or without real
names) I would appreciate hearing them.  These may be stories where you were
discriminated against, or stories where - as a manager you did or did not
hire because of age.  Please explicitly state that I may use your story in
an article for publication on the Web and let me know if you wish your name
used or just a nick.  Post it here or e-mail it to me directly.
```

#### ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T15:33:48+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3aad8bfd_2@my.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<kdbr6.103$4R2.1097@client>`

```

Editor wrote in message ...
>In several threads recently - "Where are all the COBOL Jobs??" and "COBOL
>needs your help." there has been considerable discussion of the future of
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't know about working as an employed COBOL Programmer in the United
States (I've been freelance for 27 years but worked  as an "employee" in New
Zealand for 10 years before that) but I DO know about marketplaces.

Any company that allows age, sex, skin colour, religion, education, accent,
fashion sense, sports, hobbies, sexual preference, or ANY other
non-job-related attribute to colour their judgement when hiring people for a
job (ANY job, not just Programming) is just plain stupid.

These companies invariably get the people they deserve, and you should count
your lucky stars you DIDN'T get to work for them.

The ONLY criterion should be ability to meet  (or exceed) the requirements
of the job. Obviously, if you make "social interaction and ability to fit
in" part of the job requirement, then some of the things above MAY assume
more importance.

I have employed people who were complete social misfits (made me feel at
home...<G>) and have even been warned NOT to employ certain people by their
previous employers, but gone ahead and done it on merit anyway. I have NEVER
regretted this policy and in nearly thirty years I've NEVER had to fire
someone I hired. (I have had to fire people who were hired by others.)

Sadly, there are companies where young Managers will feel threatened by an
older employee. The best thing to do is to be open and friendly at the
interview. Show your enthusiasm (this is an ageless and priceless commodity
and people who demonstrate it get my vote every time) and be interested in
listening to what they are doing, what their problems are, and suggest how
you believe you could contribute solutions to them. Demonstrate how you
enjoy working as a team player and interacting with others to solve
problems. Be energetic and enthusiastic. By listening  to and showing a
supportive attitude, the person hiring will not even consider how old you
are.

It's a market place. They need skills; you need a job.

Leave them feeling that they can't afford NOT to employ you even if you were
100!

If you approach an interview on the basis that you are likely to be too old,
don't blame the Hirer for agreeing with you.

Unfortunately there are places where you won't even get an interview once
they look at your age. If the Job market is buoyant you can afford to write
these ones off (and good riddance!). If it isn't and you REALLY want to get
to the interview then leave the date of birth blank on the interview form.
If they say they want it, lie! Claim later it was a misprint...like "1956"
becomes "1965"...OK, I'm not serious, but you catch my drift. You could
legitimately ask if there is an age range for the job and if so, why wasn't
it advertised to save you wasting time coming down there?

Try this one: "I don't like to give personal non-job-related data to people
who may not employ me. How about I fill this in after you make a decision on
whether I'm going to work here or not...?"

The bottom line is that if you sell yourself and your experience (and I
really don't mean bullshit your way in...if you've had many years writing
COBOL you can do it on merit without dishonesty), because it is a
marketplace, a deal will be struck.

If you approach it as being "over the hill", tired, non-receptive to new
ideas, then expect to be "discriminated against".

It always annoys me when people claim "discrimination" (on ANY basis) when
the reason they didn't get it was simply because they weren't right for it
or up to it.

Finally, many years ago I was a young would-be programmer trying to get a
job. I had no experience and couldn't get any unless they employed me...(see
there are TWO sides to this age argument...).

I wrote over 50 applications, made phone calls, persevered. Finally a man at
a company in Auckland agreed to see me. He said they had just taken delivery
of a new System 360 to replace their old 1401 and really needed an Operator
who would work shifts. I said I would sweep the floors, punch cards, make
the coffee if they would just give me access to it and the manuals that came
with it. Working shifts..? No problem. (I was fairly newly married but
figured I'd square it with the wife later...).

The Manager had been interviewing all day and was tired but he was amused by
my enthusiasm and asked me if I'd like to see the new machine...Next thing I
knew I was in front of this blue-grey cabinet with hundreds of twinkling
lights, in a room full of whirring tapes and a deafening 1403 that chattered
intermittently. It felt real good.

"So what do you think?" he asked.
"Can't wait to get my hands on it!" I replied fervently.

I started on the Monday. Within three months I was learning Assembler from
reading manuals on the night shift and the rest is history...

Never underestimate enthusiasm...

Pete.

important and set aside what is not. Having to ignore the nose ring can be a
"growth" process for all concerned...<G>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T08:34:48-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE5A38.3D07C1F@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<kdbr6.103$4R2.1097@client> <3aad8bfd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <Dior6.7144$6p5.641796@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3aae5182_2@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Jerry P wrote in message ...
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

IMO much discrimination is an issue of control. This applies to more than
hiring. My wife watched a TV show recently which had a woman who was fired for
refusing to extend her job to 'cover' her co-worker's absence. The issue was
that the company let the co-worker take time off because she had children but
refused to let the other worker take similar time off because she wanted to take
it off for things like working out, etc.

IMO the only thing that should affect the decision whether or not to let an
employee take time off is whether or not the employee is needed at work during
that time. Note that I said 'should'. Practical issues like illness sometimes
affect this, but if you watch for employees taking advantage then I think other
employees are willing to work around this. Personally I don't even think the
employer should ask why the employee wants to take time off, it's enough that
they do. (And even worse is the employer who is concerned whether or not the
employee can afford to take the time off. I had one of those-denied leave solely
because 'the employee didn't have enough accrued leave to cover the time
requested.' The employee was aware of this lack and was willing to take the pay
loss, but the leave was still denied. IMO the denial was simply to exercise the
employer's power. IOW granting leave in excess of that accrued was totally
discretionary & could be denied for no other reason than because the employer
wanted to deny it. That's almost as bad as the supervisor, since fired, who
called his employee's doctor to try & reschedule their medical appointments for
times outside the employee's work hours.)

Abuses are endemic.
```
