> It is good for the both the Hirer and the Employee to focus on what is
> important and set aside what is not. Having to ignore the nose ring can be
a
> "growth" process for all concerned...<G>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> -----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T07:03:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE28B4.85D7A1A4@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<kdbr6.103$4R2.1097@client> <3aad8bfd_2@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Some age discrimination is really price discrimination.  The companies don't
want to pay for veteran free agents.

Some of it is the result of socialistic practices such as company run retirement
plans which they haven't funded correctly so that they aren't paying for young
employees.  Or union seniority pay.

Sometimes a worker has proven his worth in one company, and doesn't want to
start at the bottom with another company.  So his starting price is higher.

Sometimes a company wants young bodies who are willing to work 80 hour work
weeks.  This is the reputation Microsoft has.

Some managers don't believe older workers are willing/able to change to new
technologies.  After all, if they wanted to be successful, they would be
managers themselves, right?

Of course, age discrimination may be overstated.  Because it is against the law
in most countries, nobody will state that they discriminate because of age.  So
an older worker makes assumptions about why he isn't hired.   Is it age
discrimination if a company doesn't want to pay what 50 year old workers are
used to getting, but is willing to pay what 20 year old workers are used to
getting?

The older I got, the more I did contracting.  My current job started off as a
contracting one.  A company is willing to pay more for expertise in a
contracting position - where there are no dangers of having to pay retirement
and no morale problems with some employees getting significantly more pay than
their younger peers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T10:37:47-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAE770B.CA8207DC@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<kdbr6.103$4R2.1097@client> <3aad8bfd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <3AAE28B4.85D7A1A4@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> Some age discrimination is really price discrimination.  The companies don't
> want to pay for veteran free agents.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> their younger peers.

Actually I've seen just the opposite-managers have hired younger people who are no
better qualified, and they've paid them the same as I was asking. As far as I can
see the only difference is age.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-03-17T20:46:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FWbt6.2782$A2.819612@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<kdbr6.103$4R2.1097@client> <3aad8bfd_2@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Peter, very astute observations and recomendations!

Remember, job-seekers,  the purpose of a Resume is to get you an INTERVIEW.

The purpose of the interview is to get you a job.

If the person(s) hiring have several close candidates, the one that
most of the got a 'warm fuzzy' from gets the vote/job !

If you can somehow work it in or seize the moment to bring some laughter (be
careful, no real jokes) this will be remember even subconsciously.

Be HONEST

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote in message
news:3aad8bfd_2@my.newsfeeds.com...
>
> Editor wrote in message ...
> >In several threads recently - "Where are all the COBOL Jobs??" and "COBOL
> >needs your help." there has been considerable discussion of the future of
> >COBOL programming.  I recently had a reader e-mail me with the following
in
> >response to an article in www.aboutlegacycoding.com about the COBOL job
> >market:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >I assume what the writer was talking about is the discrimination that
older
> >COBOL professionals feel when they apply for programming jobs where they
> >will learn and use newer languages; either exclusively or in parallel
with
> >COBOL?
> >
> I don't know about working as an employed COBOL Programmer in the United
> States (I've been freelance for 27 years but worked  as an "employee" in
New
> Zealand for 10 years before that) but I DO know about marketplaces.
>
> Any company that allows age, sex, skin colour, religion, education,
accent,
> fashion sense, sports, hobbies, sexual preference, or ANY other
> non-job-related attribute to colour their judgement when hiring people for
a
> job (ANY job, not just Programming) is just plain stupid.
>
> These companies invariably get the people they deserve, and you should
count
> your lucky stars you DIDN'T get to work for them.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> home...<G>) and have even been warned NOT to employ certain people by
their
> previous employers, but gone ahead and done it on merit anyway. I have
NEVER
> regretted this policy and in nearly thirty years I've NEVER had to fire
> someone I hired. (I have had to fire people who were hired by others.)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> interview. Show your enthusiasm (this is an ageless and priceless
commodity
> and people who demonstrate it get my vote every time) and be interested in
> listening to what they are doing, what their problems are, and suggest how
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Leave them feeling that they can't afford NOT to employ you even if you
were
> 100!
>
> If you approach an interview on the basis that you are likely to be too
old,
> don't blame the Hirer for agreeing with you.
>
> Unfortunately there are places where you won't even get an interview once
> they look at your age. If the Job market is buoyant you can afford to
write
> these ones off (and good riddance!). If it isn't and you REALLY want to
get
> to the interview then leave the date of birth blank on the interview form.
> If they say they want it, lie! Claim later it was a misprint...like "1956"
> becomes "1965"...OK, I'm not serious, but you catch my drift. You could
> legitimately ask if there is an age range for the job and if so, why
wasn't
> it advertised to save you wasting time coming down there?
>
> Try this one: "I don't like to give personal non-job-related data to
people
> who may not employ me. How about I fill this in after you make a decision
on
> whether I'm going to work here or not...?"
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> job. I had no experience and couldn't get any unless they employed
me...(see
> there are TWO sides to this age argument...).
>
> I wrote over 50 applications, made phone calls, persevered. Finally a man
at
> a company in Auckland agreed to see me. He said they had just taken
delivery
> of a new System 360 to replace their old 1401 and really needed an
Operator
> who would work shifts. I said I would sweep the floors, punch cards, make
> the coffee if they would just give me access to it and the manuals that
came
> with it. Working shifts..? No problem. (I was fairly newly married but
> figured I'd square it with the wife later...).
>
> The Manager had been interviewing all day and was tired but he was amused
by
> my enthusiasm and asked me if I'd like to see the new machine...Next thing
I
> knew I was in front of this blue-grey cabinet with hundreds of twinkling
> lights, in a room full of whirring tapes and a deafening 1403 that
chattered
> intermittently. It felt real good.

<a id="t-60585eb91e"></a>
## Thread: Sub level to level 77 — 6 msgs

### Sub level to level 77

- **From:** auto73594@hushmail.com (Aleph3)
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T06:45:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<targcjksm2n94c@news.supernews.com>`

```
Hi there

The other day I encountered what seems like a bug. When you put sub level 03 
to a level 77 field on MF Server Express there is no compiler error. The same 
program compiled with Net Express generates an error msg (* 219-S*********    
Illegal level number).

Here is the listing of the SE compile:

=======================================================


* Micro Focus Server Express         V2.0 revision 000 13-Mar-01 04:03 Page   
1
* test77.cbl
* Options: int(test77.int) anim csi verbose nolist endp list(test77.lst)
     1 id division.
     2 working-storage section.
     3 77  xx.
     4     03  yy          pic xx.
     5 procedure division.
     6     display yy
     7     stop run
     8     .
* Micro Focus Server Express         V2.0 revision 000 Compiler
* Copyright (C) 1984-2000 MERANT International URN RXCTG/ZZ0/00000R
*                                              REF GNR-099060000AD
* Total Messages:     0
* Data:         472     Code:          55
=======================================================

Now, as I recall, level 77 has become obsolete on Cobol 85. It behaves 
internally exactly like level 01.
So I wonder which compiler is at fault.

Your comments

Aleph3
```

#### ↳ Re: Sub level to level 77

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T04:13:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98krte$h1f$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<targcjksm2n94c@news.supernews.com>`

```
I am checking with my MERANT sources on why this didn't get an error with
Server Express - but I did want to make clear to the group that 77-levels
are not "obsolete" in the '85 Standard or the draft Standard.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sub level to level 77

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T07:23:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98l71g$e0k$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<targcjksm2n94c@news.supernews.com> <98krte$h1f$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My "usually reliable source" at MERANT indicated that this is an "old" bug -
that was fixed "a while ago".  I suggest that you get a current maintenance
level of Server Express and see if it doesn't (correctly) issue a compiler
error.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sub level to level 77

- **From:** auto73594@hushmail.com (Aleph3)
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T14:07:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tasaac6f6g0f04@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<targcjksm2n94c@news.supernews.com> <98krte$h1f$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <98l71g$e0k$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks Bill & everybody.

True, this bug was fixed in the latest version.
And true that my memory is probably faulty.

Aleph3

In article <98l71g$e0k$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>My "usually reliable source" at MERANT indicated that this is an "old" bug -
>that was fixed "a while ago".  I suggest that you get a current maintenance
>level of Server Express and see if it doesn't (correctly) issue a compiler
>error.
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Sub level to level 77

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T04:13:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98l6jr$52j$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<targcjksm2n94c@news.supernews.com>`

```
I am checking with my MERANT sources on why this didn't get an error with
Server Express - but I did want to make clear to the group that 77-levels
are not "obsolete" in the '85 Standard or the draft Standard.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sub level to level 77

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T06:16:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98kvhv$t1i$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<targcjksm2n94c@news.supernews.com>`

```
Level 77 cannot be divided up into level 03's.  It is *not* the same as a
level 01...

"Aleph3" <auto73594@hushmail.com> wrote in message
news:targcjksm2n94c@news.supernews.com...

same

<a id="t-81115a6219"></a>
## Thread: Advice — 1 msgs

### Advice

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <dleahy@wave.home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T15:02:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3Eqr6.369751$Pm2.5662110@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Now that I am approaching my 20th anniversary in this business (yeah, I know
that makes me just a whipper-snapper compared to some of you old coots on
this list), the time has come to look back and reflect upon some of the
advice that I've been given over the years:

1981 : "Stay away from COBOL...it's a dead language."
1982:  "Structured analysis, structured design, structured code"
1983:  "There won't be any more batch processing within 5 years, everything
will be online"
1984:  "Find another line of work.  4GLs will make programmers obsolete."
1985:  "What, you're still using VSAM?  Get a database!"
1986:  "Hierarchical data bases are obsolete.  If you're not relational,
you're not in the game!"
1987:  "*Still* using COBOL?  You dumb #$@!"
1988:  "CASE tools will solve the application backlog"
1989:  "Code reuse will solve the application backlog"
1990:  "Forget CASE tools.  Desktop development is much faster!"
1991:  "The mainframe is dead.  Go client server"
1992:  "Find another line of work.  Overseas outsourcing will put you out of
a job"
1993:  "OO analysis, design and development will solve the application
backlog"
1994:  "What? You're still using IMS??"
1995:  "Forget Cobol.  Learn C".
1996:  "Forget C.  Learn C++".
1997:  "Y2K will be the end of the world as we know it"
1998:  "Okay, maybe COBOL isn't dead....but it *will* be after Y2K is
finished"
1999:  "All future application development will be in JAVA"
2000:  "The mainframe is the best platform for e-business"

The true test of wisdom is knowing which advice to heed and which to ignore.
;-)
```

---

Unfortunately, this is not a very good list of COBOL books.    Most
important,
the more recent books on Object COBOL are absent.   Also, at least one
of the
books listed is filled with out-of-date, perfectly hideous COBOL
examples.

Perhaps someone with a little time on their hands could put together a
really
good list so those who are searching for decent coverage of COBOL would
not be led into such dark corners of the literature.

Richard Riehle

apps.

<a id="t-1323c72e7e"></a>
## Thread: COBOL CGI/Windows2000 Web Server — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### COBOL CGI/Windows2000 Web Server

- **From:** Kate Parker <KateParker@MailAndNews.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T12:39:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB90A23@MailAndNews.com>`

```
Good morning,

I'm trying to find a Windows2000 compatible web server under which I can run 
COBOL CGI for RMCOBOL and access data on a separate (Novell) server.  
SAMBAR4.4 handles the CGI and the login to the other server fine, but it 
doesn't see the COBOL generated cookies.  IIS handles the CGI and cookies 
but 
is running as a service which causes it to login under a different 
account/login name and thus not have access to the data.

I think if I could run IIS as an application it might work for me, but 
haven't 
figured out how to do that yet.  Or if I knew what login it is using to 
access 
the other server I could set up a user for it.....or if I knew how to 
control 
what login it uses I could force it to use the same login.

Has anyone out there ever successfully used COBOL CGI with the data files on 
a 
different server?

Thanks!
-Katie Parker
cobolnotdead@hotmail.com

Unrelated.. has anyone noticed the increase in system resources that Fujitsu 
PowerCobol v6 uses now?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL CGI/Windows2000 Web Server

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-14T07:06:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AAF187B.D2C13FD7@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3AB90A23@MailAndNews.com>`

```
Kate Parker wrote:
> 
> I'm trying to find a Windows2000 compatible web server under which I can run
> COBOL CGI for RMCOBOL and access data on a separate (Novell) server.

You could try Xitami (www.Xitami.com) or Apache.

> Has anyone out there ever successfully used COBOL CGI with the data files on
> a different server?

I have a couple of sites with light traffic using Xitami on Windows95/98
accessing data on a System Manager machine.  (You probably won't have
heard of System Manager, nor want to).  I don't have any problems with
cookies or access, but it isn't Win2000.  Same programs also run on
Linux/Apache.
```

---

Look for OPEN EXTEND.

HOWEVER, I should also say that for IBM mainframe (OS/390) users, it is also
possible (and probably more common) to use OPEN OUTPUT in your program - but
DISP=MOD in your JCL.

Therefore, the answer to your question MAY depend on the specific compiler
and operating system.
```

<a id="t-71d0ef75e0"></a>
## Thread: IBM COBOL and CICS — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM COBOL and CICS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-13T14:07:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<98lulp$5gl$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
IBM watchers, COBOLers, or just those interested in "when to do compiler
upgrades",

you might want to look at a "semi-hidden" paragraph in today's IBM CICS
Announcement at:
  http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_201-060

Look for the paragraph that says,

"Support remains in CICS TS V2.1 for the earlier (pre-Language Environment)
compilers OS/VS COBOL (5740-CB1, 5740-LM1, and 5734-CB4), VS COBOL II
(5668-958, also 5688-023 and 5688-022), OS PL/I Version 2 (5668-910), and
SAA AD/Cycle� C/370T (5688-216). Translator support for these compilers is
withdrawn in CICS TS V2.2. It is planned that run-time support for load
modules created using these compilers, that do not use Language Environment,
will be withdrawn in the release following CICS TS V2.2."
```

### Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org (Volker Englisch)
- **Date:** 2001-03-17T14:52:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn9b6ugr.6v.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <slrn9avbrr.bp.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB0748D.4C092484@Azonic.co.nz> <slrn9b20sc.a8.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <4uvs6.397337$f36.13382877@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Calin Macrinici <cmac@pathcom.com> wrote:
>I wonder what would be the file status if you check it after the WRITE
>statements.

Oops. The file status after the first write (file opened with output)
is correctly 00. After the second write (extend) it is 92.

>Also, I don't think OPEN OUTPUT is mandatory, I think OPEN EXTEND
>creates the file for you if it doesn't exist.

I tried it that way first, but for it didn't show the result I
expected, I tried it to first create the file by an OUTPUT to be sure
if the file-existance was the problem...

cu
Volker
```

#### ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T11:32:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<992rbl$a7u$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <slrn9avbrr.bp.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB0748D.4C092484@Azonic.co.nz> <slrn9b20sc.a8.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <4uvs6.397337$f36.13382877@news20.bellglobal.com> <slrn9b6ugr.6v.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org>`

```
"Volker Englisch" <eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> wrote in message
news:slrn9b6ugr.6v.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org...
>
> >Also, I don't think OPEN OUTPUT is mandatory, I think OPEN EXTEND
> >creates the file for you if it doesn't exist.
>

Whether an OPEN EXTEND does or does not "create" a NEW file - if it doesn't
already exists - depends on whether you do or do NOT have the "optional"
keyword in your Select/Assign statement. Check this out in your Language
Reference Manual.


P.S.  For those of you who missed one critical note in this thread - the
people "with" the Tiny COBOL project have already admitted they have a "BUG"
in their run-time system and that the original reported problem is with
TinyCOBOL itself.

This does NOT change the fact that
 A) you shouldn't "Play with" the FD area while a file is closed
 B) the original program could/should have checked the file status after the
WRITEs as well as the OPENs.

However, even if both of these "changes in source program" had been done,
TinyCOBOL still wouldn't have worked correctly.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>

#### ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T07:07:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB5B020.1BBD1425@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <slrn9avbrr.bp.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB0748D.4C092484@Azonic.co.nz> <slrn9b20sc.a8.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <4uvs6.397337$f36.13382877@news20.bellglobal.com> <slrn9b6ugr.6v.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org>`

```
Volker Englisch wrote:
> 
> Calin Macrinici <cmac@pathcom.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> is correctly 00. After the second write (extend) it is 92.

I noticed that your code appeared to check the whole file status for
zero.  You should only check the first byte for being zero as success. 
When the first byte is zero the 2nd byte need not be.  For example
writing an indexed record with a validly duplicated alternate key will
give a status of '02'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T14:33:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99364i$sfn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <slrn9avbrr.bp.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB0748D.4C092484@Azonic.co.nz> <slrn9b20sc.a8.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <4uvs6.397337$f36.13382877@news20.bellglobal.com> <slrn9b6ugr.6v.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB5B020.1BBD1425@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
I believe that whether you check only the first byte or both bytes of a file
status code should (in most cases) depend upon the I/O statement that it
follows.

There is no ANSI conforming file status code after an OPEN that is
successful *and* that has a non-zero value in the 2nd byte.

Thus the original code was "reasonable" in its use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T14:42:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9936l3$aku$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <slrn9avbrr.bp.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB0748D.4C092484@Azonic.co.nz> <slrn9b20sc.a8.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <4uvs6.397337$f36.13382877@news20.bellglobal.com> <slrn9b6ugr.6v.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB5B020.1BBD1425@Azonic.co.nz> <99364i$sfn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just to correct something in my last post.

It is possible to get an "05" on an OPEN statement - but not on the OPEN
statement in the original example.  An "05" is described as follows (which
was NOT the case in the original code sample)

"I-O status = 05. An OPEN statement is successfully executed but the file is
described as optional and the physical file is not present at the time the
OPEN statement is executed. If the open mode is I-O or extend, the physical
file has been created."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T18:00:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB6494B.22590035@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <slrn9avbrr.bp.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB0748D.4C092484@Azonic.co.nz> <slrn9b20sc.a8.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <4uvs6.397337$f36.13382877@news20.bellglobal.com> <slrn9b6ugr.6v.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB5B020.1BBD1425@Azonic.co.nz> <99364i$sfn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> >
> > I noticed that your code appeared to check the whole file status for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > give a status of '02'.
 
> I believe that whether you check only the first byte or both bytes of a file
> status code should (in most cases) depend upon the I/O statement that it
> follows.

> There is no ANSI conforming file status code after an OPEN that is
> successful *and* that has a non-zero value in the 2nd byte.
> 
> Thus the original code was "reasonable" in its use.

My advice was of a more general nature.  If the file status code is
checked in the first byte for '0' meaning success before getting into
more detailed checking then there will be fewer problems (IMHO).  It
would be a good habit to get into to check success of _all_ file i-o
statements just be the first byte.

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Just to correct something in my last post.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> was NOT the case in the original code sample)

Why is this not the case ?  What originally created the file, or had it
existed 'forever' ?

Actually there being no ANSI conforming file-status codes would be
beside the point anyway, even if it had been true.  There is nothing to
prevent additional codes being added into the _next_ standard, just as
some were added into '85.  Many of my programs started as Cobol'74 and I
want them to operate without conversion, as far as possible, with
whatever new standards arrive.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T00:23:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9948hu$kvt$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <slrn9avbrr.bp.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB0748D.4C092484@Azonic.co.nz> <slrn9b20sc.a8.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <4uvs6.397337$f36.13382877@news20.bellglobal.com> <slrn9b6ugr.6v.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <3AB5B020.1BBD1425@Azonic.co.nz> <99364i$sfn$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3AB6494B.22590035@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3AB6494B.22590035@Azonic.co.nz...
> William M. Klein wrote:
> > >
 <snip>
> >
> > It is possible to get an "05" on an OPEN statement - but not on the OPEN
> > statement in the original example.  An "05" is described as follows
(which
> > was NOT the case in the original code sample)
>
> Why is this not the case ?  What originally created the file, or had it
> existed 'forever' ?
>

An "05" file status code may *only* be issued for an OPEN EXTEND if the
Select/Assign statement as the OPTIONAL keyword in it - and the original
source code did NOT have it. (and it may NOT be issued for an OPEN OUTPUT).
```

<a id="t-615a2c9b27"></a>
## Thread: Appending to a sequential file — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** "mangogrower" <mangogrower@telocity.co>
- **Date:** 2001-03-17T21:00:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VWbt6.2790$A2.820012@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <OFwr6.2211$CW3.205764@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
You can override that with the 'CBLQDA' system parm.

'COBOL QSAM Dyanmic Allocation'.  Added in COBOL II on IBM, if you omit
or otherwise screwup the SElect/DD, the runtime dynamically allocates the
QSAM
file and at STOP RUN/GO BACK, deletes it.  How useful is this?


Many places do NOT allow DISP=MOD because it makes restarts very hard.


Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:OFwr6.2211$CW3.205764@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...
>
> William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> I use OPEN EXTEND even for "OUTPUT" opens on IBM/MF. Whay, you ask?
Because
> I use FILE STATUS, and if you specify OPEN OUTPUT and the file is not
> available (e.g., mistyped the DD name in the JCL), the FILE STATUS comes
> back '00' and COBOL thinks everything is hunky-dory. That is, until EOJ,
> when it turns out your data is not in your newly catalogued dataset.
(Which
> makes sense, since you were writing the wrong DD name!).
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T19:34:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<993nq3$7o7$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <OFwr6.2211$CW3.205764@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <VWbt6.2790$A2.820012@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
Note that this is definitely one time that VM (and even PC's and Unix) are
MUCH more user friendly than MVS or OS/390.

Under VM, if you don't have the CBLQDA option set to "OFF" (so you ARE
running in ANSI conforming mode), it creates a file that *IS* easy to find
and use after your program does a "stop run" or "goback".  Same for Windows
and Unix.

I have been telling IBM for *years* that they *could* have implemented the
ANSI conforming behavior in a USEFUL manner - by simply creating a
*cataloged* dataset with
  DSN = program-name.filename
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Appending to a sequential file

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T04:39:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB58D4F.D3B1F7D2@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<slrn9aqfcm.1at.eh@rabbit.allgaeu.org> <98lof8$mvl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <OFwr6.2211$CW3.205764@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <VWbt6.2790$A2.820012@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <993nq3$7o7$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Note that this is definitely one time that VM (and even PC's and Unix) are
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

I don't know about other MVS or OS/390 shops, but in my shop that kind
of file name would be a problem for the security system.  We use ACF2,
but I suspect it would be equally a problem for RACF.  I asseum the
most likely result would be an immediate security violation attempting
to create an illegal dataset name. 

I think your suggestion might work a little better if there were an
additional option to provide for a system-wide HLQ that could have a
generic security rule applied.  Perhaps something like
HLQ.jobname.stepname.ddname.

We currently have the options set to abend the job if the DD is not
present.  Programmers who are used to OS/VS COBOL seem to accept this
pretty well.
```

---

<a id="t-9f148832dc"></a>
## Thread: alternate index in an entry sequenced data set — 3 msgs

### Re: alternate index in an entry sequenced data set

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T04:48:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB43E0A.F10B0920@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<98vlj6$tv8$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at> <330A8E1C779A8957.924944EF936DBD4A.E2205E1033721A3B@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 
> Are you sure the file is an ESDS file?  Because by definition, an ESDS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the ALTERNATE RECORD KEY clause in your SELECT statement.

It is definitely possible to have alternate indexes over an ESDS base
file.  We do that all the time in CICS, but we use EXEC CICS calls to
browse and read the records by alternate indexes.  The alternate index
is built normally, but instead of a primary key to reference the KSDS
base record it uses the RBA to reference the ESDS base record.

In CICS, writing the ESDS base record causes all alternate indexes to
be updated.

I'm not sure how to code standard batch COBOL verbs to access this. 
My guess is you would treat the alternate index as if it were a KSDS
file, and reading the alternate key would also obtain the ESDS record.

I have maintained IDCAMS JCL that built alternate indexes and paths
over ESDS files. 

Regards,
```

#### ↳ Re: alternate index in an entry sequenced data set

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T02:03:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ab4c1c6_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<98vlj6$tv8$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at> <330A8E1C779A8957.924944EF936DBD4A.E2205E1033721A3B@lp.airnews.net> <3AB43E0A.F10B0920@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Thanks Arnold,

You said exactly what I was going to say. There is a popular misconception
that  VSAM ESDSs cannot be indexed. In fact, it is often (not always) more
efficient to use them than it is to use KSDSs and that is one reason why DB2
defaults to ESDS (or it did the last time I used it on a mainframe...10
years ago now).

To answer the original poster's question, try incuding the ALTERNATE RECORD
KEY  clause in the definition of the ESDS which (if memory serves) is
defined as a standard sequential dataset (I believe you can't use the RBA
from COBOL, but I could be very wrong on this). Not sure, but it should
work.

Pete.

Arnold Trembley wrote in message <3AB43E0A.F10B0920@worldnet.att.net>...

>>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://arnold.trembley.home.att.net/

##### ↳ ↳ Re: alternate index in an entry sequenced data set

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T11:13:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<813892463872EB83.0CFFD672BA9264B6.0A0346E8D0F74B22@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<98vlj6$tv8$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at> <330A8E1C779A8957.924944EF936DBD4A.E2205E1033721A3B@lp.airnews.net> <3AB43E0A.F10B0920@worldnet.att.net> <3ab4c1c6_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
On Mon, 19 Mar 2001 02:03:37 +1200, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> enlightened us:

>Thanks Arnold,
>
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----

Okay, I was confused.  I was ready to make these arguments that Arnold
and Pete were the ones that were confused because it made no logical
sense that an ESDS file, which is defined as NON-INDEXED, could have
an alternate index.  Obviously this restriction has either never been
there and I just never saw it or it is a recent change.  I was
convinced this could be done after reading the following in the latest
IBM Access Method Services manual:

RELATE(entryname[/password]) 
names the alternate index's base cluster. The base cluster is an
entry-sequenced cluster or a key-sequenced cluster to which the
alternate index is to be related. You cannot relate an alternate index
to a reusable cluster or a relative record cluster. 

RELATE, for those who don't know, is a required paramater on the
DEFINE ALTERNATEINDEX command.

I will now go back to driving my 1960 VW Bus, thank you very much.

Regards,



          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I like kids.......... But I don't think I could eat a whole one.


Help Save The Rainforest
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

## Thread: Age Discrimination — 21 msgs

### Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T07:58:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8K9s6.18$yq5.820@client>`

```
Over the years I've experienced sex discrimination and religious discrimination
but not age discrimination - then again I haven't quite hit that point in time
that would apply (maybe - I don't advertise my age and I'm told I don't look
close to it).

I've been refused a promotion due to my sex and in college I had a professor
that deliberately scheduled important exams during important religious
holidays.  I could do nothing about the professor but I did get a different
job.
```

#### ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T01:34:42+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ab4bab3_2@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<8K9s6.18$yq5.820@client> <20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com>`

```

YukonMama wrote in message <20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com>...
>Over the years I've experienced sex discrimination and religious
discrimination
>but not age discrimination - then again I haven't quite hit that point in
time
>that would apply (maybe - I don't advertise my age and I'm told I don't
look
>close to it).
>
>I've been refused a promotion due to my sex and in college I had a
professor
>that deliberately scheduled important exams during important religious
>holidays.  I could do nothing about the professor but I did get a different
>job.

I was quite moved by this. We hear all the time about sex discrimination and
some of us  (well, me, actually...<G>) tend to minimise it as the demented
ravings of the Feminist movement.

Having never done it myself, I have difficulty in accepting that others do
it in any serious way.
Some of the best programmers I've worked with have been females, and in
general, over the years, I believe women have been treated more fairly in IT
than in many other professions.

I have never failed to promote someone on merit, whether they were female or
not.

I HAVE had a quiet discussion to open up the fact that the girl concerned
would be managing some guys who could be "difficult" and offering my
support. Never had any problems. Because promotion is seen to be on merit,
the people in the team realise that the best person for the job got it.

Hearing of first hand experience makes it more real. Reasons can be offered
to excuse sexual discrimination... ("no, it wasn't about sex it was about
those dresses she wears...", or "She was all over me at the Xmas party...so
what's the problem now?"etc... but no such even flimsy excuse can be made
for the Religious discrimination you describe. There is NO good kind of
bigotry, but this is one of the worst.

Was there no appeal you could make to get a private test on a different
date? If this particular Professor was in the habit of doing this, weren't
other students also affected by it?

I felt genuinely sorry when I read your message. Good to see you came
through it all and rose above it.

Pete.



-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T13:23:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3Y7t6.259$045.19916@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<8K9s6.18$yq5.820@client> <20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3ab4bab3_2@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote:
> YukonMama wrote:
> >Over the years I've experienced sex discrimination and religious
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >holidays.  I could do nothing about the professor but I did get a
different
> >job.
>
> I was quite moved by this. We hear all the time about sex discrimination
and
> some of us  (well, me, actually...<G>) tend to minimise it as the demented
> ravings of the Feminist movement.

You can bet it's real!  Unfortunately, it will never be stopped by
passing laws.  People discriminate because of sex, race, religion,
looks, voice quality, height, weight, and thousands of other factors.
Picking out a few such factors and trying to legislate them away is
like a drop in the ocean.  And for certain jobs, some of those factors
might actually be important; such as looks for a model, voice quality
for a radio job, height and weight for a pilot, sex for a clothes
changing room attendant, religion for jobs with religious groups,
etc., etc.  Laws to regulate such things just complicate the situation,
generally creating more problems than they abate.
```

There is an interesting article about women in programming at Computerworld.
COBOL Reportz sent out a link to it in their weekly blurb.  It claims that
41% of women in IT are considering leaving their position because of the
stress.
I have no experience, pro or con on this - all of the female programmers I
have worked with have been extremely competent and hard working.  But I
wonder if the general (mis)perception that Women don't want to work long
hours or handle stress contibutes to a subtle discrimination against them?.
Here is the article link:
http://computerworld.com/cwi/story/0,1199,NAV47_STO58637,00.html

Buddy Ray
-------------------------------

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T03:00:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB6CA15.CF22FAE4@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8K9s6.18$yq5.820@client> <20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3ab4bab3_2@news.newsfeeds.com> <zAot6.6$Ow1.152@client> <996ahj$6s4$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```


Luis Gomez-Almeida wrote:

> Does it say how many men in programming are considering leaving their
> positions because of stress caused by incompetent, ignorant and insuferable
> management?  I am talking about those managers who have no techniccal
> skills, no leadership skills, no administrative skills, and whos people
> skills are nowhere to be seen.  Mostly about 95% of IT management.

So what's new - it was like that 30 years and will be the same in 2030 ! You
should know being in or near our nation's vibrant capital <G>

A little tale - I reported as Systems Manager to the DPM who in turn reported to
the Head of God Knows What (he covered Org & Methods plus EDP). Anyway a new
joker gets that top job - definitely a "No-No" say the head-hunters, but he gets
the job 'cos the Financial Director had worked with him aeons ago at Craven A
(UK cigarette manufacturer).

In comes this ashen faced, Jesuit educated bimbo, (bias - I was educated by
Christian Brothers). His first edict, we mutts in Org and EDP must take a speech
course, so we can 'speak with authority to our seniors in management'. Never
occurred to him that he was the very first one who should have taken the course.
As the Chief Accountant told me some time later, at a conference, he, (bimbo)
pronounced 'Friday and Saturday are the two busiest days in retail". (We didn't
open Sundays in those days). The CA was astounded and told me "I didn't know
that until he told us!".

Anyway take your kicks with a laugh. The course was a three-day delight, but
none of us came out as competitors to Richard Burton or Larry Olivier. Mr.
Casson Jnr the instructor, was absolute fun to listen to and full of amusing
quips. His parents were 'pillars of the English Thea-TAH', Dame Sybil Thorndike
and Hugh Casson.

BTW you can catch Mr. Casson Jnr on film. His one minute of glory was - recall
Neville Shute's 'On the Beach'. When they filmed it Down Under the scene was a
quay, that part of Aussieland still habitable, the rest of the world gonzo
because of the nuclear holocaust. With USN Commander Greg Peck saluting from his
submarine for the last time and Ava Gardner tearfully waving goodbye, young
Casson, dressed as a Sally Ann major, armed with baton, is conducting the local
Sally Ann band in "Abide with Me" <G>
.
Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T08:02:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4sJt6.1$by2.154@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<8K9s6.18$yq5.820@client> <20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3ab4bab3_2@news.newsfeeds.com> <zAot6.6$Ow1.152@client>`

```
"Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com> wrote:
> There is an interesting article about women in programming at
> Computerworld.  COBOL Reportz sent out a link to it in their weekly
> blurb.  It claims that 41% of women in IT are considering leaving
> their position because of the stress.

I have always been pleased that, at least in my experience, there is
less sex bias in IT than most fields.  I don't think I have ever seen
a shop large enough to be statistically significant that had less than
about 1/3 female programmers.  I agree with you, the women programmers
that I have known have been, on the average, just as competent as the
men.  But on average the women, with notable exceptions, do not drive
quite as hard as the men do.  Not that they don't do a good job.  It is
a difference in attitude, not ability, and it isn't drastic.  But one
of the hardest drivers I know is a middle aged woman, who sometimes
works such long hours that it affects her health.  And she doesn't have
to do it, no one pushes her but herself; she doesn't even get overtime.
She actually intimidates some of the men in the shop, with her drive
and ability. :-)

At any rate, anyone who has both male and female children and thinks
that the psychological/emotional differences between male and female
are all environmental is either blind or has the perception of a potato.
Though individuals vary enormously of course, as a group, females tend
not to enjoy the more 'techie' subjects, such as math or science, in
school or at work, as much as males.  There is concern right now in the
medical community, for example, that women are not entering the more
technically demanding and esoteric branches of medicine at nearly the
rate of males, and they are needed and would be welcome there.  Males
and females tend to be intelligent in different ways, and interested in
different things, from the very beginning.  There is vast common ground,
of course, and all individuals vary greatly.  Though males tend to enjoy
more and be better at math than females, some of the best mathematicians
are women.  I was amused at a study several years ago by a rabid feminist
psychologist who studied young school children to prove, once and for
all, that there was no built-in math bias between sexes; it was all
environmental.  After six years, she admitted through her clenched teeth
that yes, there really was a built-in bias, that males did generally
like math better, and were generally better at it than females, and
males generally tended to have better 'spatial perception'. :-)  Females
are just as smart, of course, but they tend to be smart in not quite the
same way as males.  There was another interesting study done on babies
from two or three weeks to two or three months old.  Part of the study
was to put the babies individually in an enclosed space where the only
thing they could see was a video screen.  A pretty, colorful picture was
displayed on the screen, and there was a string attached to the baby's
hand such that every time the baby pulled the string the picture on the
screen changed.  The babies were observed until it was clear that the
baby had learned to pull the string and change the picture.  After a
while, the string was switched off so the baby could no longer change
the picture.  The films of this are very amusing.  Almost invariably,
the female babies would start to cry, but the male babies would get
angry and start jerking at the string with all their might.  Does that
sound familiar to anyone? :-)

We're just different, folks.  Neither is 'better' or 'smarter' than the
other, but males and females are different in many more ways than the
obvious physical ones, and the learned differences are trivial compared
to the built-in ones.  Anyone who has been married for more than a week
and doesn't know this is also blind or has the perception of a potato. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T10:32:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uGKt6.17$mr2.22@client>`
- **References:** `<8K9s6.18$yq5.820@client> <20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3ab4bab3_2@news.newsfeeds.com> <zAot6.6$Ow1.152@client> <4sJt6.1$by2.154@news2.atl>`

Well said Judson.
And I think the wise IS manager takes advantage of the strenghts of all of
his staff.  Some are better at conceptual design, others love to just crank
code but hate debugging, some love the detective work of debugging.  Women
do (as a group and on the average - there are always unique exceptions) have
specific personality traits that make them better at some IS tasks.  Seek
those out and make use of them.

Personally, I think we need more women in Tech Support.  They are (on the
average) better at soothing the savage user and getting to the heart of the
problem.

Buddy Ray
Editor
www.AboutLegacyCoding.com

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T12:35:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99880j$kkf$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8K9s6.18$yq5.820@client> <20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3ab4bab3_2@news.newsfeeds.com> <zAot6.6$Ow1.152@client> <4sJt6.1$by2.154@news2.atl>`

```
An interesting "cultural difference" - when I worked for Micro Focus and was
located in their UK headquarters, I found that their programming staff was
SIGNIFICANTLY less well balanced between the sexes than any shop that I had
worked for in the US.  This was in the early '90's - so it may have changed
by now.

Even when I went to UK versus US user groups, I saw a much smaller
percentage of women programmers in the UK.

When it came to middle (much less senior) programming management, the
numbers/percentage was even MORE noticeable. (I.e.  I think I only knew of
one female middle manager at Micro Focus in Newbury - and none in senior
management).  There were females in OTHER management positions (sales,
quality assurance, etc) - but minimal in programming.

Then, of course, we could also discuss the fact that their programming staff
was so overwhelmingly WHITE that it could have looked like a "KKK" meeting.
On the other hand (and probably related to this) the area of the country
where Micro Focus was located seemed "poorly" integrated to me (at least
when compared to London and other "metropolitan" areas of England).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** ar656@freenet.carleton.ca (Luis Gomez-Almeida)

Up until ten years ago it was common to read help wanted ads in European
newspapers where they specified the age range preferred.  The fact that my
wife went back to school for two years at the age of 43 in order to get a
better job when re-entering the job market, and got a job that pays well
would have been perceived as incredible by people outside North America ten
years ago.

Regards,
Luis

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Age Discrimination

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-23T00:48:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k3xu6.2168$ue1.149454@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<8K9s6.18$yq5.820@client> <20010318025854.24449.00001626@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3ab4bab3_2@news.newsfeeds.com> <zAot6.6$Ow1.152@client> <4sJt6.1$by2.154@news2.atl>`

```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net

> males generally tended to have better 'spatial perception'. :-)

One of the reasons females were much less successful in entering
Colleges of Dentistry than medical schools was that dental schools
administered a 'spatial reasoning' test (under the theory that a
dentist could sometimes not 'see' what he was working on but instead
had to visualize the tooth structure.

Female admission to dental schools achieved parity when dental schools
(for the most part) eliminated these 'spatial reasoning' tests.

Empirically, we notice this sexual difference in spatial perception
when comparing how men and women treat a map: women tend to rotate the
map, orienting it to coincide with the direction they are facing.
These women are then able to translate the map into directions. Men,
on the other hand, tend to rotate the map in their head before
discarding the information as useless.
```

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)

>From: "Peter E. C. Dashwood" dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.

Thank you Pete :)

In answer to the question - yes I could take an alternate test on a different
date.  The alternate test was twice as long as the original, and the date was
always a week before the original.  The reason for this was since I requested a
different date I must know the material and he would need the extra week to
grade the test.  BTW - the subject was Calc 101.  If other students were
affected I wasn't aware of them and he was a tenured professor - nothing you
can do to them short of shooting them.

I'll also add the 'reason' behind the promotion refusal - a woman in the
company left to stay home and have a baby shortly after her promotion to a VP
position.  I was told 'you will just get married, get pregnant and leave just
like so and so did'.  I was engaged at the time. The particular woman
referenced came back to work about 2 years after the baby was born to a lower
position.  The manager who made the decision not to promote me was demoted
about 4 years later - I don't know the reasons behind the demotion, maybe he
tried the same thing with someone else in front of witnesses (there were none
around when he made his remarks to me).
```

<a id="t-6b057d1b2e"></a>
## Thread: COBOl Files v. Databases — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T01:49:29+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ab4c1c4_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <_dvs6.53528$p66.16346451@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3AB29BB5.BF39AF9@home.com> <98ua5o$8jb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <RUJs6.305$f27.62350@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3AB411B3.CA237FAE@melbpc.org.au>`

```

Tim Josling wrote in message <3AB411B3.CA237FAE@melbpc.org.au>...
>A lot of packages use VSAM because they do not want to require a
>DB2 licence. Another reason is that VSAM uses only about 5% as
>much CPU time as DB2 for a traditional transactional application.
>That can add up.


As both VSAM and DB2 are IO bound, and the DB2 physical files are based on
VSAM ESDS, how do you come to measure performance in CPU time, and where do
you arrive at a figure of 5%? If you had said 5% of EXCPs it would at least
have a ring of truth to it, (I'd still query it because there is no such
thing as a "traditional transactional application") but what you say above
smacks of "urban myth" ("Everybody knows....") Can you quote a source for
this figure?

If you can't, will you withdraw your obviously biased comment? (Or shall I
post some equally fatuous biased comments in defense of DBs...<G>?)

Pete.



-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T05:02:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB592D0.D11ECAB8@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <_dvs6.53528$p66.16346451@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3AB29BB5.BF39AF9@home.com> <98ua5o$8jb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <RUJs6.305$f27.62350@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3AB411B3.CA237FAE@melbpc.org.au> <3ab4c1c4_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Tim Josling wrote in message <3AB411B3.CA237FAE@melbpc.org.au>...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >That can add up.

If I had a relatively small mainframe application to develop, I would
probably want to go with VSAM first, simply because I am more familiar
with it, and I wouldn't need a lot of help from the DBA's.  It's nice
not to have to mess with DB/2 binds, and to always have full control
over your data without going to another department.  That may simply
reflect the organization structure where I am.

If I had a system that required a lot of alternate indexes, then I
think DB/2 would probably be a better solution.  I don't think
alternate indexes are very easy to maintain in VSAM.  If I were
working with a manufacturing inventory system, bills of material, MRP,
then some kind of database becomes almost mandatory just to manage all
the relationships.

> 
> As both VSAM and DB2 are IO bound, and the DB2 physical files are based on
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Ten years ago you could probably say that VSAM was faster than DB/2. 
Not now.  I'm a believer.  DocDwarf will probably remember something I
posted here a few months ago.  We converted a CICS application with
multiple alternate indexes over base ESDS VSAM to DB/2, and got a big
increase in performance.

Still, for the typical programmer, if there's no need for alternate
indexes, I think VSAM is easier to set up.

There's an awful lot of basic backoffice batch processing that works
perfectly well with sequential files and sorts.
```

## Thread: Fujitsu 6.1 Install — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu 6.1 Install

- **From:** Rob Lec <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T14:59:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB4CD5F.362C0FE7@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<98s835$5tn0$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Thanks for the heads-up.  I too have postponed installing the 6.1
upgrade for fear of unforseen hassles, at least until I'm done with some
current projects (as if that ever happens!).  Anyway, I saved your msg
for reference in case anything like this happens to me.

By the way, you may want to check into a couple things.  See if the
files you were "missing" are actually somewhere else on your system. 
Fujitsu stores a lot of excess baggage in far-off folders.  Their
install also can do severe damage to your autoexec.bat file (exceeding
the DOS length limit, unless you're using the shell command or running
NT).  
Anyway, I did a couple things which I *know* will complicate the
install.  1) I have copied several of the frequently used libraries
(f3bicblr, kernel32) into my Projects folder, which by the way is NOT in
with the obscure Fuji folders, and 2) modified my DOS path to include
Fuji's LIB folder so when I test apps outside of the IDE they can find
all the DLL's.

Just thought you might want to check and see if those "can't find"
messages merely meant the necessary files just weren't where the install
expected them to be.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu 6.1 Install

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T23:35:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<994256$7pqs$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

different version than the original.  Perhaps v 6.1 installed

DLL and LIB files.  The mix of v5 and v6 files you have is bad.  I
would blast the whole thing, then re-install v6.1 and see what
happens.  When it doesn't work, call Fujitsu support.

#### ↳ Re: Writing Output to Multiple Files

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T20:55:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99400s$4c4$1@news.chatlink.com>`
- **References:** `<TTCs6.2983$Im6.408807@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <990oqo$5sj$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <bv4t6.6950$227.760910@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
There is a callable dynamic allocation assembler routine available on the
www.mvshelp.com bulletin board.  Got to the web-site, and perform a search on
DYNALLOC.  The assembler code is posted, as well as a COBOL copybook with which
to call it from COBOL.  I think it should still be there, but mvshelp has just
transferred to another ISP so it might not - worth checking out anyway though.

What you want can be accomplished via dynamic allocation.  The program flow
should look something like this:

Build new dataset / member name (ie. my.dataset.name1).
Call DYNALLOC program to allocate the new dataset / member.
open COBOL SELECT file for output.
write JCL to file.
Close COBOL SELECT file.
Call DYNALLOC program to unallocate the dataset / member.

repeat process with next dataset name (ie. my.dataset.name2).
...
repeat process with next dataset name (ie. my.dataset.name99).

Hope it helps!

Todd Lucas
E-Mail:  Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net
Web:   http://www.c-cubed.net


GER:-) <auntpeachy@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:bv4t6.6950$227.760910@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net...
>
```

Hi Leo,

It may have changed in later releases, but my understanding of ESDS files 
was that aix(s) are supported in CICS but not batch. Same goes for RBA ref-
erencing. You can read, write(append), rewrite, but not insert or delete
recs. I do not know if you can use an ESDS file created for CICS use in 
batch so long as you don't mess with the AIX(s). Maybe someone with main-
frame access can test this and let us know. 

Hope this helps, Jack.

>
>On Sat, 17 Mar 2001 13:33:27 +0100, "Leo Toifl" <leo.toifl@utanet.at>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>Steve
```

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T17:32:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
It is not batch per-se' that's the problem. It is that COBOL does
not support RBA access to the AIX. I've done it in batch with
an assembler program.

John H Sleight Jr wrote:
> 
> Hi Leo,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hope this helps, Jack.

There was one application that I maintained that was batch, and used
alternate indexes over an ESDS cluster.  But in batch COBOL it was
accessed through an assembler subprogram.  I think you could read this
data with native COBOL verbs, but I haven't tried it.  That
application never updated the file, it was always built with IDCAMS
steps.  The alternate indexes were used to search it.

In CICS we used alternate indexes over ESDS for transaction data that
was always added to, but we needed online inquiry by various alternate
keys.  For this particular application we had a performance concern
about capturing the data in real time as quickly as possible.  
```

```
Hi Arnold,

You're right. I should have said, "AIX(s) are supported in CICS but not batch
COBOL". In fact, they're not supported in COBOL, period. BTW, I did some further
research. The COBOL for OS/390 & VM (I think it's their latest & greatest
version) Programming Guide (SC26-9049-05, Sect. 1.9.1) in a table comparing 3 of
the 4 VSAM file types, states that ESDS w/AIX is not supported in COBOL.

### Re: alternate index in an entry sequenced data set

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T19:33:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0it8btsb42sh0e9mah3jvc2vp827n418jq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<98vlj6$tv8$1@rohrpostix.uta4you.at> <330A8E1C779A8957.924944EF936DBD4A.E2205E1033721A3B@lp.airnews.net>`

```
On Sat, 17 Mar 2001 14:05:22 -0500, SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:


>Are you sure the file is an ESDS file?  Because by definition, an ESDS
>file does not have an index 

true

>and therefore could not possibly have 3 alternate indicies.

non sequitur, and false

A VSAM ESDS does indeed allow an alternate index.  You define the ESDS file, and specify
in the definition of the alternate index where the key in the ESDS record is located. This
key is an alternate key (possibly non-unique).  And what is the primary key?  The RBA.
VSAM maintains the relationship between alternate key and RBA in the alternate Index/path

## Thread: COBOl Files v. Databases — 16 this month / 17 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** "charles" <fjlk@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-18T22:14:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lqat6.117205$t67.1708087@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com>`

```
I work for a community college and we have nothing but VSAM files on our IBM
Mainframe.  Some of our VSAM files are around 150,000 records or so.  In the
big scheme of things that isn much compared to a large corporation.

For many people that use smaller mainframe's it can be a major cost to
migrate to a database system.  Besides just changing the file structure you
would have to change hundreds of programs.  Also keep in mind that would
require changing about 200 online CICS programs all at once in our case.  In
other words, it would require a complete system redevelopment.  This would
be no easy task and would probably cost at least a few hundred thousand
dollars if not a million.  We priced possible replacement for a total system
replacement from people like People Soft and 1 million dollars was among the
bids.  We have just 3 mainframe programmers.  No job schedulers or system
developers or anything else.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** "Calin Macrinici" <cmac@pathcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T18:43:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lrst6.488753$JT5.14056018@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <Lqat6.117205$t67.1708087@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com>`

```
I'm not sure if I should comment in here, as I've been working on PCs for
the last 7-8 years (although before that I programmed on a PDP-11 for about
the same amount of time), but here's my two cents anyway (like you can tell
me to shut up!)

We used ISAM files for a long period of time, mostly in DOS environments and
everything was peachy (some users had problems, some crashes would screw-up
your data, mostly fixable with a rebuild).  Never even wanted to check DBs
out because "it wasn't worth it", although now, looking back it probably was
because they weren't COBOL enough!  (does that makes sense?)

Then Windows came along, and with it our perennial printing problems.  We
first tried to use windows APIs and graphical printing (for accounting
reports!!!!!!!!!), and it was pretty much OK, until more and more people
asked about the nice Crystal reports.

So we had to byte the bullet and start using Crystal.  No easy feat here,
but we did it.  Well guess what?  Crystal is much better at handling
databases (for one thing if you call their tech support and tell them you do
anything with COBOL they kind of laugh at you), MicroFocus accepts btrieve
files using the same IO techniques (i.e. COBOL syntax) so of we went.

Then we started to introduce more and more OO in our code.  If the OO COBOL
starts making any headways and the vendors don't change their approach
(MicroFocus allows SQL statements embedded in their COBOL programs; also web
based data access, CGI, etc., but file IO in an OO program is not made any
easier), me thinks that ISAM / COBOL files are dead (let me spell that: d e
a d!)

Why, you're asking?  Well for one thing accessing a file from a COBOL
class/object is difficult!  You cannot use FC/FD in the instance (Jimmy, you
know too well what I'm talking about), so you're stuck with hoop-jumping at
its best!  COBOL programmers actually exchange ideas as how to access data
from OO code!

So, now we're btrieve, next comes (probably) SQL, although I would have
never thought...

Whoa, that was way longer than I intended, so I'll leave it here.

Again, I apologize for barging w/o any mainframe info, but anyway...


Calin, TORONTO
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T12:37:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB66016.42EC07CF@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <Lqat6.117205$t67.1708087@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com> <lrst6.488753$JT5.14056018@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Databases put the emphasis of the IS shop where it belongs, with the data.  Let
the IS shop handle issues of the data of record, security, data integrity, data
accuracy, data currency, and access within the database instead of within
programs.

If this is done, we can use tools of choice for various applications.  As
hardware gets cheaper, adding an extra layer becomes more cost effective.   This
layer functions in a similar way as TCP layers do - applications do not need to
have an intimate detail knowledge, and the IS shop can make changes which are
transparent to the applications.

Functions which need to be centralized can be centralized, while the users can
easily have control on their own side - do they want the data retrieved into a
spread sheet?  Printed in a report?  Doesn't matter to the IS shop.

If a DBA decides to add a key to a file, the applications don't care, they just
run faster (if the DBA was correct).

The biggest costs are opportunity cost in not having data available, and
maintenance.  Then comes design and programming, and finally operating and
hardware.  Concentrating on getting the data correct is cost effective.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-21T02:52:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB819AA.FE1FC1D1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <Lqat6.117205$t67.1708087@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com> <lrst6.488753$JT5.14056018@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

>
> Why, you're asking?  Well for one thing accessing a file from a COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Well things do move on........<G>.  As you've got beyond COBOL files, you are
probably no longer interested - but if you are I can now give you file classes
for relative, sequential, line sequential. There's a model for indexed from Pete
Dashwood, which I haven't yet pursued. But for the first three, using just three
separate file classes, you can throw 5,000 + record formats at the file class
and the only thing the file class needs to know is the full pathname and record
size varying ...... If you have a business program that needs say 10
sequentials, you (invoke) load the one source and have 10 instances.

I'm hoping I can take a parallel approach using DB, keeping existing Business
Logic,  the intermediary DBI - the only changes being required at the file class
level, where for the moment at least, I would store SQL syntax..
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T21:53:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB67FC6.BBDD7DD7@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <Lqat6.117205$t67.1708087@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Specifically, you use 'flat-files' - in either the support/rejection of DBs have
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Well actually I use both flat files and Databases (since I load some
large flat files into databases).  I also wrote stored procedures,
tested on a flat file and then altered into embedded SQL calls and
finally direct stored procedures - as the records extracted needed to
jump through some elaborate hoops.

I do not see a need for an either or with COBOL and DB's (or rather flat
files and relational files) each have a purpose and need.

The DB's I designed (& load or cause to be loaded with production job
streams) are used for ad-hoc decision support requests.  Other
applications may need to add or update large volumes of data with
complex matching or complex selection, not a strong area for a database
- the data from these files (in general) produces fixed reports in fixed
formats.

It is a matter of the best application to meet the specific need in a
timely and cost effective fashion

		Susan A
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T19:11:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<996722$74dc$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <Lqat6.117205$t67.1708087@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com> <3AB67FC6.BBDD7DD7@boeing.com>`

```
"Susan A" wrote:

> It is a matter of the best application to meet the specific need in a
timely and cost effective fashion.

I'd have to agree with Susan on this one.  If I had the time, and the
knowledge, I'd test out the SQL capabilities of using the NX-5600 as an SQL
server, since we do get "occasional" requests to look up a specific
account's transactions for a particular day.  An ideal "query" application,
I must say.  The flat file (I use this term loosely) used for check capture
needs to stay more or less in a sequential mode, even though it is actually
defined as a "random access" file in the select statement.  If you ever
notice all the endorsement stuff on the back/front of your checks, the
document identification number (DIN) is how most any bank researches
questions on your account. This is exactly how the items passed through the
sorter, and this is how they were captured into the file for posting to your
account.  We can get the account information using a COBOL program to access
the captured file, but I have a hunch that the SQL approach would be
quicker.  Bottom line, though, is that we just don't get all that many
requests to do this.

However, the stats that I was talking about in the Access DB, are basically
volume/reject/operator stats.  The software we run puts out a total record
for every unit of work we process (about every 3000 checks, give or take),
so rather than read through the whole database, we just grab the total
record (randomly on the NX-5600), then move the content into the database,
using the Fujitsu COBOL PC program.  I don't want to go into a lot of
detail, but the way any rejected items are tied back to the original
captured items might be beyond any SQL structure at this stage of the game.
For us, the above approach is both timely and cost effective.

As Susan says, depends on what you need to do with the data.

Being the age that I am, I am still in awe that we can read checks at
upwards of 2000 checks per minute, endorse them, microfilm them, image them,
pocket them and capture them to the "flat file" without going nuclear.  But
I love that fact that we can use both PC's and the mainframe as a truly
"open" platform.  We won't even get into the FTP side of it!  That's even
more fun.  Can you tell that I enjoy my work???

Denny
```

In article <3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com>, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
writes:

>
>Specifically, you use 'flat-files' - in either the support/rejection of DBs
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

I'll chime in here. Probably out of context and on the wrong tangent.
In my current work environment, we have a group that works to maintain
optimum performance of our Databases thru defining alternate 'sets/tables'
into the main data structure.  Even with the best 'tuned' access 'set', we have

found that our "batch" processing works best with an UNLOAD to flat file at
the beginning, do all our work on the flat files, and then RESTORE from the
flat files back to the database.   This is processing a volume of approximately
60 institutions with 1.5M accounts.  As we have somewhere around 8 such 
databases on 8 machines (64 total) to process each night,  this has proven
to make the difference of making delivery 'on time'.

Hope that this set of comments is not too far off-topic.  It does seem to fit
you discussion, in my mind.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Files v. Databases

- **From:** Rob Lec <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>

Sff5ky wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> writes:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> flat files back to the database. 

Likewise, I have in many cases found it much more efficient to extract a
chunk of data from the system stores and process it locally using native
ISAM files.  This not only provides the convenience of creating any
necessary indexes (which may not exist within the database, or even be
worthwhile maintaining once the update process has completed), but also
has several additional benefits such as a substantial reduction in
network traffic and reduced processing overhead, and also allows
concurrent maintainance or backup of the master database.

-------------------------------------------------
(To reply by email change dash to dot in address)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Files v. Databases

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-22T14:06:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FFnu6.105760$p66.30092768@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com> <20010319233114.11923.00000681@nso-fq.aol.com> <3AB94B56.FBA0E818@cinci-rr.com> <3AB9697A.7E890D26@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message news:3AB9697A.7E890D26@home.com...
> 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > >

<< SNIP of an interesting performance tuning tip >>

> > worthwhile maintaining........
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> what you consider most effective .......

Jimmy, you're getting mixed up in the jargon. While it is possible to seek out every "field", that doesn't mean there is an index that will get you to that field efficiently. Keys and indices in tables work pretty much as they do in an indexed file. The DB manager keeps track of some statistics which it uses to find it's way to the piece of data as efficiently as possible. Sometimes the DB is forced to do an exhaustive scan (think of it as doing a READ NEXT) of the table to find the row(s) you are after. 

- Karl

> 
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Files v. Databases

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-22T16:20:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LDpu6.178$dx2.88153@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com> <20010319233114.11923.00000681@nso-fq.aol.com> <3AB94B56.FBA0E818@cinci-rr.com> <3AB9697A.7E890D26@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3AB9697A.7E890D26@home.com...
> Whoa!. Hold it right there and I'm ABSOLUTELY no DB expert. But as I
understand if
> EVERY field in a DB is in effect 'keyed'  - specifically if using SQL.

Well, no, that's not quite true.

It just 'seems' that each field (column) is keyed. The whole idea behind SQL
language was to create a method of data access independent of the specifics
of the datamanager - that is, the application can use the same commands if
the application is using Oracle, DB/2, dBase, Access or any other
SQL89/92/99 compliant datamanager."How" the datamanager does it is why some
databases sell more than others.

An SQL statement tells the datamanager to select records based on the value
of certain columns (fields). If those columns are defined as an index, the
datamanager uses (well, *should use*) that index for efficiency. If that
column is not defined as an index, the datamanager just reads everything and
selects qualifying records.

MCM
```
