###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Files v. Databases

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jeff Baynard <jeffbaynard@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-31T02:08:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B6EAA661.4D9%jeffbaynard@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB645D8.BE209EFC@home.com> <20010319233114.11923.00000681@nso-fq.aol.com> <3AB94B56.FBA0E818@cinci-rr.com> <3AB9697A.7E890D26@home.com> <LDpu6.178$dx2.88153@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
No, there are explicit keys on records in every database I've seen.  You
could technically create an index on each column of a record though.

In Informix SQL, I can create and drop indices on the fly, on whatever
columns I want, on whatever tables I want.  That allows me to tune the SQL
procedure, and I have found this to really improve performance in certain
situations (large tables 1 million rows+).  It's all about overhead vs
latency...  Should you take the time to do some work now, at a set amount of
time, in order to maybe save time later.

I have seen routines not complete after 12 hours of processing, then after
putting the correct indices on, complete in 20 minutes.  SQL, Informix at
least, in really pretty dumb if you use it wrong.

I would imagine you can do indices in Oracle SQL+ also.

Jeff

in article LDpu6.178$dx2.88153@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net, Michael Mattias at
michael.mattias@gte.net wrote on 3/22/01 8:20 AM:

…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-03-24T15:57:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3abcc37e.299783387@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
I have a monthly process where I do the same thing.  When they were
trying to do the process with a Query they gave up after it ran for 30
hours.  It had to do subqueries for each row returned from the main
query.  It was creating a file of the last 6 months activity for any
account that had over 2000  any time in the last 6 months.  The query
was not hard to design, but even with re-indexing the table it was too
slow.  I extract the raw data to an indexed file and create the
resulting spreadsheet.  My process takes about 30 minutes - including
the extract.

If you learn SQL, then you can do DB2.  SQL is not that hard if you
understand how data is connected in a database.  It takes a little practice
though.

Many systems have middlewear so UNIX is a always a pretty good idea.

The squeaky wheel gets the oil, so start learning and get the best job you
can.

Jeff

That should read - If you can do DB2 you can do SQl - not the other
way round. There is a helluvalot of application programmers time spent
working with things like BMC utilities, table optimisation, etc, beyond
basic SQL.

My current project is about 800 hours to migrate a table from a
Non-Large tablespace to a Large Tablespace. Without this change, our
BMC load & Reorg jobs bomb - having already used up our 64Gb. And
without using BMC LoadPlus, we'd need 31 hours a day, to maintain our
1/2 billion row table.

SQL is useful, especially if your DBA's have created lots of stored
procedures
for you, and if they're going to do all the table maintenance. Otherwise,
it's vital
for the performance of any IMS, JDBC, DB2-Connect process using the data,
for you to understand the basic nature of the table and it's environment.

<a id="t-94e44dbca2"></a>
## Thread: COBOl Files v. Databases — 6 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T00:10:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99448d$3og2$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <98vdqk$c7n@netnews.hinet.net>`

```
Jason and Jimmy, Calgary AB

<snip from Jason>
Finally, for the portability of cobol programs, I  think each cobol vendor
should provide utility for converting their data files to  ascii files.
Ascii files means greater freedom even converting to DB.
I have converted my old MF cobol files to FJ cobol by writing programs
do the work.
<unsnip>

Although this group is predominately IBM oriented, I work for a major
Midwest bank that processes between 600,000 - 900,000 checks a day on a
Unisys NX-5600 open platform system.  We have an PC MS Access database for
our statistics, so that all the people that "number crunch" can  retrieve
them from the Access DB.  How do we feed the database?  Using Fujitsu Cobol,
we can actually map a Network drive to the NX-5600, and read the necessary
numbers from a flat sequential Mainframe file that is created as a byproduct
of the huge random access file of those 600,000-900,000 items created by 3
check sorters.  A portion of the NX-5600 operating system named NX-Services
handles the EBCDIC to ASCII conversion automatically.  And the Fujitsu
program reads the file exactly as defined on the mainframe as far as record
size, blocksize, filename, etc.

Also, this system is capable of being an SQL server on it's own, but there
simply is no need to create a database from items that come in directly from
these sorters.  We need to be able to see a transaction as it was
"captured", i.e. deposit of a check or two, maybe a utility and a loan
payment, etc.  To set up a database for this type on environment would not
be needed.  Plus, has anyone ever attempted to find info in a database this
size?  It certainly can be done, but in my opinion, it isn't pretty and it
certainly isn't speedy.  In this particular case, Mainframe COBOL file
structure rules!!!

Denny
```

Denny,

Good ! I'm glad one of our 'banking boys' jumped in because of the obvious
volumes you handle.

Certainly there is a distinct difference between front-end input as you describe
(MICR for banks,  OCR for utilities(electrIcity, gas etc.), and from my own
experience, (design, not programming), using OCR to input payroll; add to that
list the front-end for POS systems.- set against that  the approach you might
take with a truly interactive system - which is mostly what new PC applications
are about.

POS crosses the line here. For the big retailers it's the front end approach
similar to what you are describing. In the case of the Mom and Pop store using a
PC as POS - then arguably having said, 'OK print sales slip' the transaction
could directly enter a DB system. To illustrate this point - we've gone private
on liquor stores here. The word 'private' is questionable since the provincial
government still does warehousing and controls distribution.

Now many of these small retailers are strapped for cash. Around a 'holiday'
period, they can't wait for the traditional approach of producing a sales
summary at the end of the day. Given a system which 'updates immediately' they
would see a benefit from an application which allows them to query sales at
11:45, 15.00 17.30 or whatever - and believe me they'll dash out the door to
pick up a couple of cases of whiskey, based on the cash currently sat in the
till.

I was curious about your comments about query the 'falt-file/DB' for
transactions, specifically where you have zillions of transactions, and this was
something mulling in the back of my mind.

Flat files - as a generalization postal/zip code is not usually considered a
'keyed' field.
(It is when part of your application is geared to customized mailing lists).It
makes sense to limit Alternate keys. Now the DB 'argument(?)' would be that
every field is 'keyed' and use SQL to get at a specific feature.

So taking the two, flat files or DB, and with LARGE volumes, do they come out
equal :-

(a) Flat files
- could add an Alternate Key if it becomes apparent this is going to be a
regular feature, or
- Sequentially read Customer/Consumer file picking up on postal/zip value, to
produce a specific listing.

(b) DBs
- use SQL syntax to 'search', (I'm not into SQL yet so I don't have the buzz
phrase on my lips) where the postal/zip fits the criteria.

Now taking those two options and a LARGE file - will one be more effective than
the other ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```

> regular feature, or
> - Sequentially read Customer/Consumer file picking up on postal/zip value, to

It still seems odd to me to see someone mentioning a keyed file as being flat.

One old file system which was very efficient, but which seems to have disappeared
was relative files.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T20:12:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yKtt6.82752$p66.23724119@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <98vdqk$c7n@netnews.hinet.net> <99448d$3og2$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <3AB65D80.5582A1A@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message news:3AB65D80.5582A1A@home.com...

<<SNIP>> 

> So taking the two, flat files or DB, and with LARGE volumes, do they come out
> equal :-
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It depends... :o). If you are processing the records sequentially, and all records will be accessed, then it's a close race between DB and indexed files. Sorted sequential files win that race because they don't have any overhead doing index lookup. In a fair comparison, sequential and indexed file processing and would be slowed a bit by checkpoint restart,  rollback recovery, and optional journalling that are usually handled by the database manager.

However, if you want to know all the unique postal codes in the database, or those postal codes that represent a certain minimum value of sales, or some such thing, then the SQL query will be easier to write/maintain, and there's a good chance that the DB will be able to retrieve the information faster, all things being equal. The DB is able to optimize the query based on all indices that may be referenced by the query, rather than having to sequentially process all those duplicate records looking for the ones of interest.


Karl
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T21:22:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **Date:** 2001-03-20T22:37:16+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-8DB58A.22371620032001@news.m.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3AB260A2.A708A555@home.com> <98vdqk$c7n@netnews.hinet.net> <99448d$3og2$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <3AB65D80.5582A1A@home.com> <yKtt6.82752$p66.23724119@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
In article <yKtt6.82752$p66.23724119@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>, "Oscar T. 
Grouch" <dustbin@home.com> wrote:

> It depends... :o). If you are processing the records sequentially, and 
> all records will be accessed, then it's a close race between DB and 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Ok if we are talking DB2 on MF, then you have to include the cost of the 
SORT before you run your cobol program. In which case you might just be 
supprised how quick that DB2 DB can perform. DB2 will sort the keys for 
you so that you can access the records "sequentially". It will then 
pre-load the data pages into memory such that the program will 
experience minimum I/O delays for the access.
```

## Thread: COBOL World 2001 - Keynote speaker — 7 this month / 10 total

### COBOL World 2001 - Keynote speaker

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T01:57:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<994e24$4rh$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those who don't get the "weekly" notification of what's new at
  www.cobolreport.com

the following may be of interest:

"Former Vice President Al Gore to deliver Cobol World 2001 Keynote

Great news - We are thrilled to announce that former Vice President Al Gore
will deliver the keynote address at Cobol World 2001. Mr. Gore gets hundreds
of requests to speak at various engagements and we are proud that he
personally chose to speak at Cobol World 2001. We will be making a public
announcement within the next 3 to 4 weeks.

A word about "Inventing the Internet" thing: Whether you are a Republican,
Democrat, or other Al Gore is one of the most knowledge nationally elected
leaders when it comes to understanding, funding, and promoting information
technology.

No less an authority that Vint Cerf - who is considered the father of the
Internet - states, "The Vice President was among the first of the members of
Congress to recognize the importance of the Internet. optical networks, high
performance computing, that led to programs such as the National Research
and Education Network program, the Next Generation Internet program and so
on." "[H]e'd be quite correct to say, 'I helped create the Internet' -
because of his work to provide an environment of support for research,
technology transfer and ecommerce initiatives so fundamental to the Internet
today.

I think the Vice President is very deserving of credit for his active
support for the Internet and the businesses that depend upon it daily."

Vint Cerf was tapped earlier this year to chair the Internet Corporation for
Assigned Names and Numbers (ICANN) and cleanup the mess left by outgoing
chair Esther Dyson. Cerf is also a MCI WorldCom vice president.
http://www.worldcom.com/about_the_company/cerfs_up/internet_history/q_and_a.
phtml#question_11

(go to www.cobolreport.com for more information on the COBOL World 2001
conference)
```

Group,

I guess you all misunderstood my point.

I have no problem and encourage nationally known figures speaking on COBOL's
behalf.

It just seems the not about his 'inventing the internet' seems to be an
attempt to promote him as a candidate
and not as a known figure, former V.P., Senator and Congressman.

As for George Bush Sr., why not point out that Senator Gore was one of the
Senator's who rose taxes under
the now-famous Bush administration's 'no new taxes' regimes. Bush did not
enact the taxes himself,
Congress (house and senate) had to pass the bills.

In short, I am just fed up with all of the technology magazines, music
magazines and TV/cable stations pandering to
the 'left' with free publicity disguised as news.

- **References:** `<994e24$4rh$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <Pkqt6.5773$9J5.463631@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3AB64811.7A950946@brazee.net> <3AB69CD1.736B7C9F@home.com> <o_At6.6745$M%2.531264@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

Bill wrote:

> In short, I am just fed up with all of the technology magazines, music
> magazines and TV/cable stations pandering to
> the 'left' with free publicity disguised as news.

Me too, but the "I invented the internet" publicity was making fun of Gore.  And
it was done by the media.

The left know how to give sound bytes to a receptive media much better than the
right seem to be able to do.  This is natural since stories are a lot more about
incidents which "need someone to do something about".  When conservatism is
about the status quo, it is against good stories.  (Now the far right can be as
radical as the far left, but that's a different topic).   At any rate, Gore's
hyperbole was a good story which probably cost him enough votes to cost him the
election.  And his speaking at the CoBOL is a story which is good for CoBOL
users.   Where stories effect reality we need to use them for our own purposes.
```

It is a shame that the people who were in on the ground floor of
developing the Internet and had the most to do with Arpanet becoming
the Internet are virtually unknown today.  Vint Cerf being just one of
those people.  In my opinion he and the others who worked with him
should be as famous and wealthier than Bill Gates.

Did you know the idea for the Internet came about as a strategy for
how  U.S. authorities could communicate after a nuclear war.  The
basic idea of the internet was put forth by a Rand Corp. staffer, Paul
Baran.  There is a short, excellent (though lacking in names) history
about the formation of the internet at: 

http://w3.aces.uiuc.edu/AIM/scale/nethistory.html

and another, more exhaustive, at:

http://www.dei.isep.ipp.pt/docs/arpa.html 


And don't forget Dr. Robert E. Kahn.

Regards,

- **References:** `<994pns$3gm$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <996fd8$lfo@netnews.hinet.net>`

```
But what about NetExpress ?

I thought it was possible to generate code that could be distributed without
runtime costs but a sales girl Laura Justice (nice name) said that runtime
licences were required at �70 per seat. One client has a network of 25,000
workstations which all could in theory access my software, Ouch !

NetExpress is by far the better development environment but the run-time
licensing would force me to go with Fujitsu.

"sandman" <vyjssm@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:996fd8$lfo@netnews.hinet.net...
> I use FJ.
> FJ runtime is free and I don't care how much NetExpress charges.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Run time licence

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T07:37:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<997mig$inm$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<994pns$3gm$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <996fd8$lfo@netnews.hinet.net> <996ukp$m23$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
A few things that I think *might* be relevant (but I can't swear to any of
them)

1) Do these applications run off a "common server" - or does each of the
25,000 "users" have the entire application on their own machines.  If it is
the former, I believe (but won't swear to it) that you would only need the
license for the server where the run-time actually "resides".

2) I am quite (not exactly 100%) certain that MERANT sells run-time licenses
in "bulk" so the cost of 25,000 run-time licenses is *not* 25,000 times the
cost of one.  You might want to check with your sales rep again to find out
what "bulk" rates they charge.

3) Older versions of MERANT (then Micro Focus) products *did* provide a
"bundling" technique that allowed you to create applications that did *not*
need for you to distribute a run-time to the target machine.  Whether
actually RUNNING these applications on other machines did or did not
THEORETICALLY involve a "run-time" fee was (and remains) a matter of
"interpretation" and also varied from country to country (as I recall).
```

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

No. Using the Net Express runtime like this still requires payment per

>2) I am quite (not exactly 100%) certain that MERANT sells run-time licenses

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Run time licence

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-21T06:38:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB84C75.1DED7B1A@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<994pns$3gm$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <996fd8$lfo@netnews.hinet.net> <996ukp$m23$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <997mig$inm$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> 3) Older versions of MERANT (then Micro Focus) products *did* provide a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> "interpretation" and also varied from country to country (as I recall).

It wasn't a matter of interpretation at all.  There was a set of rules
in the manual and in the developers licence.  If you built .EXEs and
.DLLs and did not use certain facilities (referred to as the OSX) the
programs could be distributed, including specified run-time components,
without requiring a licence fee.

Of course the MF sales people may have claimed that this manual, the
paragraphs in the licence, and/or the run-time components did not exist,
but waddatheyknow about anything ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Run time licence

- **From:** "Calin Macrinici" <cmac@pathcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T18:26:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PhNt6.417894$f36.13896586@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<994pns$3gm$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <996fd8$lfo@netnews.hinet.net> <996ukp$m23$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Based on their documentation (which I could attach, but the document is
copyrighted, so I don't know), MF (NetExpress) only charges for:
- remote animation support;
- CCI (Common Communications Interface) support;
- AAI suport;
- fileshare server;
- client/server bindings.

Everything else is free.

Don't know about Fujitsu.

Still, the best way to go is to take with a sales rep from either company.

Cheers,
Calin, TORONTO
```

in the email by Calin - CCI, AAI, etc.).  This is stated clearly in the
"license.txt" file that is shipped with the original v3.0 product.

NetExpress v3.1 licensing was changed to require the RTS be purchased if
distributed, which is why I have never upgraded to version 3.1!  :-)

TL

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Run time licence

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-21T14:34:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IZ2u6.98071$p66.27858736@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<994pns$3gm$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <996fd8$lfo@netnews.hinet.net> <996ukp$m23$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <PhNt6.417894$f36.13896586@news20.bellglobal.com> <998ip7$70t$1@news.chatlink.com> <1fRt6.419990$f36.13918188@news20.bellglobal.com> <9990bn$ns1$1@news.chatlink.com> <QT1u6.72$2g6.23648@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Seeing as I manage customer support for a software vendor, I feel obliged to comment on this. 

My company supports versions of our products dating back about 20 years. Some of those versions have bugs that were fixed in subsequent releases. Some of those versions were written a decade or more before the operating system that some client is trying to run them on. Try juggling 8 or more different versions of your product on 10 or more different releases of a given operating system, with 5 or more releases of any particular operating system and you quickly realize that the information you're expecting the support people to keep track of grows exponentially. By the time you add in any other third-party tools like compilers, databases, and version control systems, in all their myriad releases it's miracle that you can get an answer at all. 

When the support staff knows that the problem you are reporting has been fixed, and especially when no workaround exists, why are they wrong to suggest upgrading. We fixed the problem. You chose not to install the fix. Who's the one responsible for failing to resolve the problem?

I don't work for MERANT but I can speculate how the support group feels about the licensing changes. They've got an improved product, that installs pretty much painlessly over earlier installations. There is every technical reason to upgrade, but some bonehead strategic decision sets up a huge barrier to migrating. The support staff find themselves between a rock and a hard place watching customer satisfaction plummet and support revenues fall as a consequence of a decision that's supposed to make the corporation more profitable.

Karl




"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:QT1u6.72$2g6.23648@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net...
> Lucas, Todd <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net> wrote in message
> news:9990bn$ns1$1@news.chatlink.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Run time licence

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-21T09:30:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99am7g$loo$1@news.chatlink.com>`
- **References:** `<994pns$3gm$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <996fd8$lfo@netnews.hinet.net> <996ukp$m23$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <PhNt6.417894$f36.13896586@news20.bellglobal.com> <998ip7$70t$1@news.chatlink.com> <1fRt6.419990$f36.13918188@news20.bellglobal.com> <9990bn$ns1$1@news.chatlink.com> <QT1u6.72$2g6.23648@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <IZ2u6.98071$p66.27858736@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
I didn't mean to imply that ALL company customer support is bad.

What I meant to stress was that by upgrading to version 3.1 of NetExpress is
foolish because of the licensing differences with version 3.0!  For instance,
let's say that I am utilizing your software (v3.0 let's say) to build a product
for which I market and sell to customers.  The Run Time System (RTS) for your
product is free to distribute with the application that I built with your
product.  Now a few months later, you come out with a new release (v3.1) and you
say "oh, by the way, you now have to pay us X number of $ for every piece of
software that you sell that utilizes our RTS".  How do I feel about passing this
cost onto my customers?  Not very well!  In fact, so not well, that I will not
upgrade to your new version.  It's greed upon Merant's part - plain and simple!
They have a great tool of which I love to use, but they are making the noose
that eventually will hang themselves.

BTW - I too support multiple versions of our products across 2 different major
platforms, as well as the myriad of Windows versions.  I know what it takes to
maintain and support applications in these types of environments.

TL

## Thread: Writing Output to Multiple Files — 4 msgs

### Re: Writing Output to Multiple Files

- **From:** "L. Bertolini" <bertolini.1@osu.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T08:21:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB607D1.A8BF65E0@osu.edu>`
- **References:** `<TTCs6.2983$Im6.408807@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Could you... set up one file in your COBOL program,
and write JCL to it, in "IEBUPDTE" format, i.e.:

./ ADD NAME=MEMBER1
//jobname JOB (acct),'blah blah blah',...
//
./ ADD NAME=MEMBER2
//jobname JOB (acct),'blah blah blah',...
//
./ ENDUP

Then run IEBUPDTE in a subsequent step.

"GER:-)" wrote:
> 
> No, I am not in school anymore...this is a real life problem and because we
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> very much...
```

```

## Thread: Writing Output to Multiple Files — 2 msgs

### Re: Writing Output to Multiple Files

- **From:** alex <alexinaustin@bwn.net-3>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T07:24:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB60892.E972CCA2@bwn.net-3>`
- **References:** `<TTCs6.2983$Im6.408807@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <1lb7bt0hfdsct5ddkc40r4ntofscnmbp0g@4ax.com>`

```
Frank Clarke wrote:

> On Sat, 17 Mar 2001 05:47:31 GMT, "GER:-\)" <auntpeachy@earthlink.net>
> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Join us at www.rexxla.org

How about running TSO batch (IKJEFT01).  Write out the jcl
into a single file first.  Use sepator records to indicate the separation.

Use a CLIST to read this single file.  Any time you run across the
separator record, allocate a new output file, write all the records after
the separator record, until you run into a new separator record.  Repeat
this process until the single file is exhausted.
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing Output to Multiple Files

- **From:** Frank Clarke <nisus@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T23:22:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lp4dbt482m4a6c5nkeoad3aihm9hv7mtjp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<TTCs6.2983$Im6.408807@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <1lb7bt0hfdsct5ddkc40r4ntofscnmbp0g@4ax.com> <3AB60892.E972CCA2@bwn.net-3>`

```
On Mon, 19 Mar 2001 07:24:34 -0600, alex <alexinaustin@bwn.net-3>
wrote:

>How about running TSO batch (IKJEFT01).  Write out the jcl
>into a single file first.  Use sepator records to indicate the separation.
>
>Use a CLIST to read this single file....

Again, File Tailoring handles this all.   I've even gotten into the
habit of embedding things like skeletons and panels right in the REXX
source code.  Because REXX can read its own source, you can have a
routine like DEIMBED (below) which results in a custom-built ISPSLIB
(or ISPPLIB) in VIO... use it once and discard.   Then you do FTOPEN
TEMP, FTINCL <skel>, FTCLOSE, and you are -DONE-!  And because the
components are local to the code which uses them you don't run into
any "configuration management" concerns; it's 100% unitized.

/*
   Parse out the embedded components at the back of the source code.
.  ---------------------------------------------------------------- */
DEIMBED: Procedure expose,             /*@                          */
   (tk_globalvars)  ddnlist  $ddn.  daid.

   address TSO

   fb80po.0  = "NEW UNIT(VIO) SPACE(5 5) TRACKS DIR(40)",
                   "RECFM(F B) LRECL(80) BLKSIZE(0)"
   parse value ""   with  ddnlist $ddn.  daid.

   lastln   = sourceline()
   currln   = lastln                   /*                           */
   if Left(sourceline(currln),2) <> "*/" then return

   currln = currln - 1                 /* previous line             */
   "NEWSTACK"
   address ISPEXEC
   do while sourceline(currln) <> "/*"
      text = sourceline(currln)        /* save with a short name !  */
      if Left(text,3) = ")))" then do  /* package the queue         */
         parse var text ")))" ddn mbr .   /* PLIB PANL001  maybe    */
         if Pos(ddn,ddnlist) = 0 then do  /* doesn't exist          */
            ddnlist = ddnlist ddn      /* keep track                */
            $ddn = ddn || Random(999)
            $ddn.ddn = $ddn
            address TSO "ALLOC FI("$ddn")" fb80po.0
            "LMINIT DATAID(DAID) DDNAME("$ddn")"
            daid.ddn = daid
            end
         daid = daid.ddn
         "LMOPEN DATAID("daid") OPTION(OUTPUT)"
         do queued()
            parse pull line
            "LMPUT DATAID("daid") MODE(INVAR) DATALOC(LINE)
DATALEN(80)"
         end
         "LMMADD DATAID("daid") MEMBER("mbr")"
         "LMCLOSE DATAID("daid")"
         end                           /* package the queue         */
      else push text                   /* onto the top of the stack */
      currln = currln - 1              /* previous line             */
   end                                 /* while                     */
   address TSO "DELSTACK"

return                                 /*@ DEIMBED                  */

----At the back of the source you have.... ------------
/*
)))SLIB SOMESKEL
.....the skeleton code goes here...
)))SLIB NUTHRSKL
....code for the other skeleton....
)))PLIB PANEL01
... code for a panel...
*/



Frank Clarke
Tampa Area REXX Programmers' Alliance
REXX Language Assn
Join us at www.rexxla.org
```

<a id="t-d2318d9255"></a>
## Thread: Is COBOL Dying? — 1 this month / 2 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Re: Is COBOL Dying?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T08:09:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Srot6.16$jI.1482@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<1103_983741242@micron-450> <98eit0$dk7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AAE877E.7F8A52EA@home.com> <fm50btsc1thvj5und03bo8l2quh5ha0nqq@4ax.com>`

```
"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> but cobol itself is not going to be swallowed. the hardware will be
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> difference.

Though I agree with your reasoning, unfortunately, that *is not* the
reasoning at my two largest clients, both local government.  At one
of my clients, top management met some yahoo 'consultant' (the kind
that talk, but don't actually know how to DO things) at a cocktail
party.  This yahoo said "Client Server GUI are the *only* way to go,
and mainframes are archaic."  Now, that organization is spending many
millions of dollars building a Client Server architecture and hiring
Oracle programmers.  The old MIS people had been living on a shoe
string because management refused to fund them properly, but the MIS
clients were pretty happy.  Now, they are blowing money like it's
going out of style, have spent millions already, increased the staff
in the budget by *24* programmers (when the mainframe MIS people had
to struggle to get one extra slot!).  Now, they're hiring Oracle
programmers, and there is a moratorium on hiring *any* more COBOL
programmers.  Of course, Oracle isn't even installed yet (though it's
paid for), and the Oracle programmers are sitting around reading the
newspaper, for naturally, they know zip about COBOL.  It will take *at
least* three to five years to rewrite all their application software,
and that is a very optimistic estimate.  The MIS staff is fairly old,
and people are retiring.  And sadly, one of their staff, a friend of
mine, recently died from long term cancer with many complications (he
was a heavy smoker from his youth).  The good news is that I am sending
four copies of a signed contract down there this morning to put three
COBOL people in their shop for a year, with expectation that the
contract will be signed by them (it ain't real until it's signed!),
and be renewed for one or two additional years.  I may wind up making
more money from this client, at least in the near future, then I would
have had management had better sense, but I hate stupidity and waste.

At the other client I mentioned, top management has gotten ticked at
the MIS department.  All I will say is, I know all the top management
and the MIS people; some are very good friends of mine.  This is an
unfortunate personality issue that should have been resolved.  But
management is Yeah Verily Ticked, so they have 'replaced' (if that
is the correct word) their core business systems with purchased C/S
software.  Essentially, the vendor obtained five separate C/S systems,
and spend many months trying to make them work together.  Knowing it
would be ugly, I have intentionally avoided actually looking at the
abomination that resulted, but I understand from my friends in MIS
and top management that it is flaky and much slower than the old
systems.  They seem to shudder when it is mentioned. :-)  The word
'inefficient hardly covers it, since every one of the originally
disparate systems keeps demographic and other data separately.  But
the thing 'will work', for management is not about to admit they
did something really stupid.  I didn't tell them it was stupid, but
I did tell them it was a serious mistake and they would regret it.
I have a contract with this client too, and have a good rep with both
MIS and top management (though for what seem to me to be emotional
reasons, they didn't listen to me on this), so I hope to keep getting
at least some work from them.

Note that in both cases, cost/benefit never entered the picture.  In
both cases, people were simply convinced, for their own reasons, that
C/S and GUI were 'the way' to go.  I fear that this, as stupid as it
sounds, is the real danger to mainframe COBOL systems.  As we have
hashed to death already, I believe the PC COBOL vendors themselves
are doing things which will kill, or at least severely damage, the PC
COBOL market.  You know, last figures I saw were that over 45% of all
new software development efforts failed from cost and time overruns
and inability to meet design goals.  I can tell you that if I had ever
had even remotaly that kind of 'success' rate, I would have been out
of business a long time ago!  Why would any normal business application
have less than a 100% success rate?  Why can't people see that all this
'gee whiz' stuff is expensive in both time and money, can be horribly
inefficient, is fragile, and in many cases, offers no advantages over
previous technology?  This "C/S and GUI are the answer to everything"
mindset is truly mind-boggling, at least to me.  Not that I dislike
either C/S or GUI, as long as they are used where appropriate.

For over 3 decades I have been very optimistic about the future of
COBOL.  But within the last two years I have seen changes that disturb
me greatly.  I am no longer optimistic, but pessimistic, about COBOL's
future.  I'm not happy about it, either.
```

<a id="t-8eb6007492"></a>
## Thread: What's good and what's bad about CoBOL's directions — 6 msgs

### What's good and what's bad about CoBOL's directions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T07:31:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB61848.2ECB0A05@brazee.net>`

```
I want to start off by saying that changing a language to fit all needs
never was a good idea, and is a worse idea now when we have the ability
to interact with other languages which specialize in different tasks.

One strength of CoBOL is that it is English Language based.   Status
checking should be in English.  Checking status codes is not CoBOL and
should have been replaced decades ago.

There should be a standard way to check to see if function calls worked
correctly though.

Making the Report Writer standard will just make compilers more
expensive.  Instead, CoBOL should have found a better way to interface
with specialized report writing programs such as Crystal Reports.

Calling API's in their native format is ugly and anti-CoBOL.  I'm not
sure what should be done here.  Certainly it should be easier to create
pseudo or true conversational CoBOL using other languages for the I-O.

Bloat by committee will add desired features - but at what cost?
```

#### ↳ Re: What's good and what's bad about CoBOL's directions

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T21:24:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ab7c7fc.15977644@news.open.org>`
- **References:** `<3AB61848.2ECB0A05@brazee.net>`

```
In April of 2000, I had the privilege to be able to attend a talk by
Jeff Freedman of Computer Associates about the upcoming COBOL
standard.  He works for CA supporting their CA-Reala COBOL compiler,
and also happens to be a member of the international COBOL standards
committee.

What I learned from this short session is that developing new COBOL
international standards is really an incredible feat.   He covered in
detail some issues that ran into some bizarre roadblocks; i.e. Japan
won't sign the standard unless they get something in that is key to
their culture, or Norway won't sign the standard unless they get a
proposal removed from the standard.

How many other languages in the world even have a truly accepted
"international" standard that has been officially signed off by all of
the countries?

Report Writer sidebar:
==================
I only use COBOL on a mainframe system.  We have a lot of legacy COBOL
report writer software we must support, and I have never heard of a
mainframe product that could do what Crystal Reports does.  I think
this just emphasizes that every standard decision must be considered
for all platform requirements as well as for all international
requirements.

Pete



On Mon, 19 Mar 2001 07:31:36 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>I want to start off by saying that changing a language to fit all needs
>never was a good idea, and is a worse idea now when we have the ability
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: What's good and what's bad about CoBOL's directions

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-21T02:36:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB81611.11AEA7C9@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB61848.2ECB0A05@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> I want to start off by saying that changing a language to fit all needs
> never was a good idea, and is a worse idea now when we have the ability
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bloat by committee will add desired features - but at what cost?

I'm really surprised no one picked up on this topic. So taking some of the
points you raise Howard :-

1. Yes, we should be communicating with other languages that specialize.
For instance at the current time, one person on J4 has said, 'while not
committed, they are keeping their options open on COBOL GUIs". My response,
"Forget it - we are too bloody late; ten years ago maybe". Besides which if
you've done a switcheroo to SP2 or Java or ......, why would you drop you
existing tools in favour of a COBOL GUI toolset - and in all probability it
would be a balls-up designed by a committee.

2. Checking status codes in 'English' - not sure if this is what you are
after; check out the FCD on Exception Conditions, file status included,
they do have 'English' names; Bill if wrong, correct me.

3. Not sure about your function calls - may also be in that Exception
Condition list ????

4. Report Writer - Bingo ! I've just told five J4 members exactly the same
thing.
I didn't mention Crystal - but that's the obvious choice isn't it.

5. APIs - I guess you are not referring to GUIs as you specifically mention
I/O. On the thread 'COBOL files v DB' - that's the other question I could
have asked - "Given that the COBOL file system was dropped completely, what
would you choose as an alternative ?".

6. Bloat - I think many of us are agreed on this, trouble is, nobody is
S-C-R-E-A-M-I-N-G- Bloody Blue Murder at  J4 !

I can't remember if that's how I started the thread "COBOL files v DB", but
it did start with me musing elsewhere :-

    "If you had a stripped-down COBOL, excluding things like Screen
Section, (sorry Judson <G>), R/W and COBOL file system......" which got the
reaction 'COBOL files are the language's major asset'.

I started out as a COBOL purist, dreaming/hoping the one language could do
it all for me in one comfortable and known coding environment. I agree with
your observations - we have a strong BUSINESS oriented language,  make it
gel with other languages or tools which do a better job with the ancillary
stuff covering UI and DBI. We would hardly consider COBOL as the language
to use for voice recognition - would we.

Meanwhile - we are still going to get the camel from the committee instead
of the horse we wanted.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

>
>The selling job we want isn't to make Java programers switch to CoBOL, it
is to
>make Java programmers add CoBOL to their toolbox to do stuff CoBOL does

Agree with everything you wrote Howard and have expressed same on numerous
occasions elsewhere.

I  would just add to the above that it is also important to get COBOL
progammers to learn Java. Not as a REPLACEMENT, but as a COMPLEMENT to it.

The other advantage of learning Java is that you automatically acquire
Object Oriented concepts along the way, and these can then be related back
to OO COBOL (which I found extremely difficult to learn when coming to it
WITHOUT these concepts). Soon as I learned Java my OO COBOL took off...

And why should anybody learn OO?

Because it is the key to Components, and Components are the Keys to the
Kingdom...<G>

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: BOOK: Structured COBOL Programming, 7th Edition

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T03:15:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC1C7DB.F0544C37@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<5Pot6.5174$A2.1343513@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```


JP wrote:

> http://s1.amazon.com/exec/varzea/ts/exchange-glance/Y01Y3273008Y1668469
>
> My price $9.99
> Amazon: $59.95

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

One of my favorite books dealing with the ANSI-1985 COBOL standard was
written
by Jim Inglis and published by Wiley in 1989.  It was not a beginner's
book, but it
was extremely well organized and demonstrated COBOL as it ought to be
written.

Can we assume that this new book emphasizes fully-formed elementary
structures,
nested COBOL subprograms callable as protected modules via parameters,  the

many forms of the PEFORM statement (including illustrations where in-line
perform
is useful,  built-in functions,  multiple examples of the EVALUATE
statement,
reference modification, and other features that make it possible to
actually do
structured programming in COBOL.

In my view, any book that demonstrates an if statement without an end if is
leading
new programmers astray.   In fact, all of the statements in which an
end-form (READ,
SEARCH, etc.) should be encouraged with that end-form.

I suppose I need to order a copy of the book to find out for myself.
However, if someone
already has gotten a copy, it would be interesting to hear some evaluation
regarding the
conformance to structured programming.    So many COBOL books I have seen
fail to
properly demonstrate good coding style, including some of those touted as
most popular.

Lao Xiao Hai
```

### Re: COBOl Files v. Databases

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T07:39:08-07:00

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

figure out how to "correct" these.  Providing an public domain (open source?)
external file manager might be a way to do this.  I have always believed this
was the operating system's job - while 90% of what Windows does has nothing to
do with being an operating system.

aph@redhat.invalid wrote:

> Sure, you could label a teapot intelligent if you wanted but no-one
> would have to agree.  The magic of Turing's test is that they would
> agree.

But if you're writing an AI application, Turing's is probably not relevant.
We don't need a computer to act like people, we have plenty of people
available to do that.  We need an AI program to do specific tasks with
intelligence tailored to those tasks.

#### ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** J Thomas <jethomas@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T09:35:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB6273D.E6C18536@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org> <3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net> <98m43a$fe2$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AAFB378.AB166E8C@ieee.org> <98q3f1$bid$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB0DFC6.FC100A64@ieee.org> <98sq19$ton$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB61B58.CF536ECA@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> aph@redhat.invalid wrote:

> > Sure, you could label a teapot intelligent if you wanted but no-one
> > would have to agree.  The magic of Turing's test is that they would
> > agree.
 
> But if you're writing an AI application, Turing's is probably not 
> relevant.  We don't need a computer to act like people, we have 
> plenty of people available to do that.  We need an AI program to do 
> specific tasks with intelligence tailored to those tasks.

Yes.  I can see the Turing test as a philosophical device.  In Turing's
time england still had a lot of prejudice about ethnic groups whose
members looked just like the people who discriminated against them.  And
so somebody might say "I don't like <ethnic group>, they're all <ethnic
stereotype>" and somebody else could say "Well, you know Joe, that
you've been casually friendly with all this time, he's <ethnic group>
and you've never complained about him being <ethnic stereotype>."  And
of course Joe would be very thoroughly assimilated.
It doesn't work as well now that the <ethnic groups> are more likely to
be physically distinguishable.  So anyway, I can easily see Turing
wanting to apply the same standards exactly.  "You say human beings are
intelligent and computers can't be.  But you didn't notice that the
person you just had that long conversation with was really a computer!" 

Incidentally, I've seen some slight evidence that to pass the Turing
test it helps to have the program simulate some sort of raving bigot. 
It can rave and ignore what people say and drag the conversation firmly
back to its stupid beliefs, and nobody will expect anthing different. 
It doesn't have to actually say anything intelligent, just behave in the
particular unintelligent way people expect of whichever sort of bigot
it's pretending to be.  It makes sense that the easiest person to
simulate would be a rude, stupid person that they didn't particularly
want to talk to anyway.

So apart from the philosophical pooint that it probably isn't workable
to say "intelligence is whatever humans do and not what anything else
does" I don't see much value there.  Useful machines will do things
easily that people don't do easily, and if they include models of human
beings at all the models will probably be used to predict what the
humans will want, to be better ready to supply it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T09:21:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB631EE.858EC6DC@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org> <3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net> <98m43a$fe2$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AAFB378.AB166E8C@ieee.org> <98q3f1$bid$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB0DFC6.FC100A64@ieee.org> <98sq19$ton$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB61B58.CF536ECA@brazee.net> <3AB6273D.E6C18536@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Your post is interesting and made me think of how we discriminate against
groups.

If that person from that other group is indistinguishable from people from
my group - then he is a person and OK.   But if he persists in his old
cultural habits we can look down at him.  Sometimes these are language
styles - Stallion's accent sounds like he's dumb to many Americans, and if a
NBA player's speaking style sounds lazy, we call him lazy.   We use the same
types of prejudice with Turing's test.  We test to see if it is OUR type of
intelligence, rejecting different values.

Turing's point is more applicable to "sentient".   This is a word without
any measurable meaning.  We combine sentiency with intelligence to set our
morals (our kind is most important, and this is how we define our kind).  So
how do we decide whether a being is sufficiently like us to be protected?
If we can't tell the difference!


J Thomas wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
> > aph@redhat.invalid wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> humans will want, to be better ready to supply it.
```

- **From:** J Thomas <jethomas@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T10:49:48-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3AB638AC.BA3A0132@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org> <3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net> <98m43a$fe2$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AAFB378.AB166E8C@ieee.org> <98q3f1$bid$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB0DFC6.FC100A64@ieee.org> <98sq19$ton$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB61B58.CF536ECA@brazee.net> <3AB6273D.E6C18536@ix.netcom.com> <3AB631EE.858EC6DC@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> So how do we decide whether a being is sufficiently like us to be 
> protected?  If we can't tell the difference!

That reminds me of an ethnic joke.  I hesitate to tell it since I'm not
a Baptist.

A river was in flood, and a man saw somebody in the river, obviously in
trouble, and managed to rescue him.  The half-drowned man thanked him.  
"Oh, it's just what's expected of me, I'm a christian."
  "A christian?  So am I."
  "I'm a Baptist."
  "What a coincidence!  So am I!"
  "I'm a Free-will Baptist."
  "Yes, same here."
  "A Jesus Heart Free-will Baptist."
  "Me too!"
  "Are you a Purity Jesus Heart Free-will Baptist?"
  "No, I'm Holiness."
So he pushed him back in.

The thing I find encouraging about the joke is the implication that if
the man hadn't been christian at all, there wouldn't have been any
problem.
```

: to say "intelligence is whatever humans do and not what anything else
: does"

no-one has made it!

Andrew.

aph@redhat.invalid wrote:
> J Thomas <jethomas@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> : Incidentally, I've seen some slight evidence that to pass the 
> : Turing test it helps to have the program simulate some sort of 
> : raving bigot.

> It's easy for a computer realistically to mimic deranged people:

> was intelligent.  How would that be a pass?

Did I misunderstand the test?  I thought it was a pass if the examiner
couldn't tell the conversation was with a human.  I didn't see asnything
there about rating how smart the human was.

> : So apart from the philosophical pooint that it probably isn't 
> : workable to say "intelligence is whatever humans do and not what 
> : anything else does"

> That would be an unintelligent point to make, which is presumably why
> no-one has made it!
 
Since I thought the test measured how well the program could pass for
human, naturally I thought the assumption was that human = intelligent.
```

- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org> <3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net> <98m43a$fe2$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AAFB378.AB166E8C@ieee.org> <98q3f1$bid$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB0DFC6.FC100A64@ieee.org> <98sq19$ton$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB61B58.CF536ECA@brazee.net> <3AB6273D.E6C18536@ix.netcom.com> <995csm$qhf$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB63AD4.390A1F59@ix.netcom.com>`

```
In comp.lang.forth J Thomas <jethomas@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
: aph@redhat.invalid wrote:

:> It's easy for a computer realistically to mimic deranged people:
:> Parry, for example.  But the examiner wouldn't believe the program 
:> was intelligent.  How would that be a pass?

: Did I misunderstand the test?  I thought it was a pass if the examiner
: couldn't tell the conversation was with a human.  I didn't see asnything
: there about rating how smart the human was.

In which case, reductio ad absurdum, a human who couldn't type and a
computer that was switched off would be indistinguishable, so the
computer would pass the Turing test.  Clearly this wasn't Turing's
intent, and both the computer and the human being must appear to be
intelligent.  Both failing isn't enough.

Andrew.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** Jerry Avins <jya@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T12:59:13-05:00

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** Leonard Zettel <zettel@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T12:36:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB643A0.805F7ACA@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org> <3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net> <98m43a$fe2$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AAFB378.AB166E8C@ieee.org> <98q3f1$bid$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB0DFC6.FC100A64@ieee.org> <98sq19$ton$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB61B58.CF536ECA@brazee.net> <3AB6273D.E6C18536@ix.netcom.com>`

```


J Thomas wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes.  I can see the Turing test as a philosophical device.  

<snip>
<sigh>
You have to remember context.  And what artificial intelligence used to
mean.

To start with *you* know you are "there" in a very intimate manner.
"Cogito, ergo sum". -Descartes

Which leads to the question:  How do you know the other guy is there?
After all, solipsism is a valid philosophical position.

The naturalist position is that you can only make inferences from
observed behavior (and other sensory data).

Artificial intelligence used to mean making a computer be somebody -
a self-conscious entity.

As James Crick reaffirmed in his latest (very good) work recently, we
still don't have an operational definition for consciousness, and that
lack makes it very hard to study scientifically.

So how do you know when you have created an artificial personality that
can claim some kind of equal footing with yourself? Turing proposed you do
it the same way you do it with Joe Schlunk - you converse and make a
judgment.  Formally, this usually includes expert reports and a court
hearing.

That may not be a very good answer, but I have yet to see a better one.
The fur will fly when the evidence gets good enough to convince some and
not others.  Remember, some Spaniards justified their treatment of native
Americans by claiming they were some kind of exotic animal, and not human beings.
To the credit of the church, most clergy who went along argued otherwise.
   -LenZ-
```

<a id="t-5ba1220fac"></a>
## Thread: Is COBOL Dying? — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Re: Is COBOL Dying?

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T09:45:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IQot6.13$Ow1.173@client>`
- **References:** `<1103_983741242@micron-450> <98eit0$dk7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3AAE877E.7F8A52EA@home.com> <fm50btsc1thvj5und03bo8l2quh5ha0nqq@4ax.com>`

```
I think the hardware you are referring to (with the Pentium Chip) is
probably the Unisys ClearPath machine.  It has a "mainframe" side and an NT
side closely married in the same box.
Their most recent hardware is all NT with an emulator for the Mainframe
side.

I agree with other posts in this forum pointing out that there are some
systems where the number of transactions and the size of the data really
can't be handled by anything other than a "mainframe" system today.
However, there are a HUGE number of companies out there, with really well
written COBOL programs that would run just fine on a large Unix or NT
system.  In most cases, when those systems were written, years ago, the
Mainframe was chosen because it was the only option.  In many cases these
systems also don't really need five nines of uptime - they are batch jobs
that could wait a few minutes if an NT box has to be rebooted (Oh, but wait,
NT boxes never have to be rebooted - I forgot :-).

But over time, the cost of maintaining their "big iron" hardware (HW and SW
maintenance, facilities, AC, power, 24/7 operators, etc.) became huge
comparied to maintaining a smaller Unix box.  And a Unix or NT box now has
more than enough horsepower to get the job done.

My company has largely made an entire service business out of porting
systems from mainframes to Unix or NT systems.  We are in process of bidding
on a job right now with a large Insurance Company.  Their ROI on moving the
code is about 18 months and getting shorter all the time as the Mainframe
manufacturer sticks them with higher and higher maintenance fees the older
the box gets.

So no - COBOL isn't dying - it's just going to move to less expenisve
hardware where appropriate.
It's the hardware that's dying.  And did any of us expect it to live forever
anyway?

Buddy Ray
The Progeni Corporation
www.progeni.com

"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:fm50btsc1thvj5und03bo8l2quh5ha0nqq@4ax.com...

>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >such "unromantic" applications which live or die by their
COBOL-programmed
> >support can afford to shut off the mainframes for a few years while we
replace
> >all our old junk with newer and much more fragile junk.  Then COBOL can
die
> >in piece.
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> nonesofar
```

```
Ray:

If the company in question got an IBM Multiprise 3000 (60 - 120 mips),
would it be an appropriate size box?  If so, depending on what they are
currently running, they MIGHT be able to save on hardware, software and
environmental costs.  Also the current IBM COBOL compilers have some
really interesting optimizations.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.

## Thread: ERROR QUESTION — 9 msgs

### ERROR QUESTION

- **From:** "Jova" <jclockridge@cablevision.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T15:35:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net>`

```
I'm using Micro-focus compiler and when I try to run my program
it's telling me that "NO ROOM IN DIRECTORY" does anyone know what this
means.
```

#### ↳ Re: ERROR QUESTION

- **From:** "Jova" <jclockridge@cablevision.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T15:53:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CXpt6.246073$Vj5.32025331@news02.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net>`

```
Its says "NO ROOM IN DIRECTORY FOR FILE".
"Jova" <jclockridge@cablevision.com> wrote in message
news:LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net...
> I'm using Micro-focus compiler and when I try to run my program
> it's telling me that "NO ROOM IN DIRECTORY" does anyone know what this
> means.
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ERROR QUESTION

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T09:01:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB71C78.9B916652@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net> <CXpt6.246073$Vj5.32025331@news02.optonline.net>`

```
Jova wrote:
> 
> Its says "NO ROOM IN DIRECTORY FOR FILE".
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > means.

It usually means that you have specified an invalid name for a file.  If
it is MF 3.x then you may have tried a name that is not a DOS 8.3
structure (including directory names).  Or perhaps the name has a space
in it (which terminates the name).
```

#### ↳ Re: ERROR QUESTION

- **From:** "Anthony Borla" <ajborla@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T06:16:58+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2Nst6.36696$v5.80473@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net>`

```
"Jova" <jclockridge@cablevision.com> wrote in message
news:LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net...
> I'm using Micro-focus compiler and when I try to run my program
> it's telling me that "NO ROOM IN DIRECTORY" does anyone know what this
> means.
>

It sounds as if the compiler is attempting to write an output file, a
temporary file perhaps, and there is not enough disk space available for
storing it. Perhaps you could check this, and remedy via:

* Delete / compress some old / unused files to clear some space
* Arrange for your space allocation / quota (if applicable) to be
  increased

I hope this helps.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ERROR QUESTION

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-19T22:05:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AB682C3.DB7FACE0@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net> <2Nst6.36696$v5.80473@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Anthony Borla wrote:
> 
> "Jova" <jclockridge@cablevision.com> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> I hope this helps.

Also check for an infinite loop - you could be writing one record over
and over until you use up all available space in the directory

	Susan A
```

- **From:** "Gary Susott" <susott@atta-boy.com>

```

news:2Nst6.36696$v5.80473@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
> "Jova" <jclockridge@cablevision.com> wrote in message
> news:LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: ERROR QUESTION

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T08:25:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2NJt6.5$by2.1222@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net>`

```
"Jova" <jclockridge@cablevision.com> wrote:
> I'm using Micro-focus compiler and when I try to run my program
> it's telling me that "NO ROOM IN DIRECTORY" does anyone know what
> this means.

If you are compiling in, or writing a file to, the root directory,
it is possible that the root directory is full.  Root directories of
all hard drives, floppy disks, zip disks, etc., are finite, the number
of file *names* that it can contain is fixed and determined at format
time.

P.S.- If you have put so many files in the root directory of your
hard drive that you have filled it up, then God help you, for either
you don't have a clue about subdirectories, or have a sense of order
better suited to a packrat, and everything you own is probably lying
on the floor at home. ;-)
```

- **References:** `<LGpt6.245751$Vj5.32016573@news02.optonline.net> <2NJt6.5$by2.1222@news2.atl>`

- **References:** `<OMKr6.6992$rB2.460753@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com> <3ab0cefe.3507320@news.epix.net> <3AB2A7AB.24099E6@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>not currently one of the features of the proposed OO COBOL.

Jimmy:

I hesitate to answer the question because I'm really not sure.  Don
Tees and Thane Hubbell understand how sp2 creates GUI screens
dynamically and they also have a much better understanding of OO than
I do.

Perhaps they can comment on this.

>What makes me query - I have one fairly large Dialog, primarily a table of 10 x
>24 entryfields. Performs very well in quickly generating when required (the
>table part being dynamic creation). But at the back of my mind, if it was
>sitting on disk as a 'file' - might just improve the performance.

A 10 x 24 table could be created dynamically in sp2 or it could be
defined as a static "repeat group" (occurs clause) in sp2.

I don't believe that there would be any noticeable difference in
performance regardless of how you choose to display the array.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com

<a id="t-0974d3efc3"></a>
## Thread: Function NUMVAL Quirk? — 6 msgs

### Function NUMVAL Quirk?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T04:39:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010319233926.00488.00001781@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
I'm testing a plain data entry CICS program and once the fields are edited I
have to move them into an output file.  The input consists of several numeric
fields one of which is quantity and another price.  Quantity is defined on the
screen as a PIC X(9) field and price as PIC X(10).   After all detail records
are processed I have to write a total record to the file which is the total of
all the (quantity * price)s.

The quantity field ends up on the output file as a PIC S9(10)V9999 and the
price as PIC S9(14)V9999 (no - I did not define these fields and yes they are
carved in stone).

I use the function NUMVAL to move the screen fields to the output record
fields.  The price converts just fine - but the quantity does not.

Straight from my Xpediter screen (it is a bit contorted from cut/paste but I
think you can see what I mean):

                        
03 ED810O-300-QTY-INVD 
    .000000004000{      
    0FFFFFFFFFFFFC      
    80000000040000      
    1...*...10....      


What's with the x'08' in the first position?  

Here are the other fields - again cut/paste from Xpediter:

LV ----- COBOL DATANAME ---------- -- ATTRIBUTES -- ----+---10---
02 WS-TOT-AMT                      S9(8)V99         +0000005336  
02 WS-TOT-AMT2                     S9(8)V99         +2800005336  

The first total was obtained using the 9 position working storage field for the
quantity, the 2nd using the output file field in the compute statement.  The
'28' is actually a truncation - the number is more like 16040...28 :). 

LV ----- COBOL DATANAME ---------- -- ATTRIBUTES -- ----+---10----+---2
03 ED810O-300-QTY-INVD             S9(10)V9(04)     .000000004000{     
03 ED810O-300-UM                   X(02)            EA                 
03 ED810O-300-UNIT-PRICE           S9(14)V9(04)     +000000000000133410

You can see the price field here and the quantity field not in hex.  I also
noticed that the quantity field has the sign ( { ) at the end while the price
has +.

What makes this even more interesting is that when looking at the field in the
output record that position is x'F0'.  And when I use it in the multiplication
I do not get an ASRA (S0C7) but a wild total amount.

If I use a PIC S9(9)  field as the target field for the function the conversion
is just fine.

Any ideas?  Reasons?

Eileen
```

#### ↳ Re: Function NUMVAL Quirk?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T13:16:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uKIt6.214$693.60882@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<20010319233926.00488.00001781@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
If worse comes to worst and you can't figure out/get the numeric bits into
the field, you could redefine the quantity field:

 03 ED810O-300-QTY-INVD       PIC S9(10)V9(4).
 03 FILLER REDEFINES ED810O-300-QTY-INVD
     05 ED810O-300-QTY-INVD-WHOLE  PIC 9(10).
     05 ED810O-300-QTY-INV-FRAC       PIC SV9(04).

Then you can UNSTRING the text entry delimited by decimal point (or use
function numval on a reference-modifed portion of the input). You'll have to
adjust the sign to make sure the sign overpunch ends up in the "frac"
portion but not in the "whole" portion.

Crummy way to code, yes, but when you gotta go, you gotta go.

(Good thing the output is USAGE DISPLAY. This gets real ugly when it's
BINARY or PACKED-DECIMAL).
```

#### ↳ Re: Function NUMVAL Quirk?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T07:30:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<997m0q$l7o$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010319233926.00488.00001781@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
I know that you have "described" what you do - but could you post three
lines of your original source code:

Data definitions of your sending and receiving fields for quantity - and the
EXACT COBOL statement that does the "conversion"?

P.S.  I assume that your sending field does *not* include the
attribute-byte(s) from your screen.  That is what that high-order X'8' looks
like to me - but I may be mistaken.
```

#### ↳ Re: Function NUMVAL Quirk?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T14:56:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010320095632.23800.00000003@nso-me.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20010319233926.00488.00001781@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20010319233926.00488.00001781@ng-ce1.aol.com>, yukonmama@aol.com
(YukonMama) writes:

>
>What makes this even more interesting is that when looking at the field in
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Is it possible that it is having a problem with the old rule of 18-digit
numeric?
When adding a sign, you are looking at a max of 17-digits + 1-sign position.
Granted that this is a display formatted field, but the internal function might
be working in binary.
No real basis for this assumption, but it is the only thing that comes to mind.
```

#### ↳ Re: Function NUMVAL Quirk?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T12:14:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28B607CE564992B0.710FDCE7DD5D0C1F.D6CD6C6EE7313B76@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<20010319233926.00488.00001781@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
On 20 Mar 2001 04:39:26 GMT, yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama) enlightened
us:

>I'm testing a plain data entry CICS program and once the fields are edited I
>have to move them into an output file.  The input consists of several numeric
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>What's with the x'08' in the first position?  

A hex 08 is a screen attribute byte.  It indicates a field is
unprotected, numeric with high intensity on.  It would seem then that
your definition of the input field or the field you moved to
ED8100-300-ATY-INVD is off by 1 byte.  Another disturbing (at least to
me) fact is that if this indeed the screen field and it's attributes,
the attribute for a data entry field that was entered should have the
MDT on.  So the attribute for this field should be a hex 09.  

We are talking CICS and BMS maps here are we not?

>
>Here are the other fields - again cut/paste from Xpediter:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>Eileen

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Often, when I am reading a good book, I stop and thank my teacher.
That is, I used to, until she got an unlisted number.


Don't believe the propaganda about the oil in the Artic National
Wildlife Refuge as spewed forth by Interior Secretary Gale Norton.

Learn the true facts, please see:  
http://www.nrdc.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Function NUMVAL Quirk?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T22:26:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_NQt6.14203$Im6.1484034@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<20010319233926.00488.00001781@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```

"YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com>
>
> I use the function NUMVAL to [[[ move]]] the screen fields to the
output record
> fields.  The price converts just fine - but the quantity does not.

 Your choices are:

MOVE FUNCTION NUMVAL(X) TO Y
or
COMPUTE Y = FUNCTION NUMVAL(X)

Are you sure you're using the right one? COMPUTE (or its variant) is
correct; MOVE is incorrect
```

## Thread: Einstein's Riddle — 4 msgs

### Re: Einstein's Riddle

- **From:** wtanksle@dolphin.openprojects.net (William Tanksley)
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T07:15:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.neural-nets,comp.lang.apl,comp.lang.awk,comp.lang.beta,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn9be0sj.mug.wtanksle@dolphin.openprojects.net>`
- **References:** `<VIPq6.396633$ge4.138469576@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3AACB567.A59B8497@Azonic.co.nz> <3AACE6CF.7F05484D@ieee.org> <0W8r6.178$fo5.14165@news.get2net.dk> <3AAD60F3.120F284A@ieee.org> <3AAE371A.2F9F596F@brazee.net> <98m43a$fe2$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AAFB378.AB166E8C@ieee.org> <98q3f1$bid$1@localhost.localdomain> <3AB0DFC6.FC100A64@ieee.org> <98sq19$ton$1@localhost.localdomain> <slrn9b56m8.4se.wtanksle@dolphin.openprojects.net> <98vjh9$p2g$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Sat, 17 Mar 2001 06:40:54 -0500, donald tees wrote:
>"William Tanksley" <wtanksle@dolphin.openprojects.net> wrote in message

>> Frankly, I'm not interested in a system that could pass the Turing test. I
>> already know how to produce such a system in fairly short order.  I'm more
>> interested in systems which fail the Turing test yet are intelligent.

>Where is your Nobel prize?

The problem is the cost of the setup. I'm working on it!  I need to get a
license first, and some other preliminaries.  After that it should be
between three and five years before I'm able to run my first Turing test.
I'm entirely confident that my results will be reproducible; the level of
education required to achieve a Turing-capable system is VERY low, and the
only reason I'm spending so much is that I want to do it right.
```

## Thread: COBOL Newbie: Where Should I Start? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### COBOL Newbie: Where Should I Start?

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T08:38:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2GEt6.12484$992.75573@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Greetings All,

I have just commenced studying/learning COBOL as part of my Commercial
Programming course at university. A search on several WWW search engines has
revealed limited sources of information for newbies such as myself.

Where should I start? (ie. any helpful web resources or books out there?)

I'd also appreciate any suggestions on what compiler would be best for a
beginner such as myself as there seems to be so many versions floating
around, and I don't have a clue what makes one better than the other. I am
currently running win98 on my system.

Thanks in advance.

eRRaTiK
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Newbie: Where Should I Start?

- **From:** "Eric Gauthier" <ericg@dgcsolutions.qc.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-03-20T16:50:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yiQt6.4820$r%.79577@weber.videotron.net>`
- **References:** `<2GEt6.12484$992.75573@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Look at those links:
http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
http://www.flexus.com/cobol.html

It seems that Futjisu and AcuCobol are very popular.

We are using RM-Cobol.

Hope this help.
Eric

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Newbie: Where Should I Start?

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-03-21T15:39:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99ahvj$g10$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<2GEt6.12484$992.75573@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Amazon.com always has lots of books for sale on programming although you have
to research what is best to buy.  Some people recommend books like learn
COBOL in 24 days or something similar which usually comes with a compiler
from Fujitsu or some other places.  If you are taking a first basic
programming class an older compiler may work fine.  I have used a compiler
from Merant (Microfocus) that was designed for Windows 3.1 and it seemed to
run fine on Windows 95, but I have never tried it on Windows 98.  I have not
seen any sites dedicated to how to program for beginners in COBOL.  I have
thought of building one but I just do not have the time.
