
###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol compiler for linux and SQL

- **From:** qIroS <qIroS@tlhIngan.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T21:20:02+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8os1ct0o92ji1rljoppbtdue0b16ofsmqk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<99km2d$q8t1@imsp212.netvigator.com> <6lhsbt850s73s27i6ph9nsvp8ihlknltu6@4ax.com> <3ABF8235.8EDEA35E@att.net>`

```
ghItlh aflinsch <avflinsch@att.net> 


>Sounds pretty cool, any chance you would be willing to share your
>work?

I could I guess.

The problem is it makes two very big assumptions:

1) It works with the way _I_ write SQL (HV definition assumptions
mostly, such as all HVs belong to a single 01 level HOST-VARIABLES and
all HV's are 02 levels beneath this)

2) It produces C code suitable for running through Oracle Pro*C and
may not work with Ingres/DB2/whatever precompilers for C without
modification.

Some neat things it DOES do is allow you to use (for example)
Packed-Decimal HVs in COBOL and the preprocessor will handle
converting these to and from C to Cobol.

IMO, if you want COBOL and SQL on Linux, CA OpenIngres 2.0 is the only
way to go; It's the only Linux RDBMS that I know of that has a native
COBOL precompiler for embedded SQL.

Cheerie pip.

If people are genuinley interested in seeing the preprocessor, I may
put it on line. If there is sufficient interest.
```

---

<a id="t-3c227d1a08"></a>
## Thread: OO's Failure — 4 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO's Failure

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T20:37:06+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-109023.20370625032001@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
Iv'e been thinking (strange as that may seem!) about why OO seems to jar 
so much with many of us who think that it is a case of the "Emperor's 
new Clothes".

There appear to be some fundamental problems with OO that have not been 
addressed.

The problem of OVERLOADED OPERATORS.
Now while this is not a requirement for a OO system, overloaded 
operators are frequently associated with OO designs. The problem is that 
once you do this you neccessarily obscure the definition of the 
operator. This makes it difficult to determine extactly what operation 
is to be done.

The problem of TOO MANY METHODS.
In OO design, there is a great temptation to write a method for 
everything. In some cases (true OO) you are forced to write a method to 
update and read back every variable in an object to make them available 
to the invokers of the object. This is plainly rediculous. Who needs to 
write a MOVE for every variable in a object. One ends up writing a new 
programming language - one that is tailored to the needs of the problem 
being solved.
Now, I don't know about you, but I find that this precludes the ability 
to quickly find problems as you first have to re-learn the proprietary 
problem language and then dig into that to find the problem.
When writing new code, it becomes a task of scanning the existing code 
to find a method that is close and then to re-factor that object 
overloading existing methods and creating new methods to perform the 
task.
It is often said that OO is to programming what IC's are to circuit 
design. But that is false. In ciruit design, the IC's function is fixed 
and so are the interfaces. It is the combination of IC's and their 
arrangement that is designed. You don't find the average circuit 
designer overloading the pin functions with new meaning.
In OO the function (methods) and Interfaces of the IC's (Objects) are 
subject to change. I would venture to suggest that the closest thing to 
IC's in the IT industry is the Cobol Procedure Division statement 
definitions. It is the lack of new statements tailored to today's needs 
that have forced the usage of this unsatisfactory OO paradigm.

The COMPILER DEVELOPERs Slackness.
Much easier to provide a generic CALL interface or some cobbled up 
INVOKE objects that to try to design language elements that would 
standardise the way programmers interact with the new interfaces.
Just why are there no statements to help build and maintain server side 
WEB pages. Why are there no statements to allow TCP/IP communication. 
Why in an age of online transactions are there no statements to create, 
manipulate and communicate to other processes. Why do we have to rely on 
various server manufacturers to provide interfaces. Why doesn't the 
langage mandate and standardise them. How else can you take a program 
from system a and re-compile and run it on system b.

The FAILURE OF CONCEPT.
OO assumes that all DATA has associated methods. But this is plainly 
absurd. When you have trillions of pieces of data, associating them all 
with a set of methods becomes unmanagable. What to you do when you need 
to add another method to them all, change the name of a method, etc.
One of the most important things learnt in the online transaction game 
is to jealously SEPERATE your data from the programs and never ever 
trust the data. Any given program on a given day may process trillions 
of bits of data. You don't need trillions of copies of the program to do 
this - just one. And whilst OO systems can have one copy of a method, 
they all have to associate that copy with each of the trillions of bits 
of data.

Programs are a scarce resource. Data is overwhelmingly changable, 
wherease the programs do not need to be. A small subset of programming 
function may be able to cope with vast amounts of data and data 
variablilty. OO has got it exaclty BACKWARDS.
```

#### ↳ Re: OO's Failure

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T12:38:27+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3abe943a_5@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<josephk-109023.20370625032001@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
Real good post, Joe.

I had to respond... <G>

comments intermingled below.

Pete.
Joseph J Katnic wrote in message ...
>Iv'e been thinking (strange as that may seem!) about why OO seems to jar
>so much with many of us who think that it is a case of the "Emperor's
>new Clothes".
>
It is a logical development from Structured modular programming. (See the
forthcoming article on "aboutlegacycoding" where I examine this at length).
It is therefore reminiscent of an already established technique, hence the
"Emperor's New Clothes" feeling. However, in this case, the Emperor is
definitely not naked...

>
>There appear to be some fundamental problems with OO that have not been
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'm not quite sure what you mean by "overloaded operators". **Function**
overloading is common in OO systems and is one of the forms of polymorphism.
Basically, it enables the same Method to accept different parameters. This
is useful. I can't see how this obfuscates the function to be done, so I
guess I have misunderstood you. Could you elucidate?

>>>
>The problem of TOO MANY METHODS.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>to the invokers of the object.
<<<
No you're not!

GET and SET  Methods CAN be used to obtain Properties but they don't HAVE to
be. (And be very careful what you refer to as "true OO" - claimants for this
title are ten a penny...<G>) What you refer to as "variables" (more
correctly in OO, "properties") can be passed during invocation. So, if you
wanted you, you could access/update a complete COBOL record definition with
ONE Method, simply by providing the data when you invoked it.
(INVOKE...USING). Similarly, you can directly reference the Properties of an
Object once you have established a reference to it, and provided they are
Public properties.

(In COBOL:   move field1 of myObject to whatever
where "myObject" is an Object reference to an instance of some Class.

In Java:  <type> whatever = my Object.field1)

Sometimes there are a plethora of Methods but this is a design problem, not
an intrinsic failure in OO.

>>>
>This is plainly rediculous. Who needs to
>write a MOVE for every variable in a object. One ends up writing a new
>programming language - one that is tailored to the needs of the problem
>being solved.
<<<
It isn't ridiculous because you don't need to do it...<G>.  see above.


>Now, I don't know about you, but I find that this precludes the ability
>to quickly find problems as you first have to re-learn the proprietary
>problem language and then dig into that to find the problem.

Nope. I don't find this to be a problem. What "proprietary problem language"
are you referring to?


>When writing new code, it becomes a task of scanning the existing code
>to find a method that is close and then to re-factor that object
>overloading existing methods and creating new methods to perform the
>task.

That's exactly what you would do in structured COBOL... Find a module that
is close to what you want then either modify it to accomodate the new stuff
or clone a new module from it. What's the difference ? (apart from the
jargon...<G>)

>>>
>It is often said that OO is to programming what IC's are to circuit
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<<<

Won't comment on this as I never drew the parallel with ICs...


>>>
>The COMPILER DEVELOPERs Slackness.
>Much easier to provide a generic CALL interface or some cobbled up
>INVOKE objects that to try to design language elements that would
>standardise the way programmers interact with the new interfaces.
<<<

Having once been a compiler writer (in another lifetime <G>) I can't let
this one go...<G>

People who  write compilers are constrained by the standard for the Language
they are writing a compiler for. That's why, when we get the new COBOL
standard in 2002 (yes, I believe in flying pigs...), we will have a much
better COBOL which WILL have some of the features you are complaining about.
Please believe me when I say there is NOTHING slack about doing this job. It
is many hours of hard grind trying to imagine what users are likely to do
with your code.

And I can promise you, OO has not been added to COBOL in order to make the
Compiler writers' lives  easier...<G>

>Just why are there no statements to help build and maintain server side
>WEB pages. Why are there no statements to allow TCP/IP communication.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Because if we didn't do the above you would be expecting EVERY aspect of
EVERY development, from On-line telemetry to Web Hosting to doing Sales
analysis, to be available as part of COBOL and there would be no other
languages which are specifically designed (and therefore better suited) to a
specific purpose. Can you imagine how thick the Manual would be? Can you see
students rushing to embrace it? <G>

>The FAILURE OF CONCEPT.

>OO assumes that all DATA has associated methods. But this is plainly
>absurd.

Not to me it isn't. If you don't intend to manipulate data why would you
define it? (COBOL caters beautifully for the rare occasions when you HAVE to
define it by providing FILLER ("I have to define some bytes here which I
have no intention of ever referencing") sadly, use of FILLER has fallen into
disrepute and the modern trend is to simply omit it...I liked it and still
use it...)

If you DO intend to manipulate the data then you will need some code to do
so. Whether it is structured or OO.

>>>
>When you have trillions of pieces of data, associating them all
>with a set of methods becomes unmanagable. What to you do when you need
>to add another method to them all, change the name of a method, etc.
<<<
It is no more "unmanageable" than using structured code. If you can
associate them with a Procedure Division, you can associate them with a
Method. Where's the difference?

>>>
>One of the most important things learnt in the online transaction game
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'm missing something here. Spent many years writing transaction processes
on both mainframes and PCs. The only reason the data was separated from the
code was because the code needed to be re-entrant and/or re-usable. It was
still necessary to associate an appropriate instance of data with the code
before running it...whether you used the COMMON area in CICS or the SPA in
IMS-DC or a Core Allocatable Buffer or Terminal Dependent Data area in other
systems. I believe you may be confusing Classes and Objects here. The CLASS
associates Properties with the Methods which manipulate them; the OBJECTS
simply represent instances of  the data (the methods are not necessarily
duplicated; it depends on the system. An ActiveX component, for example, may
have many instances of data (all pointed at by Object References), but only
ONE re-usable copy of the code. It is analogous to CICS or Taskmaster if you
want to think of it like that. Remember, the OO CLASS represents the "cookie
cutter" (HATE that analogy!) and the OBJECTS are the "cookies" that get
stamped out of it.

As for "trusting" the data, OO Classes contain Validation Methods (well,
mine do...<G>) just like any other program would...

>>>
>Programs are a scarce resource. Data is overwhelmingly changable,
>wherease the programs do not need to be. A small subset of programming
>function may be able to cope with vast amounts of data and data
>variablilty. OO has got it exaclty BACKWARDS.
<<<
With an estimated 5 billion lines of code being written each year in COBOL,
it is hardly fair to say that programs are a scarce resource. Programmers,
maybe (we certainly don't have 5 billion of those, last estimates, 2,000,000
COBOL programmers...(around half the population of New Zealand...<G>))

I would certainly question the wisdom of not changing programs when data
changes...I seem to recall something from the old days called a "System
Abend 0C7"... but maybe on PCs it doesn't matter...? ("Just let 'em process,
no-one will notice as long as it doesn't crash...")<G>

Seriously, I take your point that small subsets of code DO process vast
quantities of data. But I can't see the connection between this fact and OO.

I believe that many of the points above have arisen from a misunderstanding
of OO. I have great sympathy for this. It is a new concept to COBOL
programmers (even though it is based in the same ideas as structured modular
code). I had some difficulty with learning it (and I am not normally
backward at picking up new things). That's why I advocate learning Java. It
is simple and clear and strips away all the baggage. It is also DESIGNED as
an OO programming language. Once you master the concepts, OO COBOL becomes
much easier.

I would stress that I am NOT an OO Evangelist. I use it (the same way I use
all computer tools) because it does the job I want done and is definitely
the best tool for some particular jobs. I really don't care if nobody
reading this ever moves to OO COBOL or Java or anything else.

OO allows me to develop components. I can trade these for other components.
It is fun and it is interesting and it allows me to develop killer Windows
applications with very little effort. (If you would like a killer Windows
application, see my thread here on DECLGEN...<G>)

Joe, learn Java! (It will allow you to re-examine the points above...)

Pete.

http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO's Failure

```
In article <3abe943a_5@news.newsfeeds.com>, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" 
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam> wrote:

<snip>
> It is a logical development from Structured modular programming. (See the
> forthcoming article on "aboutlegacycoding" where I examine this at 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Surely, that's a matter for each to determine. But I take your point 
that you see the "Emperor" has clothes on. Let's just say that I find 
insufficient evidence to make that determination at this time. 

> I'm not quite sure what you mean by "overloaded operators". **Function**
> overloading is common in OO systems and is one of the forms of 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sorry, just my incorrect terminology. I believe I meant functions by 
your definition.

OK. So I have CLASS A which defines object AA which has function BLAH. 
BLAH takes two parameters of objects AB and AC.
Now I create CLASS B as a sublass of A (is that right?). I overload 
function BLAH and give it parameters AB, AC and I change the code to do 
something totally different.

When I come across BLAH in the code - WHICH BLAH am I refering to. No 
peeking at the CLASS source now! 

> No you're not!
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
This may be true in COBOL OO, but Smalltalk - the original OO requires 
GET and SET methods. Obviously Newer implementations are doing away with 
this requirement.

<snip>
> >This is plainly rediculous. Who needs to
> >write a MOVE for every variable in a object. One ends up writing a new
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Sorry I was not making myself plain. Too many thoughts at once!
OK. What I meant was that constructing CLASSES, Objects and Methods is a 
way of extending the inherent programming language with new commands so 
that the new command set is tailored to the problem at hand. Essentially 
a CLASS is a template for Data Definition along with Methods that can be 
used against the data. What you end up with is a set of methods whose 
names are hopefully related to the problem. The programming task can 
then be reduced to combining the newly minted methods with the objects 
to solve the problem.
E.g. Create a CLASS Screen. Define objects Screen, window etc.
Define methods MOVE, DISPLAY, SET, DELETE etc.
You see we have created extensions to the language to move windows 
around a screen etc. Now MOVE can mean MOVE a window around a screen as 
well as the traditional meaning. 
> 
> Nope. I don't find this to be a problem. What "proprietary problem 
> language"
> are you referring to?
> 
See above.
If in creating a solution for a bank, you create objects for accounts 
etc and methods of credit and debit etc. You ultimately end up 
programing in terms of debit and credit of accounts. Which is great 
whilst you are programming it, but very tricky when maintaining it.
If I find a bug in the debit method, I have to test it against all the 
objects that might use it, EVEN if it is only a bug in the local context 
and could be changed by a local fix.
What do I mean by this. Well suppose I have a creditor object that 
invokes debit against an account object. I discover a bug that only 
applies to the creditor objects use of the debit method. How to fix this?
> 
<snip about writing new code> 
> 
> That's exactly what you would do in structured COBOL... Find a module 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Actually, what really happens is that the programmer gives up searching 
the source library after about 30 minutes and just writes the code 
anyway.
> 
> >It is often said that OO is to programming what IC's are to circuit
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
No, But that's the great hope of OO isn't it. The holy grail of 
re-usability that the IC industry has used to reduce the prices of it's 
products.
> 
> >The COMPILER DEVELOPERs Slackness.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
When I write slackness, I don't mean that they aren't busy or 
overworked. They are just busy and overworked on the wrong things.
As for Standards. This is a free world isn't it. Whilst you have to 
provide the minimum standards as set forth by the language guru's, whats 
to stop you from adding features that they haven't got a clue about and 
would take 20 years to get into the standard even if they wanted it to 
be there!
> >Just why are there no statements to help build and maintain server side
> >WEB pages. Why are there no statements to allow TCP/IP communication.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> students rushing to embrace it? <G>
YES!
Can you imagine how big the manuals are that describe all the 
classes/objects/methods to do the above? Can you see students rushing to 
embrace it?
But more seriously, there are already functions and OO classes that can 
be called to do the above things. All I'm asking for is a bit more 
thought and integration into the language. The  problem of documenting 
the language is faced by Operating System designers and they solve the 
problem by breaking the manuals up into sets of related functions. This 
can be done by the language developers as well. A sort of beginners 
reference with basic language elements, then a Process related manual, a 
Web server manual, a GUI manul, a DB manual, etc.
> 
> >The FAILURE OF CONCEPT.
<snip> 
> >When you have trillions of pieces of data, associating them all
> >with a set of methods becomes unmanagable. What to you do when you need
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I don't associate them with a procedure, rather the running procedure 
uses the data as it has been programed to do from a functional design.

<snip>
I see what you are getting at.
The class is like a template that gets applied to the data to create 
objects. (very broadly speaking)
So in this case, until the data has been instantiated into an object, It 
does not have a pointer to the object it belongs to.

In which case there is no problem in referencing the trillions of bits 
of data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO's Failure

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T23:36:27+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ac07b83_1@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<josephk-109023.20370625032001@news.m.iinet.net.au> <3abe943a_5@news.newsfeeds.com> <josephk-B06FCB.21530826032001@news.m.iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph J Katnic wrote in message ...

>
>OK. So I have CLASS A which defines object AA which has function BLAH.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This is a simple confusion between overRIDING and overLOADING. I'm not going
to pedantically correct the terminology in what you wrote, as we both know
what you meant, but please accept that I may not respond with exactly the
same terminology you used.

Class A instantiates object AA, which uses the BLAH Method of Class A and
this Method requires object references to objects AB and AC.

Class B is a subclass of Class A (therefore inherits all of its Methods) but
OVERRIDES (not overloads) the BLAH Method.

The Object Method BLAH, defined in Class B, takes the same parameters as the
original Method BLAH in Class A, but behaves in a totally DIFFERENT manner.

This is all perfectly legitimate Object Oriented technology, irrespective of
language. I could post code samples for COBOL and Java but as you simply
snip them I haven't bothered...<G>

Your concern (If I read you correctly) is that there are now TWO methods
called BLAH which both have the same interface but behave differently. How
do you tell them apart?

The answer is quite simple. You don't.

You look at where the Method is invoked from and that will tell you which
one you're dealing with. It is no longer possible to invoke Method BLAH of
Class A from within an object instantiated from Class B, UNLESS you qualify
that you want this Method by specifiying SUPER. The default is to the
overridden Method in Class B. Here are some examples which should help to
clarify ...I have chosen Java because it is quicker to write than OO COBOL.

From within object BB (instantiated from Class B):

1. this.BLAH (AB AC)   Runs the overridden Method BLAH in Class B.

2. super.BLAH (AB AC) Runs the original Method BLAH in Class A.

3. BLAH (AB AC) Runs the overridden Method BLAH in Class B. (the default).

So there really is no problem with modern OO languages where Methods are
either default or self, or they are qualified to super.

Joe I really don't want to degenerate this discussion into an OOP 101
course, but can you accept that your knowledge may be based on SmallTalk,
the original OO language which is now nearly 20 years old, and things have
moved a long way since then? I'd really like to pursue this with you after
you have learned Java...but I believe there would be nothing to pursue.


>> No you're not!
>>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>this requirement.


Yes they are. And not just in OO COBOL. Both C++ and Java, which are
intrinsically OO Languages, can reference and set structures as parameters
to method invocation. And if it's "obviously", why are you using it as
ammunition against OO? We can agree it is no longer true that EVERY property
MUST have a GET and SET method, strike that particular objection from your
list, and move on....

><snip>
>> >This is plainly rediculous. Who needs to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>
Well, I thought what you said was very plain, but I'm quite happy to change
the grounds of the argument...<G>

>>>
>OK. What I meant was that constructing CLASSES, Objects and Methods is a
>way of extending the inherent programming language with new commands so
>that the new command set is tailored to the problem at hand.
<<<
Well, I have heard some OO programmers say that, but I don't necessarily
agree with it and it certainly isn't the reason I personally use OO. I don't
believe the capabilities of Java or COBOL are extended by adding new Methods
to programs written in them, any more than adding some new performed
sections extends the inherent capabilities of structured COBOL.

What adding Methods does is extend the capabilities of the APPLICATION and
that is a lot different to extending the capabilities of the LANGUAGE.
Sometimes programmers get carried away...<G> Not sure I want to argue this
one because we may have no issue. I don't really agree with this perception
any more than you do.

>>>
> Essentially
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>well as the traditional meaning.

But who decided to call the MOVE Method of the Screen Class "MOVE"? or
"DISPLAY" or "SET" "DELETE" etc.? Is it fair to blame OO Methodology if the
person using it chooses stupid names?

If I had to do the above (and it is very unlikely that I would, for reasons
I won't go into here) I would probably use "MoveIt" "ShowIt" "SetIt"
"DeleteIt".  These would then be referenced as "screen.MoveIt (x y)" or
"invoke screen1 "MoveIt" using x y end-invoke".

Is there likely to be any confusion between this and the normal facilities
inherent in the Language? Could you possibly confuse this with a COBOL
"MOVE"? (If you honestly answer "yes" then you shouldn't be programming...
<G>)


>If in creating a solution for a bank, you create objects for accounts
>etc and methods of credit and debit etc. You ultimately end up
>programing in terms of debit and credit of accounts.

The Business would argue that this is a VERY desirable outcome. At least
they can communicate with you without being blinded by technobabble...


>Which is great
>whilst you are programming it, but very tricky when maintaining it.
>If I find a bug in the debit method, I have to test it against all the
>objects that might use it, EVEN if it is only a bug in the local context
>and could be changed by a local fix.

Software is available in modern development environments, to show every
place where a given Method is used, but getting to your point... If the bug
exists in the local context only, then you fix it in the local context
because it ISN'T a bug in the Method, it is a bug in the way you are using
the Method. If the bug IS in the Method it will affect every object that
uses it, but it only needs to be fixed ONCE; in the Method code.


>>>
>What do I mean by this. Well suppose I have a creditor object that
>invokes debit against an account object. I discover a bug that only
>applies to the creditor objects use of the debit method. How to fix this?
>>
Easy. Fix the Creditor object's use of the Method. That's where the bug is,
you said so yourself.


>>>
><snip about writing new code>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
<<<
Not on my watch!

>> >It is often said that OO is to programming what IC's are to circuit
>> >design.
Not by me it isn't...
<snipped IC analogy because it is dubious.>


>>
>> Won't comment on this as I never drew the parallel with ICs...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Nope. Software cost reduction due to re-usability has NOTHING to do hardware
price reductions because of ICs and economies of scale in production
environments.

 If re-usability in OO software is the "Holy Grail" then call me "Galahad",
for I have seen it.

It is NOT a "great hope" it is a reality. But then, it always has
been...ever since we started writing modular software and saving the modules
in Object Libraries. I wouldn't mind betting that the place where you work
has a library of "Installation Standard" modules which are used over and
over again and have paid for themselves many times over.

All that OO does is further push this concept and make it even easier to
implement. Ironically, it wasn't until certain pressures caused the need to
have cross-platform objects and classes, so the code could be re-used in
different environments, that unifying standards were developed and OO
evolved into Component Based Technology. (Harps sound stage left...faint
sounds of choirs singing "Hallelujah!" stage right...<G>)

Nowadays, I develop components in preference to Classes. But I NEED OO to do
it. It is fundamental to the concept of cross-platform re-usable code. Even
with the advent of "field-levelling" .NET from MS, we will still need
standard component interfaces as provided by COM and CORBA in order to usher
in the new age of component based technology.

Summing up:

You started a thread where you postulated that OO has failed. I have
attempted to refute this allegation because it works very well for me, and
because I believe the points you cited were specious (we have uncovered at
least two misunderstandings at a basic level here).

In making this attempt, there is a real danger that the discussion will
descend into "AuntSally" or cocoanut shy (set 'em up, knock 'em down) and
that is to no-one's benefit (and I for one, don't have time to defend OO
here three times a week...<G>).

I respect 100% your right to believe whatever you like, and I believe that
by posting here you were looking for a discussion. However, I don't believe
we will achieve much more as your knowledge seems to be based on OO
technology which is long outdated. (Even SmallTalk has been updated)

I believe that your knowledge needs updating and I strongly recommend you
teach yourself Java (but then, I strongly urge EVERYBODY to teach themselves
Java...<G>)

Having learned a modern OO language, written a few working programs, if you
still feel it is a "failed technology" I'll be more than happy to discuss it
with you.

The fact is that whether we like it or not, agree with it or not, resist it
or not, OO is a NECESSITY for advanced programming. It opens the way to
components, and components are the Keys to the Kingdom...<G>

Pete.
```

```
I try to convert a few old cobol programs from Microsoft Cobol Version 1.12
to Fujitsu.
Now I used the "CHAIN" command in the Micorsoft Cobol programs to go from
cobol .exe program to another cobol .exe program passing information thru
the 'Procedure Division chaining ..........'  command.

In Fujitsu there is no "CHAIN" command.

Can anyone tell me how to solve this on a simply manner ?

Thanks.
```

```

"G. Feenstra" <gfeenstra@mail.com> wrote in message
news:985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl...
> I try to convert a few old cobol programs from Microsoft Cobol Version
1.12
> to Fujitsu.
> Now I used the "CHAIN" command in the Micorsoft Cobol programs to go from
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T07:30:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YwHv6.527$aD4.39234@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl> <99l7as$r12$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Being sure to immediately CANCEL each one in turn. :-)

    CALL "xxxxx"
    CANCEL "xxxxx"

If you need to make decisions in the modules about which other module
to call next, pass the module a 'signal field'.  For example:

Working-Storage section of calling module:

    01  signal-field    PIC X(?).

Procedure Division of calling module:

    CALL "xxxxx" USING signal-field
    CANCEL "xxxxx"
    IF (signal-field = "???")
        CALL "yyyyy" USING signal-field
        CANCEL "yyyyy"
    ELSE
        CALL "zzzzz" USING signal-field
        CANCEL "zzzzz"
    END-IF

Linkage Section of called module:

    01  signal-field    PIC X(?).

Procedure Division header of called module:

    PROCEDURE DIVISION     USING signal-field.
```

#### ↳ Re: a suggestion

- **From:** Rob LeClaire <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T21:11:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ABE1905.DEEC6340@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl>`

```
You might try this:  Write a small program and run it thru the Fujitsu
dialect converter, which is probably on your CD or you can download it
from adtools.com.  See what code the dialect converter substitutes for
your procedure then use that as a guide. 

EG:	Procedure DIvision.
	Begin.
		Call Program "Progname".
		stop run.

"Call Program" is the functional equivalent of CHAIN in ICOBOL, and
Microsoft/Microfocus supports it too thru the $set DG directive, so you
could presumably use either the ICOBOL or Microfocus dialect converter.
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T07:15:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ABEECA2.E2FBB135@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl>`

```
G. Feenstra wrote:
> 
> I try to convert a few old cobol programs from Microsoft Cobol Version 1.12
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can anyone tell me how to solve this on a simply manner ?

Chain was useful for CP/M systems because is completely overwrote memory
leaving the maximum size for the next program.  However, it was
non-standard.  The best way (IMHO) is to have a small base program that
has the interface data in working-storage that calls and cancels each
required program in turn.  Add a Current-Program and Next-Program field
to the interface data so that each program can specify what is to follow
when it EXIT PROGRAMs.  The base program should:

             PERFORM
                 UNTIL Current-Program = "FINISH"

                 CALL Current-Program
                     USING Interface
                 CANCEL Current-Program
                 MOVE Next-Program TO Current-Program
                 MOVE "systmenu"   TO Next-Program
              END-PERFORM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion - a comment

- **From:** Rob LeClaire <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T20:58:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ABE15F8.DAC9D41F@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl> <3ABEECA2.E2FBB135@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:
> The best way (IMHO) is to have a small base program that
> has the interface data in working-storage that calls and cancels each
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>               END-PERFORM

  If I may make a comment...  I have used this technique on other
platforms (ICOBOL) but had trouble recently attempting to use it with
Microsoft Cobol v3.  A main program calls each "sub" program, passing to
it some parameters as well as the name of the next program for it to
call (usually a return to the main program).  This worked ok in vanilla
DOS and seemed to work ok in Win95's real mode DOS, but it would crash
under Win98 with a stack overflow.  I never figured out exactly why, but
just came up with a different way of getting the job done.

  Please note that in the early days of ICOBOL (I'm talking CS-40 on a
DG Nova), this was the *only* way of moving from program to program. 
And with a 32KB limit on program size, most tasks were broken up into a
number of such small, chained programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion - a comment

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T12:30:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<985567053.1412739851@aklobs.org.nz>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl> <3ABEECA2.E2FBB135@Azonic.co.nz> <3ABE15F8.DAC9D41F@cinci-rr.com>`

```
On Mon, 26 Mar 2001, Rob LeClaire wrote:
>
>  If I may make a comment...  I have used this technique on other
>platforms (ICOBOL) but had trouble recently attempting to use it with
>Microsoft Cobol v3.  

MS Cobol v3 is fairly old now, 1987 ?

> A main program calls each "sub" program, passing to
> it some parameters as well as the name of the next program for it to
> call (usually a return to the main program).  

What do you mean by 'for it to call' ? Are you saying that each 'sub'  uses a
CALL to get to the next subprogram ?  It should be doing an EXIT PROGRAM to get
back to the main which would then call the next as required.

> This worked ok in vanilla
>DOS and seemed to work ok in Win95's real mode DOS, but it would crash
>under Win98 with a stack overflow.  I never figured out exactly why, but
>just came up with a different way of getting the job done.

MS Cobol v3 on Windows 9x would not surprise me by failing in mysterious ways. 
If, as you indicated, each was calling the next then stack overflow is entirely
possible.

>  Please note that in the early days of ICOBOL (I'm talking CS-40 on a
>DG Nova), this was the *only* way of moving from program to program. 
>And with a 32KB limit on program size, most tasks were broken up into a
>number of such small, chained programs.

I suspect that you are confusing CALL and CHAIN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion - a comment

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Rob Lec <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T04:03:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ABEBF6B.222FC949@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl> <3ABEECA2.E2FBB135@Azonic.co.nz> <3ABE15F8.DAC9D41F@cinci-rr.com> <985567053.1412739851@aklobs.org.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 26 Mar 2001, Rob LeClaire wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> MS Cobol v3 is fairly old now, 1987 ?

Agreed, but I'd perhaps prefer the term 'venerable'.  It has been a very
reliable compiler over the years, and combined with a little assembly,
has in most cases producing highly predictable results.  In addition to
commissioned applications, I have written a number of handy utility
programs which I still use frequently  each day.

Please note that the author of the original post is converting from MS
Cobol v1.12, which even predates Microsoft's repackaged Microfocus
product known as version 3.

> 
> > A main program calls each "sub" program, passing to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What do you mean by 'for it to call' ? 

Sorry, "Invoke" might have been a better word.  And I should have
referred to "subsequent" programs, not subprograms.

> Are you saying that each 'sub'  uses a
> CALL to get to the next subprogram ?  It should be doing an EXIT PROGRAM to get
> back to the main which would then call the next as required.

Normally a main program would CALL a sub program which would then EXIT
PROGRAM and return to the main calling program (or in some
implementations the verb GOBACK is used).  However, in a CHAINing
methodology, each program completely overwrites the previously executing
module.  In fact, in ICOBOL there was no CALL and no EXIT PROGRAM.  The
verb CALL PROGRAM was the equivalent of CHAIN.

> 
> > This worked ok in vanilla
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> possible.

To be honest, this was the first problem I had experienced with code
produced from this compiler.  I suspect that the problem was something I
was not doing properly, as the chaining logic was thrown together
hastily, and when it failed to perform, was abandoned quickly.  Instead,
I have converted these particular modules to Fujitsu COBOL.

> 
> >  Please note that in the early days of ICOBOL (I'm talking CS-40 on a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I suspect that you are confusing CALL and CHAIN.

Again, "CALL PROGRAM" is the equivalent of CHAIN.  Many implementations
do not support this verb, and I realize that it is non-standard but it
was quite commonly found in small compilers.
 
-------------------------------------------------
(To reply by email change dash to dot in address)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion - a comment

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-30T07:41:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC438BE.F9BB628F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl> <3ABEECA2.E2FBB135@Azonic.co.nz> <3ABE15F8.DAC9D41F@cinci-rr.com>`

```
Rob LeClaire wrote:
> 
>   If I may make a comment...  I have used this technique on other
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> call (usually a return to the main program). 

What do you mean by 'usually' ?  If sub1 is told to call sub2 then how
does sub2 get back to main ?  Does sub2 then call main ?  It should exit
program back to sub1 then sub1 exit program back to main.

> This worked ok in vanilla
> DOS and seemed to work ok in Win95's real mode DOS, but it would crash
> under Win98 with a stack overflow.  I never figured out exactly why, but
> just came up with a different way of getting the job done.

Calling will add to the stack, exit program will subtract from the
stack.  If there are not an equal number of call and exit program then
eventually the stack will overflow.

There is another explanation with MF/MS in that the CALL is by file name
(ie CALL "XYZ" will load file XYZ.xxx where xxx is .INT, .GNT or .EXE or
.DLL) but CANCEL is by PROGRAM-ID.  If XYZ.EXE's Program-id is not "XYZ"
then the CANCEL "XYZ" will not work.  If your Program-IDs do not match
the file name then the numbers of programs in memory may exceed some
internal constraint.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion - a comment

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Rob Lec <RobLec@cinci-rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T21:38:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC3AB56.C25787A2@cinci-rr.com>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl> <3ABEECA2.E2FBB135@Azonic.co.nz> <3ABE15F8.DAC9D41F@cinci-rr.com> <3AC438BE.F9BB628F@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Rob LeClaire wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I'm sorry there was so much confusion regarding this post.  I definitely
used the wrong terminology (my mistake).  The original post was about
CHAINing from program to program.  In the ICOBOL implementation, the
verb used to CHAIN is "CALL PROGRAM".  There is no CALL verb (and no
Exit Program either) due to the limited memory available and there is no
mechanism for returning to a calling program.  In other words, EVERY
program is a main program and there are no subprograms.  Please
substitute the term CHAIN where I used CALL, and substitute SUBSEQUENT
PROGRAM for "Sub" program.

The way it works is:

	PROGRAM-ID.  PROGRAM1.
	CALL PROGRAM "PROGRAM2".

	PROGRAM-ID.  PROGRAM2.
	CALL PROGRAM "PROGRAM3".

	PROGRAM-ID.  PROGRAM3.
	CALL PROGRAM "PROGRAM1".


> > This worked ok in vanilla
> > DOS and seemed to work ok in Win95's real mode DOS, but it would crash
> > under Win98 with a stack overflow.  I never figured out exactly why, but
> > just came up with a different way of getting the job done.

The curious thing is why the stack overflow *ONLY* occurs in Win98.

> 
> Calling will add to the stack, exit program will subtract from the
> stack.  If there are not an equal number of call and exit program then
> eventually the stack will overflow.

Again, no CALL was used and no EXIT PROGRAM was used.

> 
> There is another explanation with MF/MS in that the CALL is by file name
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> internal constraint.

Good point, and is something to keep in mind, especially for those of us
(like me) who "clone" programs and sometimes forget to change the
internal name.

But in this particular case, each program was compiled as a stand-alone
EXE.  And in the end, I assumed that I had made some erroneous
assumption, as the technique of chaining from program to program was not
the way that Microfocus was intended to be implemented.  

The author of the original post was faced with a similar situation...
that is, converting a system based on CHAINING to a method of CALLng and
RETURNing.  This can be a daunting task, as systems which had been
designed without the call/return constraint may tend to meander about
from one task to another, not necessarily having any main program other
than whatever happened to have been the point of original entry.

-------------------------------------------------
(To reply by email change dash to dot in address)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion - a comment

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T19:39:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a0o4a$nea$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<985536003.6270.0.pluto.d4ee7d82@news.demon.nl> <3ABEECA2.E2FBB135@Azonic.co.nz> <3ABE15F8.DAC9D41F@cinci-rr.com> <3AC438BE.F9BB628F@Azonic.co.nz> <3AC3AB56.C25787A2@cinci-rr.com>`

```
For those who are reading this thread but are NOT familiar with the "chain"
concept *but* are familiar with CICS,

   CALL is to CHAIN
      as
   EXEC CICS LINK is to EXEC CICS XCTL
     (or even <G>)
   PERFORM is to GO TO

***

I have a *VAGUE* memory that there was discussion EARLY in the revision
process about adding a "CALL but don't return" statement.  I can't remember
why it was not followed up on - but have asked the J4 chair in case he
remembers.
```

## Thread: Get Version of DLL with Win32API using NetExpress — 3 msgs

### Get Version of DLL with Win32API using NetExpress

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T18:05:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010325130525.08351.00000725@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

Does anyone know what the Win32API is for getting the version of a DLL ?  I
tried the GetFileVersion API, but that did not seem to work.  Plus according to
the documentation it only works on image files.

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Get Version of DLL with Win32API using NetExpress

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T14:47:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99lp0c$s1h$1@news.chatlink.com>`
- **References:** `<20010325130525.08351.00000725@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
Bob,

The following sample code will do what you want.  You may have to play with the
Hex bytes in the "move z'\StringFileInfo\040904B0\FileVersion'" statement, as
they relate to specific code-page and language identifiers.  All of our software
version info is stamped using the same code-page and english language.  Change
the "FileVersion" to whatever version info block key you want to retrieve (ie.
ProductVersion, ProductName, etc.).  I think I got all of the WS fields used
(code excerpt) - let me know if I didn't.

Hope it helps ...

Todd Lucas
Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net


Sample Code:

       environment division.
       configuration section.

      ******************************************************************
      * Calling Convention notes:                                      *
      *    66  - Win32-bit C calling convention - dynamic linking.     *
      *    74  - Win32-bit C calling convention - static linking.      *
      ******************************************************************
       special-names.
           call-convention 66 is win32nol
           call-convention 74 is win32api.

       working-storage section.
       01  ws-miscellaneous.
           03  ws-product-log-path         pic x(256) value spaces.
           03  ws-buffer                   pic x(2048) value spaces.

       01  ws-subroutines.
           03  cob32api-sub                pic x(8) value 'cob32api'.

      * Win32API call fields - GetVersionInfo:
       01  ws-verinfo-ptr                  pic s9(9)  value +0   comp-5.
       01  ws-verbloc-len                  pic s9(9)  value +0   comp-5.
       01  temp-pointer                pointer    value null.
       01  load-dll-ptr                procedure-pointer.
       01  win32api-rc                     pic s9(9)  value +0   comp-5.

      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      * This section will retrieve the product version info.   *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       1010-Get-Version-Info  section.

      * Load the Microsoft Version information DLL:
           set load-dll-ptr            to entry "VERSION.DLL".

      * Assign the C3 module name to query for version information:
           move z'C3TCPCAL.DLL'            to ws-product-log-path.

      * Check for version info - will return 0 if none:
           call win32nol 'GetFileVersionInfoSizeA' using
               by reference ws-product-log-path
               by value     +0 size 4
               returning    ws-verbloc-len
           end-call.

      * If no version information exists for this file then we are done:
           if ws-verbloc-len equal +0
               go to 1010-exit
           end-if.

      * Retrieve the version information resource from the module:
           call win32nol 'GetFileVersionInfoA' using
               by reference ws-product-log-path
               by value     +0 size 4
               by value     ws-verbloc-len
               by reference ws-buffer
               returning    win32api-rc
           end-call.

      * If API call failed then we are done:
           if win32api-rc equal +0
               go to 1010-exit
           end-if.

      * Retrieve 'FileVersion' information from VersionInfo buffer:
      * The Lanuage and Codepage Identifier ("040904B0") is hardcoded
      * because we always know that it will be in English for C3 mods:
           move z'\StringFileInfo\040904B0\FileVersion'
                                           to ws-product-log-path.
           call win32nol 'VerQueryValueA' using
               by reference ws-buffer
               by reference ws-product-log-path
               by reference temp-pointer
               by reference ws-verbloc-len
               returning    win32api-rc
           end-call.

      * If API call failed then we are done:
           if win32api-rc equal +0
               go to 1010-exit
           end-if.

      * Retrieve specific version information from version info buffer:
      * The Lanuage and Codepage Identifier ("040904B0") is hardcoded
      * because we always know that it will be in English:
           move low-values                 to ws-product-log-path.
           call win32nol 'lstrcpyA' using
               by reference ws-product-log-path
               by value     temp-pointer
               returning    win32api-rc
           end-call.

      * Move the version information to the log buffer:
           move ws-product-log-path        to plf-c3-version.
           inspect plf-c3-version replacing all low-values by spaces.

       1010-exit.
           exit.





Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010325130525.08351.00000725@ng-co1.aol.com...
> Good Afternoon,
>
> Does anyone know what the Win32API is for getting the version of a DLL ?  I
> tried the GetFileVersion API, but that did not seem to work.  Plus according
to
> the documentation it only works on image files.
>
> Thanks,
> Bob Hennessey
>
```

<a id="t-b45e797175"></a>
## Thread: BATCH COBOL/DB2 SUBROUTINE PROBLEM — 3 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: BATCH COBOL/DB2 SUBROUTINE PROBLEM

- **From:** awkisall <awkisall@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T12:23:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ABE53A7.C48E3504@qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<3AB31D47.13A7611E@qwest.net> <3ab375d5_4@my.newsfeeds.com>`

```
Thanks for taking the time to respond to my question, sorry I took so long--too
long?-- to respond.

The calling program performs a sequential read of a variable length file,
containing 3 types of records, received from another job. One of the record
types contains check data (and EFT, or direct deposit, data, but these
transactions receive transaction numbers from the same table--different row--in
the same manner as checks, so they are only mentioned here) which is processed
to produce an image file for printing.

The program does not know how many checks it will write in advance, and the
check numbers must be contiguous in 2 ways: within the run and spanning multiple
runs (process 1 writes numbers 1 - 900, process 2 writes checks 901 - 1000,
process 3 1001 - 1050, the next day process 1 writes checks 1051- 1800 etc.).

The calling program is designed to get the last check number used by any
previous process in its initialization paragraph by performing a call to a COBOL
subroutine that accesses a DB2 table with a field, last_trans_nmbr, that would
contain the last number used by the previous run (process 2 above would find
900).

We want the subroutine and the table to be used by more than one program, and we
don't want to insert a new row with each run, we just want to update the the row
for our system's checks (there are other rows for other systems and types of
payments, e.g. EFT) by updating last number used, by what program, and a
timestamp field.

So, in initialization the program calls the subroutine, gets the last number
used, adds +1 to it giving a working-storage field, ws-current-chk-nmbr.  Once
it has the last number used the calling program ends the call with GOBACK.  Then
it processes the input file and each time it writes a check it moves
ws-current-chk-nmbr to the check image and adds +1 to it for the next check.

After the file has been read, everything processed and all files closed, the
calling program subtracts +1 from ws-current-chk-nmbr giving ws-last-chk-nmbr.
It the moves ws-last-chk-nmbr (and 3 key field variables) to a record-include
with a format identical to the DB2 table DCLGEN and used in the Linkage-Section
of the called program for passing values, and calls the subroutine again to
update the the last_trans_nmbr column with the value of last-chk-nmbr.

In the subroutine I declared a cursor exactly as you suggested (...with hold for
select...for update of  last_trans_nmbr).  It is declared in the Data Division.

In the Procedure Division,

during the initial call the subroutine:

1. opens the cursor
2. fetches the last_trans_nmbr into a host variable (from the DCLGEN)
3. closes the cursor
4. moves the host variable field to the record-include field in the
Linkage-Section
5. ends with GOBACK

during the final call it

1.  opens the cursor
2.  updates the last_trans_nmbr
3.  closes the cursor
4.  ends with GOBACK

It seems like a nice enough design, but my thought is, and hence my original
question, that my initial GOBACK (or even close cursor) releases the table and
any lock I might have on a row.  This would allow other programs to access the
same table/row, get the last_trans_nmbr while my program is writing checks and
before it has updated last_trans_nmbr, and start their numbering processes using
the same beginning number.

Hopefully, I've explained well enough to adequately describe our problem.
Perhaps the problem is the design?

Thanks again for your time.



"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> You can hold the lock by using WITH HOLD in the Declare (DB2 supports this):
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: BATCH COBOL/DB2 SUBROUTINE PROBLEM

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T18:30:24+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3abee22f_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<3AB31D47.13A7611E@qwest.net> <3ab375d5_4@my.newsfeeds.com> <3ABE53A7.C48E3504@qwest.net>`

```
Comments below,

Pete.

awkisall wrote in message <3ABE53A7.C48E3504@qwest.net>...
>Thanks for taking the time to respond to my question, sorry I took so
long--too
>long?-- to respond.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>transactions receive transaction numbers from the same table--different
row--in
>the same manner as checks, so they are only mentioned here) which is
processed
>to produce an image file for printing.
>
>The program does not know how many checks it will write in advance, and the
>check numbers must be contiguous in 2 ways: within the run and spanning
multiple
>runs (process 1 writes numbers 1 - 900, process 2 writes checks 901 - 1000,
>process 3 1001 - 1050, the next day process 1 writes checks 1051- 1800
etc.).
>
>The calling program is designed to get the last check number used by any
>previous process in its initialization paragraph by performing a call to a
COBOL
>subroutine that accesses a DB2 table with a field, last_trans_nmbr, that
would
>contain the last number used by the previous run (process 2 above would
find
>900).
>
>We want the subroutine and the table to be used by more than one program,
and we
>don't want to insert a new row with each run, we just want to update the
the row
>for our system's checks (there are other rows for other systems and types
of
>payments, e.g. EFT) by updating last number used, by what program, and a
>timestamp field.
>
>So, in initialization the program calls the subroutine, gets the last
number
>used, adds +1 to it giving a working-storage field, ws-current-chk-nmbr.
Once
>it has the last number used the calling program ends the call with GOBACK.
Then
>it processes the input file and each time it writes a check it moves
>ws-current-chk-nmbr to the check image and adds +1 to it for the next
check.
>
>After the file has been read, everything processed and all files closed,
the
>calling program subtracts +1 from ws-current-chk-nmbr giving
ws-last-chk-nmbr.
>It the moves ws-last-chk-nmbr (and 3 key field variables) to a
record-include
>with a format identical to the DB2 table DCLGEN and used in the
Linkage-Section
>of the called program for passing values, and calls the subroutine again to
>update the the last_trans_nmbr column with the value of last-chk-nmbr.
>
>In the subroutine I declared a cursor exactly as you suggested (...with
hold for
>select...for update of  last_trans_nmbr).  It is declared in the Data
Division.
>
>In the Procedure Division,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>It seems like a nice enough design, but my thought is, and hence my
original
>question, that my initial GOBACK (or even close cursor) releases the table
and
>any lock I might have on a row.  This would allow other programs to access
the
>same table/row, get the last_trans_nmbr while my program is writing checks
and
>before it has updated last_trans_nmbr, and start their numbering processes
using
>the same beginning number.
>
>Hopefully, I've explained well enough to adequately describe our problem.
>Perhaps the problem is the design?
>
 Yes, the design is seriously flawed if concurrent processes are able to hit
the "control" table and pick up last_trans_numbr while a task is already
processing, exactly as you describe. You have attempted to prevent this by
locking the table row. (You are correct that you will lose this lock as soon
as you close the cursor, even if you specify WITH HOLD). The solution is not
to lock the table row, it is to re-think the design.

Supposing you had been successful in locking the row. The task attempting to
run would simply sit and wait...and wait....and wait...until the currently
executing task has completed and released the lock, or the database software
or the Operating System abends it. NOT a good solution. Although it would
achieve the desired result, the problem is that the held lock could affect
other rows (it depends on how your system is configured, normally a virtual
page is locked even if the programmer thinks it is "only" a row...)

However, from what you have described, only one program at any given moment
is "allowed" to use the number in the row  and other programs must not use
it until  it completes. It sounds as if you are batch processing these
blocks of checks (incrementing in WS is batch processing...) so why not
control it thru Job dependency? (One run can't start until another
completes). If that is not a viable option (any process can start at any
time and may need to use the row) then you could hold the lock by NOT
closing the cursor (GO BACK will not lose it, but closing the cursor will).
As mentioned above I really don't like this as a solution, but it is
feasible...with the possibility of the pending task being aborted...talk to
your systems people before you proced with this one.

To me, a better way would be to add another column to the row indicating
that it is in use. A Boolean flag would do it. You access the row, get the
number, set the "in use" flag to true and update, releasing all locks. Any
other task trying to access, sees the flag  and waits until  it is clear
(you would do a certain  (largeish) number of tries, interspersed with a 1
second wait, then notify a human... the row contains the name of the program
which has locked it , so you have no trouble controlling it. )

When the current processing completes, the program grabs the row, sets the
last used cheque number in the table,  clears the "in use" flag, updates and
exits. On its next access the pending task finds the flag is clear so away
it goes again, setting the flag and its own program ID into the table. This
involves minimum locks and is not too heavy an impact on the table
definition and the programs which access it.

Summarising how I see the options:

1. Ideally, drop the whole idea of programs doing blocks of cheques and and
let any program do any cheque, getting the highest current cheque number
from the output cheques table using the MAX function of SQL (as I described
last time). (This is a more transactional based approach and, although you
have indicated batched blocks of cheques are what is required, I would query
this with the Business and make sure it has to be that way. With this
approach there is no need for a control table at all. Of course, if the
volumes are large, then there is no reason NOT to use batch processing, but
let's accept that that is what it is, and not pretend it is going to run as
a transaction...)

2. Given that option 1 is non-viable and we must do batched blocks of
cheques:

2.1. Lock the table by NOT closing the cursor. EXIT PROGRAM should NOT lose
the lock as cursors are held within the database software, not the user
program, but check with your DBA (It's 15 years now since I was one...things
change <G>)

2.2. Use batch job dependency through JCL to control the processes.

2.3  Set an "in use" flag on the table row (this would be my personal
preference).

There are other options but I don't like any of them... Best is to review
with the Business the way cheques are produced and the volumes of them.
Maybe set up some batch runs overnight just specifically to produce batches
of cheques and then it can all be controlled by JCL.

Hope this helps,

Pete.





-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: BATCH COBOL/DB2 SUBROUTINE PROBLEM

- **From:** awkisall <awkisall@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-30T12:16:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC4E99A.B53CB6AD@qwest.net>`
- **References:** `<3AB31D47.13A7611E@qwest.net> <3ab375d5_4@my.newsfeeds.com> <3ABE53A7.C48E3504@qwest.net> <3abee22f_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Comments below,
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, we are batch processing.

You've helped a great deal.  Explaining the problem helped me too.  Obviously,
I'm new to DB2, have been attempting to write this little subroutine upon
receiving minimal specs from a senior analyst who's not much more familiar with
DB2 than I am (at least in batch), and my SQL was initially written with manuals
in front of me.

Ironically, my ideas have paralleled your suggestions.  After explaining what I
was doing, I thought of not closing the cursor as in your suggestion 2.1 (it
jumped out of the screen at me as I typed my 1, 2, 3 etc. outline--if I close
the cursor it's not going to be controlling anything!).

This worked, but, as you correctly stated, not in an ideal manner.  When the
first calling program is running, any other task attempting to use the held row
will wait, but only for 1 second of CPU time, then everything goes to hell.
After waiting for more than 1 second, the second program ends its call, somehow
managing an SQL return code of zero, starts writing checks at check #1, and of
course ABENDS because it is coded to do so if the check numbers aren't within a
certain range.  The calling program currently runs in less that 1 second CPU
time, so it "works", but this remains unacceptable.  And this is without the
possibilty of page locks, as you mentioned.  These would defeat the purpose of
the subroutine and table.

We'd also thought of a flag early on, but for some reason dismissed it in favor
of the row lock.  We have a meeting scheduled with our DBA for Monday, April 2.
I'm going to revisit possibly adding a flag column to the table.

JCL dependencies would be ideal were it not for the politics and human
(operators) elements involved.

Thanks again,

Greg
```

## Thread: Student's -- Sequential Update question — 19 msgs

### Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** Sarah Mellings <sarah@whoofmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T21:30:06
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com>`

```
hi
i am a student and needs some help to point me to the right direction
my problem is below and i have tried producing a code for it. Looks
like it is a standard seq update program but not simple for me!!
The code is just fine for the compiler as it is but my concerns are
highlighted in the coding as My problem (A)(B)...so on..
I would have been ok if there wasn't any negative ref no involved
as an end of file indicator..I have already done a similar program working 
ok
without a -ve ref no as a field to show eof.

*-------------------------------------------------------------------------
* Write a program to meet the following requirements:
* Two sorted sequential files A and B on magnetic media are to be merged
* to produce a third file, C, also on a magnetic medium. All records have
* the following format:
*
* FIELD 1     Numeric Reference Number (Key)
* FIELD 2     Stock Balance
* FIELD 3     Date (in the numeric form YYMMDD)
*
* A dummy record with a negative reference number is the last record on
* files A and B and is to be written at the end of file C.
*
* Records with the same key may appear on both file A and B in which case
* the record with the later date is to be written to file C. If the two
* dates are the same, the record with the larger stock balance is to be
* written to file C.
* ------------------------------------------------------------------------

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

 PROGRAM-ID.    SECAQ1.

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.


 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

 FILE-CONTROL.

     SELECT SORTED-FILE-A ASSIGN TO 'FILE-A.DAT'
            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

     SELECT SORTED-FILE-B ASSIGN TO 'FILE-B.DAT'
            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

     SELECT NEW-FILE-C ASSIGN TO 'NWFILE-C.DAT'
            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

 DATA DIVISION.

 FILE SECTION.

 FD  SORTED-FILE-A.
 01  SORTED-FILE-A-RECORD.
     03  A-REFERENCE-NO            PIC X(05).
         88 END-OF-SORTED-FILE-A              VALUE HIGH-VALUES.
******* Compiler does not allow me to code PIC S9(05) above
******* as i also want this key to be NEGATIVE for EOF 
indicator>>>>>>>>>>>>>My problem (A)
     03  A-STOCK-BALANCE           PIC 9(05).
     03  A-DATE                    PIC 9(06).

 FD  SORTED-FILE-B.
 01  SORTED-FILE-B-RECORD.
     03  B-REFERENCE-NO            PIC X(05).
         88 END-OF-SORTED-FILE-B              VALUE HIGH-VALUES.
******* Compiler does not allow me to code PIC S9(05) above
******* as i also want this key to be NEGATIVE for EOF 
indicator>>>>>>>>>>>>>My problem (B)
     03  B-STOCK-BALANCE           PIC 9(05).
     03  B-DATE                    PIC 9(06).

 FD  NEW-FILE-C.
 01  NW-FILE-C-RECORD.
     03  C-REFERENCE-NO            PIC X(05).
******* My problem is to hold a negative ref no *****
******* in this above OUTFIELD FOR NEW FILE**********>>>>>>>>>>>>>My 
problem (C)
     03  C-STOCK-BALANCE           PIC 9(05).
     03  C-DATE                    PIC 9(06).

 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

 01  W-NEW-REC-C.
     03  W-REFERENCE-NO            PIC S9(05).
******* Myproblem is to hold a negative ref no in this above 
field**>>>>>>>>>>>>>My problem (D)
     03  W-STOCK-BALANCE           PIC 9(05).
     03  W-DATE                    PIC 9(06).

 01  W-NEGATIVE-REC.
     03  NEG-REFERENCE-NO          PIC S9(05).
******* Please see paragraph TERMINATION for use of above field***



 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

 MAIN-PARA.
     PERFORM INITIAL-PROCESS
     PERFORM MAIN-ITERATION UNTIL
             END-OF-SORTED-FILE-A AND END-OF-SORTED-FILE-B
     PERFORM TERMINATION
     STOP RUN.

 INITIAL-PROCESS.
     OPEN INPUT  SORTED-FILE-A
                 SORTED-FILE-B
          OUTPUT NEW-FILE-C

     PERFORM READ-SORTED-FILE-A
     PERFORM READ-SORTED-FILE-B.

 READ-SORTED-FILE-A.
     READ SORTED-FILE-A AT END
        MOVE A-REFERENCE-NO TO NEG-REFERENCE-NO
        MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO A-REFERENCE-NO
     END-READ.

 READ-SORTED-FILE-B.
     READ SORTED-FILE-B AT END
        MOVE B-REFERENCE-NO TO NEG-REFERENCE-NO
        MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO B-REFERENCE-NO
     END-READ.


 MAIN-ITERATION.

     EVALUATE TRUE

       WHEN A-REFERENCE-NO < B-REFERENCE-NO
            PERFORM WRITING-FROM-A
            PERFORM READ-SORTED-FILE-A

       WHEN A-REFERENCE-NO > B-REFERENCE-NO
            PERFORM WRITING-FROM-B
            PERFORM READ-SORTED-FILE-B

       WHEN A-REFERENCE-NO = B-REFERENCE-NO

            IF A-DATE > B-DATE THEN
               PERFORM WRITING-FROM-A
            ELSE
               IF B-DATE > A-DATE THEN
                  PERFORM WRITING-FROM-B
               ELSE
                  IF A-DATE = B-DATE THEN
                     IF A-STOCK-BALANCE > B-STOCK-BALANCE
                        PERFORM WRITING-FROM-A
                     ELSE
                        PERFORM WRITING-FROM-B
                     END-IF
                  END-IF
               END-IF
            END-IF

               PERFORM READ-SORTED-FILE-A
               PERFORM READ-SORTED-FILE-B

     END-EVALUATE.

 WRITING-FROM-A.
     MOVE SORTED-FILE-A-RECORD TO W-NEW-REC-C
     WRITE NW-FILE-C-RECORD FROM W-NEW-REC-C.

 WRITING-FROM-B.
     MOVE SORTED-FILE-B-RECORD TO W-NEW-REC-C
     WRITE NW-FILE-C-RECORD FROM W-NEW-REC-C.

 TERMINATION.
     WRITE NW-FILE-C-RECORD FROM W-NEGATIVE-REC
     CLOSE SORTED-FILE-A
           SORTED-FILE-B
           NEW-FILE-C.
------------------------------end of coding--------------------------------
------------

My problem (A)(B)(C)(D)..seem silly and *may* refer to only *one problem*
Please pardon me for this.

TIA
Sarah. M.
```

#### ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T16:43:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99lsa6$o5o$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I am *NOT* saying that the approach you have decided on is the best way to
solve your actual assignment, but if your class has already had information
on "REDEFINES" - I think that will solve most of the problems that you
actually mentioned.  If you haven't gotten to them in your course already -
then you can look them up in your reference material - or you can use
SEPARATE fields for your numeric and non-numeric "values" and move
information between them.

The reason that I started out this note the way that I did was that it
(normally?  often?) isn't a good design decision to use the same field for
two "very different" things.  There may be times that this is the best
approach - or there may be reasons why you HAVE to do this, but if possible,
I would recommend against doing it - unless absolutely necessary.  It seems
to me, that you want the same fields to be used to do two different things -
and this OFTEN gets real messy as the application grows in complexity.
```

```
:>* the following format:

:>* FIELD 1     Numeric Reference Number (Key)
:>* FIELD 2     Stock Balance
:>* FIELD 3     Date (in the numeric form YYMMDD)
:>*
:>* A dummy record with a negative reference number is the last record on
:>* files A and B and is to be written at the end of file C.

Note: NEGATIVE number, not high-values.

It is always very important to read the specifications and not assume that you
know what things look like.

:>*
:>* Records with the same key may appear on both file A and B in which case
:>* the record with the later date is to be written to file C. If the two
:>* dates are the same, the record with the larger stock balance is to be
:>* written to file C.
:>* ------------------------------------------------------------------------

   [ snipped ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T08:09:51+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<985637842.177206@aklobs.org.nz>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <fqstbtoaunjondq03ba2715r45pi1319a3@4ax.com> <0C42362F73661FDD.E525C3BFA456E1D1.2247884F133CF3CC@lp.airnews.net>`

```
On Tue, 27 Mar 2001, SkippyPB wrote:
>
>Not to throw a monkey wrench into things, but if the last record is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>by reference number?

If it is BINARY or COMP-5 then a value of -1 has the highest collating sequence
as a bit pattern (all bits set).  

However, the spec doesn't indicate sorted, only that it will be in sequence
(except for the final record).  While a sort does produce a file in sequence,
it is not the only way to do so.

An actual dummy record with a negative reference, however, does seem rather
superfluous as 'AT END MOVE -1 TO File-Reference' would have the same effect
without needing the last record.
```

#### ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T08:13:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uSCv6.2722$fy.4081@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Sarah,

I think your problem is using the hi-vals 88 in the ref-no fields; they should 
be separate 1 byte ws indicator fields. If you take the 88s out of the ref-no
definitions and define them as pic s9(05). You may also want to verify that your
i/p ref-nos are numeric display (e.g. X'F1F2F3F4F5') and not packed decimal
(X'000012345F'). 

You can check for EOF either by:

1) testing for the negative ref-no, or
2) letting the system tell you that there are no more recs on the file.

No special processing is required to move the neg ref-no to the o/p rec,
just move it.

Hope this helps, Jack.



In article <tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com>, Sarah Mellings says...
>
>hi
…[186 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T15:00:00+00:00

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T01:35:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ABFF0AF.86DB6D42@home.com>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Sarah Mellings wrote:

> Shall i write the program again?, i am all confused now with all the
> answers, what would be the *standard technique* to solve this?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Susan,

Seeing as you asked, yes for your own piece of mind, I would give this one a
re-think. You picked up on the phrase 'standard technique'. The particular
problem is a 'classical' one going back to mainframes and mag tapes only - and
is designed to test your logic processes.

Don't throw away your existing code - but completely put it aside for the
moment.
I'm not sure it is used too much in the 'real world' but go back to a
student's basic tool, flowcharting. Get your template out and flowchart for
the three files. I haven't even referred to your question/answer, but a couple
of tips in that flowchart - which eventually become code.

1. Two input files - are you familiar with Level 88s.

    01 FileFlag1                       pic 9.
        88 File1NotFinsihed        value 0.
        88 File1Finished             value 1.

    01 FileFlag2                        pic 9.
        88 File2NotFinished        value 0.
        88 File2Finished               value 1.

2. Open input and output files and
        set File1NotFinished,
             File2NotFinshed to true

3. You'll appreciate several possibilities here File1 finished before File2
and vice versa. (I'm not going to confuse the issue but you could also use
Decision Tables - doesn't help you here, so check that out as background info
later - perhaps google.com or something in your library).

4. Depending upon the file finsih state, then of course in comparing keys
you've got to make a decision :-

    File1Finished ?    **** Both of these 'Read Next at End
    File2Finished ?    ****              set FileXFinished to true ( 1 or 2 as
appropriate)

    key1 < key2
    key1 = key2
    key 1 > key2

I think taking a flowchart approach, getting all you decision triangles
squared off, will lead you to re-code what you already have. Now any one of us
could knock off a quick answer for you - but doing it yourself, you will
finish up being real pleased with yourself, armed with a comprehension of the
logic problem you have solved.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
