##### ↳ ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T04:04:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HjUv6.3239$fy.5364@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Sara,

Please disregard previous msg.:-)

What I was going to say was that I have a dictum that has served me well over
the years: Know your data!

Using an edit tool look at the input files. Is your ref# comp, comp-3, display?
Is it 5 bytes long (comp-3, display), 2 bytes, 4 bytes long (comp)?

Look at the last record; is its ref# a minus#; is it all X'FF's; is the entire
rec X'FF's.

Are the files sorted in ascending or descending key seq? If the ref# of the last
rec is truly a minus#, they may be sorted descending (if you're lucky), if not,
that last rec process gets tricky. 
Remember, that minus ref# in the last rec is the big question to be answered.
What does it look like? Is it in sequence with the rest of the i/p recs?

Once you answer these ques, you can decide your approach. No, I don't think you
have to start over. I think you'll find the changes required relatively minor.
The foundation of the pgm is good. Its modularity will help you.

In my 1st post I should have also redefined your ref#s as, e.g.:
03  A-ref               pic s9(05).
03  A-ref-x             redefines
A-ref               pic  X(05).

so that you can move high-vals to A-ref-x at EOF. Again you may want to move a
true numeric value e.g. +99999, -99999 depending on the answers to the questions
posed above.


Don't get frustrated, what you're going thru is par for the course; a day in the
life; its what we live for. :-)

Regards, Jack          



In article <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com>, Sarah Mellings says...
>
>Shall i write the program again?, i am all confused now with all the 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T04:15:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9tUv6.16373$ue1.1383149@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Sarah Mellings" <sarah@whoofmail.com> wrote in message
news:tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com...
> Shall i write the program again?, i am all confused now with all the
> answers, what would be the *standard technique* to solve this?

It's like asking 100 artists to draw a 'standard' flower. You'll get
100 different drawings. Programmers are like dentists: technically
trained and scientifically oriented, but still creative.

> I mean question does say there is a negative ref no for eof
indication
> i.e.
> Please refer to the question:
> >>* A dummy record with a **negative reference number** is the last
record
> >>* on files A and B and is to be written at the end of file C.
> (not a separate field than reference number)

Here's the problem. Your definition of 'Reference Number' is X(5).
We assume your file looks like:
00001
00003
01234
77661
etc
with possible trailer records of
-00001 or
b-999     (b=blank)

the problem is that any concievable 'negative reference number' used
as a trailer would, because of the collating sequence used by the
compiler, come before live reference numbers in the file. If the file
were 'sorted,' then the trailer record (as defined) would be at the
beginning of the file.

There are three fixes:
1. Assume this is a 'trick' question and produce a file containing
only a trailer record.
2. Use a 'sentinel' record with a value guaranteed to never be
confused with data. In your case, HIGH-VALUES.
3. Let the operating system detect the end of the file - there are
codes the operating system returns when something happens (they vary
by operating system) (00=success, 10=end of file reached on a read
operation, etc.).

Since you probably don't want to do #1, and you can't control #2 (this
is common - often programmers have to deal with data generated by
fools ("It's mostly sorted")), I'd opt for #3.

JerryP

P.S.
Your problem becomes trivial (from a programming perspective) if you
can use the SORT verb or indexed files.

P.P.S.
This is not a COBOL problem; it's a logic problem. How do you merge
two files, not only by key, but by content. That is, when there are
duplicate keys. It is an enhancement of the problem of merging two
files with no duplicate keys. If you like this, wait 'til you see the
next step.


>
> tia
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.help.com/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T09:31:24+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5le0ct0p8ds9kk3tvt5if284m4atvfgftv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com> <9tUv6.16373$ue1.1383149@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Tue, 27 Mar 2001 04:15:01 GMT "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote:

:>"Sarah Mellings" <sarah@whoofmail.com> wrote in message
:>news:tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com...

:>> Shall i write the program again?, i am all confused now with all the
:>> answers, what would be the *standard technique* to solve this?

:>It's like asking 100 artists to draw a 'standard' flower. You'll get
:>100 different drawings. Programmers are like dentists: technically
:>trained and scientifically oriented, but still creative.

Not really.

It is more "draw a red flower with a circumference of 8 inches, using number
two red color, with eight petals each of an oval shape with a length of etc.".

The programmer may choose how to formulate the color, in what order to draw
the picture, whether to sketch first, etc. but the final results of the work
should look the same.

Of course, if the spec is not complete, one would expect different results.

:>> I mean question does say there is a negative ref no for eof
:>indication
:>> i.e.
:>> Please refer to the question:
:>> >>* A dummy record with a **negative reference number** is the last
:>record
:>> >>* on files A and B and is to be written at the end of file C.
:>> (not a separate field than reference number)

:>Here's the problem. Your definition of 'Reference Number' is X(5).
:>We assume your file looks like:
:>00001
:>00003
:>01234
:>77661
:>etc
:>with possible trailer records of
:>-00001 or
:>b-999     (b=blank)

:>the problem is that any concievable 'negative reference number' used
:>as a trailer would, because of the collating sequence used by the
:>compiler, come before live reference numbers in the file. If the file
:>were 'sorted,' then the trailer record (as defined) would be at the
:>beginning of the file.

Or all programs which use this file know what it looks like and always put the
negative number when complete.

Perhaps they use this method to verify that the file creation program executed
properly and/or that the tape was not damaged in a way to generate a false end
of file.

:>There are three fixes:
:>1. Assume this is a 'trick' question and produce a file containing
:>only a trailer record.

And lose a letter grade (or, in the real world, lose a job/get a bad
reputation)?

If the spec is not clear, one goes back to the end-user/instructor and asks
for further details.

:>2. Use a 'sentinel' record with a value guaranteed to never be
:>confused with data. In your case, HIGH-VALUES.

But that is not the spec.

What about other programs that use this file and expect a negative number?

Unless one is the only user of a file one does not change the file description
merely to make a programming job easier.

:>3. Let the operating system detect the end of the file - there are
:>codes the operating system returns when something happens (they vary
:>by operating system) (00=success, 10=end of file reached on a read
:>operation, etc.).

Perhaps the lack of the negative number record indicates an error condition?

Perhaps a multi-reel unlabeled tape file where by terminating at end-of-file
one has ignored the records from the next tape? 

:>Since you probably don't want to do #1, and you can't control #2 (this
:>is common - often programmers have to deal with data generated by
:>fools ("It's mostly sorted")), I'd opt for #3.

But you have altered the spec.

If the spec is not clear one goes back to the end-users who have requested the
program and clarifies the points. A good programmer does not unilaterally
alter a spec to make the programming job easier.

:>JerryP

:>P.S.
:>Your problem becomes trivial (from a programming perspective) if you
:>can use the SORT verb or indexed files.

1. The file is already described as sorted. Why would the SORT verb be needed?

2. Perhaps both the source and resulting files are used on a completely
different computer system which does not use the same form of indexed files? 

:>P.P.S.
:>This is not a COBOL problem; it's a logic problem. How do you merge
:>two files, not only by key, but by content. That is, when there are
:>duplicate keys. It is an enhancement of the problem of merging two
:>files with no duplicate keys. If you like this, wait 'til you see the
:>next step.

I think a bigger point of this was "can one read and understand a spec".

That is a big issue. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student's (Sarah: DO NOT READ)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T13:35:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dG0w6.626$Uw.77905@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com> <9tUv6.16373$ue1.1383149@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <5le0ct0p8ds9kk3tvt5if284m4atvfgftv@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com

>
> :>P.S.
> :>Your problem becomes trivial (from a programming perspective) if
you
> :>can use the SORT verb or indexed files.
>
> 1. The file is already described as sorted. Why would the SORT verb
be needed?

The 'description' is equivocal, as we pointed out. If the layout is as
offered, the file is not sorted; if the file is, in fact, sorted, then
the layout is wrong. But, assuming facts not yet in evidence:

Feed both files as input to a sort routine. Do the match logic on the
sort return. Or, use one file to build an indexed file and use the
other to update it. In both cases, write an output file from the
result, sticking a negative number (or a Fiat muffler, or a happy
face, or whatever the user wanted) as the last record.

And, what the heck, I'd drop in a few lines the CHECK whether the
input files are really sorted. I don't trust them bastards! Using the
SORT verb or an indexed file means I don't really care whether the
input is sorted.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student's (Sarah: DO NOT READ)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T17:01:17+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16a1ctgnk9oqq7bq8f44vpmqmgkmol2uu9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com> <9tUv6.16373$ue1.1383149@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <5le0ct0p8ds9kk3tvt5if284m4atvfgftv@4ax.com> <dG0w6.626$Uw.77905@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Tue, 27 Mar 2001 13:35:05 GMT "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote:

:>"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com

:>> :>P.S.
:>> :>Your problem becomes trivial (from a programming perspective) if
:>you
:>> :>can use the SORT verb or indexed files.

:>> 1. The file is already described as sorted. Why would the SORT verb
:>be needed?

:>The 'description' is equivocal, as we pointed out. If the layout is as
:>offered, the file is not sorted; if the file is, in fact, sorted, then
:>the layout is wrong. But, assuming facts not yet in evidence:

:>Feed both files as input to a sort routine. Do the match logic on the
:>sort return.

What if the files are stacked with a record with a negative value separating
the stacks, i.e., this program reads the first stack, and another program
skips until the negative number and then starts processing?

You cannot make such decisions without knowing what the data looks like. The
data was purported as being in sequence.

:>             Or, use one file to build an indexed file and use the
:>other to update it. In both cases, write an output file from the
:>result, sticking a negative number (or a Fiat muffler, or a happy
:>face, or whatever the user wanted) as the last record.

A valid approach. No problem in using an indexed file (or a hash table in
memory) as an intermediate stage.

:>And, what the heck, I'd drop in a few lines the CHECK whether the
:>input files are really sorted. I don't trust them bastards! 

Always verify sorted files are in order.

:>                                                            Using the
:>SORT verb or an indexed file means I don't really care whether the
:>input is sorted.

As long as you keep to the specs.

You have no idea how these tapes are structured, i.e., why the program is
supposed to treat a negative number as end of file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

- **From:** Sarah Mellings <sarah@whoofmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T20:30:04
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tc1u2cfab47m73@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com> <9tUv6.16373$ue1.1383149@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Original question:-
*-------------------------------------------------------------------------
* Write a program to meet the following requirements:
* Two sorted sequential files A and B on magnetic media are to be merged
* to produce a third file, C, also on a magnetic medium. All records have
* the following format:
*
* FIELD 1     Numeric Reference Number (Key)
* FIELD 2     Stock Balance
* FIELD 3     Date (in the numeric form YYMMDD)
*
* A dummy record with a negative reference number is the last record on
* files A and B and is to be written at the end of file C.
*
* Records with the same key may appear on both file A and B in which case
* the record with the later date is to be written to file C. If the two
* dates are the same, the record with the larger stock balance is to be
* written to file C.
* ------------------------------------------------------------------------

Right, now there has been some progress in clarity of this spec.
I asked the teacher:
1) Are the files sorted?
reply: yes, both file-A as well as file-B
2) how?
reply: ascending number of FIELD-1 i.e. Numeric Reference Number (Key)
3) If they are sorted in ascending key order, why is the -ve ref key
turns out to be the last record?
reply: I don't know - this question is supposed to test a programmer's
ability to code program logic. This is not a real world scenario.
In this case I will let you assume that this -ve record was added to
the files after the SORTING, and as a last record.
4) Are the files on tapes?
reply: magnetic medium, does not necessarily mean tape.

I hope this will help you all.

Thank you all very much for all the help. 

Sarah. M.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student's -- Sequential Update question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T22:00:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G9tw6.539$bY4.811@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<tbsoquk5aanv99@corp.supernews.com> <tbvd70egdvkg72@corp.supernews.com> <9tUv6.16373$ue1.1383149@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <tc1u2cfab47m73@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Sarah,

My previous post didn�t execute properly. This is a resend.

Based on the new info, it looks like the only change you�ll need is to change
the read pgraphs, e.g.:

READ-SORTED-FILE-A.
READ SORTED-FILE-A
at end
perform file-err-abend 
not at end
if A-ref-no is negative 
move A-ref                 to neg-ref
set  end-of-sorted-file-a to true
end-if
end-read
```

#### ↳ Re: HELP ME PLZ => read this ...

- **From:** "Anthony Borla" <ajborla@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T08:28:03+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h7uv6.3263$45.15741@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<hmpsbts103ufb03nqvo23doj6i4vvnv7lj@4ax.com>`

```
"BUD" <atirage@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:hmpsbts103ufb03nqvo23doj6i4vvnv7lj@4ax.com...
>
> hi i'm a belgian IT student
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hans,

Are you sure that you are going to score a higher grade if you add
so-called, 'cool things', to your program ?

My understanding of university assignments is that certain tasks are
specified, and specific marking criteria are applied to each of those tasks,
as well as to the project overall. I'm not sure though, that bonus marks are
normally awarded for simply adding extra facilities that were not specified
in the first place, and, in fact, you might even be penalised by having
added something that was not originally asked for, since this means you did
not adhere to the assigned specification !

Might it not be possible to gain a higher grade if you took greater care to:

* Ensure your program(s) is clearly, and comprehensively documented,
   both by comments within the code, and supporting external
   documentation

* Ensure that the 'quality' aspect of your code is high. For example:

  - Adhere strictly to any coding standards that have been set
  - Use consistent naming conventions for variables, and
    procedures
  - Use extensive error checking / reporting. For example:
    * ON SIZE ERROR checks for arithmetic operations
    * ON EXCEPTION checks when calling subroutines
    * FILE STATUS checks in file I/O
    * Checking for missing / incomplete / corrupted data

* Present a comprehensive 'test plan' outlining things such as:

  - Test data used
  - Expected and actual output results obtained
  - White and black box tests performed

In short, if you can present a project that does exactly what was asked,
does it very, very, well, and that it possesses qualities that make it easy
to understand and modify, you are, surely, bound to score a perfect grade !

I know that I did not mention any 'cool things', but I hope that at least a
couple of these suggestions help.
```

         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Why do we sing "Take me out to the ball game" when we are already
there?


Don't believe the propaganda about the oil in the Artic National
Wildlife Refuge as spewed forth by Interior Secretary Gale Norton.

## Thread: DECLGEN for MS ACCESS — 4 msgs

### DECLGEN for MS ACCESS

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T12:54:28+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3abe9445_5@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```
I have now completed the Windows version of this software.

If you are considering accessing MS ACCESS databases from any kind of COBOL,
you will save a lot of time using this.

It analyses your Access database directly by OLE Automation, figures out the
structure of your tables, "translates" this into Host variable definitions
and COBOL Record definitions for inclusion in your COBOL program, and (in
the version you pay for), generates a structured COBOL generic access module
or an OO generic access Class to maintain each table on your database. This
means it actually writes the COBOL for the "bottom" tier of your multi tier
application. It also means that COBOL programmers with no knowledge of SQL
can deal with tables on the database using the familiar record layouts they
are used to; the translation to SQL is done in the "bottom tier" generated
code.

This software is free to members of this Newsgroup for a limited period. (I
need a bunch of beta testers...<G>)

All you have to do is mail me and ask for it. I'll also send you a document
giving step-by-step instructions on HOW to use MS ACCESS from your PC COBOL.

Get it while it's free!

Pete.



-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

<a id="t-78b8c0fb7e"></a>
## Thread: index vs subscript performance — 19 this month / 22 total

### index vs subscript performance

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T02:10:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>`

```
as a member of an application performance tuning team I am interested in 
the relative performance difference between using indexes vs subscripts. 
I have written a few very short programs to test this and I would 
speculate that the difference is nearly a factor of 4  (actually 
subscripts use 3.75 times as much CPU time).  Time is MONEY and we are 
analyzing some of our largest resource "hogs" in an attempt to reduce 
them. We are using STROBE to do the analysis...

=====>>>  Cobol II rel 4
=====>>>  OS/390
=====>>>  IBM's largest mainframes...

ps...   we are migrating to Cobol/390...  will recompiling generate any 
performance savings ???
```

#### ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-25T20:38:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99ma30$sca$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>`

```
I suggest that you check out the IBM "tuning" paper at:

 http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/library/booksabc/cobpf120.pdf

that talks specifically about this issue.

P.S.  That tuning paper is a "couple of releases" out of date, but should
give you some guidance.

VS COBOL II goes out of service (in the US) at the end of next week, so I
hope/assume that when you talk about going to "COBOL/390" - you actually
mean IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R2 - which is the "latest and greatest"
release and DOES include some binary data "performance improvements"
```

#### ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** "Peet Grobler" <peetg@absa.co.za>

- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>`

```
In my experience, and I'm pretty sure everyone will agree, when moving to
better hardware, it's always recommended to re-compile everything. The
Cobol/390 compiler is probably better than the one you're using now, and
performance-tuned for the hardware. It will be worth the effort to
re-compile.

#### ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T07:50:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WPHv6.536$aD4.40142@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>`

```
"Charles Haatvedt Jr." <clastname@nospam.com> wrote:
> as a member of an application performance tuning team I am interested in
> the relative performance difference between using indexes vs subscripts.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> them. We are using STROBE to do the analysis...

Here are a few of points to consider.

1. The relative difference is very dependant on the compiler and
   hardware platform.  For example, because the COBOL standard does
   not specify how indexes are to be constructed, some compilers
   default to simple numeric pointers, identically equivalent to
   subscripts.

2. Whether to use subscripting or indexing in a particular situation
   is a complex issue, and the answer depends primarily on these
   factors:
   a. Cost of setting/incrementing/decrementing index vs. subscript
   b. Cost of reference using index vs. subscript
   c. Ratio of times index/subscript is set/inc/dec vs. reference
   d. Frequency of conversion between index and numeric field

In general, a program that does many references to a table for every
time the index/subscript is set, indexing will tend to be faster.
But if the ratio of table references to times the index/subscript is
lowered, particularly if an index is often converted to/from numeric
data fields, subscripting can be faster, even much faster (e.g. set
index to numeric variable, reference table only once.)

Another thing to consider is, in the tests we have done here in the
newsgroup, if you are using either indexing or subscripting to scan
a field character by character, it will probably be faster if you
use a numeric field and reference modification.  The difference can
be sigbificant.  But again, it very much depends on the compiler and
platform. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T11:04:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xGKv6.608$tG3.42424@news1.atl>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news> <WPHv6.536$aD4.40142@news2.atl>`

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>
> 2. Whether to use subscripting or indexing in a particular situation
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    d. Frequency of conversion between index and numeric field

The above is concerned purely with efficiency.  In practice, other
considerations such as readability and shop standards must always be
considered.  Just didn't want anyone to think I'm a 'Performance is
the Only Thing' programmer. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-26T18:07:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99ohjr$va7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>`

```
IHMO, indices will always outperform subscripts, simply because register
notation and usage is used primarily with indices. The only subscripts that
will even come close, are the ones defined as fullword binary, which in IBM
speak is S9(08) S9(09) BINARY, coupled with the TRUNC(OPT) compiler option.
The WORST data type you could use for subscripts is Display Numeric,
followed by Packed-Decimal. However, usage of fullwords and compile options
TRUNC(STD) or TRUNC(BIN) will reduce any possibility of efficiency to that
of the BRUTAL inefficiency of Display Numeric.

HTH....

Bill

"Charles Haatvedt Jr." <clastname@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news...
> as a member of an application performance tuning team I am interested in
> the relative performance difference between using indexes vs subscripts.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>           email ===>>>  chaatvedt  at  home dot com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T03:50:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<36Uv6.16278$ue1.1378062@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news> <99ohjr$va7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com> wrote in message
news:99ohjr$va7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> IHMO, indices will always outperform subscripts, simply because
register
> notation and usage is used primarily with indices. ...

I just performed an experiment:

PERFORM 100000 TIMES
   SET INDX TO 0
    PERFORM VARYING J FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL J > 10000
      SET INDX UP BY 1                            [Line 'x']
      MOVE 'A' TO A-ARRAY(INDX)        [Line 'y']
      MOVE 'B' TO B-ARRAY(J)                 [Line 'z']
      END-PERFORM
   END-PERFORM


With Lines 'x'  'y'  and 'z' commented (no MOVEs executed), the
overhead averaged 5.98 seconds.

With Line 'x' and 'y' commented, such that we move by SUBSCRIPT,
average execution time 11.22 seconds

With Line 'z' alone commented, such that we move by INDEX, average
execution time 11.55 seconds.


Conclusions:
1. Subscripting is faster by 0.33 seconds for 100 million single bytes
moved, 3.3 seconds per gigabyte.
2. It is doubtful whether anyone cares.


The tests were run using the Fujitsu compiler (debug mode) on an
900MHz PC.

Your results may vary, but as for me, I say to hell with indexes; they
should be banned from COBOL.  Where do I write?
```

that - in at least one common case - that indexing and subscripting
are indistinguishable. I think next I'll have a look at the SEARCH
verb. Maybe, just maybe, we can define indexes as 'archaic' (or at
least 'silly').

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>

- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news> <99ohjr$va7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <36Uv6.16278$ue1.1378062@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <f201ct8h7slmdmhnolcn8g4dcdag7h8ql3@4ax.com> <Es0w6.603$Uw.74978@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <mh81ctseo5hovj163i676679a8pf04koid@4ax.com>`

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com
>
> You have proven it in the case where subscripts and indexes are
identical.
>
> In other words, you have proven nothing.
>

As you say, I proved the case that "subscripts and indexes are
identical". Substantially more than nothing.

If someone can generate a common programming case where the timing
results between S & I are substantially different, I'd certainly like
to know about it.

So far, all I've seen is an IBM technical paper asserting that
subscripting could be 400%+ slower. It could be, however, that
advances in compiler technology since the publication of the paper
have diminished the differences. If I remember correctly, IBM timed
various data representations of the subscript variable (packed,
display, etc.). But that's not a fair test of S vs. I; let's see a
timing test of S vs. I with only the logic at issue. That is, define
the subscript as a COMP variable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T20:13:25+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g6a4ctg65t1s7g1qrfn2d1fmd88d4n2e8r@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news> <99ohjr$va7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <36Uv6.16278$ue1.1378062@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <f201ct8h7slmdmhnolcn8g4dcdag7h8ql3@4ax.com> <Es0w6.603$Uw.74978@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <mh81ctseo5hovj163i676679a8pf04koid@4ax.com> <9xlw6.4394$aP5.356855@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Wed, 28 Mar 2001 13:19:01 GMT "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote:

:>"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com

:>> You have proven it in the case where subscripts and indexes are
:>identical.

:>> In other words, you have proven nothing.

:>As you say, I proved the case that "subscripts and indexes are
:>identical". Substantially more than nothing.

Perhaps I wasn't clear.

With a table entry size of one byte, the subscript really doesn't require
manipulation to be converted into an index. In the case of a one byte table
entry one would not expect to find a difference between a subscript or an
index.

If the table entry was two bytes, the subscript would have to be doubled to
become and index. This would probably be done via shift.

If the table entry was 243 bytes, the subscript would have to be multiplied to
be converted into an index. Now we are talking some additional processing
time.

:>If someone can generate a common programming case where the timing
:>results between S & I are substantially different, I'd certainly like
:>to know about it.

A longer table entry (assuming that the implementation of INDEX is good).

:>So far, all I've seen is an IBM technical paper asserting that
:>subscripting could be 400%+ slower. It could be, however, that
:>advances in compiler technology since the publication of the paper
:>have diminished the differences. If I remember correctly, IBM timed
:>various data representations of the subscript variable (packed,
:>display, etc.). But that's not a fair test of S vs. I; let's see a
:>timing test of S vs. I with only the logic at issue. That is, define
:>the subscript as a COMP variable.

Multiplying costs. Depending how many fields are in each table entry you might
have a bit of work.
```

>
> With a table entry size of one byte, the subscript really doesn't require
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> become and index. This would probably be done via shift.

Pardon me if I missed something, but the above is only true on some
hardware platforms.  If the computer memory is word addressed (e.g.
Unisys A Series) what you say above would not be accurate. :-)

On other platforms (e.g. the now [AFAIK] defunct B1x00 series), it
takes precisely as long to do an integer multiply as it does to add,
subtract or divide.
```

Amen!  Having worked on both IBM and Unisys Mainframes, it didn't take me
long to realize that we need to be careful what we assume.  I have found
that subscripting on the Unisys platform is as quick or generally quicker
than indexing was on the IBM platform.  And subscripts are much easier to
find in Unisys memory dumps, than indexes. :~)

Denny

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T10:42:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99qfur$5o1$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news> <99ohjr$va7$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <36Uv6.16278$ue1.1378062@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <f201ct8h7slmdmhnolcn8g4dcdag7h8ql3@4ax.com>`

```
As someone who is familiar with at least one PC "optimization" and one IBM
mainframe "optimization" - let me say that they tend to work VERY
differently when it comes to "subscripts" versus "indexing".  In fact, there
is even an option in Micro Focus to "guarantee" that indexes *are* stored as
"subscripts".  (I believe one other PC compiler *only* stored them that
way.)

Bottom-Line:
  Deciding when to use subscripting versus indexing FOR PERFORMANCE reasons,
requires testing in "your environment" - with code that is identical (or at
least similar) to the code in question.
  Deciding when to use subscripting versus indexing for "maintainability" or
"readability" reasons, requires that you know what your "shop" standards
are - or at least what may or may not "reasonably" be expected of those who
will be working on the program at any time in the future.
```

#### ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <John_member@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T04:36:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pNUv6.3249$fy.5332@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>`

```
Hi Chuck,

Some other points you may want to consider:

1) I remember reading somewher that the breakeven pint is at about 70 elements,
beyond that, indexing is the preferred method.

2) index v subs isn't the only performance consideration. The distribution
of access into the table can also be a big consideration. To take a loaded
example to illustrate the point: 
You have a table of 100 entries accessed a million times; of that million 900
thousand access the three entries of the table the other 100 thou are evenly
distributed. Whether you use indexes or subs, three if statements before the
search will save tons of CPU.

3) If the pgm does a lot of calculations, etc. after finding the entry in the
table, CPU savings can be realized by moving the element to a work area
(unindexed, of course) and doing the calcs with the work field saving all the
CPU required to translate the index and moves to save areas, etc. 

Just my 2c, Jack.     



In article <MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>, Charles Haatvedt Jr. says...
>
>as a member of an application performance tuning team I am interested in 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>          email ===>>>  chaatvedt  at  home dot com
```

#### ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T19:32:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ac0e91b.64834306@news.open.org>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news>`

```
After reading the threads that this subject has generated, I think I
see a common opinion that regarding performance, the only appropriate
generic answer is "it depends".  Even on the same platform it still
depends because the nature of what you are doing with the table will
significantly impact performance, and in some cases can make
subscripts perform better than indexes.

If "it depends" is the final answer, then can we ever really justify a
standard regarding subscripts vs. indexes?

By the way, my mainframe shop here has no standard.  The developer
gets to code it either way, and no one really cares as long as the
results are correct.  Regarding the "Time is MONEY" statement, we have
a philosophy that the machines are so fast today that it would be very
wasteful of programmer time to force a programmer to use indexes when
they are uncomfortable doing so.

Pete


On Mon, 26 Mar 2001 02:10:32 GMT, Charles Haatvedt Jr.
<clastname@nospam.com> wrote:

>as a member of an application performance tuning team I am interested in 
>the relative performance difference between using indexes vs subscripts. 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>          email ===>>>  chaatvedt  at  home dot com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T11:03:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RCpw6.1976$aD4.114968@news2.atl>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1528611fcf33ddff989685@news> <3ac0e91b.64834306@news.open.org>`

```
"Pete Wirfs" <petwir@saif.com> wrote:
>
> After reading the threads that this subject has generated, I think I
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> they are uncomfortable doing so.

That's about it, Pete.  Good programmers understand the tradeoffs
between indexing and subscripting and will choose the one they feel
is best for the situation.  If the programmer is any good, they will
probably be correct most of the time, with very little extra penalty
on the times they aren't.

As far as shop standards, I believe it would be better to educate
the programmers on the issues discussed in this thread, and let them
use their own judgment in practice. :-)
```

Just a reminder that in less than one week now, support for VS COBOL II
ends.  This (of course) does *not* end support for VS COBOL II object code -
when link-edited and run with a supported LE run-time/link-edit library.
However, it does mean that (at least in the US) the last IBM mainframe
pre-LE COBOL "environment" is going, Going, GONE !!!
```

If you are surprised by this, someone in your shop hasn't been "keeping"
current with IBM's announcement.  The drop-date for support of VS COBOL II
was QUITE a while ago.

Yes, people will "still use it" (they still use OS/VS COBOL - and probably a
few ANS COBOL V4).  HOWEVER, if they have any "software" problems (not
application problems) - they are OUT OF LUCK.

FYI,
  IBM has already announced that a "soon to be delivered" release of CICS
won't even support the VS COBOL II (much less OS/VS COBOL) run-time
libraries.

Sure "old" object code lasts forever; even old compiler "hang around" - but
the longer you use them for "mission critical" applications, the more danger
there is of REAL problems.
```

## Thread: newbie--micro-focus personal compiler — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: newbie--micro-focus personal compiler

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T06:07:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC02E09.6AD91C5@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<mNDv6.12448$P4.1090384@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
count_zero wrote:
> 
> Hi group,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> this doesn't work?

This is what I find in your program:

200-PROCESS.
    IF LINE-COUNT > 50
        MOVE 0 TO LINE-COUNT
        PERFORM 110-HEADINGS
            AFTER ADVANCING PAGE.

The "After advancing" clause is only valid for the "Write" verb.  It
is not valid for the the "Perform" verb.

Generally, you would use the "after advancing" clause on Write
statements for your report file.  The first line on a new page would
be generated by a "Write (record-name) after advancing page"
statement.  All other lines would be written after advancing 1, 2, or
3 lines, depending on the desired vertical spacing.

Your report lines defined in working-storage always have a final
80-byte filler field, but the FD for your output file specifies
80-byte records.  Your print lines are too long for the output record,
so they are being truncated.

Regards,
```

```
Hi guys, a simple question.
Hoping it is the right place to write it.
I'm a perl programmer and in these days I'd need to play with a RM/COBOL
database. I've searched some modules to do the job but, gosh, I didn't
find anything. I've found a document that describe how RM/COBOL archives
are composed but is not an easy work to do.
So my question: anyone has some code written in perl (or C is the same) to
deal with those type of archives?
I need nothing complex , only procedure to query the database and , if
possible, to update some entries.
Please send me a copy of the replay via email please.

Many thanks to everybody 

Mauro Fontana
Undergraduate Student of CS Bologna - Italy
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL and PERL

wrote:

> Hi guys, a simple question.
> Hoping it is the right place to write it.

The easiest way is to write the code in COBOL and compile it with the 
RM/COBOL compiler. Outside of that, contact the manufacturer of the 
compiler and find out if you can get some ODBC drivers that will work 
with the RM/COBOL data files.
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL and PERL

- **From:** "John Bradley" <k5mwe@austin.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T04:32:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8Vyw6.3933$wx.464528@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.LNX.4.21.0103270837001.5072-100000@ambrogio.cs.unibo.it>`

```
Dear Mauro,

What you need is Liant's Relativity product.  It will allow you to define a
relational database representation of the RM/COBOL files, and then access
(including modification of) the data via ODBC.  You should probably contact
RM Italia, at http://195.103.161.170/rmi/rmitalia.htm

Regards,
John


"Mauro Fontana" <fontana@cs.unibo.it> wrote in message
news:Pine.LNX.4.21.0103270837001.5072-100000@ambrogio.cs.unibo.it...
> Hi guys, a simple question.
> Hoping it is the right place to write it.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### COBOL - 2 YEAR CONTRACT - BROOKLYN, NY

- **From:** beau@nyc-search.com
- **Date:** 2001-03-27T13:16:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC0D91A.38625B46@nyc-search.com>`

```
�
COBOL - 2 YEAR CONTRACT - BROOKLYN, NY

START DATE APRIL 1st - 3 POSITIONS AVAILABLE - US CITIZENS or GREEN CARD
HOLDERS ONLY

I am a recruiter, working with a recruiter on this and a few other gigs,
so third parties should not reply.

The location is Brooklyn, NY and the candidate should live in the
tri-state area or be willing to move here for the duration of the
project.

My client is the innovator of the industry's most advanced information
technologies, including computer systems, software, networking systems,
storage devices and microelectronics.

Money:� Pretty much anything you're used to getting per hour.

Systems/Tables Analyst (Universal Biller) - Business/Systems analyst
with conceptual knowledge of relational tables design, DB2, SQL, SPUFI.
Knowledge of COBOL/COBOL II� in an MVS/ESA operating system environment,
and skills in TSO, File-aid a plus.� Candidate needs to have the ability
to work independently in a team environment following direction from
development team lead. Very good oral and written communication skills
essential.

OPERATING SYSTEMS

MVS/ESA, Windows 95/98, or Windows NT

PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES

DB2, SQL, SPUFI

TOOLS

E-Mail (Lotus Notes or MS/Outlook) Microsoft Word, TSO/ISPF
```

(and no, DD - I don't need to include a rate or range of rates for this)

From one of the other lists that I follow:

 The following link is to an article that appeared in the Chicago Sun Times
on March 25, 2001 entitled "COBOL programmers in demand":

 http://www.suntimes.com/output/weinstein/wein252.html
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Programmers wanted

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T17:25:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78pw6.1001$cI1.7824@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<99rt6c$qdh$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <99rt6c$qdh$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>(and no, DD - I don't need to include a rate or range of rates for this)
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.suntimes.com/output/weinstein/wein252.html

From the above:

--begin quoted text:

"The challenge is not just finding veteran COBOL people, but finding COBOL 
developers who know the Internet," Payson says. "It is easier to train
veteran COBOL staff in the Internet than it is to teach dot-comers the
complex business rules of COBOL. For the most part, young dot-comers don't
know COBOL, nor do they want to learn it."

--end quoted text

Hmmmmmm... wasn't there an article recently which stated, more-or-less,
the exact opposite?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Programmers wanted

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T18:43:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hqw6.1028$cI1.8023@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<99rt6c$qdh$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <78pw6.1001$cI1.7824@iad-read.news.verio.net> <mfa4ctclr1covtu517f3o0i0g8r5ia2lei@4ax.com>`

```
In article <mfa4ctclr1covtu517f3o0i0g8r5ia2lei@4ax.com>,
Binyamin Dissen  <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:
>On Wed, 28 Mar 2001 17:25:23 GMT docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote:
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>I doubt it.

From http://www.infoworld.com/articles/op/xml/00/11/06/001106oplewis.xml

--begin quoted text:

The report begins, "Gartner has warned IS organizations that migrating
their mainframe COBOL developers to Internet and Java development would be
a painful experience. Here, we measure in dollars just how painful this
process would be." What's painful is reading the report. It totes up every
imaginable cost associated with retraining COBOL programmers in Java,
compares it with one of the costs of new Java programmers (their
compensation), and concludes that Java recruits cost about $20,000 less.

--end quoted text

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Programmers wanted

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T03:46:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010328224608.19033.00003984@ng-ci1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<mfa4ctclr1covtu517f3o0i0g8r5ia2lei@4ax.com>`

```
From: Binyamin Dissen postingid@dissensoftware.com 
>Date: 3/28/01 10:22 AM Pacific

>Programming is not that difficult.
>
>The bigger issue is to understand the business case that the programming is
>supposed to address.
>

Agreed.  There is one programmer in my group that supports part of the shipping
system.  This person has worked on this for over 2 years and still doesn't have
a clue how it works or what affect it has on the total functioning of the
business.  While I'll be the first to admit that the key programs are a bunch
of crappy code and desperately need to be rewritten (both for maintainablity
and functionality) there is no excuse for not getting the big picture in that
amount of time.

I know my system inside and out.  I know how it fits into the total scheme of
things and I try to determine how to make it better for the users and the
company.  I'm supposed to be training another programmer in the system, all
it's little quirks etc.  I'm tired of the blank looks and the why bother
attitude I get at time when I explain the how and the why of what changes or
enhancements are being made.   Ah well :)

Eileen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Programmers wanted

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@isot.n0spam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T23:13:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ac2c4da$0$9166@wodc7nh6.news.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<mfa4ctclr1covtu517f3o0i0g8r5ia2lei@4ax.com> <20010328224608.19033.00003984@ng-ci1.aol.com>`

```
Yeah- but what happens when you have 6 systems to support? Or a dozen, or
two dozen and they
range from legacy COBOL to Enterprise Java Beans? (*sigh*)

The world changes faster than we do, and sometimes, you not only don't want
to know a system
'inside out', you simply cannot afford to take the time to do it. I don't
think this is the case with
the person you mentioned, but it is the case with a lot of us.

I hit an average of 6 systems and 4-5 different major software systems per
day. All of which
have their own incompatible idiocies to contend with.

-Paul

You are my kind of programmer. I just spent two weeks getting up at six in
the morning, and comuting to a customer site so I could work as a
dispatcher.  Purpose: I am putting the final touches on a dispatch system.

Over the years, I have found my job becomes a thousand time easier when I
try to make my users actually like the system.  The easiest way to do that
is to learn what sucks at their job first, then fix it.

I got to sleep in today ... two more people bought a copy<S>.

> of crappy code and desperately need to be rewritten (both for

> and functionality) there is no excuse for not getting the big picture in
that
> amount of time.

> I know my system inside and out.  I know how it fits into the total scheme
of

- **From:** ar656@freenet.carleton.ca (Luis Gomez-Almeida)
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T21:38:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a0reb$f35$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`
- **References:** `<mfa4ctclr1covtu517f3o0i0g8r5ia2lei@4ax.com> <20010328224608.19033.00003984@ng-ci1.aol.com> <R7Gw6.562984$JT5.15589588@news20.bellglobal.com>`

I agree with the statements below. While saying that "programming is not
difficult" is clearly an exageration (probably with the intention of
increasing the effect), what is true is that the main issue is to understand
the business.  Can we imagine an Engineer building a solid, perfect bridge
for cars without any regards to the fact that what is needed is a railway
bridge?  Or a physician giving you the perfect prescription for an ailment
that you do not have, while not paying attention to what your illness is?
Or a cab driver that gets you in record time to a place that is in the
opposite side of town of where you needed to go?  I could go on, but you get
the picture.

I do not think that programming is easy.  But what I say is that in 20+
years in IT, 13 of which as a consultant/contractor, I have seen or heard of
many projects that went wrong, and many IT people failing.  Of all these
cases I only remember one where the problem was a person who was totally
unable to work in IT.  All the others were either personality conflicts, bad
data architecture caused by incomplete specifications, and ignorance of
user's needs.

Regards,
Luis

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Programmers wanted

- **From:** freewheelin <mano@mano.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-31T16:24:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<06lcct0c0jhdc4m4qcag39a2q691292snl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<99rt6c$qdh$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Tue, 27 Mar 2001 23:35:25 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>(and no, DD - I don't need to include a rate or range of rates for this)
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.suntimes.com/output/weinstein/wein252.html


Hi there, any chance you could post this article?  Would like to read
it, but it seems to be gone now.   I came across a similar article
(posted below) last March in a Minneapolis area paper.   A recruiter
makes an interesting point at the end, prefering to retrain COBOL
programmers over dot-commers.   Also, the rumor mill in my neck of the
woods expects COBOL programmers to be a hot commodity by year end
2001.  

TIA  freewheelin.

-------

Tech Watch by Bob Weinstein of Infotrack Inc., March 2001
(St Paul Tribune ?  - not sure)

COBOL PROGRAMMERS ARE BACK IN THE GAME

You're wrong if you think COBOL programmers are doomed to go the way
of the Edsel.
The world kept turning after Y2K.  The job market for COBOL (COmmon
Business Oriented Language) programmers suddenly plunged - and so did
their salaries.  But the declne has turned around, thanks to the Net's
transition into a shopping mall selling everything from toothbrushes
to tractors.
A couple of years ago, Bill Lockhart, 63, a veteran COBOL programmer
from Los Angeles, was hard-pressed to find a company that would take
advantage of his 30-plus years of experience.  His experience as a
COBOL programmer dates to 1966, when he worked as a systems
programmer.  More recently, as a database specialist, he worked on
several assigments for IBM writing in COBOL, PL/I, REXX and assembly
language.
"The tables have turned," said Lockhart. "IT [information technology]
industries need my COBOL skills to get going on the web."
"Most of the world's business data, approximately 75-85 percent, is
written in COBOL," said Bill Payson, head of Senior Techs, a Net-based
job bank for IT professionals in Campbell, Calif.
COBOL is used in almost all Fortune 500 companies.  Many of these
firms have a large pool of COBOL-based applications that are primary
business systems.  E-business requires these systems to be integrated
and connected to the outside world.
Paul Halper, director of traditional development solutions at Merant,
a Web-enabling training company in Mountain View, Calif., said: "If
all the COBOL programs stopped working, the U.S. economy would
collapse.  Nine out of 10 of the top internet brokers use COBOL with
CICS [Customer Information Control Systems].  Chances are when you use
an ATM card, you are starting a COBOL/CICS process."
About 1.5 million COBOL programmers are available worldwide.  "The
challenge is not just finding veteran COBOL people, but finding COBOL
developers who know the internet," Payson said.  "Our strategy is
based on the belief that it is easier to train veteran COBOL staff in
the Internet than it is to teach dot-commers in the complex business
rules of COBOL.  For the most part, young dot-commers don't know
COBOL, nor do they want to learn it.
```

<a id="t-641a7fd7f5"></a>
## Thread: Age Discrimination — 6 msgs

### Re: Age Discrimination

- **From:** Lao Xiao Hai <laoxhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T02:53:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC1C2AD.A7FEB872@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<kdbr6.103$4R2.1097@client> <3aad8bfd_2@my.newsfeeds.com> <Dior6.7144$6p5.641796@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3aae5182_2@news.newsfeeds.com> <WXwr6.4035$8a7.58211@news2.atl> <3aaf8204_4@news.newsfeeds.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

in response to a comment about it being against the law to be a jerk ...

> Well, in New Zealand it isn't... (I'd be locked up if it was...<G>) We have
> the most wonderful society of jerks, weirdos, eccentrics, arseholes,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> which the 20th Century gave rise to.

This is a wonderful short list of why I so much like New Zealand.   My most
recent visit was 1999.  Need to return for a Haka fix.   Nothing like a rousing
dance-along to Ka Mate to get one in the right frame of mind for taking on the
world. You did forget to include immigrants from the Planet Salo in the Crab
Nebula.   Must not overlook those peculiar but enchanting inhabitants.  :-)

Agree with those who understand that an employee's personal life is not his
work life.   It especially does not matter about a person's politics, ethnicity,

sexual prefererence, planet or country of ancestral origin, physical handicaps
unrelated to job performance, or even minor mental disability.  With regard to
the last category,  programming actually attracts people with certain mental
problems, and those same people are often able to turn that seeming disability
into an asset when programming computers.

The real test is for the manager to understand how to skillfully leverage the
differences into an effective team.   This is essential because so many projects

these days do require a programming team, and the members of the team must
learn how to work together.   Sadly, most managers are not competent enough
to do this well.   As the struggle to come to grips with their own inadequacies,

they often substitute stereotype and unconscious bigotry for those shortcomings.

The attempt to be humorous about certain categories of people by some authors
in this forum (nipple rings,  liberals,  vegetarians, etc.) only highlights the
deeper
issue that began as a discussion of "age discrimination."    The perception that

older workers will not be as productive as younger workers has long since
been discredited by serious research.   It has been correctly observed that
younger managers are often threatened by the idea of managing those who
are older and more experienced.    Many of these young managers are
inexperienced
as managers let alone as managers of those with more life behind them.
Therefore,
it is important for the older worker, and older worker job candidate, to put
them
at ease and help them understand that they are willing to be team players.  The
older worker must put that maturity to work on his/her own behalf instead of
taking a defensive stance early in the recruitment process.   Unfortunately,
when
one expects to be rejected on the basis of some anticipated prejudice, that
expectation often comes through during the interview.    It is the classic
example of a self-fulfilling prophecy.

Almost all managers want people who can do the job, especially in programming.
Even younger managers will hire older workers if they believe they can sustain
their own control needs and get good results.  We older workers must be careful
not to demonstrate a kind of reverse discrimination by assuming that younger
managers are not competent to evaluate our abilities correctly.   They will
see our prejudice toward them just as we might think we see theirs.

So, in addition to all the other people who we discriminate against, let's
include
those young programmers, young managers, and young entrepreneurs who might
just have the job opening that is perfect for you, if you demonstate your
maturity
instead of your resentment.

Peter. Kia kaha.  Kia menemene.  Kia manawanui.

Lao Xiao Hai
```

## Thread: Announcement: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2001) — 1 msgs

### Announcement: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2001)

- **From:** pazpax@earthlink.net (TCS2001)
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T17:39:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<pazpax-2803011239470001@1cust13.tnt36.tco2.da.uu.net>`

```

        MASTER THE NEW CHALLENGES AND DEVELOPMENTS IN SOFTWARE TESTING!

              18th International Conference and Exposition on 
                     TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2001)

                             June 18-22, 2001

             Washington, D.C. * Hyatt Regency (Bethesda) * USA
 
          CONFERENCE THEME: Meeting the New Challenges of Testing 

        Conference + Workshops + Tutorials + Exhibition + Networking 

  A printed brochure can be downloaded and printed in a portable document
format (PDF) at:

           http://www.uspdi.org/conference/printbrochure.html

                                 * * * * *
 
The TCS2001 program is designed to prepare you for the current and
emerging challenges of software development.

- Two 2-day workshops and eight full day tutorials on advanced topics 
  by leading experts of software testing 
- Thirty-eight concurrent and parallel sessions in eight tracks present
  the latest developments in testing
- Vendor Exhibit of products and services to increase testing
  productivity
- Birds-of-a-Feather sessions and Discussion Groups to share mutual
  interests and solutions
- Conference Volume provides a useful reference for solving problems 

The KEYNOTE speakers of TCS2001 will present: 

"Real Time Priority and Risk Management for World Class Software Quality"
   by Tom Gilb
"Is That Your Final Answer? Surviving Cross Examination in the Courtroom"
   by Warren S. Reid
"Efficient Testing with a Measure to Select Testing Strategies"
   by John Musa
"Educators Join the Fray -- Tester Training for Undergraduates"
   by Edward L. Jones
"A World-Wide Web of Intrigue -- Deceptive Testing"
   by Simon Mills
"Wrap-up Session: Conference Reflections" by Marnie Hutcheson

The TCS2001 web site at http://www.uspdi.org/conference gives you all the
details of the sessions, speakers and other related information about the
Conference and location. You can also register on-line at: 
http://www.uspdi.org/conference/register.html 

You can register, obtain the printed TCS2001 brochure or get other
information by contacting us:
 
Regular Mail: USPDI, 612 Ethan Allen Avenue, Suite 100; Takoma Park,
              MD 20912-5400 (USA)
Phone:        (+1) 301-270-1033
Fax:          (+1) 301-270-1040 
E-mail:       admin@uspdi.org (Subject: TCS2001)
Web:          http://www.uspdi.org/conference

                                    * * * * *
```

## Thread: student question fujitsu 4 win 98 — 7 msgs

### student question fujitsu 4 win 98

- **From:** Jim McNamara <mac3@slic.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC24E5E.B17C9510@slic.com>`

```
Hello-

Below is a description of an input file and a file containing
data for our next lab project.  I'm confused.  Our instructor
wants us to work with variable length records.  Why not use this
format in the data division instead??  Why try to define ISBN
twice??  Hopefully its just an error and I do understand.
BTW this is only one of the .dat files contained in our lab
assignment.

Thanks 
jim


* This is the way I think it should read
FD ORDER-FILE
     RECORD CONTAINS 9 TO 69 CHARACTERS
     DATA RECORD IS ORDER-RECORD.

  01 ORDER-RECORD.
     05 ID-NUMBER				PIC X(5).
     05 FILLER					PIC X(1).
     05 NUMBER-ORDERED				PIC 9(2).
     05 FILLER					PIC X(1).
     ISBN-INFO OCCURS 1 TO 6 TIMES
	   DEPENDING ON NUMBER-ORDERED.
     	   10 ISBN1				PIC X(10).


* Input file description from lab assignment
V. orders.dat, a file of books ordered. These records must be read into
a table.
	1-5	Student ID
	7-8	Number of books ordered (right justified)
	9	blank
	10-19	ISBN number of the first book ordered
	20-29	ISBN number of the second book ordered
	ï¿½	Repeats for the number included in 7-8

* orders.dat
12276 04 186100088X020170353X0764502980189285600X
17356 01 0137443927
19662 03 013085849803406529260201702894
21218 01 020170353X

93798 05 034065292606723145330201702894020170353X0130858498

#### ↳ Re: student question fujitsu 4 win 98

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T08:25:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<985811788.1538282090@aklobs.org.nz>`
- **References:** `<3AC24E5E.B17C9510@slic.com>`

```
On Thu, 29 Mar 2001, Jim McNamara wrote:
>
>Below is a description of an input file and a file containing
>data for our next lab project.  I'm confused.  Our instructor
>wants us to work with variable length records.  Why not use this
>format in the data division instead??  

Variable length records on disk only take up the space defined by the size
required to hold the actual data, they save space.  However, in memory the size
of the FD area or Working-storage doesn't (can't) actually change and must be
as large as required for the largest record.  So there would be no 'saving' in
having the WS area defined as variable and it would also be more difficult to
manage programatically because the 'Number-Ordered' would have to be set
correctly before accessing the IBSNs.

> Why try to define ISBN twice??  

It isn't.  What is defined is an area (ISBN-INFO) which will contain all the
info on each book ordered, and there may be up to 6 different books.  Within
this area is the ISBN (ISBN1).  You have ommitted the '05' on the table:

Note that this order is for 1 copy each of up to 6 different books.  If two
copies of the same book is required then it would have to be done by
duplicating the ISBN to give 2 books.

>* This is the way I think it should read
>FD ORDER-FILE
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>     	   10 ISBN1				PIC X(10).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: student question fujitsu 4 win 98

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T18:46:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99u0lv$6tj$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3AC24E5E.B17C9510@slic.com> <3AC28061.830F3B7C@slic.com>`

```
I think that you are having problems with "tables" rather than with
"variable length files".

What is described in your record layout is a "table" with 6 *identical*
entries for six different specific books.

Have you worked with tables (OCCURS) outside of files yet?  If so, I think
that all you need to do is think of this as a "single" table including 6
entries.  If you haven't worked with other tables yet, then you might want
to look at that in your text book or course material before trying to work
with this "file" example.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: student question fujitsu 4 win 98

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T16:44:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<985841436.2058437067@aklobs.org.nz>`
- **References:** `<3AC24E5E.B17C9510@slic.com> <3AC28061.830F3B7C@slic.com>`

```
On Thu, 29 Mar 2001, Jim McNamara wrote:
>
>> Why try to define ISBN twice??  

>>It isn't. 

>These aren't my programming specifications. They are the
>instructors.   ISBN defined twice below (10-19 and 20-29)???
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>        ï¿½       Repeats for the number included in 7-8

It isn't defined 'twice', it is defined 'number-ordered' times, the first
book's ISBN would be in ISBN-INFO(1) in character positions 10-19, the second
book in the order would be in ISBN-INFO(2) in characters 20-29, the third book
ordered would be in ISBN-INFO(3) in characters 30-39 and so on.

Image going to the sales counter with a pile of books and they will create one
variable record with the number of books in 'Number-ordered' and each book in
the array of ISBN-INFO with its ISBN in ISBN1(n).

>To tell you the truth, I'm still a little confused.

I guessed.

>
```

### Student Trying to understand Index files w table

- **From:** "For Example: John Smith" <f@r.c>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T00:33:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xpvw6.156867$p66.44447990@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Hi,  I'm stuck.
Could Someone Please Help?  I am having major misunderstandings with this.

It's a big problem so I'll understand if you want to stop reading now :)


I keep getting    "...E Expected file-name that does not refer to sort-merge
file..." when I
                                READ CUSTOMER-INPUT-FILE (below)
                            I'm not sure how many times the table occurs and
                              I'm sure there's more I haven't even seen yet!

This is the part I'm stuck with, following it I've posted a skeleton code.

I've been using http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples/Indexed/Seq2Index.htm
as a bare bones reference,
but I don't find it too helpful.

Thanks for anything...

^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
BEGIN QUESTION
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^


Create (build) an indexed customer master file from line sequential input

This program has as its input the line sequential file customer.dls.  Its
record format is as follows:
   1 -   5  customer account number
   6 - 30  customer name
 31-  40  customer telephone number
 41 - 49  monthly sales targets (number of cases)
  41 - 43 product group 1
  44 - 46 product group 2
  47 - 49 product group 3

The records of the customer master file (customer.di) have the format shown
on page 640 of your text.  Since this record format is used in several
programs it is to be implemented in your project using the COBOL COPY
directive.  You must develop the COPY code and use that code whenever the
customer master file is used in your project. The records must be defined
with case sales, case target sales and dollar sales defined as 3 tables.
They must be referenced as tables as well.

 For each input record read from customer.dls, a corresponding record will
be created on the indexed customer master file.  Fields for actual sales
figures on the output record are to be set to zero.  The key to this file
will be the 5-character customer number.

                    So I have:
__________________________________________________________

 01 CASE-SALES-TABLE.
    05 CASE-SALES-REC OCCURS ?? TIMES.
       10 COLA   PIC 999.
       10 DIET-DRINKS PIC 999.
       10 ENERGY-SUPP  PIC 999.

 01 CASE-TARGET-SALES-TABLE.
    05 CASE-TARGET-SALES-REC OCCURS ?? TIMES.
       10 COLA   PIC 999.
       10 DIET-DRINKS PIC 999.
       10 ENERGY-SUPP  PIC 999.

 01 DOLLAR-SALES-TABLE.
    05 DOLLAR-SALES-REC OCCURS ?? TIMES.
       10 COLA   PIC 9999V99.
       10 DIET-DRINKS PIC 9999V99.
       10 ENERGY-SUPP  PIC 9999V99.
__________________________________________________________

For each input record read from customer.dls, a corresponding record will be
created on the indexed customer master file.  Fields for actual sales
figures on the output record are to be set to zero.  The key to this file
will be the 5-character customer number.

________________________________________________________
Example of Customer.dls  File:

            00167Abner's Grocery          8185557095010020010
            00195Bill's Cash & Carry      2135551002050060050
            .
            .
            .
________________________________________________________


^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
END QUESTION
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
What I've done (I've tried to keep it REAL short...) :


 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. CREATE-CUSTOMER-INDEX-FILE.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

 FILE-CONTROL.
  SELECT CUSTOMER-FILE
   ASSIGN TO "A:\CUSTOMER.DLS"
   ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

  SELECT CUSTOMER-MASTER-FILE
   ASSIGN TO "A:\CUSTOMER.DI"
   ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
   ACCESS MODE IS RANDOM
   RECORD KEY IS CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM.

 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.

 FD CUSTOMER-FILE
  RECORD CONTAINS 49 CHARACTERS.

 01 CUSTOMER-INPUT-FILE.
    88 EOF-CUST-INPUT VALUE 'F'.
    05 CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM  PIC 99999.
    05 CUSTOMER-NAME   PIC X(25).
    05 CUSTOMER-TELE-NUM   PIC X(10).
    05 MONTHLY-SALES-TARGETS  PIC 9(9).
    05 PRODUCT-GROUP-1  PIC 999.
           05 PRODUCT-GROUP-2  PIC 999.
    05 PRODUCT-GROUP-3  PIC 999.

 FD CUSTOMER-MASTER-FILE.

 01 CUSTOMER-MASTER.
    05 CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM  PIC 99999.
    05 CUSTOMER-NAME   PIC X(25).
    05 CUSTOMER-TELE-NUM   PIC X(10).
    05 MONTHLY-SALES-TARGETS  PIC 9(9).
    05 PRODUCT-GROUP-1  PIC 999.
           05 PRODUCT-GROUP-2  PIC 999.
    05 PRODUCT-GROUP-3  PIC 999.


 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.


 01 CASE-SALES-TABLE.
    05 CASE-SALES-REC OCCURS ?? TIMES.
       10 COLA   PIC 999.
       10 DIET-DRINKS PIC 999.
       10 ENERGY-SUPP  PIC 999.

 01 CASE-TARGET-SALES-TABLE.
    05 CASE-TARGET-SALES-REC OCCURS ?? TIMES.
       10 COLA   PIC 999.
       10 DIET-DRINKS PIC 999.
       10 ENERGY-SUPP  PIC 999.

 01 DOLLAR-SALES-TABLE.
    05 DOLLAR-SALES-REC OCCURS ?? TIMES.
       10 COLA   PIC 9999V99.
              10 DIET-DRINKS PIC 9999V99.
       10 ENERGY-SUPP  PIC 9999V99.


 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 BEGIN.

  OPEN INPUT CUSTOMER-FILE.
  OPEN OUTPUT CUSTOMER-MASTER-FILE.

  READ CUSTOMER-FILE
   AT END SET EOF-CUST-INPUT TO 'T'
  END-READ.

  PERFORM UNTIL EOF-CUST-INPUT
   WRITE CUSTOMER-MASTER FROM CUSTOMER-INPUT-FILE
    INVALID KEY DISPLAY "ERROR IN FILE"
   END-WRITE
   READ CUSTOMER-INPUT-FILE
    AT END SET EOF-CUST-INPUT TO 'T'
   END-READ
  END-PERFORM.

  CLOSE CUSTOMER-INPUT-FILE CUSTOMER-MASTER-FILE.

  STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Trying to understand Index files w table

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-28T20:04:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99u56a$7kh$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Xpvw6.156867$p66.44447990@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <99u149$g4j$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <6Rvw6.156993$p66.44523299@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
The thing after you "READ" verb needs to be a file-name (specified in the
FD) and *not* a record-name (specified with an 01-level).

The OPPOSITE is true for the WRITE verb.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Student Trying to understand Index files w table

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T02:37:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tdxw6.5325$Uw.522783@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<Xpvw6.156867$p66.44447990@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <99u149$g4j$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <6Rvw6.156993$p66.44523299@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <99u56a$7kh$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <JXww6.157126$p66.44706043@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```

"For Example: John Smith" <f@r.c> wrote in message
news:JXww6.157126$p66.44706043@news3.rdc1.on.home.com...
> Thanks William,  I see the mistake and feel a whole lot dumber than
I
> thought I could ever be.
>
> I still can't get the copy statement to work, any thoughts?
>
You told the compiler what to copy. Did you tell the compiler where to
look? There are two separate ways to do this:

1. Explicitly within the code as part of the COPY statement:

COPY 'V:\COBOL\SECT234\CLASS-A\EXER4\CPYBKS\FROG.CBL'.

2. Implicitly thru some external setting - usually particular to your
system - such that when the compiler sees:

COPY 'FROG.CBL'

the compiler uses the external setting to prefix your 'what' with a
'where.'

These external settings usually take the form of SET commands or they
are added to the compiler options.
```

>                             I'm not sure how many times the table occurs
and
>                               I'm sure there's more I haven't even seen
yet!
>
> This is the part I'm stuck with, following it I've posted a skeleton code.

shown
> on page 640 of your text.  Since this record format is used in several
> programs it is to be implemented in your project using the COBOL COPY
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…

be
> created on the indexed customer master file.  Fields for actual sales
> figures on the output record are to be set to zero.  The key to this file
…[108 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: Student Trying to understand Index files w table

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T04:05:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010328230501.19033.00003986@ng-ci1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Xpvw6.156867$p66.44447990@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
What Bill is saying is you have to READ what you put in your SELECT and FD, and
WRITE what you put as the 01 of the output file. (BTW Bill - at my very first
programming job one of the old timers put up a poster with your comment in my
cube :D).

I suggest that you call your files FILE (Select and FD) and your records (01
levels) -RECORD or REC and then look at your code again.  This should help make
things a little clearer.

Example:  SELECT CUSTOMER-INPUT-FILE

FD CUSTOMER-INPUT-FILE
01 CUSTOMER-INPUT-REC

Also - what is the 88 level after the 01 in the CUSTOMER record (input)?  88's
are for nemoics for a switch or field in a record.

Example:
01  EOF-SW           PIC X  VALUE 'N'.
      88  NOT-AT-END VALUE 'N'.
      88  END-OF-FILE VALUE 'Y'.

IF NOT-AT-END
   do something

is the same as

IF EOF-SW = 'N'
    do something

Eileen
```

##### ↳ ↳ Something New in the draft Standard( was: Student Trying to understand Index files w table

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T09:19:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99vjph$lde$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Xpvw6.156867$p66.44447990@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <20010328230501.19033.00003986@ng-ci1.aol.com>`

```
I didn't want to confuse the student who is just starting - but for those
who don't follow what is in the draft COBOL Standard, there is now a *new*
format of the WRITE statement that actually *does* allow you to WRITE the
file-name (rather than the record-name).  There are even some "tricks" so
that if there are multiple 01-level records under the FD, it can figure out
"which" record type to write.

It will DEFINITELY take me a long time to get used to this new "enhancement"
(once it becomes available).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Something New in the draft Standard( was: Student Trying to understand Index files w table

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T17:03:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<99vpt8$c7n$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
Oh NO!  I can't teach my students to

	Read a FILE
	Write a RECORD

any more?

:)  Floyd Johnson

Oh Boy! Can't wait for that 2002 standard...

This is what COBOL really needs...simplification by confusion. It does my
heart good to know that all those years between standards isn't wasted. The
Committee has obviously been applying itself to stuff that really NEEDED
fixing...

Now, what about an ADD verb that actually inverts the sign and subtracts, or
a PERFORM that says "No, not now, I'm tired..." or a READ JOINED UP WRITING
verb...or...aww, What the Hell...

Pete.

    INVOKE "ONIPOTENCE"
        USING  JUST-GO-AND-DOIT-AND-QUIT-ARGUEING.

class in Version 7.  It does an inspect source-code, relpacing all errors
with new-constructs, then invokes a Java Applet that hires seventeen new
programmers over the web.  After that, it compiles and loads under 360
emulation, and runs a batch job that fixes everything.  I have the URL
somewhere here ... under all this paper ... maybe the cat took it ...
damn, where did that pointer go ...

Donald Tees wrote:

>

As an amateur practioner in OO - I can't help but think your invoke does too
many things. Remember simplicity ? Perhaps it would be clearer to have :-

INVOKE "ONIPOTENCE"
        USING  JUST-GO-AND-DOIT
INVOKE "ONIPOTENCE"
    "FINALIZE-ARGUMENT"

and for sheer delight, why not add a method

INVOKE "ONIPOTENCE"
    "PRINT-IN-REVERSE"

Just a 'Teddy Bear's' thoughts ?
```
