<a id="t-d1b9d27fdd"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and my college — 17 this month / 33 total

### COBOL and my college

- **From:** deek40@aol.com (Deek40)
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T22:20:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```


1)My school has just reported we are the final cobol students for the school,
they aren't teaching it next year.  While I enjoyed cobol they are saying cobol
is dead and they are adding visual basic and i believe java and python. 

2) A quick question, just for talk assume i graduate college and decide to
pursue cobol.  Right now I know i am far from knowing everything about cobol, 
this is my 2nd semester and i still get errors and other stuff but for the most
part understand the concepts and can fix most of the errors.  Is it absolutely
necessary for a undergraduate student to master the language with a semester of
it behind them?  Or would a company send for more training, because after these
past semesters in my class i only see maybe 1 or 2 people that mastered the
language the rest like me are either doing ok or doing terrible.  Is this
normal?

Thanks,
Deek
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T23:54:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3AC3CD86.A1429871@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```


Deek40 wrote:

> 1)My school has just reported we are the final cobol students for the school,
> they aren't teaching it next year.  While I enjoyed cobol they are saying cobol
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Deek

Deek,

Oh dear. Just for background - exactly where is the school that you are located ?

So you are really posing a two-part question (1) Is COBOL my scene (2) Where do I
go from here

(1) Is COBOL my scene

You can only know the answer to this. I'm guessing you have taken some aptitude
test somewhere, indicating you should do 'programming'. Having gone that route -
are you happy with yourself. No harm in you self questioning your abilities - I
like that ! Beats the bullshitters every time, in the long run. If you truly have a
desire to succeed at COBOL or any other language - plug away at it, the expertise
will come in due course. Just reflect - the 'old hands' here were spotty faced
youths at one time. We didn't descend from Mount Sinai with the COBOL commandments
firmly etched in stone. So first supply your own answer to this one - then go on to
(2)

(2) Where Do I Go From Here

DO NOT DROP COBOL - become an 'expert' (hate the word - try 'specialist') in
business applications. The quick look I took is that Python is primarily a
scripting language - extremely useful. (But Python may do a lot more than I
viewed).

The one you have to learn is JAVA - NOT instead of COBOL but to COMPLEMENT your
COBOL expertise. Check out the Kiwi's article (Pete Dashwood). Well put together
and he avoids being 'snotty' like he can sometimes be with me <G>. His article
points you directly at the route you should take.

I can't recall which compiler you are using - but definitely as an ADDITIONAL arrow
in your quiver - LEARN OO COBOL.

Follow these up as appropriate :-

        objectz.com  - COBOL and OO COBOL
        merant.com    - Net Express OO
        fujitsu.com     - POWERCOBOL OO (don't know correct web address)
        Pete's article  - http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-30T14:16:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ac48815_2@news.newsfeeds.com>`
- **References:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com> <3AC3CD86.A1429871@home.com>`

```
You didn't seriously think I'd let you get away with this, did you, Jimmy?
<G>

Comments below...

James J. Gavan wrote in message <3AC3CD86.A1429871@home.com>...
>
>Just reflect - the 'old hands' here were spotty faced
>youths at one time. We didn't descend from Mount Sinai with the COBOL
commandments
>firmly etched in stone.
>
You speak for yourself, you Scots/Irish/Canuck brown-arm man.  I was NEVER a
spotty faced youth. After being synthesized in Daddy's Laboratory during the
Great Thunderstorm, I tore off the restraints, rose up from the operating
table and headed for the booze cupboard...<hic>

After a long consultation with my friend Jack (from that distinguished old
Lynchburg family, the Danielseseseses...) I decided that computer
programming would be a good career choice. As a lot of people were outside
with pitchforks and fire (and I don't smoke unless you light me) I went with
Daddy through the secret passage and we finally got away. However the
experience has etched itself in my memory and to this day I still see people
with pitchforks and fire calling for my blood, especially after posting to
CLC...)

As for the COBOL commandments, well I remember telling God that Performed
Sections weren't a good idea, but He can be very persuasive... You tend to
want to stay on His good side. (I knew Lot's wife very well...nice girl but
innately curious. Funny thing was, she NEVER used salt in her cooking...I
guess that was Himself's idea of irony...)

I also wanted ALL the Commandments  (including the "other" Ten) on
MicroFiche but He insisted they had to be in stone. Nearly broke poor Moses'
back and he dropped the 11th one on his foot ("Thou shalt not swear") and
kicked it over a precipice before unleashing a stream of profanity...

>So first supply your own answer to this one - then go on to
>(2)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>DO NOT DROP COBOL - become an 'expert' (hate the word - try 'specialist')
in
>business applications. The quick look I took is that Python is primarily a
>scripting language - extremely useful. (But Python may do a lot more than I
>viewed).
>
>The one you have to learn is JAVA - NOT instead of COBOL but to COMPLEMENT
your
>COBOL expertise. Check out the Kiwi's article (Pete Dashwood). Well put
together
>and he avoids being 'snotty' like he can sometimes be with me <G>. His
article
>points you directly at the route you should take.
>
<<<
Well, thank you very much, Mr. Gavan. I didn't realize it was published,
although I knew it was in the pipeline.

I didn't 'avoid' being snotty, just had no reason to be...no-one was
misquoting, misunderstanding, attacking, or pushing my words beyond where I
intended them to go...(think about it <G>) I'm really a pussycat when you
get to know me...no, really...honest...<curls up and catnaps>

In Deek's case, I would endorse the sound advice Jimmy has offered (with the
possible exception of reading my article; it is unlikely to further Deek's
career).  I would add the following, Deek...

There is no need for you to "drop" COBOL. Besides, you can't really
"unlearn" what you spent considerable time battling with and acquiring.

As you might expect from my recent posts here and elsewhere, I would
strongly urge you to learn Java. It will introduce OO Concepts in a simple,
understandable way, and it is a great addition to COBOL (especially if you
are planning to work in the Network, Web Enablement, or Server areas.)


>I can't recall which compiler you are using - but definitely as an
ADDITIONAL arrow
>in your quiver - LEARN OO COBOL.
>
Yes, that too. But I would do Java first.



>Follow these up as appropriate :-
>        fujitsu.com     - POWERCOBOL OO (don't know correct web address)
It is: http://www.adtools.com

and well worth a visit...

Good luck in your enterprise and don't worry too much about the
short-sighted policy your school is implementing.

Pete.
```

```
Laboratory during the
Great Thunderstorm, I tore off the restraints, rose up from the operating
table and headed for the booze cupboard...<hic>

After a long consultation with my friend Jack (from that distinguished old
Lynchburg family, the Danielseseseses...) I decided that computer
programming would be a good career choice.

As a lot of people were outside with pitchforks and fire (and I don't smoke
unless you light me) I went with Daddy through the secret passage and we
finally got away. However the experience has etched itself in my memory and
to this day I still see people with pitchforks and fire calling for my
blood, especially after posting to CLC...)

As for the COBOL commandments, well I remember telling God that
Perform...Thru
was NOT a good idea, but He can be very persuasive... You tend to
want to stay on His good side. (I knew Lot's wife very well...nice girl but
innately curious. Funny thing was, she NEVER used salt in her cooking...I
guess that was Himself's idea of irony...)

I also wanted ALL the Commandments  (including the "other" Ten) on
microfiche, but He insisted they had to be in stone (Traditionalist...).
Nearly broke poor Moses'
back and he dropped the 11th one on his foot ("Thou shalt not swear") and
kicked it over a precipice before unleashing a stream of profanity...

>So first supply your own answer to this one - then go on to
>(2)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>DO NOT DROP COBOL - become an 'expert' (hate the word - try 'specialist')
in
>business applications. The quick look I took is that Python is primarily a
>scripting language - extremely useful. (But Python may do a lot more than I
>viewed).
>
>The one you have to learn is JAVA - NOT instead of COBOL but to COMPLEMENT
your
>COBOL expertise. Check out the Kiwi's article (Pete Dashwood). Well put
together
>and he avoids being 'snotty' like he can sometimes be with me <G>. His
article
>points you directly at the route you should take.
>
<<<
Well, thank you very much, Mr. Gavan. I didn't realize it was published,
although I knew it was in the pipeline.

I didn't 'avoid' being snotty, just had no reason to be...

No-one was misquoting, misunderstanding, attacking, or pushing my words
beyond where I
intended them to go...(think about it <G>)

I'm really a pussycat when you get to know me...no, really...honest...<curls
up and catnaps>

In Deek's case, I would endorse the sound advice Jimmy has offered (with the
possible exception of reading my article; it is unlikely to further Deek's
career).  I would add the following, Deek...

There is no need for you to "drop" COBOL. Besides, you can't really
"unlearn" what you spent considerable time battling with and acquiring.

As you might expect from my recent posts here and elsewhere, I would
strongly urge you to learn Java. It will introduce OO Concepts in a simple,
understandable way, and it is a great addition to COBOL (especially if you
are planning to work in the Network, Web Enablement, or Server areas.)


>I can't recall which compiler you are using - but definitely as an
ADDITIONAL arrow
>in your quiver - LEARN OO COBOL.
>
Yes, that too. But I would do Java first.



>Follow these up as appropriate :-
>        Fujitsu.com     - PowerCOBOL OO (don't know correct web address)
It is: http://www.adtools.com

and well worth a visit...

Good luck in your enterprise and don't worry too much about the
short-sighted policy your school is implementing.

Pete.








-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-30T12:51:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0D455311F8D9BF83.F93FCE8F33304347.A5CB880501B036FD@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<20010329172002.28981.00000810@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
On 29 Mar 2001 22:20:02 GMT, deek40@aol.com (Deek40) enlightened us:

>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Deek

Deek any college that has a computer science department head that
thinks Cobol is dead should be avoided!  Or at least boycotted.  Or
maybe shown the light?

Personally, I believe that 1 year of Cobol in college is not enough.
At the university I attended I was required to take two years of
Cobol.  I also took 2 semesters of RPG (hey, don't laugh..it was big
in those days), 1 semester of Fortran and I voluntarily took 2
semesters of Assembler (and have never, ever regretted it).  

What you take along side your computer courses kind of depends on what
type of business you'd like to work in.  Someone suggeseted going to
DICE.COM, entering Cobol and see what the required sub skills are.
Problem with that is a lot of those skills you can only get by on the
job training.  

Someone suggested that Java would be good to take.  I think you'd be
better served learning Unix first then Java.  Also, you should take a
fair amount of math, accounting and economics.

Good Luck.


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Why do we sing "Take me out to the ball game" when we are already
there?

DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and my college

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-31T20:40:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a64ef$vop$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<wSax6.2182$bY4.7651@www.newsranger.com> <20010331014848.01277.00001226@ng-fr1.aol.com> <b54bct852ahteged3d8cvkg20hekhmbvj2@4ax.com> <9a619t$9d$1@ins21.netins.net>`

```
Deek -
  The one question that I have for you is
      Have you been able to learn how to use your "interactive debugger"
yet?  This is something that I don't think is "well taught" in many
courses - and as I recall, you use a different compiler than your teachers
do.  However,  I think that the more you get into programming, the more you
will learn about the ADVANTAGES of a good debugger.

Just like a compiler is a LOT better at catching "syntax" errors - than a
visual check; a GOOD interactive debugger can REALLY help you with those
"hard to catch" logic problems. It never hurts to do a "reasonable"
desk-check of your program's logic, (often a good trick to manually "walk" a
problem thru the code that you have designed) but knowing how to maximize
the benefits of your debugger can save you HOURS of "blind spots" where you
know what your code was SUPPOSED to be doing - but just isn't.
```

<a id="t-370771c81a"></a>
## Thread: IBM and reaffirmation of the current ANSI COBOL Standard and Amendments — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### IBM and reaffirmation of the current ANSI COBOL Standard and Amendments

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-29T19:18:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a0moe$kfl$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those interested in IBM's position on the reaffirmation of the current
ANSI COBOL Standard and its two amendments, please see:

 http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/01-0251.doc

It states (in part),

"Reaffirmation of ANSI X3.23:1985 and its amendments sends a positive
message to businesses:  they can rely on continued existence of stable
compilers meeting a common specification.  Perception is important. Failure
to reaffirm creates a perception that COBOL vendors lack commitment to
provide such compilers.  That perception could lead organizations to make
decisions detrimental to their information processing investments, and could
have negative impact on COBOL vendors, tool makers, consultants, educators,
application providers, and programmers. "

Notice their statement about the "perception that COBOL Vendors lack
commitment to provide such compilers" *if* the Standard and amendments are
not reaffirmed.  Notice also that they do not EXPLICITLY state that this
perception is "false" (at least as far as IBM goes).  My *personal* guess is
that IBM will continue to provide such compilers (and prove the "perception
wrong") but if you are a current IBM customer, you might want to verify this
thru your "normal" IBM marketing and support channels.
```

```
But still, I am in total agreement with their statement.
```

```
items as tables.  however when I compile them I get Restrictions where
indicated.  There MUST be a way to go around this or my teacher would not
have assigned it.  Does anyone see where or what to do?

Tanks.


 01 CUSTOMER-MASTER-REC.
    05 CM-CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM  PIC 99999.
    05 CM-CUSTOMER-NAME   PIC X(25).
    05 CM-CUSTOMER-TELE-NUM  PIC X(10).
    05 CM-CASE-SALES-TABLE.
       10 CM-CASE-SALES-REC
   OCCURS 50 TIMES.
          20 CM-CS-COLA    PIC 999.
          20 CM-CS-DIET-DRINKS  PIC 999.
          20 CM-CS-ENERGY-SUPP   PIC 999.
           05 CM-CASE-TARGET-SALES-TABLE.
           $
restriction
       10 CM-CASE-TARGET-SALES-REC
   OCCURS 50 TIMES.
          20 CM-CT-COLA    PIC 999.
          20 CM-CT-DIET-DRINKS  PIC 999.
          20 CM-CT-ENERGY-SUPP   PIC 999.
    05 CM-DOLLAR-SALES-TABLE.

$
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trying to get around Educational ver Restriction help...

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2001-03-31T13:46:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D618A6BA854F9A60.8AC7C646D54EAEED.44EE2B771F4F4F31@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<19%w6.163945$p66.48044471@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3AC4F24B.F1C7AA28@qwest.net> <Y27x6.165603$p66.48662152@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
On Fri, 30 Mar 2001 21:40:08 GMT, "For Example: John Smith" <f@r.c>
enlightened us:

>Neither worked.  I removed the line stating "CONTAINS 76 CHARACTERS"  and
>tried the index (every variation of these two) still I get the same error.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If this is indeed your record layout:

 01 CUSTOMER-MASTER-REC.
    05 CM-CUSTOMER-ACCOUNT-NUM  PIC 99999.
    05 CM-CUSTOMER-NAME                  PIC X(25).
    05 CM-CUSTOMER-TELE-NUM          PIC X(10).
    05 CM-CASE-SALES-TABLE.
       10 CM-CASE-SALES-REC
            OCCURS 50 TIMES.
           20 CM-CS-COLA                            PIC 999.
           20 CM-CS-DIET-DRINKS              PIC 999.
           20 CM-CS-ENERGY-SUPP           PIC 999.
     05 CM-CASE-TARGET-SALES-TABLE.
         10 CM-CASE-TARGET-SALES-REC
               OCCURS 50 TIMES.
              20 CM-CT-COLA                        PIC 999.
              20 CM-CT-DIET-DRINKS          PIC 999.
              20 CM-CT-ENERGY-SUPP       PIC 999.
    05 CM-DOLLAR-SALES-TABLE.
        10 CM-DOLLAR-SALES-REC
             OCCURS 50 TIMES.
             20 CM-DS-COLA                       PIC 9999V99.
             20 CM-DS-DIET-DRINKS         PIC 9999V99.
             20 CM-DS-ENERGY-SUPP       PIC 9999V99.

This is a valid layout and all of the syntax is valid.  The only thing
you might want to verify is that the 05 levels were you are getting
your error start in at least column 12.

Good luck.

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Why do we sing "Take me out to the ball game" when we are already
there?

DON'T DRILL in the Artic National Wildlife Refuge
Read the facts and sign a petition at:

http://www.savepolarbears.org/

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Trying to get around Educational ver Restriction help...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-03-31T15:38:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9a5ij4$lh9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19%w6.163945$p66.48044471@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <3AC4F24B.F1C7AA28@qwest.net> <Y27x6.165603$p66.48662152@news3.rdc1.on.home.com> <D618A6BA854F9A60.8AC7C646D54EAEED.44EE2B771F4F4F31@lp.airnews.net> <3AC6DAE1.25EB4D86@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Depends on the compiler.  In the ANSI '85 Standard (but not the draft of the
next one), 05-levels (or any level greater than 01) needs to start in the
"B-margin" which begins with column 12.

Almost all the PC compilers have an extension that does not enforce this
rule - but other compilers do (or do with certain options set).
```
